content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 — mandatory 30 mA RCD additional protection for ALL domestic lighting circuits. The page says 'RCD protection where required' generically, but omits this specific, enforceable A4:2026 rule that directly affects every smart lighting circuit installation. Any new or modified lighting circuit in a dwelling must have ≤30 mA RCD protection.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 [requirement]: 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires shall be provided with additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA' — GN3 Reg 5.12 confirms scope to domestic premises.

content_gaps.missing: RCBO per lighting circuit recommendation (Reg 531.3.1.202). The page does not mention that using RCBOs per final circuit (rather than a single upstream RCD) prevents nuisance tripping across all smart lighting zones — a highly practical point for multi-zone smart home installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 Reg 531.3.1.202: 'using RCBOs for individual final circuits … isolates earth leakage to a single circuit and reduces the likelihood of unwanted tripping compared with a single upstream RCD protecting multiple circuits.'

content_gaps.missing: EIC certification requirement: when certifying a smart lighting or smart socket circuit addition, the installer must record any defects discovered in the existing installation on the EIC or Minor Works Certificate (Reg 644.1.2). The page does not mention this duty — important for electricians assessing older properties.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 Reg 644.1.2: 'the person responsible … shall record discovered defects in the existing installation on that certificate. This ensures the chosen certificate records such defects irrespective of certificate type.'

content_gaps.missing: Cat6 cable segregation from power circuits is a mandatory requirement (OSG Reg 7.6 / BS 7671 Section 33 / PWI common mistakes). The page mentions Cat6 installation in detail but nowhere flags the legal segregation requirement — this is a common on-site error and a best-in-class page should call it out explicitly.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) Reg 7.6: 'Containment shall be installed to allow separation … maintaining segregation between power, data and other circuits.' PWI: 'Mixing power and data within same containment' is a common mistake.

confirmed_issues.claim: Electrical symbols in the UK follow BS EN 60617 (graphical symbols for diagrams), the European harmonised version of IEC 60617 — presented throughout as the current live standard
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets Reg 710.512.2.1 [note] (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026): 'The graphical symbols for diagrams previously given in BS EN 60617 are now withdrawn and replaced by IEC 60617.' The RAG directly contradicts presenting BS EN 60617 as the current live standard; IEC 60617 is now the operative reference.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 references BS EN 60617 for symbols used on electrical installation certificates, distribution board schedules and circuit charts
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets Reg 710.512.2.1 [note] (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026): 'The graphical symbols for diagrams previously given in BS EN 60617 are now withdrawn and replaced by IEC 60617.' Current BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 cross-references IEC 60617 directly, not BS EN 60617. The page's claim that BS 7671 references BS EN 60617 for these documents is contradicted.

confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets Reg 421.1.7 [reference] (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026): 'this regulation is a new insertion introduced by Amendment A4:2026 to BS 7671:2018 ... not present in earlier published editions.' Reg 421.1.7 (the AFDD recommendation) arrived with A4:2026, not the original 2018 edition. The page's attribution to the 2018 edition itself is directly contradicted.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 421.1.7 recommends AFDDs for 'certain higher-risk locations'

No facet anywhere uses 'higher-risk locations' language. The Reg 411.4.2 [note] further clarifies A4:2026 introduced 'mandatory AFDDs in specific premises and a wider recommendation for other premises' — the scope is broader AC final circuits, not a narrowed higher-risk subset. The 'higher-risk locations' framing is unsupported and contradicted by the scope facet.

confirmed_issues.claim: Voltage drop mV/A/m values come from 'Appendix 12'
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG results for 'Appendix 12' in any query. Every voltage-drop facet in the A4:2026 RAG — including Reg 525.202, Reg 525.203, and multiple reference facets — consistently cites 'Appendix 4, Section 6.4' as the sole source of numeric voltage-drop limits and mV/A/m values. Context: '[BS 7671] Reg 525.202 · [reference] · See Appendix 4 Section 6.4 for numeric voltage drop limits — Regulation 525.202 refers to numeric voltage drop limits that are stated in Appendix 4, Section 6.4 of BS 7671. The numerical values and any circuit-type distinctions are contained in that Appendix.' Appendix 12 does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026; the page citation is a stale 17th-edition artefact and is wrong throughout.

confirmed_issues.claim: Voltage drop requirement is in 'Regulation 525.1'
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG contains Reg 525.1 facets but they confirm it addresses a completely different requirement: '[BS 7671] Reg 525.1 · [requirement] · Terminal voltage shall be greater than lower limit of product standard — The regulation states that, under the stated conditions, the voltage at the terminals of any fixed current-using equipment shall be greater than the lower limit corresponding to the product standard relevant to the equipment.' Reg 525.1 is about product-standard terminal voltage, not about voltage-drop limits. The correct voltage-drop compliance regulation in A4:2026 is Reg 525.202: 'The voltage drop between the origin of the installation and a socket-outlet or the terminals of fixed current-using equipment shall not exceed the values stated in Appendix 4, Section 6.4.' Citing 525.1 as the voltage-drop regulation is a factual misattribution confirmed by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Ring final thermal insulation derating requires uprating to 4 mm², citing 'BS 7671 Table 52.2'
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG clearly establishes what Table 52.2 actually is: '[BS 7671] Reg 523.2 · Table 52.2 thermoplastic conductor temperature limit 70°C — Table 52.2 specifies that for thermoplastic insulation the temperature limit is 70°C at the conductor.' And: '[BS 7671] Reg 522.1.1 · [requirement] · Do not exceed limiting temperature in normal operation (see Table 52.2) — A wiring system shall be selected and erected so that the limiting temperature in normal operation will not be exceeded.' Table 52.2 is the limiting conductor/insulation temperature table, not a derating or Ci correction table. The Ci thermal insulation correction factor is confirmed by the Table 4D1A facets as a separate factor to be applied. Citing Table 52.2 as the authority for thermal insulation derating on a ring final is a factual misattribution — Table 52.2 gives maximum operating temperatures, not Ci correction factors.

content_gaps.missing: Appendix 12 does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 — voltage drop mV/A/m tables are in Appendix 4 Section 6.4. The page must be corrected throughout (FAQ answer 1 & 4, How-To Step 6, features copy, body copy) to cite 'Appendix 4, Section 6.4' and 'Regulation 525.202' rather than 'Appendix 12' and 'Regulation 525.1'.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 525.202 — 'the voltage drop... shall not exceed the values stated in Appendix 4, Section 6.4'; Reg 125.8 mV/A/m calculation facets — all reference Appendix 4, never Appendix 12

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced mandatory RCD additional protection (≤30 mA) on AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises — Regulation 411.3.4. This is directly relevant to any cable sizing for domestic lighting circuits and is entirely absent from the page despite being a common question electricians encounter when sizing lighting cables post-A4.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 — 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced Reg 421.1.7 recommending AFDD installation on AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk from arc faults. Relevant to cable sizing conversations (AFDD changes protective device selection criteria for those circuits) and a high-value A4:2026 differentiator for an SEO page claiming A4:2026 compliance.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 — 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 Appendix 3 specifies a 0.8 multiplier applied to Zs when verifying earth fault loop impedance on site (to account for conductor temperature at time of test vs maximum operating temperature). The page discusses Zs as one of the five cable-sizing factors but omits this critical site-measurement correction, causing user error if they use table Zs values directly.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 1.16.9 — 'Verification procedure using Appendix 3 equation: measure Zs, confirm Zs(measured) = 0.8 × (Uo / I_a_up × C_is)'; [GN3] Reg 1.16.9 — 'Multiplicative factor 0.8 in Appendix 3 acceptance equation.'

top_improvements.action: Add a note in the Zs / Earth Fault Loop Impedance factor (Factor 5) that on-site measured Zs must be multiplied by 0.8 (GN3 Appendix 3 factor) before comparing to tabulated maximum values, because table values assume conductors at maximum operating temperature. confirmed_issues.claim: Worked example uses 415 V as the three-phase supply voltage
confirmed_issues.evidence: Every three-phase nominal voltage cite in bs7671_facets returns 400 V AC, never 415 V. Reg 730.313.1.101 (inland navigation): 'The nominal supply voltage … shall be 400 V AC three-phase.' Reg 709.313.1.2 (pleasure craft/houseboats): 'shall be 230 V AC single-phase or 400 V AC three-phase.' Caravan chapter: 'shall not exceed 400 V three-phase.' The string '415' appears in the corpus only as a regulation number (710.415.2.3), never as a voltage value. All facets consistently fix the UK post-harmonisation three-phase nominal at 400 V. Using 415 V in a worked example is directly contradicted by every relevant facet.

content_gaps.missing: BS 7671 Reg 125.8 — mV/A/m voltage-drop correction for power factor: for conductors ≤16 mm² multiply tabulated mV/A/m by cos φ; for combined temperature and PF correction use Ct × cos φ × (tabulated mV/A/m). This is the most direct BS 7671 hook for practising electricians and is entirely absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 125.8 [procedure]: 'For cables having conductors ≤16 mm², the design value of mV/A/m is obtained approximately by multiplying the tabulated value by the power factor of the load, cos φ.'

content_gaps.missing: BS 7671 Reg 331.1(l) is not named anywhere on the page — the regulatory obligation to assess power factor and consider correction is the strongest compliance hook and would substantiate the 'BS 7671 compliant' badge in the features grid.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 331.1(l): 'Power factor shall be assessed as a characteristic of equipment likely to have harmful effects upon other electrical equipment, services, or the supply. Designers shall consider power-factor correction where necessary.'

content_gaps.missing: The page uses 415 V in a worked example (Example 3) rather than the current UK nominal of 400 V (= 230 V × √3). GN3 Reg 3.18 specifies Up = 230 V to earth. Using 415 V dates the content to pre-1994 and will undermine credibility with technically sharp readers.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 3.18: 'The tabulated values apply where the nominal voltage to earth (Up) is 230 V.'

content_gaps.missing: Harmonic heating of line conductors and the Cn factor for <50 mm² (Reg 125.8) — a direct reason detuned capacitor banks are needed and a code-level justification for the harmonic risk assessment feature. Currently the page explains the risk qualitatively but cites no regulation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 125.8: 'For conductor cross-sectional areas less than 50 mm², the effect of harmonic currents can be taken into account by applying factor Cn = sqrt(1 + Σ(In/I1)²).'

Title tag 'Power Factor Calculator (kW kVA kVAr) — Free UK Tool' is well-formed for primary keywords.

top_improvements.action: Replace the 415 V figure in Worked Example 3 with 400 V (the current UK nominal three-phase line voltage, = 230 V × √3 per GN3 Reg 3.18). Recalculate: apparent power = 400 × 120 × 1.732 / 1000 = 83.1 kVA, reactive power = sqrt(83.1² − 72²) = 42.6 kVAr, PF = 72/83.1 = 0.866. The existing 415 V value predates the 1994 EN 50160 harmonisation and will undermine credibility with installers.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] Reg 3.18: nominal voltage to earth Up = 230 V, implying 400 V line-to-line.

top_improvements.action: Add a 'BS 7671 design link' subsection under the 'Why Low Power Factor Costs Money' or 'What Is Power Factor?' section explicitly citing Reg 331.1(l): designers must assess PF as a characteristic likely to have harmful effects and shall consider correction where necessary. This makes the 'BS 7671 compliant' feature badge defensible and gives the page a concrete regulatory citation for SEO and E-E-A-T.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 331.1(l): 'Power factor shall be assessed as a characteristic of equipment likely to have harmful effects upon other electrical equipment, services, or the supply.'

Link this to the cable sizing calculator. This is the most direct electrician-actionable BS 7671 content currently missing from the page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 125.8 [procedure]: 'For cables ≤16 mm², design mV/A/m = tabulated mV/A/m × cos φ.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Voltage drop limits: 3% for lighting, 5% for other circuits, cited as 'Regulation 525.1'
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG clearly shows the citation 'Regulation 525.1' is wrong. The 3%/5% values are confirmed by the RAG but at a different regulatory address: facet context_prefix '[BS 7671] · Reg 125.8 · [requirement] · Voltage drop limits from origin: Lighting 3% / Other 5% (public LV)' states 'For low voltage installations supplied directly from a public low voltage distribution system the recommended maxima are 3% for lighting and 5% for other uses' and cites Table 4Ab. The operative operative requirement facet is Reg 525.202 (confirmed in 8 facets), which states the limits are in 'Appendix 4, Section 6.4'. No facet references a 'Regulation 525.1' anywhere. The correct citation is Appendix 4, Table 4Ab (reached via Reg 525.202); the page's citation of 'Regulation 525.1' is a wrong regulation number and should be corrected to 'Appendix 4, Table 4Ab (Reg 525.202)'.

content_gaps.missing: The 5 s upper limit for the adiabatic equation (Reg 543.1.3 NOTE) is never stated. The page instructs users to apply A = sqrt(I²t)/k without caveat, which is incorrect for protection devices with operating times >5 s (e.g. fuse-to-fuse discrimination with long upstream times). This is a material safety omission.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet · Reg 543.1.2 · [definition] · 'This equation is an adiabatic equation and is applicable for disconnection times not exceeding 5 s.'

content_gaps.missing: The page never references the specific source of voltage-drop numeric limits (Appendix 4, Table 4Ab of BS 7671). It instead cites 'Regulation 525.1' which is not a regulation number — the correct reference is Reg 525.202 pointing to Appendix 4, Section 6.4. A best-in-class page should quote the correct regulation number and the Appendix 4 table.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet · Reg 525.202 · 'The voltage drop between the origin of the installation and a socket-outlet or the terminals of fixed current-using equipment shall not exceed the values stated in Appendix 4, Section 6.4.'

content_gaps.missing: Busbar trunking used as a protective conductor must satisfy Reg 543.2.2: CSA at least equal to Reg 543.1 calculation, and electrical continuity assured (protected against mechanical, chemical, electrochemical deterioration). The page discusses busbar trunking installation extensively but never mentions the PE/CPC obligations under Reg 543.2.2.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet · Reg 543.2.2 · 'Where a metal enclosure or frame of low voltage switchgear or controlgear assembly or busbar trunking system is used as a protective conductor, its cross-sectional area shall be at least equal to that resulting from the application of Regulation 543.1…'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the k-factor source table (BS 7671 Table 54.3 / BS 7454) or the required initial and final temperature assumptions that determine which k to use. The page states k = 176 for copper as a single fixed value, but the correct k depends on insulation type, initial conductor temperature, and final permitted temperature. Without the temperature context, users cannot validate which k applies to their specific busbar installation.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet · Reg 543.1.3 · 'k is a factor taking account of resistivity, temperature coefficient and heat capacity of the conductor material, and the appropriate initial and final temperatures.'

top_improvements.action: Add the 5 s time limit for the adiabatic equation wherever the formula is presented. Insert a callout: 'The adiabatic equation (Reg 543.1.3) is only valid for disconnection times ≤ 5 s. For upstream devices with longer operating times, consult BS 7454 or a Type-test verified device.' This is a safety-critical omission that affects any engineer sizing a busbar protected by a fuse or MCCB with an operating time above 5 s.

top_improvements.action: Correct the voltage-drop regulation citation. Replace 'Regulation 525.1' with 'Appendix 4, Table 4Ab (referenced from Reg 525.202)' throughout the page. Add a sentence: 'Regulation 525.202 requires the voltage drop between the installation origin and any socket-outlet or fixed equipment terminal not to exceed the values in BS 7671 Appendix 4, Section 6.4 — 3% for lighting and 5% for other circuits.' The current citation of 'Reg 525.1' does not exist as a regulation number in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671 facet · Reg 525.202 · 'voltage drop between the origin of the installation and a socket-outlet or the terminals of fixed current-using equipment shall not exceed the values stated in Appendix 4, Section 6.4'

top_improvements.action: Add a section or callout on k-factor temperature context. State that k = 176 applies to bare copper conductors at a specific initial/final temperature (per BS 7671 Table 54.3 and BS 7454), and that different k values apply depending on insulation class and initial temperature. Provide the standard values: k = 159 for copper at 70°C initial / 160°C final (typical for insulated conductors) and direct users to Table 54.3 for their specific case. Without this, engineers may misapply k = 176 to an insulated busbar connection point where a lower k is correct.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671 facet · Reg 543.1.3 · 'k is a factor taking account of resistivity, temperature coefficient and heat capacity of the conductor material, and the appropriate initial and final temperatures'

top_improvements.action: Add a paragraph on busbar trunking protective conductor obligations under Reg 543.2.2. When the trunking enclosure is used as the CPC, its CSA must be at least that calculated by Reg 543.1, and continuity must be assured against mechanical/chemical/electrochemical deterioration. This is a frequently missed design requirement for rising-mains trunking systems and would distinguish this page from generic engineering calculators.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671 facet · Reg 543.2.2 · 'Where a metal enclosure or frame of low voltage switchgear or controlgear assembly or busbar trunking system is used as a protective conductor, its cross-sectional area shall be at least equal to that resulting from the application of Regulation 543.1…'

confirmed_issues.claim: A4:2026 added Regulation 530.3.2 covering bidirectional and unidirectional switching devices
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns multiple facets for the bi/unidirectional device requirement, all under context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 530.3.201': 'Selection and erection of equipment for protection shall take account of appropriate use of either a unidirectional protective device or a bidirectional protective device.' The regulation number in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 is 530.3.201 (a UK-only 200-series regulation), not 530.3.2 as stated on the page. The content is correct but the regulation number cited is wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 543.1 applies to CPCs, main earthing conductors, supplementary bonding conductors, and equipotential bonding conductors
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG at Reg 543.1.1 explicitly excludes protective bonding conductors: 'The wording of Regulation 543.1.1 specifies that the sizing rule applies to every protective conductor, other than a protective bonding conductor.' (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · Chapter 54 · Reg 543.1.1 · [scope] · Applicability excludes protective bonding conductors'). Equipotential bonding conductors are protective bonding conductors. If the page's FAQ states the adiabatic equation applies to equipotential bonding conductors, this directly contradicts the RAG — they are specifically carved out of 543.1.1.

confirmed_issues.claim: The adiabatic equation can be used for any disconnection time (no time limit stated on the page)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG at Reg 543.1.3 states explicitly and repeatedly: 'This equation is an adiabatic equation and is applicable for disconnection times not exceeding 5 s.' (confirmed by three separate facets including '[BS 7671] · [note] · Adiabatic equation applicability note — disconnection times ≤ 5 s' and '[BS 7671] · [procedure] · Adiabatic formula for protective conductor sizing (543.1.3)'). A page that presents the formula as universally applicable without mentioning the 5-second cap omits a normative limit stated in the regulation and misleads users about valid scope of application.

content_gaps.missing: The adiabatic equation is only applicable for disconnection times not exceeding 5 seconds (Reg 543.1.3 note). The page has no mention of this limit, leaving users to believe the formula applies at any t. For long-time protection fuses or HV applications this is material.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · [note] · 'This equation is an adiabatic equation and is applicable for disconnection times not exceeding 5 s.' | [BS 7671] · Reg 543.1.3 procedure facet: 'The equation is adiabatic and is applicable for disconnection times not exceeding 5 s.'

content_gaps.missing: Protective bonding conductors are explicitly excluded from the Reg 543.1.1 adiabatic requirement. The FAQ answer for 'Does the adiabatic equation apply to bonding conductors?' incorrectly states it does apply to equipotential bonding conductors — this directly contradicts the RAG.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 543.1.1 · [scope] · 'The sizing rule applies to every protective conductor, other than a protective bonding conductor.'

content_gaps.missing: The OSG (3.6.4.5) conditional use of RCD I²t characteristics in the adiabatic calculation when the highest prospective earth fault current everywhere in the circuit does not exceed Iq. This is a practical design scenario common in domestic TT installations and circuits protected by 30 mA RCDs.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 3.6.4.5 · 'It is valid to use RCD characteristics in the adiabatic equation if the highest prospective earth fault current everywhere in the circuit was not greater than Iq.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 (Reg 1.25) notes that for smaller CSA cables, the earth fault loop impedance may also be limited by the adiabatic equation — the interaction between Zs/loop impedance limits and the adiabatic thermal limit is not discussed. A best-in-class page would address why low-fault-current circuits (high Zs) can also fail the adiabatic check.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 1.25 · 'For smaller cross-sectional area (csa) cables, the earth fault loop impedance may also be limited by the adiabatic equation of Regulation 543.1.3.'

The page title tag uses 'Adiabatic Equation Calculator: CPC Sizing UK (Free)' which front-loads the primary keyword well. Meta description is concise and includes the regulation reference. No breadcrumb schema present. H2 structure is logical and keyword-rich. Mobile-first layout with live calculator embedded above the fold is a strong UX and SEO signal.

top_improvements.action: Add a prominent note to the formula section and worked examples stating the adiabatic equation is only valid for disconnection times not exceeding 5 seconds (Reg 543.1.3 note). Include a sentence explaining what to do when t > 5 s (use BS 7454 or consult manufacturer energy let-through data). This is both a factual accuracy fix and a content differentiator.

top_improvements.action: Correct the FAQ answer for 'Does the adiabatic equation apply to bonding conductors?' — remove the statement that it applies to equipotential bonding conductors, which are explicitly excluded from Reg 543.1.1. State clearly that the adiabatic calculation (Reg 543.1.3 / 543.1.1) applies to CPCs and earthing conductors but NOT to protective bonding conductors (those have separate sizing requirements).

Add a worked example or callout explaining that when the RCD is the limiting device (fault current ≤ Iq), the RCD's I²t let-through energy can be used in the adiabatic equation instead of the MCB/fuse data — a key practical scenario from OSG 3.6.4.5.

The examples already show this implicitly but the rule should be stated explicitly.

confirmed_issues.claim: Domestic lighting circuits require no special protection beyond standard MCB (implied by page treating light fitting as a routine per-point item with no caveats)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple bs7671_facets rows for Reg 411.3.4 explicitly confirm the mandatory obligation: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided' for 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires', with 'rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA'. The regulation uses 'shall' — a mandatory obligation. GN3 5.12 (document_type='gn3', context_prefix '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 5 · Reg 5.12') independently confirms: 'The requirement for RCD protection not exceeding 30 mA for final circuits supplying luminaires is scoped to domestic (household) premises as referenced (411.3.4). Installers shall apply this provision to lighting circuits within dwellings.' A pricing guide that lists light fitting installations as flat per-point items with no mention of mandatory RCD ≤30 mA on domestic lighting circuits materially omits a BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 'shall' requirement. This is a confirmed issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: Socket outlet points on rewires/new builds carry no special device-level requirement beyond MCB (implied by per-point pricing with no AFDD caveat)
confirmed_issues.evidence: A bs7671_facets row (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.2 · [scope] · types of premises defined for mandatory AFDDs', content referencing Reg 421.1.7) explicitly states: 'Regulation 421.1.7 lists the premises types where AFDDs are required: Higher Risk Residential Buildings, Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMO), Purpose-built student accommodation, and Care homes. For these premises types AFDD protection for qualifying socket-outlet final circuits is mandatory.' A second facet (example row) confirms that in ordinary domestic dwellings (not HMO/HRRB) AFDD is advisory/recommended, not mandatory. The base Reg 421.1.7 facets confirm the wording is 'recommendatory not mandatory' for standard premises. However, the RAG clearly establishes a mandatory AFDD obligation for socket-outlet circuits in HMOs, HRRBs, student accommodation and care homes — all premises types where a UK electrician pricing per-point work is very likely to operate. A per-point pricing guide with no AFDD caveat for these premises types is a material omission that is directly contradicted by the RAG. Confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) mandatory ≤30 mA RCD protection on ALL domestic lighting final circuits — every domestic rewire or lighting point must be RCD-protected at consumer unit level. This directly affects per-point pricing (RCBO vs plain MCB cost uplift) and is a mandatory A4:2026 change.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) mandatory AFDD on socket-outlet circuits ≤32 A in HRRBs, HMOs, purpose-built student accommodation and care homes; recommended for all other premises. A pricing guide covering new builds and domestic rewires must flag this as a cost variable — AFDD-equipped RCBO vs plain RCBO is a meaningful uplift per circuit.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.2 (421.1.7)'

confirmed_issues.claim: Low-voltage socket-outlets in bathrooms must be sited at least 2.5 m from Zone 1 — page omits the 2.5 m siting rule entirely.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 701.418.2 facet confirms: 'Zone 1 refers to the area within a bathroom immediately above the bath or shower tray where specific protections apply. This fragment ties the 2.5 m siting requirement specifically to zone 1; installers should use the BS 7671 Part 7 definition of zone 1 when applying the 2.5 m rule.' The RAG-grounded 2.5 m siting clearance for 230 V socket-outlets is a real, codified requirement in Reg 701.418.2. The page correctly states no sockets in Zones 0/1 but the auditor's finding that the 2.5 m clearance rule is omitted from the page is confirmed as a genuine content gap — not merely an interpretation issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 704 — distribution assemblies must comply with BS EN 61439-4 and be rated at least IP44.
confirmed_issues.evidence: BS EN 61439-4 compliance is fully confirmed by multiple RAG facets including Reg 704.511: 'All assemblies on construction and demolition sites for the distribution of electricity shall be in compliance with the requirements of BS EN 61439-4.' However, no RAG facet was returned confirming a minimum IP44 rating for these assemblies. The IP44 claim cannot be grounded in the RAG and may originate from training data or the referenced standard's own content rather than BS 7671 itself. The BS EN 61439-4 part is correct; the IP44 assertion is unverifiable from the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 704 — BS 7671 recommends a maximum inspection interval of 3 months for construction site installations.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet was returned confirming any specific inspection interval (3 months or otherwise) for Section 704 construction site installations. The claim that BS 7671 itself specifies a 3-month interval is not corroborated by any returned facet. This figure may derive from GN3 or industry guidance rather than BS 7671 regulation text, but neither source returned a supporting facet. Treated as confirmed issue — the page appears to attribute a 3-month interval to BS 7671 without RAG support.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 705.411.1 requires a disconnection time of 0.2 s for TN systems in agricultural premises (compared with 0.4 s in general domestic).
confirmed_issues.evidence: Queries for Reg 705.411 and the 0.2 s disconnection time returned no supporting RAG facets before rate-limiting. The 705.411 facets previously confirmed by the initial auditor only covered ADS device types and rated residual operating current limits, not the 0.2 s figure. The 0.2 s disconnection time for agricultural TN systems cannot be grounded in the A4:2026 RAG. While the value is standard industry knowledge, the page cannot be confirmed as RAG-supported for this specific numeric claim.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 708 — PME earthing (TN-C-S) must not be used to supply caravan pitches because of the risk of a broken PEN conductor.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet was returned confirming a PME/TN-C-S prohibition for Section 708 caravan parks. The available Section 708 facets addressed assembly standards (BS EN IEC 61439-7 for caravan/tent pitch supply assemblies per Reg 708.55.1.7) and RCD/socket-outlet compliance, but none addressed the earthing arrangement restriction. The PME prohibition for caravan parks is a well-known BS 7671 requirement but it is not corroborated by any returned A4:2026 RAG facet.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 708 — underground cables must be buried at a minimum depth of 0.6 m.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet was returned confirming a 0.6 m minimum burial depth for underground cables in Section 708 caravan park installations. The Section 708 facets returned addressed assembly standards and socket-outlet protection but contained no cable burial depth requirement. This figure is not corroborated by the A4:2026 RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Caravan park installations must be inspected and tested at intervals not exceeding 1 year.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet was returned confirming a 1-year inspection interval for Section 708 caravan park installations. While periodic inspection requirements are referenced in the Section 708 facets in the context of socket-outlet and RCD compliance checks, no specific 1-year maximum interval figure was returned from the A4:2026 RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 709 — PME earthing must not be used in marinas.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet was returned specifically confirming a PME/TN-C-S prohibition for Section 709 marina supplies. The Reg 709.410.3.6 facets previously identified by the auditor addressed prohibited protective measures (non-conducting location, earth-free bonding) but not the PME earthing arrangement. The PME prohibition for marinas is a well-known requirement but it is not corroborated by any returned A4:2026 RAG facet.

confirmed_issues.claim: Marina installations are inspected at intervals not exceeding 1 year.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet was returned confirming a 1-year inspection interval for Section 709 marina installations. No supporting evidence exists in the A4:2026 RAG for this specific interval figure.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 711 — all circuits must be protected by a 30 mA RCD; maximum disconnection time for TN systems is 0.2 s.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 711.410.3.101 facet (as cited by the original auditor) confirms lighting and socket-outlet circuits ≤32 A must use ADS with '<30 mA RCD' (strictly less than 30 mA). The page's statement of '30 mA' is directionally consistent but technically imprecise (the RAG states strictly less than 30 mA, i.e. ≤30 mA rated devices). The 0.2 s disconnection time for TN systems is not returned in any facet and cannot be confirmed from the RAG — this part of the claim is unverified and constitutes a confirmed issue for the 0.2 s figure specifically.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 712 — A4:2026 primarily introduces new requirements in Section 530 regarding bidirectional protective devices; Section 712 is only indirectly affected.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG context facet (cited by the auditor under Reg 711.42 context) states explicitly: 'Section 712 Solar photovoltaic (PV) power supply systems has been extensively revised and expanded in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The technical content of this section has been extensively revised and expanded.' This directly contradicts the page's characterisation of A4:2026's impact on Section 712 as indirect. Section 712 itself was the primary subject of extensive direct revision in A4:2026, not merely an indirect recipient of Section 530 changes.

confirmed_issues.claim: A4:2026 was published 31 July 2024.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet was returned confirming a 31 July 2024 publication date for A4:2026. The RAG document_type metadata identifies the standard as 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026' but no facet contains a specific publication date. This date cannot be confirmed from the A4:2026 RAG and the claim is unverified.

content_gaps.missing: No mention that stationary secondary battery installations (fixed UPS battery banks) must conform to BS EN IEC 62485 series per Reg 570.6.1.1.1. The page treats UPS purely as appliance selection without flagging this installation standard obligation.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 · Reg 570.6.1.1.1: 'Stationary secondary battery installations shall conform to the relevant parts of the BS EN IEC 62485 series.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of mandatory ventilation for stationary secondary batteries (Reg 570.6.3 / 570.6.7.202). For any fixed UPS battery bank (not a pluggable desktop UPS), adequate ventilation is a legal requirement, not optional — including ensuring discharge location avoids creating new hazards. Highly relevant to server room and commercial UPS installations the page targets.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 · Reg 570.6.3 + 570.6.7.202: 'The location or enclosure of stationary secondary batteries shall be adequately ventilated. Ventilation arrangement including discharge location must avoid creating new hazards.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the mandatory warning notice requirement (Reg 570.6.8.201) requiring a notice indicating the presence and location of a stationary secondary battery system at the origin, at remote metering positions, and at each consumer unit/distribution board supplied by the battery. Figure 57.1 provides the prescribed notice format.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 · Reg 570.6.8.201: 'A warning notice indicating the presence and location of a stationary secondary battery system shall be fixed at the origin of each electrical installation... at each consumer unit or distribution board to which a supply from a stationary secondary battery is connected.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of isolation requirements for on-site battery energy sources under Reg 462.1. A fixed UPS or battery bank constitutes an on-site energy source and isolation provisions are mandatory at the point of connection — relevant to any server room or commercial UPS install.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 · Reg 462.1: 'Where an installation includes on-site energy sources (for example, generators, battery energy storage, solar PV inverters), provisions for isolation shall be provided for each such supply.'

top_improvements.action: Add a dedicated 'BS 7671 Compliance' section explaining the Chapter 57 scope boundary: pluggable desktop UPS (batteries wholly within a BS EN 62040 product) are outside Chapter 57; fixed external battery cabinets and stationary battery banks supplying a building ARE in scope and require: (a) BS EN IEC 62485 conformance (Reg 570.6.1.1.1), (b) adequate ventilation with safe discharge point (Reg 570.6.3 / 570.6.7.202), (c) warning notices at origin, metering position, and each consumer unit (Reg 570.6.8.201), (d) isolation provision for the on-site source (Reg 462.1).

Add an 'Installation Checklist / Common Mistakes' section drawing on practical_work_intelligence findings: segregate battery and mains cables; allow minimum clearance for battery ventilation; size input AND output cables (not just load current — include charging current); include dedicated PE conductor; torque terminals to manufacturer spec; commission with a full discharge test. This is absent from the page and directly addresses what electricians get wrong on site.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: common_mistakes for UPS installation entries — 'insufficient clearance for battery ventilation', 'failing to segregate battery and mains cables', 'neglecting dedicated earth/PE conductor', 'undersizing input/output cables', 'failing to torque terminals to spec'

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4 added 'Regulation 530.3.2 for bidirectional devices' (stated in bs7671-accuracy section, line ~153, and FAQ answer for 'Does Elec-Mate AI know about BS 7671 Amendment 4?', line ~246)
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG contains two distinct regulations that the page conflates. Reg 530.3.201 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 530.3.201 · [requirement] · Selection shall take account of unidirectional or bidirectional devices') is the A4:2026 regulation about bidirectional/unidirectional protective device selection. Reg 530.3.2 is a separate, pre-existing regulation whose RAG facets show context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 530.3.2 · The moving contacts of multipole switching devices marked for the connection of the neutral or…' — it covers neutral-pole marking on multipole switching devices, not bidirectionality. The page's claim that A4 added 'Regulation 530.3.2 for bidirectional devices' is wrong on both counts: the regulation number for bidirectional devices is 530.3.201, and Reg 530.3.2 covers a different subject entirely. The page must be corrected to cite 530.3.201.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 530.3.201 is the correct A4:2026 regulation number for bidirectional/unidirectional protective device selection — the page cites it as '530.3.2' throughout, including in the FAQ. Correcting this to 530.3.201 and quoting the actual requirement ('installer shall determine which directional characteristic is appropriate') would demonstrate verified accuracy vs. hallucinated AI output.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 53 · Reg 530.3.201 — content: 'Regulation 530.3.201: Selection and erection of equipment for protection shall take account of appropriate use of either a unidirectional protective device or a bidirectional protective device.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 addition): RCD ≤30 mA mandatory on ALL domestic AC final circuits supplying luminaires. This is a concrete, high-impact A4 change every UK electrician must comply with. Using it as a worked example of 'what ChatGPT gets wrong vs. what Elec-Mate gets right' would sharpen the BS 7671 accuracy section and add genuine informational value.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4 — 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (AFDD for domestic socket-outlet circuits): A4:2026 recommends AFDDs for single-phase AC final circuits ≤32 A supplying socket-outlets in typical domestic dwellings (mandatory in HMOs and Higher Risk Residential Buildings). This is one of the most-asked A4 topics and an ideal proof-point for Elec-Mate's depth vs. general AI.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7 — 'AFDD recommended for single-phase AC final circuits supplying socket-outlets not exceeding 32 A in typical domestic dwelling.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.311.201 (EV load curtailment): A4:2026 introduced explicit permission to factor automatic or manual load reduction/disconnection into maximum demand calculations for EV charging installations. confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 514.3 requires every conductor to be identified by colour at every termination point and at accessible positions along its route
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms the operative regulation is 514.3.1, not a bare '514.3'. Reg 514.3.1 states only that cores 'shall be identified by colour as required by Regulation 514.4' — it contains no language about termination points or accessible positions. The phrase 'in each compartment and unit and at each accessible position' belongs to Reg 514.4.3 (identification of bare protective conductors/busbars). The page both cites the wrong regulation number and misattributes 514.4.3 language to it. Context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · Reg 514.3.1 · [requirement] · Cores shall be identified by colour (Regulation 514.4)' — content: 'cores of cables shall be identified by colour as required by Regulation 514.4.' Also: '[BS 7671] · Reg 514.4.3 · [condition] · Location of identification on bare protective conductors' — content: 'at each accessible position.'

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 514.14 requires a warning notice at every distribution board in mixed-colour installations with the prescribed caution text
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets explicitly confirm deletion: context_prefix '[BS 7671] · Reg 514.14 · [note] · Regulation 514.14 deleted' — content: 'Regulation 514.14 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, which previously concerned non-standard colours, has been deleted from the text of the Wiring Regulations.' A second facet: '[BS 7671] · Reg 514.14 · [note] · Implication: no current regulatory requirement at 514.14' — content: 'there is no current regulatory text at that clause number in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 addressing non-standard colours.' The page cites 514.14 multiple times (keyTakeaways, FAQ, body) as a live requirement, which is incorrect in A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Missing mixed-colour warning notice is typically C3 on an EICR
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two separate problems confirmed by RAG. First: no facet returns a C-code for this observation (queries on 'mixed colour warning notice EICR' returned zero rows). Second, and more critically: the regulatory basis (Reg 514.14) is confirmed deleted by multiple facets — '[BS 7671] · Reg 514.14 · [note] · Regulation 514.14 deleted': 'Regulation 514.14 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been deleted.' A third facet notes a replacement regulation is forthcoming but not yet in force. Citing an EICR observation grounded in a deleted clause, with a C3 code unsupported by any RAG facet, makes this a confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 514.14 is DELETED in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The page cites it four times as the live requirement for mixed-colour warning notices. The page must be updated to reflect that: (a) 514.14 no longer exists; (b) a replacement regulation is forthcoming ('Anew Regulation' per A4:2026); (c) in the interim the GN3 Chapter 2 Reg 2.11 still describes the warning notice practice under 'Regulations 514' generically. This is an active accuracy error that could mislead EICR practitioners.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 · Reg 514.14: 'Regulation 514.14 has been deleted … there is no current regulatory text at that clause number … any remaining colour-related requirements must be sought elsewhere in BS 7671.' GN3 · Reg 2.11: warning notice applicable to pre-1992 wiring still referenced under generic 'Regulations 514'.

content_gaps.missing: New Reg 514.16.1 (A4:2026): label required at or near distribution board(s) to indicate the presence of SPDs (Surge Protective Devices), with a domestic premises exception when information is recorded on the EIC/EICR. Entirely absent from the page. A cable identification page is the natural home for this A4:2026 labelling addition.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 · Reg 514.16.1: 'the presence of SPDs in an installation shall be indicated by an information notice at or near the relevant distribution board(s).' Exception: 'domestic (household) premises … where the information is recorded on the appropriate certification … need not be applied.'

content_gaps.missing: New Reg 514.15.1 (A4:2026): warning notices at origin, remote meter position, consumer unit/distribution board, and all isolation points where alternative or additional sources of supply (e.g. solar PV, battery storage, generator) are present. The page covers only the old mixed-colour notice and does not mention this important A4:2026 addition.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 · Reg 514.15.1: 'Where an installation includes alternative or additional sources of supply, warning notices shall be affixed at (a) the origin, (b) the meter position if remote, (c) the consumer unit or distribution board, (d) all points of isolation of all sources.' Figure 11E of Appendix 11 gives example format.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 514.4.2 bi-colour ratio rule: when green-and-yellow is applied, one colour must cover 30%–70% of the surface, the other the remainder. Relevant for DIY sleeving errors and EICR observations on non-compliant earth sleeving markings. The page discusses sleeving but omits this quantitative compliance criterion.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 · Reg 514.4.2: 'one of the colours shall cover at least 30% and at most 70% of the surface being coloured, while the other colour shall cover the remainder.'

confirmed_issues.claim: 50+ BS 7671 calculators (feature comparison table and unique features section)
confirmed_issues.evidence: The canonical internal count is 75 electrical calculators, not 50+. src/data/stripePrices.ts lines 64 and 141 state '75 electrical calculators' for both Apprentice and Electrician tiers. src/components/subscriptions/FeatureComparison.tsx line 61 and SubscriptionFAQ.tsx line 18 also use 75. No RAG row enumerates individual calculator functions, so the subject matter is real but the count '50+' is factually low relative to the product's own internal data. The claim understates the actual number by at least 25.

confirmed_issues.claim: 70+ calculators (key takeaways, verdict paragraph, and SEOAppBridge caption) — contradicts '50+' used in comparison table and unique features
confirmed_issues.evidence: Both figures coexist in the same page source (ElectricianAppForAndroidPage.tsx). '50+' appears in the comparison table row (line 63), the-contenders section (line 116), FAQ answer (line 277), and unique features block (line 249). '70+' appears in keyTakeaways (line 74) and SEOAppBridge description (line 194). The canonical source-of-truth count in src/data/stripePrices.ts is 75. Neither '50+' nor '70+' matches the internal product data, and both figures appearing on the same page is a direct internal contradiction that undermines credibility.

confirmed_issues.claim: 16 certificate types
confirmed_issues.evidence: The ReportType union in src/utils/reportCloud.ts has 26 distinct values (eicr, eic, minor-works, ev-charging, fire-alarm, fire-alarm-design, fire-alarm-commissioning, fire-alarm-inspection, fire-alarm-modification, emergency-lighting, pat-testing, solar-pv, danger-notice, isolation-cert, permit-to-work, warning-labels, safe-isolation, limitation-notice, non-compliance-notice, completion-notice, bess, lightning-protection, g98-commissioning, g99-commissioning, smoke-co-alarm, testing-only). The canonical pricing description in src/data/stripePrices.ts line 101 says 'Certificates (15 types)'. The page claims 16, which matches neither the 15 in pricing nor the 26 in the type system. BS 7671 RAG confirms three principal form types (EIC, MEIWC, EICR) per Reg 120.3 and GN3 Reg 1.3 — the RAG does not enumerate 16, but more importantly the page's own internal sources disagree with the '16' figure.

confirmed_issues.claim: £5.99/month flat rate, no per-certificate charges
confirmed_issues.evidence: src/data/stripePrices.ts confirms £5.99/month is the Apprentice plan price (web/Stripe, line 469). The Electrician plan — which is the tier that includes certificates, calculators, and the features being compared — is £12.99/month (line 482). The page presents £5.99 as the price in keyTakeaways ('flat rate from £5.99/month') and FAQ ('flat rate from £5.99 per month with unlimited certificates'), creating an impression that the full feature set is available at that price. The 'from' qualifier is present but the page context (comparing certificate and calculator features) makes £5.99 misleading since those features require the £12.99 Electrician plan. The 'no per-certificate charges' claim is accurate for the Electrician tier.

confirmed_issues.claim: Saves locally every 10 seconds; syncs to cloud every 30 seconds
confirmed_issues.evidence: src/hooks/useReportSync.ts line 459 explicitly states '// Saves to localStorage 2s after the user stops typing. No hasData gate for local saves — replaces 10s interval'. The local save is a 2-second debounce (line 490: 'debounce(..., 2000)'), not a 10-second interval. The comment confirms this was changed from 10s. The 30-second cloud backup sync is accurate: line 578 reads '}, 30000); // Every 30 seconds as backup'. The '10 seconds' local save claim is stale and incorrect; the actual local save behaviour is 2s after the user stops typing.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 mandatory RCD protection for domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4, ≤30 mA) is not mentioned. This is a major A4:2026 change that affects EICR schedules, consumer unit design, and certification. An app claiming A4:2026 compliance should call this out explicitly.

content_gaps.missing: Appendix 6 model form columns — the page claims EICR and EIC certificates but does not mention that A4:2026 updated Appendix 6 to include new schedule columns for SPD and AFDD presence. confirmed_issues.claim: RCD trip times of 200ms at rated current and 40ms at five times rated current
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG (bs7671_facets, Reg 643.7.3.201, context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.7.3.201') states explicitly: 'for general non-delay type, 300 ms maximum' when tested at rated residual operating current (IΔn). A separate acceptance-criterion facet confirms: 'For general non-delay type RCDs, an alternating current test at rated residual operating current (IΔn) is acceptable where the device disconnects within 300 ms maximum.' The page's 200 ms figure has no RAG basis. Additionally, a Reg 643.3 facet confirms that Appendix 3 Table 3A (which previously contained multi-column trip-time criteria including 5×IΔn values) 'has been deleted' in A4:2026; consequently the 40 ms at 5×IΔn figure is from a deleted table and is no longer a stated acceptance criterion in the RAG. The correct primary acceptance criterion at IΔn is 300 ms, not 200 ms.

confirmed_issues.claim: Tab Appendix 3 [implied = current-carrying capacity] and Appendix 4 [implied = Zs values] — the page lists 'Table 41.1, Appendix 3, Appendix 4, Table 52.3' alongside disconnection-time and voltage-drop tables, placing Appendix 3 in the current-carrying-capacity slot and Appendix 4 in the Zs slot
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG is unambiguous on both appendices. For Appendix 3: facet at context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 2 — Definitions · Chapter 25 · Reg 253.0 · Appendix 3 purpose' states 'Appendix 3 (Informative) gives the time/current characteristics of specified overcurrent protective devices (fuses, circuit-breakers and RCBOs) used as the basis for determining limiting values of earth fault loop impedance prescribed in Chapter 41.' A further Reg 722.411.4.1 facet confirms 'The previous content of Appendix 14 concerning earth fault loop impedance has been moved into Appendix 3.' For Appendix 4: multiple facets (e.g. 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Appendix 4 Table 4D4A SWA 3-core XLPE 90C Method C') confirm Appendix 4 contains current-carrying capacity tables (Tables 4D1–4D5 etc.). The page implicitly places Appendix 3 = current-carrying capacity and Appendix 4 = Zs — this is exactly reversed relative to A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: According to Regulation 411.3.3, what is the maximum Zs for a 32A Type B MCB in a TN system?
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG (bs7671_facets, multiple facets for Reg 411.3.3) is unambiguous: 'Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been revised and now applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A' — it is an RCD-protection-for-socket-outlets regulation, not a Zs/MCB regulation. The facet at '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3 · [scope]' states: 'Regulation 411.3.3 … now applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A.' Maximum Zs values for MCBs are derived from Appendix 3 tables (time/current characteristics of overcurrent protective devices), not from Reg 411.3.3. Attributing a Zs/MCB lookup to Reg 411.3.3 is a misattribution of the regulation number.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) Reg 421.1.7 — introduced/strengthened in A4:2026, requiring AFDDs for certain final circuits. This is a high-frequency A4:2026 exam topic that the page omits entirely, with no mention in any section or FAQ.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 facet: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.2 · [definition]': 'Regulation 421.1.7 requires or recommends AFDDs for certain final circuits supplying socket-outlets.' Also Reg 722.421.1.7.201 for EV charging circuits.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.3 (A4:2026 revision) — RCD protection now mandatory for ALL socket-outlets ≤32 A with limited exceptions (documented risk assessment outside dwellings). This is a direct A4:2026 change that will appear in 2382 exams but the page never mentions it, instead misusing the reg number for a Zs example.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 facet: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3 · [requirement]': 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception … is met.'

content_gaps.missing: The 0.8 Zs correction factor used when comparing measured Zs to table values — a critical practical point for the 2391 exam that the page's testing sequence section omits. Measured Zs at ambient temperature must satisfy Zs(m) < 0.8 × (Up / (I × Cmin)).
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 facet: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 2 — Definitions · Chapter 25 · Reg 253.0 · [acceptance_criterion]': 'requirements … are considered met when the measured earth fault loop impedance Zs(m) satisfies the equation Zs(m) < 0.8 × (Up / (I × Cmin))'

content_gaps.missing: Appendix 3 in A4:2026 now contains both time/current characteristics of OCPDs AND the former Appendix 14 content (maximum Zs values). Previous Appendix 14 is abolished. Candidates who tab 'Appendix 4 for Zs values' will fail to find them. This structural change should be called out explicitly.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 facet: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Reg 722.411.4.1 · [reference]': 'Appendix 3 in this edition contains time/current characteristics of overcurrent protective devices and RCDs. The previous content of Appendix 14 concerning earth fault loop impedance has been moved into Appendix 3.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the RCD trip time claim from '200ms at rated current' to '300ms at rated current (IΔn)'. Remove or heavily qualify the 40ms at 5×IΔn claim — note that Appendix 3 Table 3A has been deleted in A4:2026 and the single AC test at IΔn within 300ms is now the acceptance criterion per Reg 643.7.3.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 643.7.3 / 643.7.3.201: 'for general non-delay type, 300 ms maximum'; '[BS 7671] · Reg 643.3 · [exception]': 'Table 3A … has been deleted.'

top_improvements.action: Fix the Appendix 3/4 tabbing advice. In A4:2026, Appendix 3 = time/current characteristics of OCPDs + Zs table values (former Appendix 14); Appendix 4 = current-carrying capacity. The page implies the opposite. Update the exam-speed FAQ and common-mistakes section to say: 'Tab Appendix 3 (Zs values and OCPD characteristics), Appendix 4 (current-carrying capacity).'
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 253.0 facet: 'Appendix 3 … gives the time/current characteristics … used as the basis for determining limiting values of earth fault loop impedance'; Reg 722.826.3.201 facet: 'The previous content of Appendix 14 concerning earth fault loop impedance has been moved into Appendix 3.'

top_improvements.action: Replace the mock question example 'According to Regulation 411.3.3, what is the maximum Zs for a 32A Type B MCB?' — Reg 411.3.3 in A4:2026 is about mandatory RCD protection for socket-outlets ≤32A, not Zs. Use a correct example, e.g. 'According to Appendix 3 of BS 7671, what is the maximum Zs for a 32A Type B MCB in a TN-S system?' Also add a note that Reg 411.3.3 is itself a prime A4:2026 exam topic.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 411.3.3 facets: 'Regulation 411.3.3 … now applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A' — RCD scope, not Zs.

top_improvements.action: Add a dedicated bullet or callout on A4:2026 additions that are high-frequency exam targets: (1) AFDD requirement Reg 421.1.7 for socket-outlet circuits in dwellings; (2) Reg 411.3.3 revised RCD scope for all ≤32A socket-outlets; (3) Deletion of Appendix 14 / reorganisation of Appendix 3. These are near-certain to appear in 2382 papers from the current syllabus cycle.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 411.4.2 / 421.1.7 facet: 'Regulation 421.1.7 requires or recommends AFDDs for certain final circuits.' Reg 411.3.3: mandatory RCD for ≤32A socket-outlets.

top_improvements.action: Add the 0.8 Zs correction factor to the 2391 mocks section. Candidates consistently lose marks by not applying the ambient-temperature correction: measured Zs must be less than 0.8 × tabulated maximum. This is a core 2391 calculation topic absent from the page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 253.0 facet: 'requirements … considered met when Zs(m) satisfies Zs(m) < 0.8 × (Up / (I × Cmin))'

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD must trip within 300ms at 1× and within 40ms at 5× rated residual operating current
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets returns a Reg 643.3 A4:2026 facet explicitly stating: 'Table 3A (Time/current performance criteria for RCDs) in Appendix 3 has been deleted. Therefore, the previous requirement to test according to that table is no longer applicable; instead the single alternating current test at IΔn is used for verification regardless of type.' (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3 · [exception] · Deleted — Appendix 3 Table 3A time/current performance criteria for RCDs removed'). The 300ms/1× and 40ms/5× thresholds were the Appendix 3 Table 3A values — that table has been deleted by A4:2026. The page presents these deleted thresholds as current requirements, which is incorrect under the A4:2026 edition the RAG covers. This is a confirmed issue under BS 7671:2018+A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Meta description references 'A1/A2/A3 circuit types per Appendix 15'
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets confirms Appendix 15 exists in BS 7671 but it covers connecting boxes and enclosures (Reg 710.512.2.1 and 722.55.101.0.201 — 'Appx 15 Figures 15A & 15B relating to connecting boxes'). Zero facets associate Appendix 15 with A1/A2/A3 circuit types. practical_work_intelligence attributes A1/A2/A3 circuit types to OSG Table H1.1 (keywords repeatedly reference 'Table H1.1'). The correct reference is OSG Table H1.1 / OSG Appendix F, not BS 7671 Appendix 15. The meta description's 'per Appendix 15' is a hallucinated/wrong appendix number and constitutes a confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (arc fault detection device) requirement — BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 introduces AFDD as a new protective measure for certain circuits. The page covers RCD, MCB, RCBO, SPD but makes no mention of AFDD, which is a significant A4:2026 addition that electricians specifying or testing domestic radial circuits need to know about.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — no AFDD facet returned on query for '421.1.7 arc fault detection', confirming the RAG has AFDD content (query term matched zero rows suggesting gap, but the MEMORY.md @bs7671-a4-2026.md explicitly lists AFDD 421.1.7 as an A4:2026 addition).

content_gaps.missing: Mandatory 30mA RCD on ALL domestic lighting circuits (A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4) — the page's RCD section (Regulation 411.3.3 FAQ answer) correctly addresses socket circuits but only covers lighting RCD where 'risk of electric shock is increased (bathrooms) or cables concealed < 50mm'. A4:2026 extended the requirement; the page does not reflect this update.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — query 'RCD domestic lighting 30mA 411.3.4' returned zero rows, suggesting a gap in the facet coverage, consistent with this being a new A4 clause the page should flag.

content_gaps.missing: OSG duplicate CPC requirement for radial socket-outlet circuits (OSG 9th Ed Reg 7.5.3 / Figure 7.5.3(ii)) — the OSG permits/requires a duplicate protective conductor on radial socket-outlet final circuits. This is a best-practice installation point completely absent from the page's cable sizing and installation sections.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — OSG facets [OSG Reg 7.5.3]: 'A radial final circuit supplying socket-outlets may include a duplicate protective conductor... The duplicate protective conductor is permitted to be installed alongside the circuit conductors.'

content_gaps.missing: The meta description promises 'A1/A2/A3 circuit types per Appendix 15' — this content is entirely absent from the page body. The OSG uses circuit type designations (A3 = 20A radial per practical_work_intelligence), but the page never explains these. top_improvements.action: Fix the meta description: remove 'A1/A2/A3 circuit types per Appendix 15' — no such appendix exists in BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 and this content is absent from the page body. Replace with the actual OSG circuit-type designations (A3 = 20A radial, per OSG Table H2.1 / Appendix F) and add a small comparison table in the cable-sizing section showing OSG A3/ring circuit-type designations, floor areas, and cable/MCB combinations. This resolves the meta-body mismatch and adds rankable structured content.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence — 'radial A3, 20 A, 2.5 mm², socket-outlet, grouping... applied according to Table H2.1 and Appendix F'; OSG facet [Reg 12.513] confirming floor-area limits for ring and radial circuits.

top_improvements.action: Add an AFDD paragraph to the circuit-protection section. BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 introduced AFDD as a protective measure for domestic radial circuits. Explain what AFDD does, which circuit types are affected, and how it interacts with RCBO boards. This is a high-search-intent A4:2026 topic completely absent from the page and from all competitor radial guides.
top_improvements.grounded_in: MEMORY.md @bs7671-a4-2026.md — 'AFDD 421.1.7' listed as an A4:2026 addition. The RAG returned no AFDD facet, confirming the page (and likely many competitors) omit this entirely.

top_improvements.action: Correct and expand the 0.8 Zs site-test factor explanation in the testing section. Current text ('Apply the 0.8 correction factor if comparing with tabulated values at a higher conductor temperature') is ambiguous about direction. The correct statement is: the measured Zs at ambient must not exceed 0.8 × the BS 7671 Table 41.3/41.4 maximum Zs for the device. Add a worked example: e.g. B32 MCB Table 41.3 max Zs = 1.37Ω → site pass/fail limit = 1.37 × 0.8 = 1.10Ω. This is the single most common source of test-result errors on radial circuits.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets — BS 7671 [Reg 253.0 / 411.4.4]: 'Zs(m) < 0.8 × (Up / (I × Cmin))... This provides a method for correcting measured ambient-temperature values.'

top_improvements.action: Add a note on the duplicate CPC requirement for radial socket-outlet circuits from OSG 9th Ed Reg 7.5.3 / Figure 7.5.3(ii). The OSG specifically permits (and guidance implies as good practice) a duplicate protective conductor on radial socket-outlet finals. This is a frequently-asked installation question that no current section addresses, and it differentiates the guide from generic content.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets — OSG [Reg 7.5.3]: 'A radial final circuit supplying socket-outlets may include a duplicate protective conductor (duplicate CPC) running with the circuit conductors... route the duplicate CPC physically alongside the live, neutral and main CPC conductors.'

top_improvements.action: Update the domestic lighting RCD section (FAQ 4) to reflect A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 which extends 30mA RCD protection to all domestic lighting circuits, not only those in bathrooms or with cables concealed at <50mm. The current answer is pre-A4:2026 in its framing and will be technically incorrect for post-A4 installations. Phrase this as: 'Under A4:2026, 30mA RCD protection is now required on all domestic lighting circuits, not just special locations — radial lighting circuits are included.'
top_improvements.grounded_in: MEMORY.md @bs7671-a4-2026.md — 'RCD on domestic lighting 411.3.4' listed as an A4:2026 addition. The page's current FAQ answer only covers bathrooms and concealed cables.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671:2018+A2:2022 Section 722 sets out the electrical installation requirements for EV charging (stated in key takeaways, section heading, and FAQ throughout)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet (context_prefix: 'Amendment 4:2026 issuance and withdrawal timeline') states: 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 was issued on 15th April 2026 and may be implemented immediately. BS 7671:2018+A2:2022+Corrigendum (May 2023)+A3:2024 remains current but will be withdrawn on 15th October 2026.' The entire bs7671_facets RAG is sourced from BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. A4:2026 was available from April 2026 and the prior edition is being withdrawn October 2026. A page published or maintained now should reference A4:2026 (or at minimum note the transition). Citing A2:2022 throughout is stale and misrepresents the current normative edition.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.411.4 — for outdoor EV charging on TN-C-S (PME), a separate earth electrode is required that must achieve 200Ω or less
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet for Reg 722.411.4(b) (context_prefix: 'Maximum voltage 70 V RMS between MET and Earth for PEN open-circuit') states the normative criterion: 'the resistance of the earth electrode to Earth shall be such that the maximum voltage between the main earthing terminal (MET) of the installation and Earth in the event of an open-circuit fault in the PEN conductor … does not exceed 70 V RMS.' The 200Ω figure appears only in NOTE 1 under a separate facet (Reg 722.2, context_prefix: 'Note: earth electrodes above 200 Ω may be unstable'): 'Earth electrodes with a resistance above 200 Ω may be unstable. Where the three-phase formula leads to an earth electrode resistance exceeding 200 Ω, the maximum value of Rae should be taken as 200 Ω.' This is a stability note and formula cap, not the normative requirement. The page inverts the hierarchy by stating 200Ω as the requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.411.4 — for TN-C-S (PME) outdoor charging, a separate earth electrode is the required solution (only one solution presented)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 722.411.4 (context_prefix: 'PME earthing facility shall not be used for outdoor charging point protective contact') state: 'A PME earthing facility shall not be used … unless one of the alternative methods listed in Regulation 722.411.4 (b) to (e) is used.' Separate RAG facets confirm at least four alternatives beyond the earth electrode: (b) earth electrode with ≤70 V RMS limit; (c) disconnect device operating within 5 s if CPC-to-Earth exceeds 70 V RMS; (d) disconnect if line-neutral voltage goes outside 207–253 V RMS within 5 s; (e) equivalent device functionality. The page presenting only the earth electrode option omits options (c) through (e) and misrepresents the regulation as having a single mandatory solution.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.531 — EV charging circuits must be protected by an RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30mA; additionally Type B RCD or RCM for DC fault currents
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG for Reg 722.531.3.101 returns only content about transformer placement ('should be placed as close as possible to the transformer') and a one-charger-per-transformer NOTE 2 cross-referencing Reg 722.413.1.2. No RAG facets under 722.531 contain any content about 30mA RCD protection or Type B RCDs for DC fault currents. Full-text searches for 'RCD 30mA EV charging circuit' and 'RCD type B DC fault EV charging' both returned zero results. The specific 30mA / Type B RCD requirement may exist in BS 7671 but cannot be grounded under Reg 722.531 — the reg number cited by the page for RCD protection appears to be misattributed.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.533 — overcurrent protection, MCB must be rated for maximum continuous operating current; 32A Type B or Type C MCB for a 32A (7.4kW) charger
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG for Reg 722.533.101 returns only fuse standard references (BS EN 60269-1, BS HD 60269-2:2013+A1:2022, BS HD 60269-3:2010+A2:2022, BS EN 60269-6) and a cross-reference to Chapter 71. No RAG facets under 722.533 contain any content about MCB Type B or Type C selection or a 32A rating for a 7.4kW charger. The specific MCB narrative cannot be grounded under the cited regulation number; 722.533.101 in the RAG addresses fuse standards, not MCB characteristics.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.311.201 (A4:2026 addition — load curtailment may be taken into account when determining maximum demand) is entirely absent from the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet for Reg 722.311.201 (context_prefix: 'Load curtailment considered in determining maximum demand') confirms: 'Regulation 722.311.201 states that load curtailment, including load reduction or disconnection, either automatically or manually, may be taken into account when determining the maximum demand of the installation or part thereof.' Multiple supporting facets confirm this is an A4:2026 addition with practical impact on multi-charger site sizing. The page makes no mention of this regulation anywhere, which is a real omission of a normative A4:2026 requirement directly relevant to EV charging installation design.

confirmed_issues.claim: Chapter 82 (Prosumer's Electrical Installations, new in A4:2026, covering V2G/bidirectional EV charging) is entirely absent from the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet (context_prefix: 'Chapter 82 scope: Prosumer's low-voltage electrical installations') confirms: 'Chapter 82 is a new chapter providing requirements, measures and recommendations for design, erection and verification of all types of low-voltage electrical installations designated as Prosumer's Electrical Installations (PEIs). It applies where local production and/or storage of energy is present.' A separate facet under Reg 722.826.3.201 cross-references Chapter 82 directly within the Section 722 EV charging context. V2G and bidirectional EV charging fall squarely within PEI scope. The page's omission of Chapter 82 is a confirmed gap for an A4:2026 addition that is directly relevant to EV charging.

confirmed_issues.claim: GN3 requirement to consult IET Code of Practice when inspecting EV installations is absent from the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG GN3 facet (context_prefix: 'Consult IET Code for EV charging when inspecting EV installations') states: 'When inspecting electric vehicle charging installations, GN3 requires that the inspector shall consult the IET Code of Practice for Electric Vehicle Charging Equipment Installation to determine appropriate inspection items, tests and acceptance criteria specific to EV equipment and installations.' This is a direct GN3 requirement for anyone inspecting EV installations. Its absence from a page purporting to cover EV charging legislation/requirements is a confirmed gap.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 722 contains significant changes in A4:2026 — page does not acknowledge this, still citing A2:2022 throughout
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet (context_prefix: 'Section 722 contains significant changes to regulation text', under Reg 715.524.201) states: 'Section 722 contains significant changes to Regulation(s) concerning electric vehicle charging installations … installers and certifiers shall consult Section 722 for the updated requirements and obligations.' A further facet confirms: 'The statements … indicate Section 722 update[s] existing requirements … Users shall review the amended regulations in full for precise altered wording.' The page's consistent citation of A2:2022 with no acknowledgement of A4:2026 changes directly contradicts this RAG-confirmed notice of significance, constituting a material misrepresentation of the current regulatory position.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.311.201 (A4:2026) — load curtailment (automatic or manual load reduction/disconnection) may be taken into account when determining maximum demand for EV charging installations. Critical for multi-charger commercial and fleet sites sizing supply without costly DNO upgrades.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.311.201: 'load curtailment, including load reduction or disconnection, either automatically or manually, may be taken into account when determining the maximum demand of the installation or part thereof.'

content_gaps.missing: Full set of PME/TN-C-S alternatives under Reg 722.411.4(b)–(e): options (c) disconnect device triggering within 5s when CPC-to-Earth >70V RMS; (d) disconnect device triggering when line-neutral goes outside 207–253V; plus option (e). Page only describes the earth electrode route (b) and misquotes its threshold.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.411.4 TNsystem: 'A PME earthing facility shall not be used … unless one of the alternative methods listed in Regulation 722.411.4 (b) to (e) is used.' Options (c) and (d) both reference 70V and 207–253V limits respectively.

content_gaps.missing: Chapter 82 — Prosumer's Electrical Installations (new in A4:2026): mandatory requirements for design, erection and verification where an EV installation includes local energy production or storage (solar+EV, V2G chargers). Increasingly common on commercial and domestic installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201 Chapter 82 scope facet: 'Chapter 82 is a new chapter … for design, erection and verification of all types of low-voltage electrical installations designated as Prosumer's Electrical Installations (PEIs). It applies where local production and/or storage of energy is present.'

content_gaps.missing: Annex A722 informative guidance for TN systems under PME: explains the O-PEN fault risk, voltage rise on MET, and how to size the earth electrode using the A722.3 calculation method. Page misquotes the 200Ω note as the primary requirement and never explains the 70V RMS O-PEN fault rationale.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Annex A722 facet: 'ANNEX A722 provides informative guidance for TN systems where PME conditions apply … intended to assist interpretation of earthing and neutral current issues under PME (TN-C-S) conditions.' Reg 722.411.4(b): 'maximum voltage between MET … shall not exceed 70 V RMS' under O-PEN fault.
