top_improvements.action: Correct the earth electrode requirement for Reg 722.411.4(b): replace '200Ω or less' with the actual criterion — 'the resistance shall be such that the voltage between the MET and Earth does not exceed 70V RMS in the event of an O-PEN fault in the PEN conductor (Annex A722.3 gives the calculation method; 200Ω is only a stability note, not the requirement)'. Also expand to cover alternatives (c)–(e) so installers see the full set of compliant solutions.

This is the regulatory basis for dynamic load management systems on multi-charger sites and directly affects how electricians size supplies and design load management for commercial/fleet installs.

top_improvements.action: Fix the hero H1 year inconsistency ('EV Charging Regulations UK 2025') vs meta title ('2026') and the Elec-Mate CTA ('Building Code Electric Vehicle Charging 2026'). Pick one year throughout. Also insert 'O-PEN fault' terminology in the earthing FAQ to match the meta description keyword and to give the page genuine technical authority for that search intent.
top_improvements.grounded_in: SEO: meta description already uses 'O-PEN earthing rules' but the body never uses the term — keyword mismatch. [BS 7671] Reg 722.411.4 facets use 'open-circuit fault in the PEN conductor' = O-PEN.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.312 requires each EV charging point to be supplied by its own dedicated final circuit
confirmed_issues.evidence: Every facet returned for Reg 722.312 confirms it covers only the prohibition of PEN conductors in TN systems: 'A circuit supplying charging equipment for electric vehicles in a TN system shall not include a PEN conductor' (context_prefix: Reg 722.312.2.1, requirement). The dedicated-circuit requirement is grounded instead in OSG Reg 4.6.4: 'A dedicated circuit shall be provided for the connection to electric vehicles' (OSG Chapter 4, Reg 4.6.4). No bs7671_facets row ties a one-circuit-per-charger mandate to any sub-regulation of 722.312. The page's bare citation of '722.312' as the dedicated-circuit regulation is a mis-citation.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.531.3.101 requires appropriate RCD protection for EV charging circuits — type depends on charger design
confirmed_issues.evidence: All eight facets returned for Reg 722.531.3.101 confirm it is a transformer-siting requirement: 'equipment should be placed as close as possible to the transformer' and NOTE 2 states 'Only one charging point may be supplied by each transformer, see Regulation 722.413.1.2.' No RCD-protection content is present in any 722.531.3.101 facet. The RDC-DD / BS IEC 62955 cross-reference appears instead in Reg 421.1.1 NOTE 2 (Part 4) linked to Table 537.4 and 722.531.3.101 only as a secondary reference, not as the RCD-selection rule. The page incorrectly labels this regulation as the RCD protection requirement.

content_gaps.missing: RDC-DD (Residual Direct Current Detecting Device) — BS IEC 62955:2018 — is the A4:2026 preferred mechanism for enabling Type A RCD use in mode 3 installations. The page describes DC detection generically but never uses the term RDC-DD, never cites BS IEC 62955, and does not explain the cross-reference at Table 537.4 / 722.531.3.101. This is a significant A4:2026 addition that differentiated installers need to know.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.1 NOTE 2: 'BS IEC 62955:2018 (Residual direct current detecting device — RDC-DD for mode 3 charging of electric vehicles) is referenced in NOTE 2 and linked to Table 537.4 and BS 7671 clause 722.531.3.101.'

content_gaps.missing: Indent (a) of Regulation 722.411.4.1 was deleted in A4:2026. The page presents the PME solutions without acknowledging this structural change, which is essential context for installers working to the new edition. Indent (iv) was simultaneously added as a new alternative solution.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201: 'Indent (a) of Regulation 722.411.4.1 has been deleted in the revised text of Part 7 (Chapter 72).' Also Reg 722.411.4.1: 'Regulation 722.411.4.1 adds indent (iv) which provides an alternative solution requirement.'

content_gaps.missing: 70V RMS threshold from Annex A722.3 — the specific voltage limit for MET-to-earth under a PEN open-circuit fault used to calculate the required earth electrode resistance. The page only states 'dangerous potential' without quantifying the 70V design criterion, which is the key number an installer needs to size the electrode.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.411.4 TNsystem: 'Annex 722, Item A722.3 gives guidance on determining the maximum resistance required for the earth electrode in (b). This guidance supports calculation of the resistance needed to ensure the MET-to-earth voltage does not exceed 70 V RMS under a PEN open-circuit fault.'

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 722.312.2.1 — prohibition of PEN conductor in any EV charging circuit on a TN system — is entirely absent. This is distinct from the dedicated circuit requirement and is a common installer trap: running a TN-C combined neutral/earth into an EV charger is prohibited regardless of earthing arrangement.

top_improvements.action: Fix the 722.531.3.101 mis-citation for RCD requirements. That regulation governs transformer placement and the one-charger-per-transformer rule. Add the correct RCD regulation reference (check sub-regs under 722.53x) and introduce the RDC-DD term (BS IEC 62955:2018) explicitly — explaining that a charger with an integral RDC-DD meeting BS IEC 62955 enables use of a Type A RCD in place of a Type B.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 722.531.3.101 facet: 'equipment should be placed as close as possible to the transformer… Only one charging point may be supplied by each transformer.' Plus Reg 421.1.1 NOTE 2: 'BS IEC 62955:2018 (RDC-DD for mode 3 charging) linked to Table 537.4 and clause 722.531.3.101.'

top_improvements.action: Add the A4:2026 structural changes to the 722.411.4.1 section: state explicitly that indent (a) was deleted and indent (iv) was added as a new alternative solution. Map the four remaining options (b, c, iv, and any retained lettering) to what installers must now use under A4:2026. top_improvements.action: Add the 70V RMS threshold from Annex A722.3 to the PME/earth electrode section. Replace the vague 'dangerous voltage' language with the specific design criterion: the earth electrode resistance must be sized so MET-to-earth voltage does not exceed 70V RMS under a PEN open-circuit fault (not the generic 50V Ra × IΔn formula used for standard TT circuits).
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 722.411.4 TNsystem: 'Annex 722, Item A722.3 gives guidance… to ensure the MET-to-earth voltage does not exceed 70 V RMS under a PEN open-circuit fault.'

top_improvements.action: Add a short paragraph on Regulation 722.312.2.1 — the prohibition on PEN conductors in any EV charging circuit on a TN system. This is separate from the dedicated circuit requirement and catches installers who run TN-C combined N/E conductors to the charger. It belongs in the dedicated circuit or earthing section.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 722.312.2.1: 'A circuit supplying charging equipment for electric vehicles in a TN system shall not include a PEN conductor.'

top_improvements.action: Add a note in the testing section that the A4:2026 EIC model form (Appendix 6) now includes fields for SPDs and AFDDs. Installers completing the EIC for an EV charger installation must fill these fields where devices are present, and should record N/A where absent.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201: 'Appendix 6 Model forms amended to include fields for recording SPDs and AFDDs. Installers shall record SPD and AFDD details on the model forms where such devices are installed.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Socket outlets must be positioned so the connecting cable from pitch supply to caravan does not exceed 25 m (Regulation 708.55.1.2)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 708.55.1.2 facet (context_prefix '[BS 7671] … Reg 708.55.1.2 … Maximum 20 m distance from connection facility') states explicitly: 'Caravan pitch electrical supply equipment shall be located not more than 20 m from the connection facility on the leisure accommodation vehicle or tent when on its pitch.' The regulation is unambiguous — 20 m, not 25 m. The page repeats 25 m in multiple locations, all contradicted by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: The recommended periodic inspection interval for caravan park installations is 1 year (IET Guidance Note 3)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple GN3 interval queries returned zero rows for a 1-year caravan park fixed-installation interval. The only interval facets returned are: GN3 Reg 5.11 ('not more than 5 years' for EIC designer recommendation) and GN3 Reg 1.3 (EICR '≤5 years' for private rented). The practical_work_intelligence facet that mentions a 'three years, reduced to one year if used frequently' interval cites Reg 721.514.1 — Section 721 (the caravan internal installation), not Section 708 (the park supply). No RAG source grounds a 1-year interval for the park's fixed installation. Claim is unsupported.

confirmed_issues.claim: Socket outlets must be mounted at 0.5–1.0 m above finished ground level
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 708.55.1.6 facet states: 'The lowest part of any socket-outlet shall be placed at a height between 0.5 m and 1.5 m from the ground.' A further facet confirms the exception allowing exceedance of 1.5 m only in extreme environmental conditions (flooding/heavy snow). The page states '0.5–1.0 m', which understates the permitted upper limit by 0.5 m. The correct maximum is 1.5 m per the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: No more than 4 socket outlets may be grouped together in one enclosure (Reg 708.55.1.3) — requirement entirely absent from the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 708.55.1.3 facet (context_prefix '[BS 7671] … Reg 708.55.1.3 … no more than 4 socket-outlets shall be grouped together') confirms: 'In order to avoid any hazard due to long connection cables, no more than 4 socket-outlets shall be grouped together in any one enclosure.' This is a mandatory Section 708 requirement. The auditor reports it is entirely absent from the page, constituting a genuine omission.

confirmed_issues.claim: Equipment must have minimum IK08 mechanical impact protection — requirement entirely absent from the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 708.512.2.1.3 facets confirm: 'equipment shall be installed that complies with a minimum degree of protection against external mechanical impact of IK08 (see BS EN 62262)' — this is one of three permitted methods (position selection, mechanical guard, or IK08-rated equipment) to achieve AG3 impact protection in campsites/caravan parks. IK08 is an explicit RAG-grounded requirement option. The auditor reports this is entirely absent from the page, constituting a genuine omission of a mandatory Section 708 requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: For a 30 mA RCD the maximum permitted earth electrode resistance is 1,667 ohms (50 / 0.03)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Table 41.5 facet (context_prefix 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Table 41.5 RCD TT Reg 411.5.3') confirms: 'In a TT system protected by an RCD with IDeltaN = 30 mA, the maximum earth fault loop impedance Zs is 1667 ohm. For IDeltaN <= 100 mA the earth electrode resistance must not exceed 200 ohm.' The 1,667Ω figure is Zs — the total earth fault loop impedance — not Ra. Ra is a subset of Zs (it excludes the source and line impedance). The RAG explicitly caps Ra at 200Ω for any RCD with IDeltaN ≤ 100 mA. The page presents the Zs value as if it were the electrode resistance limit, which is incorrect and unsafe — a 30 mA RCD installation with Ra up to 1,667Ω would fail the 200Ω electrode resistance requirement. The Reg 411.5.3 facet confirms: 'Ra * Ign ≤ 50 V where Ra is the sum of the resistances of the earth electrode and the protective conductor.'

confirmed_issues.claim: For a 100 mA RCD the maximum permitted earth electrode resistance is 500 ohms
confirmed_issues.evidence: Table 41.5 facet (context_prefix 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Table 41.5 RCD TT Reg 411.5.3') confirms: 'In a TT system protected by an RCD with IDeltaN = 100 mA, the maximum earth fault loop impedance Zs is 500 ohm. For IDeltaN <= 100 mA the earth electrode resistance must not exceed 200 ohm.' The 500Ω is again Zs, not Ra. The RAG's explicit cap of 200Ω for Ra applies at 100 mA just as it does at 30 mA. Presenting 500Ω as the electrode resistance limit is wrong; it overstates the permissible Ra by 2.5× and could leave a TT installation non-compliant with Reg 411.5.3(b).

confirmed_issues.claim: Ra is defined as the earth electrode resistance (equated to rod resistance alone throughout the page)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 411.5.3 definition facet (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.5.3 · [definition] · Definition of Ra for RCD earth electrode resistance sum') states verbatim: 'Ra is defined as the sum of the resistances of the earth electrode and the protective conductor connecting it to the exposed-conductive-parts (in ohms).' A second Reg 411.5.3 facet repeats the same definition. The protective conductor component is an explicit and inseparable part of Ra. A page that equates Ra to rod resistance alone understates Ra and could produce a passing calculation that fails in practice once protective conductor resistance is included.

confirmed_issues.claim: Fall-of-potential test: current electrode 30-50m away, potential electrode at 62% of that distance (18-31m)
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 2.26 facet describes the three-/four-terminal fall-of-potential setup (connect electrode to Ci/P1 or X terminal, move voltage spike along the line). GN3 Reg 2.27 facet gives the only distance figure in the RAG: auxiliary spikes placed 'typically 15-25 m from that electrode' — significantly shorter than the 30–50m the page claims for the current electrode. No GN3 facet in the RAG mentions a 62% ratio for the potential probe placement. GN3 also defines three recognised methods (E1 fall-of-potential, E2 stakeless/clamp, E3 EFLI tester per Reg 2.25 and Clause 4.6 facets); the page describes only the fall-of-potential variant. The specific 62% rule and the 30–50m spacing are unverified by any returned RAG facet; the only RAG-supported distance is 15–25m.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 recommends testing earth rod resistance in dry conditions when possible
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 2.25 facet (context_prefix '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.25 · [condition] · Perform test when ground conditions least favourable') states: 'Ideally, the earth electrode resistance test shall be carried out when the ground conditions are least favourable, such as when the soil is frozen or very dry. Performing the test under worst-case soil conditions provides conservative (higher) resistance values.' A second Reg 2.25 facet confirms: 'least favourable ground conditions include examples such as when the soil is frozen or very dry. These conditions are recommended as times when the earth electrode test should ideally be carried out to obtain conservative results.' The GN3 rationale is to obtain the highest (worst-case) reading to ensure the installation passes even under worst-case conditions — not to provide a 'safety margin' as framed on the page. Although the practical recommendation (test when dry) accidentally aligns, the stated reasoning on the page inverts the GN3 logic, which is a teachable error: the standard wants conservative high readings, not a comfortable low-reading margin.

confirmed_issues.claim: Earthing conductor is typically 16mm² copper for domestic installations
confirmed_issues.evidence: No bs7671_facets row returned linking 16mm² specifically to 'domestic earthing conductor' as a general norm. The RAG returns Table 54.1 facets (Reg 544.1.1) that show 16mm² copper applies only under specific conditions: buried conductors that are (a) protected against mechanical damage AND protected against corrosion by a 2.5mm copper sheath, or (b) mechanically protected but without corrosion qualification. Earthing conductor sizing is governed by Table 54.1 conditions and Reg 542.3.1 adiabatic requirements — there is no RAG-supported blanket 'domestic = 16mm²' rule. Presenting a single figure as 'typical for domestic' without qualification against Table 54.1 conditions is not grounded in the RAG and risks being either over-conservative or under-sized depending on installation specifics.

content_gaps.missing: The 200Ω practical electrode resistance threshold for RCDs ≤ 100mA (from Table 41.5 note) is entirely absent. The page presents only the calculated Zs figures (1,667Ω / 500Ω) without the subsidiary electrode resistance cap, which is the more practically relevant limit for design and commissioning.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Table 41.5 RCD TT Reg 411.5.3 — 'For IDeltaN <= 100 mA the earth electrode resistance must not exceed 200 ohm.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 recommends testing at least-favourable (dry or frozen) ground conditions to obtain conservative resistance readings. The page gets this right by accident but never cites GN3 or explains the rationale correctly.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.25 — 'least favourable ground conditions include when the soil is frozen or very dry... recommended as times when the earth electrode test should ideally be carried out to obtain conservative results.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 defines three earth electrode test methods: E1 (fall-of-potential), E2 (stakeless/clamp tester), and E3 (EFLI tester). The page describes only E1 and does not mention E2 (clamp method), which is widely used on existing TT installations where driving auxiliary spikes is impractical.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.25 and Reg 4.6 — 'Three general methods: E1 fall-of-potential, E2 stakeless probe dedicated tester, E3 EFLI tester.'

content_gaps.missing: The isolation requirement before E1 testing: GN3 Reg 2.25 states the installation must be securely isolated from supply before performing a fall-of-potential test. This safety step is omitted from the page's step-by-step testing description.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.25 — 'it is essential to ensure that the installation is securely isolated from the supply' before E1 testing.

top_improvements.action: Correct the Ra vs Zs conflation throughout. The page currently states the maximum Ra for a 30mA RCD is 1,667Ω — this is actually the maximum Zs (total loop impedance) per Table 41.5. Ra (electrode + PE conductor resistance) must not exceed 200Ω for RCDs ≤ 100mA per the Table 41.5 note. Add an explicit callout: 'In practice, aim for Ra ≤ 200Ω for any RCD rated 100mA or below; the 1,667Ω / 500Ω figures are total loop impedance limits.' The FAQ answer at question 1 must be corrected: the 'maximum permitted earth electrode resistance' is NOT 1,667Ω.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Table 41.5 / Reg 411.5.3 — 'For IDeltaN <= 100 mA the earth electrode resistance must not exceed 200 ohm' and Reg 411.5.3 definition of Ra as sum of electrode + PE conductor resistance.

top_improvements.action: Fix the seasonal testing guidance: rewrite the FAQ answer to state that GN3 recommends testing under least-favourable (dry or frozen) conditions to obtain a conservative maximum resistance, not to 'provide a safety margin' in the current vague framing. Add the practical note that a result obtained in winter (low resistance) does not confirm compliance if the same electrode may exceed 200Ω in a dry summer.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.25 — 'least favourable ground conditions include when the soil is frozen or very dry... recommended as times when the earth electrode test should ideally be carried out to obtain conservative results.'

top_improvements.action: Expand the testing FAQ (currently only describing E1 fall-of-potential) to cover: (a) GN3's three test methods — E1 fall-of-potential, E2 clamp/stakeless tester, E3 EFLI tester; (b) the mandatory isolation requirement before E1; (c) the need to disconnect parallel metallic paths (bonded gas/water services) to prevent false low readings.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.25 (isolation, parallel paths) and Reg 4.6 (E1/E2/E3 methods defined).

confirmed_issues.claim: 2.5 mm² twin and earth cable (with 1.0 mm² CPC) has R1+R2 of approximately 25.51 mΩ/m at 20°C
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 5.78 RAG facet explicitly states: 'The table fragment includes a value 25.51 mΩ/m next to a 2.5 mm² indication for ALUMINIUM conductors.' A separate facet warns to verify against the full Table B1 before use. The RAG attributes 25.51 mΩ/m to aluminium 2.5mm², not to a copper 2.5mm²+1.0mm² CPC combination. The page incorrectly assigns this value to a copper twin-and-earth cable. For copper, the correct R1+R2 for 2.5mm²+1.0mm² is approximately 19.51 mΩ/m (7.41 + 12.10). This is a genuine error where an aluminium figure is cited as a copper cable value.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 extension of 0.4 s disconnection to final circuits rated up to 63 A with socket-outlets (Reg 411.3.1.2). Page only states 'not exceeding 32 A' which is now incomplete — the threshold for socket-outlet circuits was raised to 63 A in A4:2026.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1.2: 'final circuits rated up to 63 A that include one or more socket-outlets' and 'final circuits rated up to 32 A that supply only fixed connected current-using equipment.' GN3 Reg 1.25 and 3.72 confirm.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 AFDD requirement for certain domestic socket circuits (A4:2026). The page discusses RCD and OCPD protection but makes no mention of AFDD, which is now relevant context when advising on fault protection for socket-outlet circuits.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... Regulation 421.1.7 requires or recommends AFDDs for certain final circuits supplying socket-outlets.' A4:2026 addition.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 mandatory RCD ≤30 mA protection for domestic lighting circuits (A4:2026 new requirement). Page discusses Zs for lighting circuits (Example 2) without mentioning this A4:2026 rule, which is now mandatory for domestic installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires shall be provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.' OSG Reg 7.2.5 confirms as mandatory for domestic premises.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 Table B2 temperature correction factor method — the page describes a fixed 1.20/1.28 multiplier, but GN3 actually provides a temperature-dependent table (Table B2) that varies by ambient temperature (e.g. a = 0.94 at 5°C). The formula Zeest(max) = Ze + a(Zs − Ze) is more accurate than a fixed multiplier.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 6.13: 'a = 0.94 at 5°C from Table B2... Zeest(max) = Ze + a(Zs − Ze) to produce a temperature-compensated accurate reading using Ze = 0.35 Ω and Zs = 7.28 Ω.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the disconnection time scope: replace 'final circuits not exceeding 32 A' with the A4:2026 wording — 0.4 s applies to (i) final circuits ≤32 A supplying only fixed equipment AND (ii) final circuits ≤63 A with one or more socket-outlets (Reg 411.3.1.2). The current text is factually incomplete for socket-outlet circuits after A4:2026.

Flag the 25.51 mΩ/m R1+R2 figure for 2.5 mm²+1.0 mm² CPC as potentially incorrect: the RAG assigns 25.51 mΩ/m to aluminium 2.5 mm² conductors, not copper T&E. Verify against the full GN3 Table B1 and replace with the correct copper 2.5/1.0 combination value, or add a caveat directing readers to Table B1.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] Reg 5.78 facets: '25.51 mΩ/m next to a 2.5 mm² indication for ALUMINIUM conductors' and 'Verify fragmentary table values against full Table B1 before use.'

top_improvements.action: Add a short A4:2026 callout box covering: (a) Reg 411.3.4 — mandatory RCD ≤30 mA for domestic lighting circuits, which directly affects Zs planning on lighting circuits (as in the page's own Example 2); (b) Reg 421.1.7 — AFDD requirement for socket circuits. These are the two most practice-relevant A4 changes for an earth-loop page.

Replace or supplement the fixed 1.20/1.28 temperature correction multipliers with the GN3 Table B2 method: Zeest(max) = Ze + a(Zs − Ze), where 'a' is a temperature-dependent factor from Table B2. Cite GN3 Reg 6.13 and note the 10°C reference basis of BS 7671 tabulated Zs values. The fixed-multiplier approach is commonly taught but is less accurate than GN3's table method and the two factors (1.20/1.28) could not be confirmed in the RAG.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] Reg 6.13; OSG Reg 1.08; [BS 7671] Reg 253.0

top_improvements.action: Add E-E-A-T attribution: include a 'Reviewed by [named qualified electrician or NICEIC assessor]' byline with credentials (e.g. 'C&G 2391-52, NICEIC approved contractor'). This is the single biggest SEO gap — no person-level expertise signal on a YMYL electrical safety page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: SEO best practice; Google E-E-A-T guidelines for safety/technical content

confirmed_issues.claim: SELV safety source must be located outside Zones 0 and 1 (page consistently omits Zone 2 from the exclusion zone)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 702.410.3.4.2 [condition] facet (bs7671, 2018+A4:2026) states explicitly: 'the source for the SELV shall be installed outside zones 0, 1 and 2 (i.e. outside zones 0, 1 and 2).' This is repeated across multiple facets for both Zone 0 and Zone 1 SELV permissions. A separate [exception] facet confirms the only departure: 'It is permitted to install the source for SELV in zone 2 for basin interior equipment if its supply circuit is protected by an RCD having the characteristics specified in Regulation 415.1.1.' The page omitting Zone 2 from the exclusion and omitting the RCD exception is a material safety error directly contradicted by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Zone 1 SELV limited to 12 V AC or 30 V DC (same restriction as Zone 0) — correct Zone 1 AC limit is 25 V AC RMS
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 702.410.3.4.2 [requirement] facet (bs7671, 2018+A4:2026) states: 'in zone 1 of a swimming pool, protection by SELV at a nominal voltage not exceeding 25 V AC RMS is permitted, provided that the source for SELV is installed outside zones 0, 1 and 2.' A separate facet confirms the Zone 1 DC limit is 60 V ripple-free DC, not 30 V DC. The Zone 0 limits (12 V AC / 30 V DC) are distinct and lower. The page capping Zone 1 at 12 V AC is factually wrong and over-restrictive — it misattributes the Zone 0 voltage limit to Zone 1.

confirmed_issues.claim: Zone 2 requires minimum IPX4 (page states this without qualification, omitting that IPX2 applies for indoor Zone 2)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 702.512.2 [requirement] facets (bs7671, 2018+A4:2026) clearly differentiate: 'For bathroom zone 2 in indoor locations, electrical equipment shall have at least the degree of protection IPX2' and 'For bathroom zone 2 in outdoor locations, electrical equipment shall have at least the degree of protection IPX4.' A further [condition] facet adds IPX5 where water jets are likely for cleaning. The page stating IPX4 as the unqualified Zone 2 minimum directly contradicts the RAG's indoor/outdoor distinction and overstates the minimum for indoor pools.

confirmed_issues.claim: Outdoor hot tubs must not use PME (TN-C-S) earthing; a TT system or separate earth electrode is required
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 702.410.3.4 [note] and [condition] facets (bs7671, 2018+A4:2026) state: 'Where a PME earthing facility is used as the means of earthing for the electrical installation of a swimming pool or other basin, it is recommended that an earth mat or earth electrode of suitably low resistance, for example, 20 ohms or less, be installed and connected to the supplementary protective equipotential bonding.' The RAG treats PME as a permitted arrangement with a mitigation recommendation — not a prohibition. No facet found that prohibits PME/TN-C-S outright for pools or hot tubs. The page's statement that PME 'must not' be used and that a TT system 'is required' is unsupported and contradicted by the RAG's permissive framing.

confirmed_issues.claim: Zone 1 special case for diving boards/springboards/starting blocks (1.5 m plane from periphery) — page does not mention this at all
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 702.4 [condition] facet (bs7671, 2018+A4:2026) states: 'Where the swimming pool or fountain contains diving boards, springboards, starting blocks, chutes or other components expected to be occupied by persons, Zone 1 comprises the zone limited by: (i) a vertical plane situated 1.50 m from the periphery of the diving boards, springboards, starting blocks, chutes and other components such as accessible sculptures, viewing bays and decorative basins; and (ii) the horizontal plane 2.50 m above the highest surface expected to be occupied by persons. This replaces the standard 2 m plane for these features.' The RAG positively confirms this special-case zone definition exists. A page covering swimming pool regulations that omits it entirely creates a safety gap — readers with pools containing these features would apply the wrong zone boundaries.

content_gaps.missing: SELV source location: The regulation (Reg 702.410.3.4.2) requires the source to be outside Zones 0, 1 AND 2 (not just 0 and 1 as the page repeatedly states). The exception allowing Zone 2 placement with an RCD per Reg 415.1.1 is also absent.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 702.410.3.4.2 [condition]: 'the source for the SELV shall be installed outside zones 0, 1 and 2'

content_gaps.missing: Zone 1 SELV voltage limit is 25 V AC RMS (not 12 V AC as stated). The page conflates Zone 0 and Zone 1 limits — Zone 1 has a higher AC limit.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 702.410.3.4.2 [requirement]: 'in zone 1 of a swimming pool, protection by SELV at a nominal voltage not exceeding 25 V AC RMS is permitted'

content_gaps.missing: Zone 2 indoor IP minimum is IPX2, not IPX4. The page states IPX4 as universal Zone 2 minimum, omitting the indoor/outdoor distinction that is explicitly in Reg 702.512.2.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 702.512.2 [condition]: 'zone 2 requires IPX2 for indoor, IPX4 for outdoor, and IPX5 where water jets likely'

content_gaps.missing: Diving board / springboard / starting block special Zone 1 case: where such structures are present, Zone 1 is measured 1.5 m from the periphery of those structures (not 2 m from the pool rim), with the upper limit 2.5 m above the highest occupied surface.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 702.4 [condition]: 'Zone 1 comprises the zone limited by a vertical plane situated 1.50 m from the periphery of the diving boards, springboards, starting blocks, chutes...'

top_improvements.action: Fix the SELV source location error throughout the page. Everywhere it says the source must be 'outside Zones 0 and 1', correct to 'outside Zones 0, 1 and 2' (Reg 702.410.3.4.2). Add the Zone 2 exception: the source may be in Zone 2 if the supply circuit is protected by an RCD with characteristics per Reg 415.1.1. This is a live safety inaccuracy affecting Zone 0 and Zone 1 sections, the SELV section, and the hot tub section.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 702.410.3.4.2 [condition]: 'the source for the SELV shall be installed outside zones 0, 1 and 2'

top_improvements.action: Correct the Zone 1 SELV voltage limit from '12 V AC or 30 V DC' to '25 V AC RMS or 30 V ripple-free DC'. The current text incorrectly applies the Zone 0 limit to Zone 1. Update the Zone 1 section bullet and the SELV section bullet. This distinction matters for practical installations (e.g. pool filtration control circuits at 24V AC).
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 702.410.3.4.2 [requirement]: 'in zone 1 of a swimming pool, protection by SELV at a nominal voltage not exceeding 25 V AC RMS is permitted'

top_improvements.action: Correct the Zone 2 IP rating table and body text. For indoor pools Zone 2 minimum is IPX2, not IPX4. Update the IP ratings table to show Zone 2: IPX2 (indoor) / IPX4 (outdoor) / IPX5 (where water jets used). The current table is factually wrong for the most common residential indoor pool scenario.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 702.512.2 [condition]: 'zone 2 requires IPX2 for indoor locations and IPX4 for outdoor locations'

top_improvements.action: Add a note about the diving board / starting block special Zone 1 case: where such structures exist, Zone 1 extends 1.5 m from their periphery (not 2 m from the pool rim), measured to 2.5 m above the highest occupied surface. This is a common commercial-pool scenario and an exam topic for C&G 2382.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 702.4 [condition]: 'Zone 1 comprises the zone limited by a vertical plane situated 1.50 m from the periphery of diving boards, springboards, starting blocks, chutes...'

top_improvements.action: Add electrical separation (Section 413) as a third permitted protective measure in Zone 2 and for basin equipment, alongside SELV and ADS. Mention the single-item supply restriction. Currently the page only presents SELV and RCD/ADS as options, omitting a legitimate and occasionally-used measure that also features in 2382 exams.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 702.410.3.4.3 [acceptance_criterion]: '(c) electrical separation (single item, source outside zones unless RCD per 415.1.1) is an accepted measure for Zone 2'

confirmed_issues.claim: Shaver sockets must be IP44 rated if within Zone 2
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 701.512.2 [requirement] confirms IPX4 is the general minimum for zone 2 equipment. However Reg 701.512.2 [exception] (bs7671 facet, context_prefix '...Shaver supply units BS EN 61558-2-5 in zone 2 exception') explicitly states: 'The IPX4 requirement for equipment in zone 2 does not apply to shaver supply units that comply with BS EN 61558-2-5 when they are installed in zone 2 and located where direct spray from showers is unlikely.' A companion condition facet confirms the exception applies only where spray is unlikely. The page's blanket statement that a shaver socket 'must be IP44 rated if it is within Zone 2' contradicts this explicit, unconditional-sounding exception — a BS EN 61558-2-5 compliant shaver socket sited away from direct shower spray is not required to be IPX4/IP44. The page must add the exception or qualify the statement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 701.415.2 states supplementary bonding not required where all circuits are RCD protected at 30mA
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms three separate conditions must ALL be satisfied, plus an overarching prerequisite: the building must have a protective equipotential bonding system per Reg 411.3.1.2. Condition (d) facet: 'All final circuits of the location shall comply with the requirements for automatic disconnection according to Regulation 411.3.2.' Condition (e) facet: 'All final circuits of the location shall have additional protection by means of an RCD in accordance with Regulation 415.1.1.' Condition (f) facet: 'All extraneous-conductive-parts of the location shall be effectively connected to the protective equipotential bonding in accordance with Regulation 411.3.1.2.' The page's version ('RCD protected at 30mA and the installation is in accordance with BS 7671') captures condition (e) only and omits condition (d) (automatic disconnection per 411.3.2), condition (f) (extraneous parts bonded per 411.3.1.2), and the prerequisite main bonding system. OSG facet Reg 4.7 [condition] corroborates: 'supplementary bonding may be omitted in a bath/shower location only when the explicit conditions in 701.415.2 are satisfied' — plural, all three.

confirmed_issues.claim: Standard 13A socket outlets not permitted inside a bathroom under any circumstances (except room with only toilet/basin)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 701.512.3 [requirement] facet (bs7671, context_prefix '...Socket-outlets prohibited within 2.50 m horizontally of boundary of zone 1') states: 'Except for SELV socket-outlets complying with Section 414 and shaver supply units complying with BS EN 61558-2-5, socket-outlets are prohibited within a distance of 2.50 m horizontally from the boundary of zone 1.' OSG Reg 4.6.4 [requirement] facet independently confirms: 'Socket-outlets other than SELV socket-outlets and shaver supply units conforming to BS EN 61558-2-5 are prohibited within a distance of 2.5 m horizontally from the boundary of zone 1.' Both sources frame this as a distance-based prohibition from zone 1, not a whole-room ban. In a very large bathroom where zone 1 does not extend to 2.5 m from an external wall, a 13A socket positioned beyond that 2.5 m horizontal boundary is not prohibited by either Reg 701.512.3 or OSG 4.6.4. The page's absolute statement that 13A sockets are never permitted is an overstatement of what the regulation says.

content_gaps.missing: The 2.5m horizontal exclusion zone for standard 13A socket outlets from the boundary of Zone 1 (OSG Reg 4.6.4 / Reg 701.512.3). The page implies sockets are banned everywhere 'inside the bathroom' — missing the precise 2.5m rule which is the actual enforceable requirement and is a key examiner point.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 4.6.4 [requirement]: 'Socket-outlets other than SELV socket-outlets and shaver supply units conforming to BS EN 61558-2-5 are prohibited within a distance of 2.5 m horizontally from the boundary of zone 1.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 new requirement): RCD ≤30 mA protection now mandatory for ALL luminaire final circuits in domestic premises — not just bathroom circuits. This is directly relevant to bathroom lighting rewires and a brand-new obligation from A4:2026 that installers will encounter when doing bathroom work.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 [acceptance_criterion]: 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.'

content_gaps.missing: The full three-condition test for omitting supplementary bonding (Reg 701.415.2): must have main bonding per 411.3.1.2 (condition d/f) AND all circuits satisfy automatic disconnection (411.3.2) AND RCD protection (415.1.1). The page only cites RCD at 30mA, omitting the other mandatory conditions — a common source of EICR C2s.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 701.415.2 [condition] (d), (e), (f): 'omission permitted where...protective equipotential bonding system per Reg 411.3.1.2, AND all final circuits comply with automatic disconnection (411.3.2), AND all have RCD protection per 415.1.1, AND all extraneous-conductive-parts connected to main bonding per 411.3.1.2.'

content_gaps.missing: IPX5 upgrade required if equipment exposed to water jets (e.g. wet-room or spa shower installation cleaned by jet wash) — important practical distinction the page ignores.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 701.512.2 [condition]: 'If equipment is exposed to water jets, the IPX5 requirement applies instead.' [condition]: 'Electrical equipment exposed to water jets, for example, for cleaning purposes, shall have at least IPX5.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the supplementary bonding omission criteria (Reg 701.415.2). The current text says bonding is omitted when 'all circuits are RCD protected at 30mA and the installation is in accordance with BS 7671' — this omits the two additional mandatory conditions: (d) all final circuits comply with automatic disconnection per Reg 411.3.2, and (f) all extraneous-conductive-parts are effectively connected to main bonding per Reg 411.3.1.2. As written it will cause C2s on EICRs where main bonding is absent. Rewrite to list all three conditions (d), (e), (f).
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 701.415.2 [condition] (d)(e)(f)

top_improvements.action: Add the 2.5m socket-outlet exclusion rule. The page states 13A sockets are never permitted inside a bathroom — this is technically incorrect. Reg 701.512.3 / OSG Reg 4.6.4 prohibit sockets within 2.5m horizontally of the Zone 1 boundary; a socket beyond 2.5m in a large bathroom room is compliant. Replace the absolute ban language with the precise distance rule to avoid misleading professionals and avoid EICR disputes.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [OSG] Reg 4.6.4 / [BS 7671] Reg 701.512.3 [procedure]: 'ensure socket-outlet placement ≥2.50 m from zone 1 unless exempt.'

top_improvements.action: Add a note on A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4: all AC luminaire final circuits in domestic premises now require 30mA RCD protection, separate from the Sec 701 bathroom requirement. This is a brand-new A4 addition that directly affects bathroom lighting work — an electrician fitting new bathroom lights must comply with 411.3.4 for the whole lighting circuit, not just as a 701.411.3.3 measure. Mentioning this places the page ahead of all 18th-edition-only competitors.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 [requirement]: 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises shall be provided with additional protection by an RCD ≤30 mA.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the shaver socket IP rating statement. The FAQ and Zone 2 section state the shaver socket 'must be IP44 rated if in Zone 2'. Reg 701.512.2 explicitly exempts BS EN 61558-2-5 shaver units from the IPX4 requirement in Zone 2 when direct spray is unlikely. Replace with: 'A shaver socket conforming to BS EN 61558-2-5 is not required to be IPX4 rated in Zone 2 provided it is sited where direct spray from showers is unlikely; where direct spray is possible, IPX4 is required.'
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 701.512.2 [exception]: 'IPX4 requirement for zone 2 does not apply to shaver supply units complying with BS EN 61558-2-5 when located where direct spray from showers is unlikely.'

top_improvements.action: Add a callout on glass shower screens and low partitions. A very common installer error is assuming a frameless glass screen limits Zone 1 — Reg 701.32.2 states that partitions lower than 2.25m do not reduce zone dimensions unless minimum distances are satisfied. Add this to the common-mistakes section as it directly causes non-compliant equipment placement on EICR inspections.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 701.32.2 [exception]: 'partitions having a height lower than 2.25 m...not treated as reducing the zone unless the minimum horizontal and vertical distances are otherwise satisfied.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.1.2 requires main protective bonding conductors connected between the main earthing terminal and extraneous-conductive-parts (water, gas, etc.)
confirmed_issues.evidence: All eight RAG facets for Reg 411.3.1.2 (context_prefix ilike '%411.3.1.2%') exclusively cover maximum disconnection times from Table 41.1 for socket-outlet circuits up to 63 A and fixed-load circuits up to 32 A. None mentions protective bonding. The correct regulation is Reg 411.3.1.1, confirmed by multiple facets: '[BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1.1 [requirement] — In each consumer's installation within a building, extraneous-conductive-parts liable to introduce a dangerous potential difference shall be connected to the main earthing terminal by protective bonding conductors complying with Chapter 54.' The page has the wrong regulation number.

confirmed_issues.claim: The minimum bonding conductor size for TN-S and TN-C-S systems is 10mm² copper. For TT systems it is 6mm² copper.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 544.11 RAG facets confirm a two-regime rule with no flat TN/TT split by system type. Non-PME installations: bonding conductor must be 'not less than half the cross-sectional area required for the earthing conductor' — no flat figure. PME (TN-C-S): must follow Table 54.8 linked to PEN conductor CSA — 10mm² only where PEN ≤35mm², 16mm² where PEN is 35–50mm², 25mm² where PEN is 50–95mm². The 6mm² minimum appears only as the floor for multi-building installations (Reg 544.11 [requirement] facet), not as a TT-system rule. No RAG facet supports a flat 'TN-S=10mm², TT=6mm²' pairing. The claim is an oversimplification that will mislead on PME installs.

confirmed_issues.claim: Earth leakage should trip the RCD within 40ms at 150mA or within 200ms at 30mA.
confirmed_issues.evidence: All RCD operating-time queries returned zero facets referencing 40ms, 150mA, or 200ms trip times. The only relevant RAG time figure is from OSG Reg 11.4: 'For general non-delay type RCDs, when subjected to an alternating current test at the rated residual operating current (Ign), the device shall disconnect within a maximum time of 300 ms.' The 300ms OSG figure directly contradicts the page's 200ms claim for operation at IΔn (30mA). The 40ms/150mA figures originate from BS EN 61008/61009 product-standard operating/non-operating envelopes, not from BS 7671. No RAG facet confirms any of the specific figures quoted on the page.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 requires 30mA RCD protection for circuits serving immersion heaters.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG facets name immersion heater circuits as a specifically mandated 30mA RCD category. Reg 411.3.3 (RAG confirmed) mandates RCD protection for socket outlets ≤32A; Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026, RAG confirmed) mandates 30mA RCD for domestic AC luminaire circuits. Neither regulation names immersion heater final circuits. A fixed-wired immersion heater circuit is not a socket-outlet circuit and not a luminaire circuit, so neither of these regulations directly applies to it. The page presents this as a specific BS 7671 requirement, which is unsupported by any RAG facet.

content_gaps.missing: The page's bonding conductor sizing advice ('10mm² for TN-S/TN-C-S, 6mm² for TT') omits the Table 54.8 PME sizing rule: under TN-C-S (PME) the conductor must be selected from Table 54.8 based on the incoming PEN conductor CSA — this can require 10mm², 16mm², or 25mm² Cu. Getting this wrong means undersized bonding in the majority of UK domestic properties (which are PME). Reg 544.11 + Table 54.8.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 544.11: 'Where PME conditions apply ... main protective bonding conductor shall be selected in accordance with the PEN conductor of the supply and Table 54.8.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4: all AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises now require 30mA RCD protection. This is directly relevant — a failed immersion heater fault current can flow through light fittings on plastic pipes if bonding is absent; the new lighting RCD requirement is an important prevention layer the page omits.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the correct regulation for main protective bonding: Reg 411.3.1.1 (not 411.3.1.2 as the page cites). A page ranking for 'BS 7671 bonding regulation' should carry the accurate reg number. This is a factual error with SEO/authority consequences.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1.1: 'Extraneous-conductive-parts liable to introduce a dangerous potential difference ... shall be connected to the main earthing terminal by protective bonding conductors complying with Chapter 54.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the condition under which supplementary bonding in bathrooms (701.415.2) may be omitted: where all circuits supplying equipment in the bathroom are protected by 30mA RCD and all exposed-conductive-parts are connected to the protective conductor. This omission means electricians reading the page may add bonding where it is not required, or vice versa.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 701.415.2 (multiple facets confirm the reg covers Class I and Class II equipment and metallic pipes/central heating in bath/shower rooms).

top_improvements.action: Correct the regulation citation for main protective bonding from 411.3.1.2 to 411.3.1.1. As it stands the page points readers to a disconnection-times regulation (Table 41.1 socket circuit limits) when they look up bonding. Add Reg 544.1.2 as the location-of-bonding-connection regulation alongside 411.3.1.1.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1.1 and Reg 544.1.2 (context_prefix facets confirm the correct reg numbering for both the bonding obligation and the point-of-entry/600mm rule)

top_improvements.action: Replace the flat '10mm² for TN-S/TN-C-S, 6mm² for TT' bonding conductor sizing statement with the correct Reg 544.11 / Table 54.8 rule: under PME (TN-C-S) size from Table 54.8 per the PEN conductor CSA (10, 16, or 25mm² Cu); under non-PME size at half the earthing conductor CSA with 6mm² minimum. This is the most common sizing question electricians face and the current text will mislead on PME installs.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 544.11 facets: 'Where PME conditions apply ... main protective bonding conductor shall be selected in accordance with the PEN conductor of the supply and Table 54.8.' / 'Except where PME conditions apply, a main protective bonding conductor shall have a cross-sectional area not less than half the cross-sectional area required for the earthing conductor.'

top_improvements.action: Add a section or callout on A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 (RCD protection for domestic lighting circuits) as a preventive measure: a shock-causing fault current that routes through luminaire circuits is now covered by a mandatory 30mA RCD on all domestic lighting. Frame this as a key A4:2026 change that strengthens protection against the exact scenario described on this page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' — confirmed in full by RAG facets.

top_improvements.action: Remove or verify the RCD operating time figures '40ms at 150mA / 200ms at 30mA'. No RAG facet in bs7671_facets supports these values as BS 7671 requirements. If retained, qualify them as 'product standard maximums per BS EN 61008/61009' not BS 7671 figures, and note the test value used in GN3 verification is 5x I∆n (150mA) where the RCD must operate — but cite the correct standard. Leaving unverified numerical claims on a safety page hurts E-E-A-T.
top_improvements.grounded_in: No supporting facet found in bs7671_facets or GN3 for these specific ms values; GN3 Reg 4.1 confirms instrument/proving requirements without naming these thresholds.

top_improvements.action: Add author E-E-A-T attribution: a named qualified electrician (e.g. 'Reviewed by [Name], NICEIC Approved, 18th Edition qualified') in the GuideTemplate author slot. Google's quality rater guidelines treat health-and-safety content (including electric shock guidance) as YMYL — anonymously authored pages are scored down. This is a zero-content-effort SEO win.

confirmed_issues.claim: EV charger installations on PME supply require a supplementary earth electrode if protective equipotential bonding cannot be established.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 722.312.2.1 facets (document_type='bs7671', context_prefix 'Reg 722.312.2.1 · TN systems · [requirement]') state unambiguously: 'A circuit supplying charging equipment for electric vehicles in a TN system shall not include a PEN conductor.' A further facet for TN-C-S explicitly reads: 'the EV circuit must be provided with separate PE and N conductors downstream of the main equipotential bonding point.' No RAG facet frames the remedy for TN-C-S/PME as a supplementary earth electrode contingent on whether bonding can be established. That framing conflates TT-system practice with the TN-C-S PEN-exclusion rule. The page's wording is contradicted by the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.312.2.1 PEN-exclusion rule for EV charging circuits on TN-C-S supplies. The page currently misframes this as a 'supplementary earth electrode if bonding cannot be established' — the actual A4:2026 requirement is that the EV circuit must be supplied downstream of the PEN split with separate PE and N conductors. This is a specific, checkable A4:2026 rule that is highly relevant to Whitehaven's older TN-C-S town centre properties being fitted with EV chargers.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 722.312.2.1 · TN systems · [requirement] — 'A circuit supplying charging equipment for electric vehicles in a TN system shall not include a PEN conductor.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.311.201 EV load curtailment — A4:2026 explicitly permits automatic or manual load curtailment (reduction or disconnection) to be taken into account when determining maximum demand for EV charging installations. This is directly relevant to the older 60A/80A supplies mentioned in the page's DNO section, where load curtailment schemes can avoid expensive supply upgrades.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 722.311.201 · [requirement] — 'Load curtailment, including load reduction or disconnection, either automatically or manually, may be taken into account when determining the maximum demand of the installation or part thereof.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 AFDD recommendation — A4:2026 introduced a recommendation to install arc fault detection devices in AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk. The page covers consumer unit upgrades and rewires in Victorian/Georgian properties (higher fire-risk buildings) without mentioning AFDDs. This is a best-in-class content gap for heritage property rewiring sections.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 421.1.7 · [purpose] — 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends the installation of AFDDs with the stated purpose of mitigating the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 mandatory RCD protection for domestic luminaire circuits (A4:2026). The page mentions single-RCD consumer units as a deficiency but does not name the new A4:2026 rule requiring RCD ≤30 mA protection on all AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. This is a highly relevant upgrade-trigger for Whitehaven's post-war and Victorian housing stock and a concrete reason for consumer unit replacement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 411.3.4 · [requirement] — 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... for AC final circuits supplying luminaires... rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA.'

top_improvements.action: Add an SPD/Section 443 note under consumer unit replacement. The A4:2026 redraft of Section 443 requires a consequence-based assessment before omitting SPDs. Coastal/overhead-line properties around Whitehaven have elevated transient overvoltage exposure. A sentence noting that the SPD assessment is now a mandatory design step (not optional) adds verifiable A4:2026 content absent from all competing location pages.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 313.1 requires the prospective fault current at the origin of the installation to be determined as part of the assessment of general characteristics before design begins.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh SQL confirmed: every RAG facet with '313.1' in its context_prefix is '[BS 7671] · Part 7 — Special Installations or Locations · Chapter 71 · Reg 710.313.1', covering automatic changeover to a safety source in medical locations (Reg 560.5). There is no Part 3 Reg 313.1 facet in the RAG. The supply-characteristics determination requirement — including 'The prospective short-circuit current at the origin of the installation shall be determined by calculation, measurement, enquiry or inspection' — appears in facets labelled context_prefix '…Part 3 — Assessment of General Characteristics · Chapter 31 · Reg 312.3 · Not used'. The authoritative verification-stage obligation is Reg 643.7.3.201 (Part 6, Chapter 64): 'The prospective short-circuit current and prospective earth fault current shall be measured, calculated or determined at the origin of the installation.' Citing '313.1' for the PFC-at-origin duty is a mis-citation; the RAG provides no support for it and positively maps 313.1 to an unrelated medical-locations regulation.

content_gaps.missing: The correct regulation for mandatory PFC measurement at origin is Reg 643.7.3.201 (Part 6 — Inspection and Testing), not '313.1'. The page uses '313.1' which in A4:2026 resolves to a Part 7 medical-locations regulation. The page should cite 643.7.3.201 for the testing duty and could separately mention Part 3 Chapter 31 for design-stage assessment.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.7.3.201: 'The prospective short-circuit current and prospective earth fault current shall be measured, calculated or determined at the origin of the installation.'

content_gaps.missing: The distributor-enquiry route for PFC determination is entirely absent. OSG explicitly permits PFC to be determined by enquiry to the distributor (e.g. DNO-provided Ze/Ipf figures) rather than site measurement — an important practical point for new installations pre-energisation and for EICR preparatory checks.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG]: 'With the supply connected and energised prospective fault current measurement shall be made if not determined by enquiry of the distributor.'

content_gaps.missing: Back-up protection cross-references are incomplete. The page describes the HRC fuse upstream arrangement correctly but does not tell readers which regulations govern it. The operative regulations are 536.1 (last paragraph) and 536.5 — the page only vaguely says 'within Section 434'. Adding the correct regulation numbers (536.1, 536.5) with the I²t let-through energy acceptance criterion would be more precise.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 536.4.2.4 / 533.2.2: 'Where the short-circuit breaking capacity of the protective device is lower than the maximum prospective short-circuit or earth fault current expected at its point of installation, it is necessary to comply with the requirements of the last paragraph of Regulation 536.1 and Regulation 536.5.'

content_gaps.missing: Appendix 14 of BS 7671 (A4:2026) contains worked examples for Ips/PFC calculation that would be invaluable reference material for readers. The page makes no mention of Appendix 14 — adding a callout directing readers to it (and noting the Elec-Mate calculator mirrors the Appendix 14 method) would lift depth and authority.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.7.3.201 note + GN3 Reg 2.29: 'Appendix 14 provides worked examples of determining prospective fault current (Ips) at relevant points; these examples illustrate calculation approaches, points to include (such as the installation origin), and how to use Ips to check protective device operation.'

top_improvements.action: Fix the mis-citation of 'Regulation 313.1' in the Supply Characteristics section. Replace with the correct regulation: Reg 643.7.3.201 (Part 6, Chapter 64) for the testing-stage duty to measure PFC at origin and all relevant points. Optionally add a secondary note that Part 3 Chapter 31 covers the design-stage assessment of supply characteristics.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 643.7.3.201 context_prefix + [GN3] Reg 2.29: the RAG contains zero facets for a Part 3 'Reg 313.1' — the only '313.1' hit is the Part 7 medical-locations regulation, confirming the current citation is wrong.

top_improvements.action: Add the distributor-enquiry route for PFC determination to the 'How to Measure PFC' section. A short panel stating that PFC may be determined by enquiry to the DNO/distributor (who can provide Ze and Ipsc figures for the supply point) rather than site measurement alone — particularly relevant for new installations before energisation and for EICR preparatory work.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [OSG]: 'prospective fault current measurement shall be made if not determined by enquiry of the distributor.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the back-up protection regulatory cross-reference. The page currently says 'There is an exception within Section 434 for back-up protection' — the operative regulations are 536.1 (last paragraph) and 536.5, not Section 434. Update the Regulatory Requirements section to cite Reg 536.1/536.5 and clarify that the acceptance criterion is that I²t let-through energy of the upstream device must not exceed the withstand capability of the downstream device.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 533.2.2 / 536.4.2.4: 'it is necessary to comply with the requirements of the last paragraph of Regulation 536.1 and Regulation 536.5' where breaking capacity is below prospective fault current.

top_improvements.action: Add a callout box or panel in the 'How to Measure PFC' or 'Regulatory Requirements' section directing readers to Appendix 14 of BS 7671 for worked examples of Ips calculation. Note that the Elec-Mate PFC calculator applies the same methodology. This surfaces a high-authority normative reference that currently goes entirely unmentioned.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 643.7.3.201 note: 'Further information on the determination of prospective fault current can be found in Appendix 14 of BS 7671.' [GN3] Reg 2.29: 'Appendix 14 provides worked examples of determining prospective fault current (Ips) at relevant points.'

top_improvements.action: Expand the definition of 'every relevant point' using the GN3 language. The page currently says 'at the origin and at every distribution board' but GN3 defines it as 'every point where a protective device is required to operate under fault conditions'. For commercial/industrial readers this is a meaningful difference — e.g. a large switchboard feeding multiple downstream MCCBs. Add a note clarifying the GN3 definition.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] Reg 2.29: 'every relevant point shall mean every point where a protective device is required to operate under fault conditions, and includes the origin of the installation.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Below 1 MΩ is a failure per BS 7671 Table 61 — page cites 'Table 61' six times
confirmed_issues.evidence: The A4:2026 RAG is unambiguous: the insulation resistance minimum values table is Table 64, not Table 61. Facet context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [test] · Table 64 test voltages and minimum insulation resistance values' states: 'For circuits up to and including 500 V (except SELV/PELV) use 500 V DC with minimum 1.0 MΩ.' Reg 643.3.2 facets also reference 'Table 64' exclusively. A direct search for 'Table 61' in context_prefix returns zero rows — that numbering does not exist anywhere in the A4:2026 RAG. Every page citation of 'Table 61' is stale 17th-edition numbering and must be replaced with 'Table 64'.

content_gaps.missing: Table 64 is the correct A4:2026 table number for IR test voltages and minima. The page uses 'Table 61' throughout — a stale 17th-edition reference. This is the single highest-priority accuracy fix. Facet: BS 7671 Reg 643.3.2 context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [test] · Table 64 test voltages and minimum insulation resistance values'.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 643.3.2 / Table 64 facet

content_gaps.missing: GN3 alternative 250 V DC test: when removal of lamps or disconnection of current-using equipment is impracticable, GN3 Reg 2.21 permits connecting L and N together and testing to earth at 250 V DC as an alternative. The page omits this important practical fallback entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 2.21 procedure facet: 'If removal of lamps or disconnection of current-using equipment is impracticable, an insulation resistance test of lines and neutral connected together to Earth at 250 V DC may be carried out.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 (new): within domestic premises, AC final circuits supplying luminaires shall now have additional protection by a 30 mA RCD. This is directly relevant to the low-IR causes section (lighting circuits, halogen downlighters). An EICR on a domestic installation that finds no RCD on lighting now has a mandatory C2 observation path under A4.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 411.3.4 facets: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided [for] AC final circuits supplying luminaires … rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 AFDD recommendation: the carbonised insulation section discusses arcing as a fire risk but does not mention that BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 now recommends AFDD installation in AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk from arc faults. This is a significant A4 addition directly relevant to the content.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 421.1.7 facets: 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) … to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits … due to the effects of arc fault currents.'

The current text mentions SPDs and electronic equipment informally — naming the GN3 source adds authority and completeness.

confirmed_issues.claim: Table 41.3 = fuses (BS 88, BS 3036, BS 1361); Table 41.4 = circuit-breakers (MCBs, MCCBs)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets unambiguously contradict this assignment. Reg 411.4.201 (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.201 · [requirement] · Maximum Zs values for gG and gM BS 88-2 fuses (Table 41.2)') establishes Table 41.2 as fuses at 0.4s. Reg 411.4.202 (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.202 · [scope] · Table 41.3 applies to overcurrent devices with Up of 230 V') states: 'Table 41.3 sets maximum earth fault loop impedance (Zs) values for circuit-breakers with nominal operational voltage (Up) of 230 V' — circuit-breakers, not fuses. Reg 411.4.203 (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.203 · [scope] · Applicability of Table 41.4 for fuse-protected distribution and final circuits') states: 'Where a fuse is used … maximum values of earth fault loop impedance (Zs) corresponding to a disconnection time of 5 s are stated in Table 41.4' — fuses, not MCBs. In BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 the correct mapping is: Table 41.2 = fuses at 0.4s, Table 41.3 = circuit-breakers (both 0.4s and 5s), Table 41.4 = fuses at 5s. The page has this completely inverted.

confirmed_issues.claim: 32A Type B MCB maximum Zs per Table 41.4 is 1.37 ohms
confirmed_issues.evidence: The numeric value 1.37Ω is correct but the table reference is wrong. RAG facet at context_prefix 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Part 4 Chapter 41 Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a) Type B MCB 230V' states: 'Maximum earth fault loop impedance Zs for a Type B MCB to BS EN 60898 (or RCBO to BS EN 61009-1) rated 32 A at 230 V is 1.37 ohm. Applies to both 0.4 s (final-circuit) and 5 s (distribution-circuit) disconnection criteria.' This value is sourced from Table 41.3 (circuit-breakers), not Table 41.4. Table 41.4 covers fuses at 5s — for example a 32A BS 88-3 fuse at 5s has a Zs of 1.6Ω (a completely different device and value). The page citing 1.37Ω as a Table 41.4 figure would lead a reader to look in the wrong table and associate an MCB limit with the fuse table.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduces Reg 411.3.4: mandatory 30mA RCD additional protection for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. This is directly relevant to EICR inspectors using this guide — existing lighting circuits without RCD protection become a C2/C3 code issue under A4:2026. The page makes no mention of this significant change.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Regulation 411.3.4 requires that, within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided … for AC final circuits supplying luminaires … rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 introduces a recommendation (not mandate) to install AFDDs in AC final circuits to mitigate arc-fault fire risk. When a high-Zs circuit is being remedied (new cable run, changed device) this is an opportune upgrade that should be mentioned. The page never references AFDDs.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) … to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents. Introduced by Amendment A4:2026.'

content_gaps.missing: The GN3 0.8 factor is described by the page as a 'multiplier to the tabulated values to account for conductor temperature during normal operation' but the RAG is more precise: it is the Appendix 3 acceptance equation factor. The page should clarify it applies to cold-measured site readings and cite Appendix 3 / GN3 Reg 1.16.9 explicitly, not just describe it as a rule of thumb.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 1.16.9: 'Regulation 411.4.4 is considered met when the measured value of fault loop impedance satisfies: Zs(measured) = 0.8 × (Uo / I_a_up × C_is).' The 0.8 is the Appendix 3 acceptance criterion factor, not a general temperature correction multiplier.

content_gaps.missing: The page's table-numbering description (Table 41.3 for fuses, Table 41.4 for MCBs) is A3-edition numbering. In BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 the structure is: Reg 411.4.201 / Table 41.2 = fuses at 0.4s; Reg 411.4.202 / Table 41.3 = circuit-breakers; Reg 411.4.203 / Table 41.4 = fuses at 5s. The page must be corrected to A4:2026 table numbering to avoid misleading inspectors.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.4.202: 'Table 41.3 sets maximum Zs values for circuit-breakers.' Reg 411.4.201: 'maximum Zs values … as stated in Table 41.2' for fuses at 0.4s. Reg 411.4.203: 'maximum values … stated in Table 41.4' for fuses at 5s.

top_improvements.action: Sharpen the GN3 0.8 factor explanation. confirmed_issues.claim: Insulation resistance test uses 250 V DC when equipment is connected and likely to be influenced or damaged by higher test voltages.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms this is a real A4:2026 change that the page omits. Reg 643.3 facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3 · [requirement] · 250 V DC insulation resistance test after connection of equipment') states verbatim: 'Where equipment is connected and the equipment is likely to influence the insulation resistance verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used to verify insulation resistance as clarified in the redraft to Regulation 643.3.' A companion facet (scope) confirms: 'Regulation 643.3 … has been redrafted. The redraft clarifies requirements for testing insulation resistance where connected equipment is likely to influence the verification test or be damaged by the test.' The page mentions IR testing but gives no test voltage and does not cover this A4:2026 redrafted scenario — a material omission confirmed by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD acceptance criterion: general non-delay RCDs must disconnect within 300 ms at rated residual operating current (An).
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG explicitly grounds this value and confirms the page omits it. Reg 643.7.3.201 acceptance_criterion facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.7.3.201 · Prospective · [acceptance_criterion] · 300 ms maximum trip time for general non-delay RCDs at An') states: 'For general non-delay type RCDs, an alternating current test at rated residual operating current (An) is acceptable where the device disconnects within 300 ms maximum.' A companion note facet confirms: 'Regardless of RCD Type, effectiveness is deemed to have been verified where an RCD disconnects within the time stated below with an alternating current test at rated residual operating current (An): for general non-delay type, 300 ms maximum.' The page mentions RCD testing but gives no pass/fail criterion — this is a confirmed material omission against the A4:2026 RAG.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 AFDD recommendation: the consumer unit installation task in the AM2 now takes place under BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, which introduces a recommendation (not mandate) to fit arc fault detection devices on AC final circuits. Candidates asked about AFDD in the Section E online knowledge test will be unprepared if the page omits this. The guide has no mention of AFDD anywhere.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... the recommendation is directed at alternating-current (AC) final circuits that are part of a fixed electrical installation.'

content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA.' content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 643.3: 'Where equipment is connected and is likely to influence the insulation resistance verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used.'

content_gaps.missing: RCD trip time acceptance criterion (300 ms at An for general non-delay RCDs) — a concrete, examinable numeric threshold that the page omits entirely despite listing RCD testing as a required test. Candidates recording RCD results on the test schedule need this value.

confirmed_issues.claim: SPD requirement: BS 7671 Amendment 2 requires surge protection where overvoltage could result in serious injury or significant financial loss
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 443.4 facets (bs7671, A4:2026) show: (a) the current standard is A4:2026, not Amendment 2 — a RAG note explicitly states 'Chapter 44 and Section 443 have been redrafted' in this edition, meaning attributing the requirement to 'Amendment 2' is wrong under the live standard; (b) the actual A4:2026 trigger criteria in the RAG are serious injury/loss of life (443.4(a)), interruption of public services (443.4(b)), interruption of commercial or industrial activity (443.4(c)), and large numbers of co-located individuals (443.4(d)) — 'significant financial loss' is not one of the stated criteria. The page's framing combines two errors: wrong amendment reference and a mis-stated trigger criterion ('significant financial loss' vs the correct 'interruption of commercial or industrial activity'). This is a confirmed issue.

content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.312.2.1: 'The prohibition applies to any circuit supplying charging equipment for electric vehicles … the EV circuit must be provided with separate PE and N conductors downstream of the main equipotential bonding point.'

The actual performance criterion would make the content more accurate and authoritative.

top_improvements.action: Clarify the 6 mm² SWA / 41 A cable rating claim. The 41 A figure is from Table 4D1A for flat T+E cable Method C — SWA 3-core ratings are in Table 4D4A and differ. Add a note that 6 mm² 3-core SWA thermoplastic gives 46 A (buried) or 50 A (clipped) at reference conditions and that all correction factors must be applied before selecting this as adequate for the 32 A continuous EV load.

confirmed_issues.claim: Meta description: 9.5kW shower uses 6mm² cable on a 40A MCB
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Table 4D1A facet (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, Appendix 4, Method C clipped direct) returns Iz = 41A for 6mm² PVC T&E. A 9.5kW shower draws Ib = 9500/230 = 41.3A, which already exceeds the 6mm² Iz of 41A with zero margin and no allowance for correction factors (ambient, grouping, thermal insulation) required by Reg 622.85. Additionally, the 40A MCB has In = 40A < Ib = 41.3A, which violates the basic overload protection condition In ≥ Ib. The page body itself correctly states '9.5kW (41.3A): 10mm² on 45A MCB'. The meta description is internally inconsistent with the page body and directly contradicted by the Table 4D1A current-rating facet. This is a confirmed error in the meta description.

confirmed_issues.claim: Meta description: 12kW shower uses 10mm² cable on a 50A MCB
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Table 4D1A facet (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, Appendix 4, Method C clipped direct) returns Iz = 57A for 10mm² PVC T&E at 30°C with no derating. A 12kW shower draws Ib = 12000/230 = 52.2A. While 10mm² Iz of 57A nominally exceeds Ib, the 50A MCB has In = 50A < Ib = 52.2A, violating the overload condition In ≥ Ib. Reg 622.85 facet confirms correction factors must be applied for current-carrying adequacy — under any realistic derating (thermal insulation, elevated ambient) 10mm² margin collapses. The page body correctly states '10.8kW to 12kW: 16mm² on 50A MCB'. The 16mm² Iz = 76A per Table 4D1A, providing adequate headroom. The meta description's 10mm²/50A combination is contradicted by both the page body and the MCB sizing arithmetic against RAG current-rating data.

The page discusses shower-circuit RCD in detail but omits that the bathroom lighting circuit also now requires RCD protection under 411.3.4, which is directly relevant to EICR coding on older installs.

content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 701.32.3 [bs7671]: 'For showers without a basin, Zone 1 lateral limit is the vertical surface at a distance of 1.20 m from the centre point of the fixed water outlet.' Reg 701.32.3 note on Zone 2: 'Zone 2 is absent for showers without a basin; instead Zone 1 is extended by the 1.20 m dimension.'

content_gaps.missing: Socket outlet restriction: no socket outlets within 3m of Zone 1 boundary (Reg 701.512.3). The page does not mention this requirement, which is directly relevant when the electrician is planning the circuit layout. A common EICR defect is a socket outlet too close to a shower.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence topic on Reg 701: 'socket-outlets are not allowed within 3 metres of zone 1; common mistake: measuring distance from incorrect reference point for 3m rule (keywords: 701.512.3).'

top_improvements.action: Fix the meta description immediately — it lists '9.5kW→6mm²/40A' and '12kW→10mm²/50A' which directly contradict the body text. Correct mapping: 7.5kW→6mm²/32A, 8.5kW→10mm²/40A, 9.5kW→10mm²/45A, 10.5kW→10mm²/45A or 16mm²/50A, 12kW→16mm²/50A.
