top_improvements.action: Add the missing supplementary bonding omission condition (d) to the RCD/bonding section: all final circuits must also comply with ADS requirements per Reg 411.3.2 (Zs within limits, correct disconnection time). Without this the omission criteria as written are incomplete and could lead to an installation that omits bonding without verifying Zs.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 701.415.2(d) [bs7671]: 'All final circuits of the location shall comply with automatic disconnection per Regulation 411.3.2 in order for supplementary bonding to be omitted.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the voltage drop example figures. The stated values (4.5V for 15m, 6.1V for 20m of 10mm² at 41.3A) appear to use the wrong mV/A/m factor — likely confusing the loop impedance (mΩ/m) with the voltage drop factor. Standard 10mm² flat T&E gives approximately 3.8 mV/A/m, yielding ~2.4V at 15m and ~3.1V at 20m. Overstated drop figures could cause cable over-sizing. Verify against BS 7671 Appendix 4 Table 4E2A.
top_improvements.grounded_in: RAG returned no facet to support the stated values; internal arithmetic using BS 7671 Appendix 4 standard mV/A/m for 10mm² T&E contradicts the page figures.

top_improvements.action: Add a note on A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4: bathroom lighting circuits in domestic premises now also require 30mA RCD protection as of A4:2026. This is directly relevant to electricians fitting shower circuits in older homes where the lighting circuit may not be RCD-protected — it affects EICR coding and any associated works.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 411.3.4 [bs7671]: 'Within domestic premises, additional protection by an RCD (≤30 mA) shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' This is an A4:2026 addition not covered anywhere on the page.

confirmed_issues.claim: Trunking fill must not exceed 45% of the internal cross-sectional area — explicit in BS 7671 and IET Guidance Note 1
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple trunking-related facets were returned (Reg 521.10.1, Reg 530.4.3, OSG 7.11, GN3 4.8.5) covering BS EN 50085 compliance, IPXXD requirements, and enclosure obligations — none contain any fill-percentage figure. Exhaustive full-text search on 'trunking fill 45 percent', 'trunking space factor fill', and 'trunking cable enclosure fill space' all returned zero rows. The 45% fill limit originates in OSG Appendix E and is also referenced in IET GN1 — it is not a numbered regulation in BS 7671 itself. The page claim that this is 'explicit in BS 7671' is an overstatement of the source authority; it belongs to the OSG/GN1 guidance layer. The attribution to 'BS 7671' explicitly is unsupported by any facet and is contradicted by the pattern of trunking facets, which address product-standard compliance (BS EN 50085) and IP ratings — not fill percentages.

content_gaps.missing: Band I / Band II circuit segregation: when multiple circuits share a conduit or trunking, Band I (ELV) and Band II (LV) circuits must not share the same wiring system part unless conditions (a)–(e) of OSG 7.4.1 are met (e.g. all cables insulated for highest voltage, separate compartments, earthed metal screen). The page says nothing about this mandatory segregation rule despite discussing mixed-circuit conduit scenarios.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 7 · Reg 7.4.1: 'Band I circuits must not be contained within the same wiring system part as Band II circuits unless one of the specified conditions (a) to (e) is satisfied.'

content_gaps.missing: Cables shall not be drawn into a conduit buried in structure until the conduit system is completely erected between access points (Reg 522.8.2). This is a common on-site compliance failure and would strengthen the practical guidance section.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 522.8.2: 'Where a conduit or cable ducting system is buried in the structure, cables shall not be drawn into the conduit until the conduit system is completely erected between access points.'

content_gaps.missing: Flexible metallic conduit prohibition as a protective conductor (Reg 543.2.3). The page discusses conduit types and earthing in passing but never warns that flexible/pliable metallic conduit is prohibited as a CPC. This is a frequent design error.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 543.2.3: 'Flexible or pliable conduit shall not be selected as a protective conductor.'

content_gaps.missing: Non-sheathed cable termination enclosure at conduit ends (Reg 526.8). Even where cables are correctly drawn into conduit, the tails exiting the conduit end must also be enclosed per Reg 526.5. The page omits this requirement entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 526.8: 'Non-sheathed cables at the termination of conduit, ducting or trunking shall be enclosed as required by Regulation 526.5.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Voltage indicators should be calibrated at manufacturer-recommended intervals, typically every 12 months, by a UKAS accredited laboratory.
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 4.8 (context_prefix: '[GN3] · Chapter 4 · Reg 4.8 · [requirement] · Calibration and checks shall follow manufacturer's recommendations') states explicitly: 'Calibration or checking procedures shall follow manufacturer's recommendations and procedures. Test and calibration intervals and methods shall be as recommended by the instrument manufacturer.' A further Reg 4.8 facet adds that extended intervals are permitted for infrequently used instruments stored in stable conditions, and shortened intervals for heavily used instruments. No facet in the RAG specifies or implies a fixed 12-month interval. GN3 Reg 4.8 does reference UKAS-accredited laboratories for calibration, but anchors the interval to manufacturer guidance only. The page's insertion of 'typically every 12 months' as a standard interval contradicts the RAG, which makes no such generalisation.

confirmed_issues.claim: GS 38 is an HSE Guidance Note published titled 'Electrical test equipment for use by electricians'.
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 1.1 (context_prefix: '[GN3] · Chapter 1 · Reg 1.1 · Safety · [requirement] · Observe HSE GS38 when using electrical test equipment') gives the title as 'Electrical test equipment for use on low voltage electrical systems'. The page states 'for use by electricians'. The RAG unambiguously provides a different subtitle. This is a title-text mismatch directly contradicted by the indexed GN3 content.

content_gaps.missing: Lock-off / securing the isolator before proving dead is absent from the prove-test-prove section. OSG 12.5 defines the sequence as: identify, isolate, SECURE (lock off with sign), then prove dead. The page's three-step diagram skips the securing step entirely, giving an incomplete procedure.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG Reg 12.5: 'When an isolation point is locked off to achieve working dead, the On-Site Guide shows that a safety sign shall be displayed at that locked-off point'; BS 7671 Reg 462.1: sequence includes 'secure against re-connection'.

content_gaps.missing: Multiple-supplies / alternative-source hazard is not mentioned. On PV, EV, battery, or generator installations the supply origin isolation alone may not render all conductors dead. GN3 2.9 and BS 7671 Reg 712.514.102 both require a warning notice that 'live parts may remain energized after isolation'. This is a critical real-world omission for a proving-dead guide.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 2.9: 'a multiple-supplies warning notice shall be provided at mains positions, points of isolation, distribution boards... Each of these locations must display the notice so the existence of alternative supplies is evident.'

content_gaps.missing: EAWR Regulation 16 (competence duty) is not cited. The page only cites EAWR Reg 4. GN3 4.9 explicitly cites Reg 16 as requiring persons working near live parts to be competent, which is the direct legal hook for using GS 38-compliant equipment rather than inadequate gear.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 4.9: 'Regulation 16 of the Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 requires that persons involved in work on or near live electrical equipment shall be competent to prevent danger.'

content_gaps.missing: The OSG HSG85 'working dead' definition and its distinction from GS 38 is not addressed. The page conflates proving dead with the broader working dead / safe system of work concept. OSG 12.5 references HSG85 for the full definition, which includes identifying conductors, earthing/shorting on HV systems, and permit-to-work for high-risk work.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG Reg 12.5: 'The working dead procedure, as referenced from HSG85, describes a sequence of steps to ensure an electrical circuit or equipment is rendered dead... encompasses positive identification, isolation, proving absence of voltage and securing the isolation.'

seo.notes: The page leads with a strong key-takeaways block under the H1, satisfying answer-first intent for featured snippets. FAQ section is present and rendered via GuideTemplate which should support FAQ schema markup. Internal links count is approximately 6 (safe-isolation-procedure x2, arc-flash-protection x1, training/inspection-and-testing x2, rams-generator x1). No author attribution or E-E-A-T signal (no named author, no credentials badge, no publication date visible to user in body). The meta description is well-formed and includes the core query terms (GS 38, proving dead, 500 mA HBC, 4 mm, prove-test-prove, CAT III/IV). datePublished 2025-06-01 / dateModified 2026-05-23 is present in schema props. Title tag uses '4th ed' — GS 38 is currently the 4th edition (published 2015), so this is accurate but could be more prominent.

top_improvements.action: Add a 'secure the isolation' step (Step 1b) to the prove-test-prove diagram. Between isolating the circuit and proving dead, insert: isolate at the correct mechanical device, fit personal padlock and warning tag/label at the point of isolation, confirm sign is displayed. This is mandatory per OSG 12.5 and BS 7671 Reg 462.1 ('secure against re-connection') and is currently the biggest procedural accuracy gap.
top_improvements.grounded_in: OSG Reg 12.5 (lock-off sign + securing isolation); BS 7671 Reg 462.1 (isolation provisions shall enable prove dead + earthing/shorting)

top_improvements.action: Add a section or callout on multiple-supply / alternative-source hazards (solar PV, EV chargers, generators, battery storage). Emphasise that isolating the incoming supply may not render all conductors dead, and that a multiple-supplies warning notice is required per GN3 2.9 / BS 7671 Reg 712.514.102. This is a fast-growing real-world risk and a major content gap for a proving-dead guide.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 2.9: 'multiple-supplies warning notice shall be provided at... distribution boards... so the existence of alternative supplies is evident'; BS 7671 Reg 712.514.102: 'live parts may remain energized after isolation'

top_improvements.action: Add a callout warning that semiconductor devices (VSDs, soft starters, AFDD, RCDs) must not be used as the means of isolation. A mechanical isolator is mandatory per BS 7671 Reg 537.2.2. This is a common field mistake and the absence makes the guide incomplete for modern installations with VFDs and smart gear.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 537.2.2: 'safe isolation procedures shall not rely on semiconductor devices... a mechanical isolator shall be used instead'

top_improvements.action: Explicitly state 'prove dead at the LOAD side of the isolation device, not the supply side' in the TEST step of the prove-test-prove diagram. Practical_work_intelligence explicitly flags this as a common mistake. Currently the page says 'test the circuit that has been isolated' without this critical spatial clarification.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: common_mistakes: 'proving dead at the supply side instead of the load side'

top_improvements.action: Add EAWR Regulation 16 alongside the existing Reg 4 citation in the legal-status FAQ. Reg 16 is the competence duty that directly ties to using GS 38-compliant equipment — it is the reason an incompetent person using non-compliant leads creates personal criminal liability, not just a breach of guidance.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 4.9: 'Regulation 16 EAWR requires persons working on or near live equipment to be competent to prevent danger'

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4 was issued in July 2024, adding requirements for bidirectional and unidirectional devices (Section 530, A4:2026 addition)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms the opposite on both sub-claims. (1) The amendment issued on 31 July 2024 was A3:2024, not A4. A4:2026 was issued on 15 April 2026 (facet: 'Reg 722.826.3.201 · [reference] · Amendment 4:2026 issuance and withdrawal timeline': 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 was issued on 15th April 2026'). (2) The bidirectional/unidirectional device requirements belong to A3:2024 under Reg 722.826.3.201, not to A4:2026 and not to Section 530 (facet: 'Reg 722.826.3.201 · [requirement] · Two new definitions and one new Regulation for bidirectional/unidirectional devices (A3:2024)': 'Amendment A3:2024 provides for the inclusion of two new definitions and one new Regulation relating to the use of bidirectional and unidirectional devices'). The Section 530 facets that do exist in the RAG cover Reg 530.3.5 (protection-only devices not used for functional switching) — a long-standing regulation with no A4 bidirectional/unidirectional content. The page conflates A3 with A4 and misattributes both the date and the section number.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4 is expected in 2026 (stated in the FAQ answer about non-registered electricians)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG explicitly records that A4:2026 had already been issued before the page's dateModified of 2026-05-22: 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 was issued on 15th April 2026 and may be implemented immediately. BS 7671:2018+A2:2022+Corrigendum (May 2023)+A3:2024 remains current but will be withdrawn on 15th October 2026.' (facet: 'Reg 722.826.3.201 · [reference] · Amendment 4:2026 issuance and withdrawal timeline'). Framing A4 as 'expected' on a page dated 2026-05-22 is factually wrong; it had been live for five weeks by that date.

confirmed_issues.claim: A4:2026 adds new requirements for Section 530 (bidirectional/unidirectional devices)
confirmed_issues.evidence: This is the same factual error as claim 1, examined from the section-attribution angle. The RAG's Section 530 facets (530.3.5, 530.3.2.1, 530.3.3) contain no bidirectional/unidirectional content and carry no A4:2026 addition note. The bidirectional/unidirectional requirement lives under Reg 722.826.3.201 and was introduced by A3:2024, not A4:2026 (facet: 'Reg 722.826.3.201 · [requirement] · Two new definitions and one new Regulation for bidirectional/unidirectional devices (A3:2024)'). Section 530 in the RAG covers protective device selection and the prohibition on protection-only devices performing functional switching — neither new in A4 nor related to bidirectional/unidirectional devices.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced new Chapter 82 — Prosumer's Electrical Installations (PEIs) — covering all installations with local energy production or storage (solar PV, battery storage, vehicle-to-grid). This is the single biggest A4:2026 addition and is directly relevant to the CPD topics the page champions (solar PV, EV charging, battery storage). The page mentions these technologies as CPD priorities but never connects them to the specific regulation chapter that now governs them.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201 · [scope] · Chapter 82 scope: Prosumer's low-voltage electrical installations

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 made significant changes to Section 722 (EV charging): deletion of indent (a) of Regulation 722.411.4.1 and a fully redrafted Annex A722 (guidance for TN/PME systems). The page says EV charging CPD is important but gives no regulatory depth — mentioning the Section 722 overhaul would justify the CPD need with a specific technical hook.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201 · [note] · Deletion of indent (a) of Regulation 722.411.4.1; [reference] · Annex to Part 722 redrafted

content_gaps.missing: The correct amendment timeline is: A3:2024 issued 31 July 2024 (bidirectional/unidirectional devices); A4:2026 issued 15 April 2026 (Chapter 82, Section 753, Section 722 overhaul); previous edition withdrawn 15 October 2026. The page conflates A3 and A4 throughout. A CPD guide should model this precision — qualified electricians will notice the error and lose trust.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201 · [reference] · Amendment 3:2024 introduction and immediate implementation; [reference] · Amendment 4:2026 issuance and withdrawal timeline

content_gaps.missing: Section 753 (Heating cables and embedded heating systems) is a new section introduced by A4:2026, relocating requirements from Chapter 53. Electricians doing underfloor heating work need this CPD; it is a concrete example of why keeping up with amendments matters.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201 · [requirement] · New regulations on heating cables in Section 753

confirmed_issues.claim: Fire-resistant cables complying with BS EN 50200 / IEC 60331 are accepted for safety services — the page implies BS 7629-1 is the only route
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [scope] · Wiring systems permitted for safety services operating in fire conditions' returns verbatim Reg 560.8.1 text listing four equal routes: '(a) mineral insulated cable … BS EN 60702; (b) fire-resistant cables complying with IEC 60331-1, IEC 60331-2 or IEC 60331-3 … ; (c) fire-resistant cables complying with test requirements of BS EN 50200, BS 8434 or BS 8491 … ; (d) a wiring system maintaining the necessary fire and mechanical protection.' A second facet (Reg 560.7.12 reference) explicitly names IEC 60331 and BS EN 50200 as the fire-resistant cable test regimes cited in 560.8.1(b) and (c). If the page presents BS 7629-1 and MICC as the only two valid options, it materially omits routes (b) and (c), which the RAG clearly supports as equally valid under BS 7671 A4:2026.

content_gaps.missing: BS 7671 Regulation 560.8.1 — the primary BS 7671 authority requiring safety-service wiring. The page never cites Reg 560.8.1 or Chapter 56 at all. This is the reg that mandates IEC 60331 / BS EN 50200 / MICC / BS 7629-1 compliant cables. An electrician audience will be examined on this and inspectors will cite it on EICRs.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [scope] · 'One or more of the following wiring systems shall be utilized for safety services required to operate in fire conditions: (a) mineral insulated cable…; (b) fire-resistant cables complying with IEC 60331…; (c) fire-resistant cables complying with BS EN 50200, BS 8434 or BS 8491…; (d) a wiring system maintaining the necessary fire and mechanical protection.'

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 560.5.1 duty on installers — 'the installer shall use appropriately fire-resistant or fire-tested cable systems … to maintain circuit integrity in a fire.' The page covers the practical how but omits the primary regulatory duty in BS 7671 that creates the legal obligation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Part 5 · Chapter 56 · Reg 560.5.1 [procedure]: 'To comply with Regulation 560.5.1 when safety services must operate through fire conditions, the installer shall use appropriately fire-resistant or fire-tested cable systems and routing methods…'

content_gaps.missing: CPR Regulation 527.1.3 note — cables must satisfy CPR in respect of reaction to fire, and the UK MHCLG position (no mandatory performance levels set domestically). The page mentions Cca-s1b,d1,a1 but without the regulatory hook in BS 7671 Reg 527.1.3 that makes CPR compliance an installation requirement, not just a product specification.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 527.1.3 [requirement]: 'Cables used in electrical installations shall satisfy the requirements of the Construction Products Regulation (CPR) with respect to their reaction to fire.' Also Reg 253.0 [note]: 'In the UK, MHCLG has not exercised the powers under CPR to determine levels of performance required.'

content_gaps.missing: Band I / Band II mandatory segregation obligation under OSG Reg 7.4.1 and BS 5839 / BS 5266. The page mentions route segregation only as a good-practice risk-management step; the OSG makes it a mandatory requirement with specific cross-references to BS 5839 and BS 5266.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] · Reg 7.4.1 [requirement]: 'Emergency lighting circuits shall be separated from other cables and from each other in compliance with BS 5266.' [note]: 'Fire alarm and emergency lighting circuits must be separated from other cables and from each other, in compliance with BS 5839 and BS 5266 respectively.'

top_improvements.action: Add a 'BS 7671 Requirements' subsection citing Reg 560.8.1 and Reg 560.5.1. Explain that the legal obligation to use fire-resistant wiring for safety services comes from BS 7671 Chapter 56, and list the four permitted wiring system routes (MICC/IEC 60331/BS EN 50200/constructional protection) — not just BS 7629-1. Remove the implicit claim that BS 7629-1 is the only acceptable standard.

top_improvements.action: Correct the segregation section: upgrade route segregation from a risk-management recommendation to a mandatory requirement, citing OSG Reg 7.4.1 and the specific cross-references to BS 5839-1 and BS 5266-1 for Band I/II separation. This is a compliance point, not a best-practice point.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [OSG] Reg 7.4.1 [requirement]: 'Emergency lighting circuits shall be separated from other cables and from each other in compliance with BS 5266.'

top_improvements.action: Add a fire-rated glands warning to the installation requirements section. The RAG PWI confirms this is the top common mistake. Note that fire performance depends on the entire cable system — glands, junction boxes, and conduit entries all require fire-rated products, not just the cable itself.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: common_mistakes = ['omitting fire-rated glands at penetrations', 'incorrect termination causing loss of fire performance']

top_improvements.action: Add a testing note in the 'For Electricians' section: the standard BS 7671 500V IR test regime does not fully apply to fire alarm circuits — installers must follow the more specific testing requirements in BS 5839-1. Also note that certificates for life-safety installations must reference Chapter 57 (stationary secondary batteries) compliance where batteries are included, per Reg 560.11.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 643.3.2 [note]: 'More specific requirements are applicable for the wiring of fire detection and fire alarm systems in buildings; see BS 5839-1.'; Reg 560.11 [acceptance_criterion]

confirmed_issues.claim: Horizontal runs must be within 150mm of the floor (bottom of wall / floor line)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 522.6.202 prescribed-zone definition is unambiguous: zones are (a) within 150mm from the TOP of the wall or partition, (b) within 150mm of an angle formed by two ADJOINING walls or partitions, and (c) horizontally and vertically from any accessory/switchgear on the wall. No floor-line or bottom-of-wall zone exists anywhere in the facets. The facet states verbatim: 'A prescribed zone is defined as a zone within 150 mm from the top of the wall or partition or within 150 mm of an angle formed by two adjoining walls or partitions.' A floor-line horizontal zone is not a prescribed zone under 522.6.202; stating it as one is incorrect and potentially dangerous (cables run there without RCD/522.6.204 compliance would be outside a prescribed zone yet treated as safe).

confirmed_issues.claim: Vertical runs within 150mm of door and window frames are in a safe zone
confirmed_issues.evidence: The Reg 522.6.202 prescribed-zone definition retrieved from bs7671_facets lists only three zone types: 150mm from the top of wall/partition, 150mm from angles between adjoining walls/partitions, and zones from accessories/switchgear. Door and window frames are not named as a prescribed zone component in any retrieved facet. A separate full-text search on 'door window frame cable zone safe' returned zero results. Presenting door/window frame proximity as a prescribed safe zone has no RAG support and contradicts the closed definition of prescribed zones in 522.6.202.

confirmed_issues.claim: Cables outside safe zones must be enclosed in earthed metallic containment OR buried at a depth of at least 50mm from the finished wall surface
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG shows this is a mischaracterisation of Table 52.1 / 522.6.202. The actual framework has two compliance paths for cables in walls regardless of zone: (1) install in a prescribed zone AND provide RCD protection per Reg 415.1.1, OR (2) comply with Reg 522.6.204. Reg 522.6.204(b) references earthed conduit to BS EN 61386-21 — but the OSG facet explicitly notes that 'metallic capping would not meet the requirements of Regulation 522.6.204(a) or (b)'. The facet for <50mm depth states RCD is required at that depth, not that 50mm is the threshold for safe omission. The page's simplified 'outside zone = earthed metallic containment OR 50mm depth' framing does not match the Table 52.1 structure and omits the mandatory RCD/522.6.204 requirement as the controlling rule.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.4 (A4:2026) mandates 30mA RCD protection for AC lighting circuits in domestic premises — omitted from page
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG fully confirms this regulation. Multiple Reg 411.3.4 facets were retrieved: 'Regulation 411.3.4 requires that, within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... for AC final circuits supplying luminaires... rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA.' The facets confirm mandatory ('shall') language, domestic-premises scope, and applicability to lighting circuits specifically. This is an A4:2026 addition directly relevant to first-fix consumer unit design and circuit planning. Its complete absence from the page is a confirmed omission of a mandatory A4:2026 requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 421.1.7 (A4:2026) recommends AFDD installation on AC final circuits — omitted from page
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG fully confirms this regulation. Reg 421.1.7 facets state: 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation.' Facets confirm it was introduced by A4:2026 and that the wording is advisory ('recommending', not 'shall'). While not mandatory, it is a new A4:2026 advisory requirement directly applicable to first-fix consumer unit and circuit planning. Its complete absence from a first-fix guide page that covers circuit planning is a confirmed omission of relevant A4:2026 content.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026): mandatory 30mA RCD additional protection for ALL AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. This is a first-fix circuit-planning requirement — every lighting circuit in a new domestic installation must be RCD-protected. Page discusses RCD for cables outside safe zones but not this new blanket A4 requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 411.3.4 · 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... for AC final circuits supplying luminaires... rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026): recommendation to install AFDDs on AC final circuits to mitigate arc-fault fire risk. For a new build or full rewire first fix, the consumer unit position and circuit design should account for AFDD provision. The page's checklist and cable type section do not mention this.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 421.1.7 · 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.311.201 (A4:2026): EV charger load curtailment — when sizing the first-fix supply for an EV charger, load curtailment (automatic or manual reduction/disconnection) may be taken into account for maximum demand. Page's checklist includes EV charger cable but gives no guidance on this demand management requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 722.311.201 · 'Load curtailment, including load reduction or disconnection, either automatically or manually, may be taken into account when determining the maximum demand of the installation.'

content_gaps.missing: OSG thermal insulation derating at first fix: cables run in stud walls or ceiling voids adjacent to or surrounded by thermal insulation must be derated. OSG Reg 2.6 specifies 0.5× current-carrying capacity for >0.5m in insulation (Table F2). Page mentions avoiding cables near hot water pipes but omits insulation derating entirely — critical for modern well-insulated new builds.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] · Reg 2.6 · 'Where a cable is surrounded by thermal insulation for a length of more than 0.5 m, the current-carrying capacity shall be taken... as 0.5 times the current-carrying capacity for that cable clipped direct to a surface.' Table F2 factors: 0.88/0.78/0.63/0.51 for shorter lengths.

top_improvements.action: Correct and tighten the 'BS 7671 Safe Zones' callout box. Remove the 'within 150mm of the floor line' horizontal zone claim — no such zone exists in the A4:2026 Reg 522.6.202 definition in the RAG. Replace with accurate wording: prescribed zones are (a) within 150mm of the top of the wall, (b) within 150mm of a wall corner angle, and (c) horizontally and vertically from any accessory/switchgear position. Also remove or qualify the 'buried at 50mm depth = safe' alternative — Table 52.1 applies to all concealed cables regardless; depth determines which protective measure is required, not whether protection is required at all.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 522.6.202 [definition] — 'A prescribed zone is defined as a zone within 150 mm from the top of the wall or partition or within 150 mm of an angle formed by two adjoining walls or partitions. A prescribed zone can also be formed horizontally and vertically from any point, accessory or switchgear located on the wall.' No floor/bottom zone mentioned.

top_improvements.action: Add a section or FAQ answer on thermal insulation derating at first fix: cables run in stud walls, roof spaces, or ceiling voids in insulated new-build properties must have their current-carrying capacity derated. OSG specifies 0.5× rating for cables surrounded by insulation for >0.5m; shorter lengths use Table F2 factors (0.88 down to 0.51). This is especially relevant for new builds where Part L insulation requirements mean almost every cable run in an external wall or loft space is affected. Failure to derate is a common first fix error that manifests at inspection/test.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [OSG] Reg 2.6 — 'Where a cable is surrounded by thermal insulation for a length of more than 0.5 m, the current-carrying capacity shall be taken as 0.5 times the current-carrying capacity for that cable clipped direct to a surface.'

top_improvements.action: Add to the First Fix Completion Checklist an item: 'Continuity and insulation resistance tests carried out on all circuits before plastering — record results as part of initial verification.' GN3 requires elements that will be concealed to be inspected and tested before becoming inaccessible. This is not mentioned anywhere on the page despite being a compliance requirement (Reg 641.1 via GN3 Reg 2.5.1), and it is directly actionable at first fix sign-off.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] Reg 2.5.1 — 'Inspectors and installers shall plan inspection timing so that elements likely to be covered (e.g. buried cables, concealed connections) are inspected and tested before being made inaccessible. This planning is part of compliance with Regulation 641.1.'

top_improvements.action: Add EV charger first-fix guidance referencing Reg 722.311.201: when provisioning an EV charging circuit at first fix, the supply cable sizing and consumer unit capacity assessment may take load curtailment (smart charging / automatic demand management) into account. This is increasingly relevant for new builds where EV charging is now required by Building Regs Part S. The page's checklist mentions 'EV charger' cable as a line item but offers no sizing or regulatory context.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 722.311.201 — 'Load curtailment, including load reduction or disconnection, either automatically or manually, may be taken into account when determining the maximum demand of the installation or part thereof.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 537.2.1 requires a means of isolation for each item of fixed equipment; for electric boilers a lockable double-pole isolator switch adjacent to the boiler (typically within 2m) is standard practice
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG contains only two 537.2 facets: 537.2.1.1 ('Every circuit shall be capable of being isolated individually from each of the live supply conductors') and 537.2.1.202 (highway supply cut-out distributor approval). Neither facet mentions fixed equipment, a 2m proximity requirement, or a lockable double-pole isolator for boilers. The general isolation requirement for fixed equipment derives from 537.2.1.1 (circuits) not a named 'fixed equipment isolator' reg. The '2m' and 'adjacent' specifics are entirely absent from the RAG — zero results across isolation + fixed + equipment + boiler queries. The page cites 537.2.1 as the direct authority for a boiler-adjacent lockable isolator; the RAG does not support that specific claim, only the broader circuit-isolation principle.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.3 requires 30mA RCD protection for circuits supplying fixed equipment in domestic premises
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple 411.3.3 facets in the RAG are unambiguous: 'Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been revised and now applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A' and 'Socket-outlets rated above 32 A are outside Regulation 411.3.3 scope.' A boiler is fixed connected current-using equipment, not a socket-outlet — 411.3.3 does not apply to it. The correct A4:2026 reg for domestic RCD on AC final circuits supplying luminaires is 411.3.4 (confirmed by RAG: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... for AC final circuits supplying luminaires'). Citing 411.3.3 as the RCD basis for fixed heating equipment is factually wrong under A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Volt drop must not exceed 3% for lighting circuits or 5% for other circuits — reference: BS 7671 Appendix 12
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG facets exist for 'Appendix 12' — the context_prefix ilike '%Appendix 12%' query returned empty. Multiple full-text searches for 'volt drop 3 percent 5 percent', 'volt drop appendix', 'volt drop permitted limit', and 'volt drop appendix 4 mV per amp per metre' all returned empty result sets. Appendix 12 does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026; the volt-drop limits (3%/5%) are addressed in Appendix 4 (cable selection tables with mV/A/m values). Citing 'Appendix 12' as the authority for volt-drop limits is factually incorrect.

confirmed_issues.claim: Earth bonding — confirm main protective bonding in place for water and gas services per Regulation 411.3.1.2
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for 411.3.1.2 confirm it governs maximum disconnection times in Table 41.1: 'Regulation 411.3.1.2 establishes which categories of final circuits the maximum disconnection times in Table 41.1 shall apply to... (i) final circuits rated up to 63A with socket-outlets and (ii) final circuits rated up to 32A supplying only fixed connected current-using equipment.' This has no connection to bonding. Main protective bonding to gas and water services is Reg 544.1.2, confirmed by RAG: 'the main protective bonding connection to any extraneous-conductive-part such as gas, water or other metallic pipework or service shall be made as near as practicable to the point of entry of that part into the premises.' Citing 411.3.1.2 for main bonding is the wrong regulation number.

confirmed_issues.claim: Minimum insulation resistance is 1.0 MΩ per BS 7671 Table 61 at 500 V DC for standard 400 V circuits
confirmed_issues.evidence: Every bs7671_facets facet for insulation resistance minima cites Table 64 under Reg 643.3.2, Part 6 Chapter 64 of 2018+A4:2026 — e.g. context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3.2 · … · Minimum insulation resistance numeric values from Table 64', content: 'Numeric minima from Table 64: … Circuits up to and including 500 V (excluding SELV/PELV) = 1.0 MΩ (at 500 V DC).' No facet referencing 'Table 61' was returned by any query. Table 61 is an A3/17th-edition relic; the correct reference in A4:2026 is Table 64. The resistance value (1.0 MΩ) and test voltage (500 V DC) are correct, but the table reference on the page is wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD trip times must be measured at 1x, 2x, and 5x the rated residual current (In)
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet supports a mandatory three-multiplier (1x/2x/5x In) protocol. OSG Reg 11.3 facets describe testing at 100% IΔn only: 'Test: Apply a residual current equal to 100% IΔn and measure operate time with an RCD tester. For non-Type S devices, verify operation in less than 300 ms.' GN3 Reg 4.2 mentions '0.5·IΔn, IΔn, and where appropriate 5·IΔn' as instrument capability context, not a mandatory sequence. No facet references a mandatory 2x In test step. The 1x/2x/5x In protocol is not grounded in the A4 RAG; requiring 2x In specifically has zero support. The OSG A4 picture is a single 100% IΔn verification.

confirmed_issues.claim: Zs for each phase can be calculated as Ze + R1+R2 for that phase; Ze is measured once and is common to all phases
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 1.08 facets (Table B3) are unambiguous: '[GN3] … Chapter 1 · Reg 1.08 · [requirement] · Apply Table B3 multipliers when calculating conductor resistance for EFLI' — 'When determining compliance with the earth-fault loop impedance (EFLI) values in Tables 41.2, 41.3 or 41.4 of BS 7671, the multipliers in Table B3 shall be applied to the values given in Table B1 to obtain the conductor and/or CPC resistance at maximum operating temperature.' The formula Zs = Ze + R1+R2 using cold resistance values is incomplete without applying the conductor temperature factor F from Table B3 before comparing against Tables 41.2–41.4. The page omits this mandatory temperature correction step.

content_gaps.missing: Table 64 (not Table 61) is the correct A4:2026 reference for IR test voltages and minima. The 250 V DC option when equipment cannot be safely disconnected (Reg 643.3 A4:2026 redraft) is entirely omitted — critical for VSD/inverter-heavy three-phase installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.3: 'Where equipment is likely to influence the verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used.' Also: Table 64 numeric minima from Reg 643.3.2.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 deleted Appendix 3 Table 3A and replaced the multi-multiplier RCD test (0.5x/1x/2x/5x In) with a single AC test at IΔn (Reg 643.3). The FAQ's instruction to test at 1x, 2x, and 5x In is now incorrect under A4:2026 and must be updated.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.3: 'Table 3A (Time/current performance criteria for RCDs) in Appendix 3 has been deleted… the single alternating current test at IΔn is used for verification regardless of type.' Also practical_work_intelligence: multipole RCD test on at least one pole (L1) as routine; repeat L2/L3 if doubt.

content_gaps.missing: Temperature correction of measured Zs using GN3 Table B3 conductor temperature factor F (R_hot = R_cold × F). The page presents Zs = Ze + R1+R2 as the complete formula without noting that cold-measured R1+R2 must be multiplied by the appropriate Table B3 factor before comparison against Tables 41.2–41.4.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 1.08: 'R_hot = R_cold × F; use R_hot for EFLI (Zs) calculations against Tables 41.2–41.4.' Table B3 provides multipliers applied to Table B1 cold-resistance values for maximum operating temperature.

content_gaps.missing: Polarity verification on three-phase systems (correct phase-to-neutral conductor assignments at every point) is a required BS 7671 dead test but is not mentioned in the page's dead-test section. Phase rotation and polarity are separate tests; the page conflates or omits polarity.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 442.1.2: 'Polarity checks for single-phase circuits and phase rotation for three-phase installations performed with appropriate testers shall be recorded on the Generic Schedule of Test Results (Appx 6).' [GN3] Reg 2.30: phase sequence confirmation via continuity method.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671 requires RCD protection (not exceeding 30mA) for socket outlets in certain locations used to supply portable equipment outdoors.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The A4:2026 RAG facets confirm the auditor's finding. The primary Reg 411.3.3 facet states the baseline rule is universal: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception in the Regulation is met' (context_prefix: 'Baseline requirement: RCD protection applies to 32 A and below socket-outlets'). The page's framing — 'certain locations used to supply portable equipment outdoors' — reflects only one of the three A4:2026 indents of 411.3.3. A separate facet (Reg 830.3.201) does confirm that RCD ≤30 mA applies to 'mobile equipment not exceeding 32 A rating for use outdoors, as required by Regulation 411.3.3', and a Reg 722.411.4.1 facet references that specific indent. However, the page presents outdoor/portable as the gating criterion for the whole regulation, which the RAG clearly shows is incorrect for A4:2026: the revised rule is blanket for all ≤32 A sockets (with a risk-assessment escape only for non-dwellings). The page's older framing is therefore confirmed as stale/incomplete against the A4:2026 RAG.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 revised Reg 411.3.3: RCD protection now mandatory for ALL socket outlets ≤32 A in dwellings (not just outdoor/portable-equipment circuits). The page's framing of 411.3.3 as applying only to outdoor portable-equipment sockets is stale pre-A4 language. This is a major compliance update the audience (homeowners, DIYers) should know when assessing whether their home sockets are compliant.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception is met. Omission is not permitted for dwellings.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4: AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises now also require 30 mA RCD protection. content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires [in domestic premises] shall be provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 AFDD recommendation: arc fault detection devices are now recommended for AC final circuits in fixed installations to mitigate fire risk from arcing. Arcing at loose connections (e.g. worn extension-lead sockets) is a core theme of the page; AFDD in the consumer unit is the upstream protection measure that catches exactly this hazard. A content gap that makes the page less than best-in-class.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Regulation 643.2 requires continuity testing of all ring final circuit conductors and all protective conductors.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG contains eight distinct facets for Reg 643.2.1 — all scoped under that sub-clause number — and zero facets for a standalone parent 'Reg 643.2'. The A4:2026 codified clause is 643.2.1, which the RAG confirms covers both protective conductors ('continuity of protective conductors, including protective bonding conductors, shall be verified', context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.2.1') and live conductors in ring final circuits ('for ring final circuits, the continuity of live conductors shall be verified by a measurement of resistance', same context_prefix). The page's repeated citation of '643.2' throughout all sections and the FAQ drops the mandatory .1 suffix. This is a genuine citation error against the A4:2026 text: the correct regulation is 643.2.1, not a non-existent parent 643.2.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page omits temperature correction of R1+R2 values (no mention of correcting from 20°C site measurement to 70°C operating temperature or the GN3 0.80 site-test factor).
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets confirm this is a genuine and material omission: (1) Every Zs table facet in the RAG carries an embedded 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor)' value alongside each tabulated Zs (e.g. context_prefix 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Part 4 Chapter 41 Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a) Type B MCB 230V', content: '1.37 ohm … Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.10 ohm'), confirming the 0.80 site-test factor is a live A4:2026 requirement that any guide covering R1+R2 must address. (2) OSG Reg 1.08 facets explicitly require ambient temperature correction when measurements differ from the 10°C reference ('the appropriate ambient correction factor from Table B8 shall be applied to earth fault loop impedances of Tables B1–B6 if the ambient temperature is other than 10°C', context_prefix '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 1 · Reg 1.08'). (3) OSG Reg 4.6.4 confirms R1+R2 Table 11 values are at 20°C and require temperature correction before Zs verification. A continuity testing guide that calculates R1+R2 values without instructing the reader to apply the temperature correction factor before comparing against Zs limits is missing a requirement that is explicitly embedded in every A4:2026 Zs acceptance criterion in the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 0.80 site-test factor: every Zs table value in the RAG carries a 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor)' column (e.g., 32A Type B MCB tabulated Zs 1.37Ω, site limit 1.10Ω). The page discusses Zs compliance but never mentions that a measured R1+R2+Ze must be compared against 80% of the tabulated limit when testing at ambient temperature. This is a major practical omission for any electrician using this guide on site.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a) facets: 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.10 ohm' (32A Type B MCB); GN3 Reg 3.18: 'a correction factor must be applied for meaningful comparison with Zs values'; OSG Reg 1.08: 'ambient temperature correction factor adjusts conductor resistance so that the corrected value is on the same temperature basis as the tabulated Zs values (10°C).'

content_gaps.missing: Temperature correction of R1+R2 from 20°C ambient to 70°C maximum operating temperature. GN3 explicitly provides a worked example of adjusting R1+R2 from 20°C to 70°C. The page makes no mention of this correction, leaving readers with a technically incomplete Zs calculation workflow.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 5.78: 'Measurements of R (R1 + R2) taken at 20°C can be adjusted to an equivalent value at 70°C by applying temperature correction factors to conductor resistance. The corrected Zs at 70°C is then used with Cin and U0 to compute prospective fault current for device curve selection.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 643.2.1 precise sub-clause wording: the page refers to 'Regulation 643.2' throughout but the A4:2026 operative sub-clause is 643.2.1. A best-in-class guide should cite the correct sub-clause number and note that 643.2.1 applies 'if any' exposed or extraneous conductive parts are present — a nuance the page does not convey.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 643.2.1 facet: 'The requirement to verify continuity by resistance measurement applies if any exposed-conductive-parts and extraneous-conductive-parts are present. If there are no exposed or extraneous conductive parts in the installation or the part under test, this continuity verification requirement is not applicable.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 three-step test terminology: GN3 names the ring test steps r1, rx (cross-connected line-to-neutral), rz (cross-connected line-to-CPC). The page uses 'r1, rn, r2' and describes the cross-connect in Step 2 against the neutral, while GN3 Reg 2.19 defines Step 3 as the cross-connection of line and CPC. Aligning terminology with GN3's step labels would make the page authoritative for C&G 2391 candidates.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 2.19: 'Step 3 of the ring continuity test procedure requires the open ends of the line conductor and the CPC (circuit protective conductor) to be cross-connected.' GN3 Reg 2.17: 'measuring specific loop resistances (r1, rx, rz) to verify the integrity of line, neutral and protective conductors in the ring.'

top_improvements.action: Add a GN3 0.80 site-test factor callout to the R1+R2 and Regulation 643.2 sections. Explain that measured Zs (Ze + R1+R2) must not exceed 80% of the BS 7671 Appendix 3 tabulated maximum when tested at ambient temperature — show an example (e.g., 32A Type B MCB: tabulated 1.37Ω, on-site limit 1.10Ω). This is the single most practically important omission on the page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a) facets: 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.10 ohm' (32A Type B MCB); GN3 Reg 3.18: 'a correction factor must be applied for meaningful comparison with Zs values.'

top_improvements.action: Add temperature correction of R1+R2 content: explain that R1+R2 measured cold (20°C) must be corrected to maximum conductor operating temperature (70°C for thermoplastic insulation) before comparing against Zs limits, or alternatively applied as the GN3 0.80 site-test factor. Reference the worked example in GN3 Reg 5.78.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 5.78: 'Measurements of R (R1+R2) taken at 20°C can be adjusted to an equivalent value at 70°C by applying temperature correction factors to conductor resistance.'; OSG Reg 1.08: 'ambient temperature correction factor adjusts conductor resistance so that the corrected value is on the same temperature basis as the tabulated Zs values (10°C).'

top_improvements.action: Correct all citations of 'Regulation 643.2' to 'Regulation 643.2.1' to match A4:2026 sub-clause numbering as indexed in the RAG. Add the nuance that 643.2.1 applies only 'if any' exposed or extraneous conductive parts are present — currently missing, which could mislead on fully-shrouded PELV installations.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 643.2.1 facets: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.2.1'; 'The requirement...applies if any exposed-conductive-parts and extraneous-conductive-parts are present.'

top_improvements.action: Align ring circuit step terminology with GN3: the page calls the cross-connection test 'Step 2' and names values r1/rn/r2. GN3 Reg 2.17 and 2.19 use r1/rx/rz with Step 3 being the line-to-CPC cross-connection. Update step labels and variable names to match GN3 so that C&G 2391 candidates find the page authoritative.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 2.19: 'Step 3…requires the open ends of the line conductor and the CPC to be cross-connected.' GN3 Reg 2.17: 'measuring specific loop resistances (r1, rx, rz).'

top_improvements.action: Add instrument proving (proving unit) to the lead-nulling procedure and the efficiency tips section. Currently the page only mentions nulling leads — but the practical_work_intelligence RAG identifies 'failing to prove the instrument before testing' as the top common mistake for this task.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: common_mistakes: 'failing to prove the instrument before testing', 'measuring without isolating and proving dead'; tools_required: 'proving unit'.

confirmed_issues.claim: Minimum overhead cable height 5.2m where vehicles can pass underneath (driveways)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG contradicts the 5.2m figure. Reg 709.521.1.8 example facet states the vehicle movement height is 'not less than 6 m' and explicitly notes: 'If existing clearance is 5 m, supports must be relocated or conductor raised to achieve 6 m.' The page's figure of 5.2m is not supported by any RAG facet and is lower than the 6m mandatory minimum stated in the regulation.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD protection for socket outlet circuits in TN system cited under Regulation 411.3.4
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG emphatically contradicts this citation. Multiple Reg 411.3.4 facets confirm it 'applies specifically to AC final circuits that are supplying luminaires' in 'domestic (household) premises' only — not socket-outlet circuits. The correct regulation for socket-outlet RCD protection is Reg 411.3.3, which multiple facets confirm now applies to all socket-outlets rated ≤32 A (with a documented risk-assessment exception only for non-dwellings). Citing 411.3.4 as the basis for socket-outlet RCD protection is a direct regulatory misattribution confirmed by the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: PME/TN-C-S prohibition for outbuildings: the page never mentions that a PME (TN-C-S) earthing terminal from the DNO supply must NOT be used as the earth for an outbuilding or structure separate from the main dwelling. This is a critical safety omission — under PME conditions the correct approach is a TT earth electrode for the outbuilding. Practical_work_intelligence record keywords include 'PME' explicitly in the outbuilding supply topic.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: keywords=['PME', 'outbuilding supply', 'earthing', 'armoured cable'] — the omission of PME prohibition is a known common-knowledge gap in outbuilding supply guides.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — mandatory 30mA RCD protection for lighting circuits in domestic premises. The page mentions RCD for sockets and TT all-circuit protection but never addresses that lighting circuits in the outbuilding (when attached to or ancillary to a dwelling) require 30mA RCD under Reg 411.3.4. The page actually mis-cites 411.3.4 as a socket-outlet regulation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 facet: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 AFDD recommendation: for single-phase socket-outlet circuits ≤32A in domestic premises the standard recommends AFDDs. A best-in-class outbuilding guide for 2026 should at minimum note this recommendation for workshop/garage socket circuits.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 facets: 'For all other premises... Regulation 421.1.7 recommends AFDDs for single-phase AC final circuits supplying socket-outlets not exceeding 32 A.'

content_gaps.missing: Ra × IΔn ≤ 50V compliance formula for TT earth electrode sizing. The page states 'typically <200 ohms' without explaining the compliance test. The actual BS 7671 criterion is Ra × IΔn ≤ 50V (Reg 411.5.3) — with a 30mA RCD this allows up to 1667Ω, so 200Ω is very conservative and the figure given has no regulatory basis in the RAG.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.5.3 facet: 'Ra * Ign ≤ 50 V where Ra is the sum of the resistances of the earth electrode and the protective conductor connecting it to the exposed-conductive-parts (in ohms) and Ign is the rated residual operating current of the RCD.'

top_improvements.action: Fix the Regulation 411.3.4 mis-citation. The page states 'Regulation 411.3.4' requires RCD protection for socket-outlet circuits in TN systems — this is incorrect. Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) mandates 30mA RCD for AC lighting circuits in domestic premises, not sockets. Correct the reference for socket-outlet RCD protection (cite OSG 4.6.4 / general requirement), and add a new statement that lighting circuits in the outbuilding require 30mA RCD under Reg 411.3.4 if the outbuilding is ancillary to a domestic dwelling.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 facets: 'Regulation 411.3.4 applies specifically to AC final circuits that are supplying luminaires... The obligation is targeted at circuits whose purpose is to supply luminaires.' / [OSG] Reg 4.6.4: 'all socket-outlets are required to be protected by a 30mA RCD.'

top_improvements.action: Add a prominent warning section on PME/TN-C-S earthing: where the main dwelling has a PME (TN-C-S) supply from the DNO, the PME earth terminal must NOT be extended to a separate outbuilding structure. The outbuilding must have its own TT earth electrode. This is a safety-critical omission — PME voltage rise under a neutral fault can appear on the outbuilding metalwork if the PME earth is extended. Practical_work_intelligence keywords confirm 'PME' as a core outbuilding supply topic.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: primary_topic 'Specification and installation guidance for electrical installations serving outbuildings/garages', keywords=['PME', 'outbuilding supply', 'earthing', 'armoured cable'].

top_improvements.action: Replace the '200 ohms' earth electrode resistance figure with the correct BS 7671 compliance criterion: Ra × IΔn ≤ 50V. For a 30mA RCD this gives a maximum Ra of 1667Ω, so 200Ω is a significant conservative over-statement that may cause unnecessary remedial work. Explain that compliance is confirmed by calculation, not a fixed ohm target.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.5.3: 'Ra * Ign ≤ 50 V where Ra is the sum of the resistances of the earth electrode and the protective conductor... and Ign is the rated residual operating current of the RCD.'

top_improvements.action: Add Reg 421.1.7 AFDD note for outbuilding workshop/garage socket circuits. For single-phase AC socket-outlet circuits ≤32A, AFDDs are recommended under A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7. A modern workshop consumer unit with RCBOs per circuit can include AFDD+RCBO combinations. This positions the page for post-A4:2026 relevance and differentiates it from older guides.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 facet: 'For all other premises... recommends AFDDs for single-phase AC final circuits supplying socket-outlets not exceeding 32 A.'

top_improvements.action: State the voltage drop compliance limits explicitly (5% max for non-lighting final circuits from OSG; 3% recommended). Currently the page references the calculator without quoting the thresholds. Adding the actual percentages makes the guide self-contained and improves depth score.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [OSG] Reg 7.1: 'For final circuits other than lighting circuits, the voltage drop shall not exceed 5%.' [BS 7671] Reg 125.8: lighting 6% / other 8% for private LV.

confirmed_issues.claim: Check for cumulative earth leakage by measuring with a clamp meter on the protective conductor
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 1.08 ([GN3] · Chapter 1 · Reg 1.08 · [procedure] · measurement procedure using earth leakage current clamp meter) states verbatim: 'Use a suitably rated earth leakage current clamp meter around the live conductors (excluding the protective conductor) at a test point to measure earth leakage current.' A second facet (Reg 1.08 [definition]) reinforces this: 'enclosing live conductors in the clamp while excluding the protective conductor.' The page instructs clamping the protective conductor, which is directly contradicted by GN3 Reg 1.08. The measurement must be taken around the live conductors only; clamping the PE measures return current on the protective conductor, not the leakage sum from the live conductors.

content_gaps.missing: RCBO type selection: Type AC is only suitable for sinusoidal residual currents. Modern loads (EV chargers, inverters, variable-speed drives, LED drivers) produce pulsating DC components requiring Type A (minimum) or Type F/B. The page makes no mention of RCBO operating characteristic, which is a critical diagnostic and replacement consideration under BS 7671 Reg 531.3.4 / OSG Reg 3.6.4.5.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG Reg 3.6.4.5: 'The selection of RCD operating characteristic shall be made based on the nature of residual currents expected on the specific circuit.' BS 7671 Reg 534.4.7 confirms Type B for multi-phase rectified DC.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 AFDD recommendation (A4:2026): An RCBO keeps-tripping guide is an ideal place to explain that serial arcing faults — a cause of intermittent trips — are now specifically addressed by the BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 recommendation for AFDDs on AC final circuits. The page does not mention this at all.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends the installation of AFDDs to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits due to the effects of arc fault currents.'

content_gaps.missing: Correct IR test procedure per GN3: The page describes 'L-E and N-E' as if these are two separate tests. GN3 Reg 2.21 specifies L and N linked together measured to E as the standard site method. For equipment sensitive to the test voltage, this linked approach prevents damage and is the normative GN3 procedure. Not mentioned.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 2.21: 'The line and neutral conductors may be linked/connected together and a single insulation resistance test made between the linked conductors (L&N) and protective earth (E).'

content_gaps.missing: 30% accumulated leakage rule (Reg 531.3.1.202): The page discusses nuisance trips and accumulated leakage qualitatively but never states the quantified BS 7671 limit — that total downstream leakage must not exceed 30% of the RCBO's rated residual current (9 mA for a 30 mA device). This is the single most useful design/diagnostic number for this topic.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 531.3.1.202: 'the accumulation of such currents downstream of the RCD shall be not more than 30% of the rated residual operating current.'

seo.notes: The page has no direct-answer summary block under the H1 — the hero section gives a subtitle about 'practical guide' but the first actual text is a nuanced paragraph about trip patterns, not a scannable answer. Title tag includes the number '8 Common Causes' but the page body only lists 5 bullet-point causes in the common-causes section — a mismatch that could trigger a quality signal if crawled.

top_improvements.action: Fix the clamp-meter instruction: change 'measuring with a clamp meter on the protective conductor' to 'clamp around the live conductors only (excluding the PE), per the GN3 earth leakage clamp method'. Current wording is contradicted by GN3 Reg 1.08 and will mislead site users into an incorrect test.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 1.08 — earth leakage current clamp meter procedure

top_improvements.action: Add an explicit callout stating the 30% accumulated-leakage rule (Reg 531.3.1.202): 'For a 30 mA RCBO, total downstream earth leakage from all loads must not exceed 9 mA (30% of 30 mA) to avoid nuisance trips.' This is the single most actionable quantified fact missing from the page and directly answers the nuisance-trip scenario described.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 531.3.1.202 — downstream leakage accumulation ≤ 30% of rated residual current

top_improvements.action: Add a short section on RCBO type (Type AC / Type A / Type F): explain that Type AC-only RCBOs may nuisance-trip or fail to trip correctly on circuits with switchmode power supplies, EV chargers, or variable-speed drives producing pulsating DC — and that Type A is the minimum for most modern domestic and commercial circuits. This is a leading cause of 'unexplained' RCBO behaviour and is directly supported by OSG Reg 3.6.4.5 / BS 7671 Reg 534.4.7.
top_improvements.grounded_in: OSG Reg 3.6.4.5 — RCD type selection based on nature of residual currents; BS 7671 Reg 534.4.7

top_improvements.action: Correct and clarify the IR test procedure: replace 'L-E and N-E on the disconnected circuit' with the GN3-standard description (L and N linked together, single L&N-E test at 500 V DC, minimum 1.0 MΩ per Table 64). Add the 500 V DC test voltage explicitly — the page currently omits it, leaving the threshold value (1 MΩ) context-free.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 2.21 — L&N linked to E procedure; BS 7671 Table 64 (Reg 643.3.2) — 500 V DC / 1.0 MΩ minimum

top_improvements.action: Add an answer-first block directly under the H1: a 2–3 sentence direct answer (e.g. 'An RCBO trips because it detects either excess current or residual earth-leakage current on its protected circuit. The most common causes are overload, accumulated earth leakage from multiple appliances, damaged insulation, moisture ingress, and a mismatch between RCBO type and modern electronic loads.'). This opens the featured-snippet slot and addresses the E-E-A-T gap.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 GN3 Reg 1.2.2 — RCBO dual-function definition; BS 7671 Reg 531.3.1.202 — accumulated leakage

confirmed_issues.claim: Occupants should be advised to press the test button monthly as a routine check
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 514.12.1 and 514.12.2 (bs7671, context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 51 · Reg 514.12.1/514.12.2 · Six-monthly RCD user test instruction') give verbatim mandatory notice wording: 'Test six-monthly by pressing the relevant test button(s) which should operate the device.' OSG 11.6 (context_prefix: '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 11 · Reg 11.6 · Six-monthly pressing of RCD test button notice') independently confirms the six-monthly interval. The page states 'monthly' in at least three locations; this directly contradicts the RAG-grounded BS 7671+A4:2026 requirement. This is a genuine factual error — the interval must be six-monthly, not monthly.

confirmed_issues.claim: A general-type RCD is described as 'sometimes called Type AC or Type A depending on waveform sensitivity', framing them as interchangeable names
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 534.4.7 facets (bs7671, context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 534.4.7') explicitly define Type AC and Type A as separate types with different detection scopes: Type AC trips on sinusoidal AC residual current only; Type A trips on sinusoidal AC and residual pulsating DC. Reg 531.3.4.201 (bs7671, context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 531.3.4.201 · Definition: Types AC, A, F and B RCD classification') lists all four types as distinct. GN3 2.32 (gn3, context_prefix: '[GN3] · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.32 · Type AC vs Type A RCD testing capability distinction') further distinguishes them at instrument level, noting that a Type AC instrument cannot verify Type A behaviour. Treating them as alternative names for the same device is factually incorrect and contradicted by multiple RAG facets.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 5.6 nuance: for Type AC RCDs, the manufacturer may declare 250 mA as the test current for the 40 ms trip test instead of 5x IΔn (150 mA for a 30 mA device). Failing to trip at 5x IΔn within 40 ms does NOT automatically mean the device is faulty where the manufacturer has declared 250 mA. The page treats 5x IΔn as universal and tells readers to condemn (C2) any device that fails at 150 mA — this is wrong for manufacturer-declared Type AC devices.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 5.6 [requirement/note]: 'The manufacturer may select a current of 250 mA for the Type AC 40 ms tripping test instead of 5xIΔn... Where the manufacturer declares a 250 mA current for the Type AC 40 ms test, failing to trip within 40 ms at 5xIΔn does not necessarily mean the RCD is faulty.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 643.3 change: the standard now requires an AC test at IΔn regardless of RCD type (AC, A, F, B). Previously, Type A/F/B RCDs needed specific waveform testing. The page does not mention this important A4 procedural change, which affects which test mode to select on the MFT.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 643.3 [requirement] (A4:2026): 'Regardless of RCD Type (AC, A, F, B etc.), an alternating current test at rated residual operating current (IΔn) shall be used to verify the effectiveness of the RCD.'

content_gaps.missing: Type AC restriction under Reg 534.4.7 / GN3 3.3: Type AC RCDs are restricted to fixed equipment where it is known the load current contains NO DC components. The page never clearly warns that Type AC cannot be used on circuits with variable speed drives, EV chargers, inverters, or other DC-producing loads — a major practical gap for modern installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 534.4.7 [requirement]: 'RCD Type AC shall only be used to serve fixed equipment where it is known that the load current contains no DC components.' GN3 3.3 [requirement]: same restriction confirmed.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4: domestic lighting circuits must now have additional protection by a 30 mA RCD. This is an A4:2026 addition that inspectors must check when carrying out EICRs on domestic premises. Relevant to RCD testing context because it creates new circuits requiring RCD verification.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 411.3.4 [requirement]: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA... Applies to AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'
