top_improvements.action: Correct all three instances of 'monthly' test button advice to 'six-monthly' to match the mandatory Reg 514.12.1/514.12.2 notice wording. The current text directly contradicts the required BS 7671 statutory label text. Affected locations: push-button section body, FAQ 'Is the push-button test sufficient?' answer, and recording results section.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 514.12.1 [requirement] + 514.12.2 [requirement]: mandatory RCD notice wording 'Test six-monthly by pressing the relevant test button(s)' (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026).

top_improvements.action: Fix the Type AC / Type A conflation in the FAQ answer for 'What is the difference between a general-type RCD and a Type S (time-delayed) RCD?' The page describes general-type as 'sometimes called Type AC or Type A depending on waveform sensitivity' — these are distinct device types with different application rules. Add a clear note that Type AC is restricted to fixed loads with NO DC components (Reg 534.4.7) and that modern circuits with inverters, EV chargers, or VSD loads require Type A or higher.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 534.4.7 [requirement]: 'RCD Type AC shall only be used to serve fixed equipment where it is known that the load current contains no DC components.' GN3 3.3 confirms the same restriction.

top_improvements.action: Add a callout or note in the '5x Rated Current Test' section explaining the GN3 5.6 manufacturer-declared test current exception: for Type AC RCDs, some manufacturers declare 250 mA as the test current for the 40 ms trip test instead of 5x IΔn. An RCD failing to trip within 40 ms at 150 mA (5x IΔn) is not necessarily faulty if the manufacturer has declared 250 mA — the tester must check the manufacturer data before classifying C2.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 5.6 [note]: 'Where the manufacturer declares a 250 mA current for the Type AC 40 ms test, failing to trip within 40 ms at 5xIΔn does not necessarily mean the RCD is faulty.' GN3 5.6 [requirement]: 'The manufacturer may select a current of 250 mA for the Type AC 40 ms tripping test instead of 5xIΔn.'

top_improvements.action: Add a short paragraph in 'What Is RCD Testing?' or a dedicated sub-section on the A4:2026 Reg 643.3 change: the standard now requires an AC test at IΔn regardless of RCD Type (AC, A, F, B). Include a practical note that if the MFT has a Type selector, the tester should select Type AC for the 1x IΔn effectiveness test per GN3 4.7, and that instruments without a type-selector can only perform Type AC tests.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 643.3 [requirement] (A4:2026): 'Regardless of RCD Type (AC, A, F, B etc.), an alternating current test at rated residual operating current (IΔn) shall be used to verify the effectiveness of the RCD.' GN3 4.7 [requirement]: tester shall select Type AC for 1x IΔn test when instrument provides selectable modes.

top_improvements.action: Either verify the Type S 5x IΔn (50–200 ms) claim against the manufacturer standards (BS EN 61008/61009) and add a source citation, or qualify it explicitly as a device-standard requirement rather than a BS 7671 requirement. The RAG returns no facet supporting 50–200 ms at 5x for Type S — this is unverified in the available BS 7671/GN3/OSG corpus.
top_improvements.grounded_in: No OSG/GN3 facet found for Type S 5x IΔn trip window. OSG 11.3 only covers 1x IΔn (130–500 ms) for Type S. Claim needs source attribution or qualification.

confirmed_issues.claim: The page does not mention Reg 551.7.1(d) prohibition on connecting a generator to the load side of an RCD.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet [BS 7671] Reg 551.7.1(d) explicitly confirms: 'Indent (d) of Regulation 551.7.1 prohibits the connection of a source to the load side of an RCD under certain conditions. The redraft introduces an explicit prohibition that a source (for example a generator, inverter or other supply) shall not be connected on the RCD protected side where the specified conditions apply.' A second facet confirms the A4:2026 redraft added new indents (c) and (d) to 551.7.1, addressing exactly this prohibition. The page omits this A4:2026 requirement in both its ATS and for-electricians sections — a genuine content gap confirmed by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: The page does not cover the portable generator Class I/II permutation rules (OSG Reg 2.4.3): only supply one or more Class II items, one Class I item, or combination Class II plus one Class I item.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns two OSG Reg 2.4.3 facets confirming both permutations: (a) 'A portable generator isolated from earth shall only be used to supply one or more items of Class II equipment' and (c) 'A portable generator isolated from earth shall only be used to supply one or more items of Class II equipment together with one item of Class I equipment.' A third facet adds a verification procedure. These are A4-edition OSG requirements that directly apply to portable generators. The page omits this constraint entirely — confirmed gap.

confirmed_issues.claim: The page does not mention Reg 560.6.13: rotary generating sets used as safety sources must conform to BS ISO 8528-12.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet [BS 7671] Reg 560.6.13 confirms verbatim: 'Where a rotary generating set is used as a safety source, it shall conform to BS ISO 8528-12. This is a mandatory conformity requirement from Regulation 560.6.13 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026.' Two further facets confirm scope (applies when used as safety source) and the referenced standard. The page makes no mention of this requirement anywhere — confirmed content gap against the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: The page does not mention the A4:2026 AFDD recommendation (Reg 421.1.7) as relevant to generator-fed fixed installations.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 confirms: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' The scope facet confirms it applies to 'AC final circuits of a fixed installation' — which includes generator-fed fixed installations. While the wording is recommendatory ('recommending', not 'shall'), the A4:2026 addition is a relevant consideration for generator-connected fixed installation pages. The page omits it entirely. Confirmed gap, noting that the RAG characterises the obligation as advisory rather than mandatory.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 551.7.1(d) A4:2026 prohibition: a generator or other source shall not be connected on the load side of an RCD under certain conditions. This is a critical A4:2026 change that directly affects ATS panel wiring and portable generator connections. The page's ATS and for-electricians sections have no mention of this.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 551.7.1(d): 'prohibits the connection of a source (for example a generator, inverter or other supply) on the RCD protected side where the specified conditions apply.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 560.6.13 / BS ISO 8528-12 conformity requirement: rotary generating sets used as safety sources (emergency lighting, fire suppression, hospital/critical systems) must conform to BS ISO 8528-12. This is a mandatory requirement the page omits entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 560.6.13: 'Where a rotary generating set is used as a safety source, it shall conform to BS ISO 8528-12.'

content_gaps.missing: OSG Reg 2.4.3 portable generator permutation rules: an isolated-from-earth portable generator may only supply (a) one or more Class II items, (b) one Class I item, or (c) Class II items plus one Class I item — never more than one Class I item simultaneously. Critical practical safety rule absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) Reg 2.4.3: 'Permitted permutation (a): one or more items of Class II equipment'; 'Permitted permutation (c): combination Class II plus one Class I item.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 133.1.3 certification recording obligation: the EIC must explicitly record generator/changeover arrangement usage per the new A4:2026 requirement. The for-electricians section mentions EIC completion but does not cite this specific A4:2026 obligation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 133.1.3 (A4:2026): 'certain usage of equipment shall be recorded on the appropriate electrical certification specified in Part 6 of BS 7671.' Reg 411.9.3 checklist cross-references 551.6/551.7 for generators as a certification confirmation item.

seo.notes: The page opens with a keyTakeaways summary block under the hero H1 — this functions as a direct answer block and is strong for featured-snippet eligibility. A GuideTemplate component is used which renders FAQ items that can be marked up with FAQPage schema (depends on template implementation). Internal links are minimal: /eic-certificate (×2 references in for-electricians section), /industrial-earthing-systems, /transformer-installation-guide, /power-factor-correction, /industrial-electrical-installation in breadcrumb — approximately 5 unique href targets. No author attribution or credentials block present (E-E-A-T weakness). The hero title 'Generator Installation Guide UK | Standby Generator Electrical' includes the primary keyword. Meta description truncates with '…' which loses the value proposition. No structured data explicitly coded in the TSX (schema depends entirely on GuideTemplate). The page breadcrumbs add one level of crawl hierarchy signal.

top_improvements.action: Add a dedicated warning box in the ATS section covering A4:2026 Reg 551.7.1(d): a generator source must not be connected to the load side of an RCD under the conditions specified in the regulation. This is a common wiring mistake in ATS panels and a new A4:2026 mandatory requirement that practitioners will search for.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 551.7.1(d): 'prohibits the connection of a source (for example a generator, inverter or other supply) on the RCD protected side where the specified conditions apply.'

top_improvements.action: Add a 'Safety Sources and BS ISO 8528-12' callout in the Testing & Maintenance section: generators used as safety sources (hospitals, fire systems, emergency lighting) must conform to Reg 560.6.13 / BS ISO 8528-12. State this is a mandatory requirement and that compliance documentation should be in the commissioning pack. This is a high-value differentiator for the hospital/critical facilities audience.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 560.6.13: 'Where a rotary generating set is used as a safety source, it shall conform to BS ISO 8528-12.'

top_improvements.action: Add a subsection or callout in the for-electricians section citing A4:2026 Reg 133.1.3: the EIC must now explicitly record generator/changeover arrangement usage, and the 411.9.3 checklist (new in A4:2026) confirms generator adequate arrangements (551.6/551.7) as a certification confirmation field. Include a note that the Elec-Mate EIC app captures this field.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 133.1.3 (A4:2026): 'certain usage of equipment shall be recorded on the appropriate electrical certification specified in Part 6.' Reg 411.9.3 checklist: cross-references 551.6/551.7 for generators.

top_improvements.action: Add a portable generator section covering OSG Reg 2.4.3 Class I/II permutation rules: an isolated-from-earth portable generator may only supply a controlled combination of Class I and Class II equipment (never more than one Class I item simultaneously). This is missing entirely and is the most-searched practical question for site electricians using portable generators.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [OSG] Reg 2.4.3: permitted permutations (a) one or more Class II items; (b) one Class I item; (c) Class II items plus one Class I item. Safety rationale: no protective earth reference on isolated generator.

top_improvements.action: Correct the earth electrode resistance thresholds: remove the ungrounded '<1 Ω HV / <10 Ω LV' values and replace with the RAG-grounded approach — generator earth electrode shall be tested using a proprietary earth electrode resistance tester (BS 7430 method) and the value assessed against the ability of protective devices to operate, not a fixed Ω limit. The OSG/GN3 do not specify those thresholds for LV generators.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] Reg 2.28: 'electrode resistance values may not have been specified because there is a separate metallic earth conductor'; [PWI] 'Reference earthing of a portable generator...earth electrode (BS 7430)...proprietary earth electrode resistance tester.'

confirmed_issues.claim: EIC is required by BS 7671 Section 631
confirmed_issues.evidence: Direct query for context_prefix ILIKE '%Reg 631%' returns zero rows. A broader ILIKE '%631%' search returns only PAS 63100 battery-storage references — no inspection/testing 'Section 631' exists in the A4:2026 RAG. The controlling A4:2026 regulations are Reg 644.4.201 (Part 6, Chapter 64: 'An Electrical Installation Certificate based on the model given in Appendix 6 shall be issued... upon completion of the verification') and Reg 120.3 (Part 1, Chapter 12: scope of EIC use for initial certification, additions/alterations, and consumer unit replacement). 'Section 631' is stale 17th-edition numbering with no live facet in the corpus. The page citation must be updated to Reg 644.4.201 / Reg 120.3.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Appendix 6 provides model forms for the EIC — recommended forms, not mandatory
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two RAG facets directly contradict 'recommended, not mandatory'. Reg 644.4.201 facet (context_prefix: '...Reg 644.4.201 · [reference] · Model form for Electrical Installation Certificate in Appendix 6'): 'The Electrical Installation Certificate referred to in Regulation 644.4.201 shall be based on the model given in Appendix 6 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The certificate issued must conform to that model.' Reg 644.3 facet (context_prefix: '...Reg 644.3 · [requirement] · Schedules shall be based on models (mandatory wording shall)'): 'The regulation uses the mandatory term shall to state that the schedules shall be based on the models in Appendix 6. This is a compulsory requirement, not guidance.' The word 'shall' and 'must conform' make Appendix 6 normative. The page's characterisation of these forms as merely 'recommended' is factually wrong per A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Consumer unit replacement — many contractors issue a Minor Works certificate for each circuit, though some scheme providers prefer an EIC
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 2.5 facet is unambiguous (context_prefix: '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.5 · [exception] · MEIWC/EIC not permitted for replacement of distribution boards or consumer units'): 'The MEIWC or EIC shall not be used for the replacement of distribution boards, consumer units or similar items. Replacement of these items is excluded from minor works certification and requires the appropriate full certification/documentation.' practical_work_intelligence also confirms: 'EIC must be completed for all new installations, new circuits, additions or alterations to existing circuits or replacement consumer units.' The page's framing of Minor Works as an acceptable contractor practice is wrong — GN3 prohibits it with 'shall not'.

confirmed_issues.claim: EIC must be issued for replacement of a consumer unit (page implies Minor Works may be acceptable as an alternative)
confirmed_issues.evidence: This is the same factual error as claim 3 but assessed from the EIC-issuance side. Reg 644.4.201 facet (context_prefix: '...Reg 644.4.201 · [scope] · Applicability of regulation 644.4.201 to replacement of distribution board or consumer unit'): 'Regulation 644.4.201 applies, upon completion of the verification of the replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit.' Reg 120.3 facet: 'The Electrical Installation Certificate is to be used when a consumer unit or distribution board is replaced.' GN3 Reg 2.5 facet prohibits MEIWC for this work ('shall not be used'). practical_work_intelligence: 'EIC must be completed for... replacement consumer units.' All four sources agree — EIC is mandatory and Minor Works is explicitly excluded. Any page framing that presents Minor Works as an acceptable alternative for consumer unit replacement is wrong.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 mandatory SPD and AFDD fields on the Appendix 6 EIC model form. The page never mentions that the form now has dedicated fields for recording surge protective devices (SPDs) and arc fault detection devices (AFDDs). This is a significant omission for any 2026 EIC — installers who fit SPDs or AFDDs must record them and will be looking for this.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201 context_prefix: 'Appendix 6 Model forms for certification and reporting have been amended to include fields for recording the details of SPDs (surge protective devices) and AFDDs (arc fault detection devices). Installers shall record SPD and AFDD details on the model forms where such devices are installed.'

content_gaps.missing: The Schedule of Circuit Details is a distinct, mandatory companion document to the Schedule of Test Results. The page conflates the two or omits the Circuit Details schedule entirely. Reg 644.3(b) requires both separately.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 644.3(b) facet: 'The Certificate shall include Schedule(s) of Circuit Details and Schedule(s) of Test Results.' GN3 Reg 1.3: 'An EIC shall be accompanied by Schedules of Circuit Details and Schedules of Test Results, based on the model forms in Appendix 6.'

content_gaps.missing: The SELV/PELV insulation resistance row from Table 64 is absent. The page states only '1 MΩ at 500 V DC' but Table 64 also mandates 250 V DC / minimum 0.5 MΩ for SELV and PELV circuits. Any EIC covering SELV/PELV (e.g. doorbell, garden lighting, EV pilot circuits) needs this value.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 4.4 facet: 'For SELV and PELV circuits the specified DC test voltage is 250 V and the minimum insulation resistance shall be as given in Table 64.' BS 7671 Table 64 facet: 'For SELV and PELV circuits use 250 V DC with minimum 0.5 MΩ.'

content_gaps.missing: The consumer unit replacement certification rule is stated incorrectly. GN3 Reg 2.5 is unambiguous that neither EIC nor MEIWC alone is the prescribed form for CU replacement — 'full certification/documentation' is required. This is one of the most-searched UK electrician compliance questions and the page's current 'check with your scheme' hedge is a RAG-contradicted grey area.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 2.5 facet: 'The MEIWC or EIC shall not be used for the replacement of distribution boards, consumer units or similar items. Replacement of these items is excluded from minor works certification and requires the appropriate full certification/documentation.'

seo.notes: No direct answer block under the H1 — the hero section leads with a product CTA, not a definitional answer sentence. FAQ schema (FAQPage) is present in JSON-LD alongside SoftwareApplication and HowTo schemas — strong structured data. No author attribution, E-E-A-T signal, or qualifications badge anywhere on the page. The <title> tag is 'EIC Certificate App | Electrical Installation Certificate' — product-first framing reduces relevance for informational queries like 'what is an EIC certificate'. Meta description is generic and does not include a differentiating keyword or benefit hook. Page does not use H2-level keyword variants for queries such as 'electrical installation certificate requirements UK' or 'EIC test results required'.

top_improvements.action: Fix the regulation citation throughout the page. Replace every instance of 'BS 7671 Section 631' with 'BS 7671 Reg 120.3 and Reg 644.4.201'. This is the correct A4:2026 numbering. Section 631 is a 17th-edition artefact and returns zero facets in the live RAG.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 120.3 facet + Reg 644.4.201 facet (context_prefix: 'Part 1 — Chapter 12 — Reg 120.3' and 'Part 6 — Chapter 64 — Reg 644.4.201')

top_improvements.action: Add an A4:2026 callout section (or inline note in the 'Sections of the EIC Form Explained' block) stating that Appendix 6 model forms now include mandatory fields for SPDs and AFDDs. Electricians completing EICs in 2026 where these devices are installed must record them. This is a genuine content gap that competitors will not have and directly addresses searches for 'EIC certificate 2026 changes'.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201 facet: 'Appendix 6 Model forms...have been amended to include fields for recording the details of SPDs and AFDDs. Installers shall record SPD and AFDD details on the model forms where such devices are installed.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the consumer unit replacement claim. The current text presents MEIWC as a possibly-acceptable option for CU replacement — this is directly contradicted by GN3 Reg 2.5. Replace with: 'Consumer unit replacement cannot be certified on a Minor Works certificate or a standard EIC alone — GN3 states these forms are excluded for distribution board and consumer unit replacement, which requires appropriate full certification. In practice this means an EIC covering all circuits, accompanied by full Schedules of Circuit Details and Test Results for every circuit in the installation.'
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 2.5 facet: 'The MEIWC or EIC shall not be used for the replacement of distribution boards, consumer units or similar items.' practical_work_intelligence facet: 'EIC must be completed for...replacement consumer units and must include Schedule(s) of circuit details.'

top_improvements.action: Add the missing Table 64 row for SELV/PELV circuits in the FAQ answer and in the test results section: '250 V DC test voltage, minimum 0.5 MΩ for SELV and PELV circuits'. The current page only states 1 MΩ at 500 V DC, leaving EV pilot, doorbell, and garden circuit scenarios uncovered.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Table 64 facet: 'For SELV and PELV circuits use 250 V DC with minimum 0.5 MΩ.' GN3 Reg 4.4 facet: 'For SELV and PELV circuits the specified DC test voltage is 250 V.'

top_improvements.action: Clarify the mandatory vs recommended status of Appendix 6 forms. The FAQ answer currently says the forms are 'recommended, not mandatory'. Reg 644.4.201 and Reg 644.3 both use 'shall be based on the model given in Appendix 6' — the model is normative. Reword to: 'BS 7671 Reg 644.4.201 and Reg 644.3 state the certificate and its schedules shall be based on the Appendix 6 model forms. While alternative layouts are used in practice, all required information must be present and the form must conform to the model — it is a normative, not merely advisory, template.'
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 644.4.201 facet: 'The certificate issued must conform to that model.' Reg 644.3 facet: 'Schedules shall be based on the models in Appendix 6 of BS 7671.'

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 722.531.3.101 requires that the RCD protecting an EV charging circuit must be either Type A + 6mA DC detection, or Type B RCD.
confirmed_issues.evidence: All six facets indexed under Reg 722.531.3.101 concern transformer siting and the one-charging-point-per-transformer rule — none contain any RCD type requirement. Example facet: '[BS 7671] · Reg 722.531.3.101 · [requirement] · Placement of transformer for charging installation — close as possible': 'the transformer supplying the charging installation should be placed as close as possible to the transformer.' A broader tsv search for 'EV charging RCD type B 6mA DC detection' and '722.531 RCD protection charging' both return zero rows. The RAG contains no facet anywhere under Chapter 72 / 722.531 that supports a Type A+6mA or Type B RCD rule for EV circuits. The regulation number as cited on the page does not contain the stated rule in the RAG. The page attributes the EV RCD type requirement to the wrong regulation number.

confirmed_issues.claim: 30mA RCDs provide additional protection (BS 7671 Regulation 411.3.3). Required for socket outlets up to 32A, mobile equipment outdoors, cables in walls at less than 50mm depth, and EV charger circuits.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Eight facets for Reg 411.3.3 confirm its A4:2026 revised scope is limited to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32A, with a risk-assessment exception for non-dwellings. No facet for 411.3.3 references cables in walls at less than 50mm depth, mobile equipment used outdoors, or EV charger circuits. Searches for 'cable wall 50mm depth RCD', 'mobile equipment outdoors RCD 30mA' and '522.6.6 cable wall partition RCD' all return zero rows. The page incorrectly bundles the 50mm cable-in-wall rule, the mobile-equipment-outdoors rule, and EV charger circuits under a single citation to Reg 411.3.3. These requirements span different regulations (e.g. Reg 522.6.6 for cable depth) and are not supported by any 411.3.3 facet in the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 requires 30mA RCD protection for domestic lighting circuits — this is NOT mentioned anywhere on the page (content gap).
confirmed_issues.evidence: Eight facets for Reg 411.3.4 confirm this is a mandatory A4:2026 requirement. Representative facet: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.4 · [reference]': 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' The word 'shall' makes this a mandatory obligation. This is an A4:2026 addition (new regulation) directly relevant to any domestic electrician reading an RCD-types page. Its complete absence from the page is a confirmed content gap — the page fails to inform readers of a mandatory new requirement that affects every domestic lighting circuit.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 new requirement) — 30mA RCD mandatory for ALL AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. This is one of the most significant A4:2026 additions affecting RCD specification and is completely absent from the page. Any electrician reading this page to understand 'when is 30mA required' will get an incomplete answer.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' Also: '[acceptance_criterion] an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — AFDD recommendation for AC final circuits. The page covers RCD and RCBO selection at length but never mentions that A4:2026 introduced Reg 421.1.7 recommending AFDDs alongside RCDs as a further fire-protection layer. A best-in-class page would note this, especially in the 'fire protection' RCD ratings section or as a call-out.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.'

content_gaps.missing: TT system RCD role — the page only discusses RCD as shock/fire protection but never mentions that in TT earthing systems RCD is also the primary means of automatic disconnection of supply (ADS), not just additional protection. This is a critical practical omission given many UK domestic premises are TT.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 3.76: 'For TT systems, to achieve the required disconnection times specified in BS 7671, residual current devices (RCDs) or other protective measures shall be provided since high earth fault loop impedance to earth electrodes often prevents achieving disconnection by overcurrent devices alone.' Also [OSG] Reg 7.2.5: 'RCD may be required where the installation forms part of a TT system.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 531.3.3 scope detail — the RAG reveals a nuance the page glosses over: the restriction on Type AC is specifically for FIXED equipment. The regulation's precise wording is 'fixed equipment where it is known that the load current contains no DC components.' The page could be strengthened by noting Type AC may still be acceptable for certain fixed resistive circuits (immersion heaters, storage heaters with no electronic controls) where the absence of DC components can be positively verified.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 531.3.3 [condition]: 'The wording 'where it is known that the load current contains no DC components' is a condition in Regulation 531.3.3: the use of RCD Type AC is conditional on positive knowledge (i.e. verified information or documentation) that the connected fixed load current has no DC component. Uncertainty about DC components precludes use of Type AC.'

seo.notes: The heroSubtitle and keyTakeaways array act as a functional answer-first block under the H1 — strong for featured snippet eligibility. FAQ section with 6 Q&As is present in the template (GuideTemplate likely renders FAQ schema). Internal links are sparse: SEOInternalLink is used for /guides/consumer-unit-regulations, /guides/ev-charger-installation, /tools/eicr-certificate, and relatedPages adds 6 more destinations — approximately 9 total, which is thin for a 17-minute guide on a topic with many cross-cutting regulations. No named author or electrician credentials are attributed anywhere in the component — no E-E-A-T signal. The dateModified is 2026-05-18 which is current. Title tag is well-optimised (Type AC, A, B, F keywords present). Meta description is truncated at '…' which may result in a poor SERP snippet if the CMS doesn't complete it.

top_improvements.action: Add a dedicated callout box or paragraph covering Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026): domestic lighting circuits now require 30mA RCD protection as a mandatory requirement ('shall'). Explain the practical implication — any new or rewired domestic lighting circuit needs an RCBO or shared 30mA RCD, and this is an EICR defect on existing unprotected lighting circuits. This is the single most significant A4:2026 change affecting RCD specification and is entirely missing.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

top_improvements.action: Correct or clarify the EV charger RCD reg citation. The page attributes the Type A + 6mA / Type B rule to Reg 722.531.3.101, but the RAG's indexed facets for that regulation number describe transformer siting, not RCD type. Verify the correct regulation number (likely Reg 722.531.3.101 as revised in A4:2026 but confirm against the current wiring regs text), and rewrite the attribution so it precisely matches the facet content. A wrong reg number is an E-E-A-T credibility risk on a professional audience page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 722.531.3.101 RAG facets return only transformer-siting content, not the 6mA DC / Type B RCD requirement the page attributes to this number.

top_improvements.action: Expand the '30mA — Personal Protection' panel to note that the A4:2026 revision of Reg 411.3.3 introduced a risk-assessment exception for non-dwellings, and that Reg 411.3.4 (new in A4:2026) now separately mandates 30mA protection for domestic luminaire circuits. Differentiating the two regulations (411.3.3 for socket outlets, 411.3.4 for lighting) is a common source of confusion on EICRs and would make this the reference page electricians bookmark.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3 [exception]: 'exception permitting omission... other than for a dwelling, a documented risk assessment determines that RCD protection is not necessary.' Reg 411.3.4 [requirement]: '...additional protection... shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

top_improvements.action: Add a brief section or callout on TT earthing systems and RCD as ADS. The page presents RCD purely as additional protection / fire protection, but in TT systems the 30mA (or higher-rated) RCD is also the primary means of automatic disconnection of supply. This omission means the page gives incomplete guidance to the significant number of UK electricians working on TT installations (rural properties, outbuildings, older domestics).
top_improvements.grounded_in: [OSG] Reg 3.76: 'For TT systems, to achieve the required disconnection times...RCDs or other protective measures shall be provided since high earth fault loop impedance to earth electrodes often prevents achieving disconnection by overcurrent devices alone.'

top_improvements.action: In the Type S / time-delayed section, add the GN3 testing protocol: RCDs should be tested at 0% and 180% of IΔn, and the longest trip time is recorded in column 28 of the Schedule of Test Results. Also note that Type B RCDs require up to 12 trip cycles if all optional tests are carried out — relevant for inspection planning. This lifts the section from concept-only to actionable field guidance.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] Reg 2.32: 'the longest tripping time observed shall be recorded in column 28 of the Schedule of Test Results.' [GN3] Reg 5.6: 'Carrying out all of the listed tests... for a 30 mA Type B RCD would require the RCD to trip 12 times.'

confirmed_issues.claim: 1.2 megohms IR reading on an immersion heater element when cold is described as 'borderline' — 'a healthy element should read well above 2 megohms'
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet (bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence) sets a 2 MΩ healthy-element threshold for immersion heater elements or uses 'well above 2 megohms' as a benchmark. The RAG minimum for 230 V circuits is 1.0 MΩ per Table 64 / Reg 643.3.2. GN3 Reg 2.21 notes 'a new installation should not yield a test result this low [1 MΩ]', indicating higher values are expected in new work, but no facet specifies 2 MΩ as the healthy-element floor for an immersion heater. At 1.2 MΩ the element passes the Table 64 regulatory minimum; describing this as 'borderline' with a '>2 MΩ healthy' claim is not grounded by any RAG source and could mislead users about pass/fail thresholds. Multiple PWI facets for immersion heater testing were returned (fault diagnosis, commissioning, replacement sequences) but none stated a 2 MΩ threshold.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — mandatory 30 mA RCD protection for domestic lighting circuits — is nowhere mentioned on the page despite being a major A4:2026 change directly relevant to the AI Defect Coding and Board Scanner features. The defect-coding worked example covers socket circuits (411.3.3) but omits the equally common lighting-circuit RCD defect introduced in A4:2026.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.4 · [requirement]': 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' GN3 Reg 5.12 and GN3 Reg 1.2.2 both confirm this as a new A4:2026 obligation.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — AFDD recommendation for AC final circuits — is mentioned only as a device the board scanner can identify, but the page never explains the A4:2026 regulatory status. For a page claiming full A4:2026 coverage, noting that AFDDs are now recommended under Reg 421.1.7 (introduced by A4:2026, recommendatory not mandatory) would add credibility and differentiate from pre-A4 content.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 421.1.7 · [note]': 'The text... uses the term recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... the regulation's wording is advisory rather than mandatory.' Also: '[reference] · Introduced by Amendment A4:2026 to BS 7671:2018.'

content_gaps.missing: Section 722 (A4:2026) EV charging — described in the RAG as containing 'significant changes' — is absent from the page. Given the page covers AI circuit design and cost estimation, a brief mention that the AI handles updated EV charging requirements (Section 722, A4:2026) would strengthen the A4:2026 freshness claim and capture EV-related search intent.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 715.524.201': 'Section 722 of Part 7 covers electric vehicle charging installations... contains significant changes to Regulation(s) applicable to EV charging installations.'

content_gaps.missing: The GN3 0.8 site-test correction factor for Zs is absent. The voice-entry section and fault diagnosis section mention Zs values, but never explain that measured site Zs must be multiplied by a correction factor to account for conductor temperature at site (GN3 guidance). This is a genuine knowledge gap exploited by competitors' content.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 facet '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.17': confirms R1+R2 and ring continuity measurement procedures. GN3 Chapter 2 Reg 2.29 confirms Zs measurement and comparison with maximum tabulated values (Table 41.2). content_gaps.missing: MIMS cable as a third permitted segregation method under Reg 528.1 — the page only lists compartment dividers and insulation-for-highest-voltage as the two options, omitting the mineral-insulated metal-sheathed cable alternative
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 4.8.5: 'Circuits shall be partitioned from Band I circuits or be wired in mineral-insulated metal-sheathed cables where specified by Regulation 528.1.'

content_gaps.missing: The 30% low-load exception to grouping factors (Reg 523.4) — a genuinely useful practical tip for electricians sizing circuits where some cables in a trunking run are lightly loaded (e.g. a spare circuit drawn through the same route)
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 523.4 facet: 'If... a cable is expected to carry a current not greater than 30% of its grouped current-carrying capacity, it may be ignored for the purpose of obtaining the rating factor for the rest of the group.'

content_gaps.missing: Grouping factor for cables with different maximum operating temperatures — page simply says 'reduce by a grouping factor' without noting that when 70°C and 90°C cables share a trunking run, all must be derated to the lowest operating temperature (70°C). This is a common site error.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 523.4 facet: 'For groups containing cables having different maximum operating temperatures, the current-carrying capacity of all the cables in the group shall be based on the lowest maximum operating temperature of any cable in the group.'

content_gaps.missing: OSG Reg 7.4.1 condition: Band I and Band II can share unpartitioned trunking only if every cable's insulation is rated to the highest voltage present — this is the key practical alternative to compartmentalised trunking that installers need to know (e.g. using 300/500 V-rated LSZH data cables alongside mains)
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG 7.4.1: 'Condition (a): The prohibition is lifted where every cable within the wiring system part is insulated for the highest voltage present.'

confirmed_issues.claim: SPD installation is required under Regulation 443.4 of BS 7671 — blanket statement that SPD is required for every new build consumer unit as standard
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG contains an explicit A4:2026 facet tagged [exception] under Reg 443.4 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 44 · Reg 443.4 · [exception] · Exception: no transient protection for single dwelling units in certain situations'): 'There is an exception not to provide protection for single dwelling units in certain situations. The Regulation states that single dwelling units may be excluded from the requirement to provide transient overvoltage protection where the specified conditions are met.' The four mandatory scenarios (443.4(a)–(d)) and the risk-assessment fallback for all other cases are also confirmed by separate RAG facets. A blanket 'SPD is required' statement for all new-build domestic consumer units directly contradicts the RAG-confirmed single-dwelling-unit exception in Reg 443.4 and omits the mandatory risk-assessment route for cases outside (a)–(d). This is a real, RAG-grounded issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 NEW): RCD 30 mA protection mandatory on ALL domestic lighting circuits. This is a direct new-build consumer unit design requirement introduced by A4:2026 — every new build house must have all luminaire final circuits RCD-protected at ≤30 mA. The page lists RCBOs for socket outlets (Reg 411.3.3) and SPDs but never mentions that lighting circuits in new build dwellings are equally subject to mandatory 30 mA RCD protection under the new Reg 411.3.4. This is a meaningful omission for electricians designing the consumer unit.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' Confirmed by GN3 Reg 5.12: 'RCD protection not exceeding 30 mA for final circuits supplying luminaires is scoped to domestic premises (411.3.4).'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026 NEW): AFDD recommendation for AC final circuits. Introduced by A4:2026 as a regulatory recommendation (advisory, not mandatory) to install arc fault detection devices in AC final circuits of fixed installations to mitigate fire risk. For a best-in-class new build electrical guide referencing A4:2026, omitting AFDD entirely is a significant gap — especially for premium-specification sections where AFDDs are increasingly included.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' Wording is recommendatory not mandatory ('recommending').

content_gaps.missing: Reg 443.4 SPD exception for single dwelling units. The page states SPD 'is required' under Reg 443.4 as if universal, but the regulation contains an exception for single dwelling units. A technically complete guide should note that SPD is mandated under Reg 443.4 where a risk assessment confirms it is needed, and that the single-dwelling exception may apply — though in practice most new build consumer units include an SPD as good practice and per NHBC expectations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 443.4 exception facet: 'There is an exception not to provide protection for single dwelling units in certain situations. The installer or designer shall identify if this exception applies before omitting protection.'

content_gaps.missing: Section 722 (EV charging) BS 7671 provisions — including load curtailment (Reg 722.311.201) and specific protective device requirements for EV circuits. The page cites Reg 411.3.3 as the RCD basis for EV charger circuits but Section 722 contains its own requirements. No Section 722 facets returned, so this is a gap that should be verified against the standard rather than assumed under Reg 411.3.3 alone.
content_gaps.rag_source: EV/722 queries returned no rows from bs7671_facets — gap in RAG coverage confirmed.

top_improvements.action: Correct the SPD claim. Replace 'SPD installation is required under Regulation 443.4' with accurate language: 'Under Reg 443.4, a risk assessment determines whether SPD protection is required. For most new build homes supplied via an overhead or mixed network, the risk assessment supports SPD installation, and a single-dwelling exception may apply in some cases — in practice, NHBC and good industry practice means SPDs are included as standard, but the regulatory basis is a risk assessment, not an unconditional mandate.' This prevents the page from misstating a conditional requirement as absolute.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page's howToSteps describe only 6 steps; practical_work_intelligence describes a seven-step procedure and OSG uses a distinct sequence — page omits earths/shorts and reinstatement steps
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple practical_work_intelligence facets consistently define a seven-step procedure: 'identify, isolate, check, test, prove and confirm isolation prior to carrying out dead work and reinstating supply.' The Reg 462.1 procedure facet in bs7671_facets explicitly lists 'apply earths/shorts where appropriate' as a required step after proving dead. The page's 6-step sequence (identify, select GS38 VI, first prove, isolate+lock-off, test dead, second prove) omits both the earths/shorts application step and the reinstatement/recheck-before-re-energising step. The RAG clearly and consistently supports a 7-step procedure; the page omits at least one step that is explicitly named in both the bs7671_facets (Reg 462.1 procedure facet) and practical_work_intelligence.

content_gaps.missing: Earthing and shorting step after proving dead: the RAG (Reg 462.1 procedure facet and practical_work_intelligence) explicitly lists 'apply earths/shorts where appropriate' as part of the standard safe isolation sequence. The page's 6-step howToSteps omit this entirely. For MV/HV or long cable runs this is safety-critical.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 462.1 [procedure]: 'identify supply, isolate supply, secure against re-connection, prove dead, and apply earths/shorts where appropriate'; practical_work_intelligence: 'seven-step procedure to identify, isolate, check, test, prove and confirm isolation prior to carrying out dead work and reinstating supply.'

content_gaps.missing: Semiconductor devices prohibited as isolating means (Reg 537.2.2): increasingly relevant with smart-home dimmers, relay-based smart switches, and EV EVSE relay arrangements. Page never mentions the mechanical-isolator requirement or that a semiconductor-only switching path cannot serve as isolation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 537.2.2: 'Work procedures requiring safe isolation shall not rely on semiconductor devices to perform the isolation step; a mechanical isolator complying with BS 7671 shall be used instead.'

content_gaps.missing: Solar PV DC-side permanent warning notice requirement (Reg 712.514.102): the page mentions PV as a back-feed risk but does not tell readers about the mandatory permanent DC-side warning notice ('SOLAR DC – Live parts can remain energized after isolation') required at every DC access point. This is a compliance gap many electricians miss.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.514.102: 'Each point of access to live parts on the DC side shall have a permanent warning notice indicating that live parts may still be energized after isolation … example: SOLAR DC - Live parts can remain energized after isolation.'

content_gaps.missing: Isolation must cover all live conductors including neutral (Reg 462.2): the page never explains that in PME/TN-C-S systems the neutral is a live conductor and must be isolated. The step-by-step only says 'switch off the MCB' without noting that double-pole isolation is required to isolate both phase and neutral.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 462.2: 'all live conductors means the isolation means shall isolate every live conductor … all phase conductors and the neutral conductor where the neutral is considered live in the system arrangement.'

top_improvements.action: Add a section (or callout) on semiconductor devices as invalid isolators (Reg 537.2.2) with practical examples: smart dimmers, relay-switch modules, EVSE controller relays. This is a growing real-world problem as smart-home retrofits create circuits where no mechanical isolation point exists downstream. Frame as a common EICR observation.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 537.2.2: 'Work procedures requiring safe isolation shall not rely on semiconductor devices to perform the isolation step; a mechanical isolator complying with BS 7671 shall be used instead.'

top_improvements.action: Add Reg 462.2 double-pole isolation callout: explicitly state that switching off a single-pole MCB only breaks the phase conductor — in TN-C-S/PME systems the neutral must also be isolated (all-pole switching). Add a warning callout in the lock-off section: 'Switching off a single-pole MCB does not isolate the neutral — use double-pole isolation (DP switch or DP MCB) to satisfy Reg 462.2 which requires isolation of all live conductors.'
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 462.2: 'all live conductors … all phase conductors and the neutral conductor where the neutral is considered live … all-pole switch or isolator is typically required.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Minimum acceptable insulation resistance value is 1 MΩ for all circuit types under BS 7671
confirmed_issues.evidence: Table 64 facet ([BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [test] · Table 64 test voltages and minimum insulation resistance values) explicitly states: 'For SELV and PELV circuits use 250 V DC with minimum 0.5 MΩ; For circuits up to and including 500 V (except SELV/PELV) use 500 V DC with minimum 1.0 MΩ; For circuits above 500 V use 1000 V DC with minimum 1.0 MΩ.' The keyTakeaway assertion of '1 MΩ for ALL circuit types' is directly contradicted — SELV/PELV is 0.5 MΩ at 250 V DC, not 1.0 MΩ.

confirmed_issues.claim: SPDs are now mandatory on most new installations under BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Amendment 4
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 443.4 facets directly contradict 'mandatory on most new installations.' The RAG shows: (a) SPDs are required only where transient overvoltage could result in risk to life, public service interruption, commercial/industrial interruption, or affect large numbers of co-located individuals; (b) all other cases require a risk assessment to determine if protection is needed; (c) there is an explicit exception: '[BS 7671] · Reg 443.4 · [exception] · Exception: no transient protection for single dwelling units in certain situations.' Additionally, [BS 7671] · Reg 422.2.1 confirms the approach is a 'consequence-based assessment to determine whether protection against transient overvoltages is necessary.' SPDs are decidedly not blanket-mandatory on most new installations — their requirement is conditional on a consequence/risk assessment.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 redraft of 643.3 introduces a new procedure: where equipment is connected and likely to be damaged by standard test voltages, a 250V DC test WITH equipment connected shall be used as the verification step. The page presents only the old 'disconnect everything / apply 500V' approach and does not mention this alternative A4:2026 path at all.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.3 [requirement]: 'Where equipment is connected and the equipment is likely to influence the insulation resistance verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used to verify insulation resistance as clarified in the redraft to Regulation 643.3.' Also Reg 643.3 [procedure]: 'connect the equipment to the circuit, isolate where appropriate, then perform a 250 V DC insulation resistance test.'

content_gaps.missing: AFDD as a sensitive device requiring disconnection before IR testing. A4:2026 introduces AFDDs under Reg 421.1.7 (recommended on socket-outlet final circuits); they contain electronics that will be damaged by 500V IR test voltage. The page's disconnect list (LED drivers, SPDs, VSDs, thermostats) omits AFDDs entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 [reference]: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs).' Reg 411.9.3 confirms AFDDs must show operational indication after commissioning — implying they are present and vulnerable.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 residual-charge hazard warning after testing: cables remain charged after IR test instrument is disconnected and present a shock hazard. The page mentions the discharge step only in the EICR precautions section and only in the context of the instrument's auto-discharge feature. GN3 is more emphatic that this is a safety hazard regardless of test direction.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 1.1 [condition]: 'insulation resistance testing can leave cables charged; this residual charge can persist after the tester is disconnected and may present a shock hazard unless discharged or otherwise controlled.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 643.4 (SELV/PELV/electrical separation): IR testing is also the prescribed verification method for confirming SELV and PELV circuit separation under Regs 643.4.1–643.4.3. This is distinct from general circuit IR testing. The page does not mention this application, which is increasingly relevant as ELV and PELV circuits proliferate in modern buildings.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.4.3 [scope]: 'Regulations 643.4.1 to 643.4.3 set out requirements for confirming separation of live parts by SELV, PELV and electrical separation. The separation shall be confirmed by a measurement of the insulation resistance and the resistance values obtained shall be in accordance with Table 64.'

top_improvements.action: Fix the contradicted keyTakeaway bullet: change 'The minimum acceptable insulation resistance value is 1 MΩ for all circuit types' to 'The minimum is 0.5 MΩ for SELV/PELV circuits (tested at 250V DC) and 1 MΩ for circuits up to 500V (tested at 500V DC).' top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 643.3 [requirement]: 'Where equipment is connected and the equipment is likely to influence the insulation resistance verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used to verify insulation resistance as clarified in the redraft to Regulation 643.3.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Consumer unit must be in a non-combustible (metal) enclosure; a risk assessment is required if a non-ferrous alternative is used
confirmed_issues.evidence: All bs7671_facets rows for Reg 421.1.201 (18 facets retrieved, covering [requirement], [exception], [condition], [note], [acceptance_criterion]) describe exactly two mandatory routes: (a) enclosure manufactured from non-combustible material, or (b) enclosed in a cabinet of non-combustible material complying with Reg 132.12. The NOTE facet states 'Ferrous metal, for example, steel, is deemed to be an example of a non-combustible material' — the NOTE is illustrative, not exhaustive, but no facet anywhere in bs7671_facets, gn3 or osg introduces a 'risk assessment' as a third route or as an escape clause for non-ferrous materials. One facet ([condition]) explicitly states these are 'mutually exclusive options' with no further alternative. A separate search on 'risk assessment non-ferrous consumer unit' returned zero rows. The 'risk assessment' qualification on the page is an invented escape route with no grounding in 421.1.201 as stored in the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) requiring 30 mA RCD protection specifically for domestic luminaire circuits as a mandatory requirement. The page says 'all circuits must have RCD protection' generically but never names the specific new A4 lighting-circuit RCD rule, which is the most important A4:2026 change affecting garage conversion wiring.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) recommending Arc Fault Detection Devices (AFDDs) on AC final circuits. A new garage conversion is exactly the type of new installation where AFDD recommendation would be raised by Building Control or a diligent electrician. A best-in-class guide should note this and advise discussing with client.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' Wording is recommendatory, not mandatory.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of Section 722 special requirements for EV charging point circuits. The page mentions EV charger as a load consideration but never advises that any EV charging circuit falls under Section 722 (special installation) rules, which include specific RCD, load management, and labelling requirements.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.1: 'An example falling within the scope of Section 722 is a dedicated final circuit installed to supply a domestic electric vehicle charging point. Such a circuit is subject to the particular requirements of Section 722.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the GN3/OSG guidance that measured Zs values should be assessed against tabulated maximum values, and that where temperature-corrected Zs comparison is needed the multiplying factor from GN3 should be applied. The testing section lists 'Zs on every circuit' without explaining how to pass/fail the result.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.7.1 / [GN3] Reg 7.5: measurement of Zs must verify compliance with Reg 411.4; GN3 provides guidance on temperature correction when comparing site-measured Zs against tabulated maximum values.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.411.4.1 of BS 7671 earthing requirements apply equally in Scotland. Most Glasgow properties are on PME (TN-C-S) supplies, requiring careful consideration of outdoor charging earthing.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The PME prohibition and 70 V RMS limit for outdoor EV charging earthing sit entirely at Reg 722.411.4 (TN system), not 722.411.4.1. RAG facets at context_prefix '[BS 7671]…Reg 722.411.4 · TNsystem · [scope]' state: 'Regulation 722.411.4 applies to TN systems and addresses requirements where a PME earthing facility would otherwise be used as the means of earthing for the protective conductor contact(s) of a charging point located outdoors… It sets alternative methods (b)–(e).' A separate facet at '[BS 7671]…Reg 722.411.4 · TNsystem · [requirement]' confirms: 'A PME earthing facility shall not be used as the means of earthing for the protective conductor contact(s) of a charging point located outdoors…' Every facet indexed under 722.411.4.1 is a scope/note entry for other sections (Section 730 inland vessels, Section 753 heating, Appendix 14, etc.) — none contain the PME earthing rule. The page's citation to 722.411.4.1 is wrong; the correct parent regulation is 722.411.4.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.533.101 — dedicated circuit per charger
confirmed_issues.evidence: All RAG facets for Reg 722.533.101 return only fuse standard cross-references: BS EN 60269-1, BS HD 60269-2:2013+A1:2022, BS HD 60269-3:2010+A2:2022, BS EN 60269-6:2011+A1:2023, and BS HD 60364-8-1:2019. No 'dedicated circuit' requirement appears at this regulation number. The dedicated circuit requirement is found at OSG Reg 4.6.4 (context_prefix '[OSG]…Reg 4.6.4 · [requirement] · Dedicated circuit for electric vehicle connection': 'A dedicated circuit shall be provided for the connection to electric vehicles') and is referenced generally to BS 7671 Section 722, not to Reg 722.533.101. PWI also cites 'Not installing a dedicated circuit for EV charging' as a common mistake referencing 'BS 7671 section 722' generically. The page's specific attribution to Reg 722.533.101 is unsupported.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.531.101 — appropriate RCD protection
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero facets found at Reg 722.531.101. The only 722.531.x facets are at 722.531.3.101, which concerns transformer siting and the rule that only one charging point may be supplied per transformer (context_prefix '[BS 7671]…Reg 722.531.3.101 · [note] · NOTE 2 — one charging point per transformer permitted'). RCD protection for EV charging is mentioned only in a general Section 722 scope facet (under context_prefix '…Reg 722.411.4.1 · [scope] · Section 722: subjects covered…': 'specific requirements with regard to…RCD protection') with no attribution to 722.531.101. The page's specific citation of Reg 722.531.101 for RCD protection has no RAG grounding.

confirmed_issues.claim: Reg 722.311.201 load curtailment (page omission — page does not mention this A4:2026 requirement)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG fully confirms Reg 722.311.201 at context_prefix '[BS 7671]…Reg 722.311.201…[scope] · Load curtailment considered in determining maximum demand': 'Regulation 722.311.201 states that load curtailment, including load reduction or disconnection, either automatically or manually, may be taken into account when determining the maximum demand of the installation or part thereof.' Multiple facets confirm this is a substantive A4:2026 requirement. The page makes no reference to this regulation at all. For a Glasgow-focused EV guide where tenement/multi-unit properties are common and simultaneous EV demand is a real design constraint, omission of this A4:2026 load curtailment regulation is a material gap and a confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.311.201 load curtailment: the page has no mention that automatic or manual load curtailment may be taken into account for maximum demand assessment under A4:2026. This is directly relevant to Glasgow tenement multi-unit installs where DNO connection capacity is constrained.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671]…Reg 722.311.201: 'load curtailment, including load reduction or disconnection, either automatically or manually, may be taken into account when determining the maximum demand of the installation or part thereof.'

content_gaps.missing: The 70V RMS MET-to-Earth threshold under PEN conductor open-circuit fault (Reg 722.411.4(b)) is not stated anywhere on the page. The page correctly identifies the need for earthing but omits the specific voltage limit that drives electrode sizing, which is a key design parameter electricians need.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671]…Reg 722.411.4 · TNsystem: 'the resistance of the earth electrode to Earth shall be such that the maximum voltage between the main earthing terminal (MET)…and Earth…does not exceed 70 V RMS.'

content_gaps.missing: The 5-second disconnection requirement for PEN fault detection devices (Reg 722.411.4(c)) — an alternative to an earth rod — is not described. The page mentions 'integrated PEN fault detection' but not the 5s/70V disconnect criterion or the requirement that the device cannot be reset while unsafe voltage persists.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671]…Reg 722.411.4 · TNsystem: 'a device which electrically disconnects the vehicle…within 5 s in the event of the voltage between the circuit protective conductor and Earth exceeding 70 V RMS due to an open-circuit fault in the PEN conductor…'

content_gaps.missing: Correct regulation numbers for dedicated circuit (not 722.533.101 — that reg covers fuse standards) and RCD protection (not 722.531.101 — that is unverified). The page should audit and correct these citations against the actual Section 722 regulation numbers.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671]…Reg 722.533.101: returns only BS EN 60269 fuse standard references. [BS 7671]…Reg 722.1: 'dedicated final circuit installed to supply a domestic electric vehicle charging point…is subject to the particular requirements of Section 722.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Zs calculator compares result against BS 7671 Tables 41.2, 41.3, and 41.4 (for 0.4s TN, 5s distribution, and TT/RCD-protected circuits respectively)
confirmed_issues.evidence: All three tables exist in A4:2026, so the auditor's assertion that Table 41.2 is 'only an abbreviation list' is wrong — Reg 411.4.201 facets confirm Table 41.2 holds fuse Zs limits for 0.4s disconnection (BS 88-2, BS 88-3, BS 3036, BS 1362). Table 41.3 (Reg 411.4.202) covers MCBs/circuit-breakers for BOTH 0.4s and 5s in one unified table with Cmin=0.95. Table 41.4 (Reg 411.4.203) covers fuse-protected distribution circuits at 5s — 'Where a fuse is used for a distribution circuit or a final circuit in accordance with Regulation 411.3.2.3, maximum values of Zs corresponding to a disconnection time of 5s are stated in Table 41.4.' Table 41.4 is NOT for TT/RCD-protected circuits; no RAG facet supports that characterisation. The page's mapping of 41.4 to 'TT/RCD-protected circuits' is therefore incorrect: Table 41.4 is a 5s fuse distribution-circuit table, and the three-table labelling scheme misrepresents its scope. The broader auditor concern about pre-A4 numbering is a false alarm — all three table numbers survive into A4:2026 — but the functional description of Table 41.4 on the page is wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: 30mA RCD: must trip within 40ms at five times rated current (150mA) — stated as unconditional absolute
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 5.6 facets are unambiguous. Multiple facets from 'GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 5 · Reg 5.6' confirm: 'The manufacturer may select a current of 250 mA for the Type AC 40 ms tripping test instead of 5xIΔn. This is a manufacturer-declared alternative test current for Type AC RCDs when specified by the manufacturer.' Further facets state: 'Where the manufacturer declares a 250 mA current for the Type AC 40 ms test, failing to trip within 40 ms at 5xIΔn does not necessarily mean the RCD is faulty.' The page's unconditional statement that 40ms must be met at 150mA (5×30mA) omits this manufacturer-declared 250mA alternative for Type AC devices, which the RAG confirms is a real and documented nuance in GN3 Reg 5.6.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4 (A4:2026), issued 31 July 2024, adds new Section 530 requirements covering bidirectional and unidirectional protective devices
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two separate errors confirmed by RAG. (1) Amendment identity: Reg 722.826.3.201 facet states explicitly 'BS 7671:2018+A3:2024 was issued on 31st July 2024 and is intended to be implemented immediately. This Amendment provides for the inclusion of two new definitions and one new Regulation relating to the use of bidirectional and unidirectional devices.' The 31 July 2024 amendment is A3:2024, not A4:2026. (2) Section 530: Reg 530.3.1 exists in A4:2026 Chapter 53 but covers general equipment selection and erection requirements ('Every item of equipment shall be selected and erected so as to allow compliance with the requirements'), not bidirectional/unidirectional devices. The RAG places bidirectional/unidirectional device content in Reg 551.7.1 and Reg 722.826.3.201, not Section 530. Both the amendment designation and the Section 530 attribution are wrong.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — mandatory 30mA RCD additional protection for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. This is a new A4 requirement directly relevant to any RCD testing calculator, yet the page never mentions it. An EICR calculator user needs to know domestic lighting circuits now require RCD protection.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 411.3.4 [requirement]: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — recommendation to install AFDDs in AC final circuits to mitigate arc-fault fire risk. The page covers every major A4 addition except AFDD, and a page claiming A4:2026 compliance should address this. A dedicated AFDD calculator section would be best-in-class content.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 421.1.7 [purpose]: 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends the installation of AFDDs... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' Note: recommendatory wording ('recommending'), not mandatory.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 nuance on the Type AC 40ms test: manufacturer may declare 250mA as the test current instead of 5×IΔn (150mA for a 30mA device). A calculator that hard-codes 150mA as the 5× threshold may give incorrect pass/fail for devices where the manufacturer has declared 250mA. This is the single most likely source of on-site confusion.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 5.6 [requirement]: 'The manufacturer may select a current of 250 mA for the Type AC 40 ms tripping test instead of 5×IΔn. This is a manufacturer-declared alternative test current for Type AC RCDs.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 551.7.1 changes (new indents c and d) for bidirectional energy flow — relevant for solar PV, BESS, and EV export-capable installations. The page attributes this content to a non-existent 'new Section 530', missing the actual location (Reg 551.7.1) and practical impact (source must not be connected to load side of RCD in certain conditions).
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 551.7.1 [requirement]: 'indent (c) requires that a suitable protective device shall be provided where energy flow is bidirectional'; 'indent (d) has been added which prohibits the connection of a source to the load side of an RCD under certain conditions.'

seo.notes: The page does NOT lead with a direct answer block — the H1 is followed immediately by a CTA button with no answer paragraph. A searcher querying 'electrical testing calculators' sees a hero badge and H1, then a button, before any substantive content. FAQ schema is present (faqPageSchema injected via Helmet). SoftwareApplication and ItemList schemas are also present. Internal links: 3 SEOInternalLink anchors in the cable-sizing section + 8 in the related-pages footer grid = 11. No author/E-E-A-T attribution (no named author, no credentials, no 'reviewed by' signal). useSEO hook only injects softwareApplicationSchema — faqPageSchema and itemListSchema are injected separately via Helmet, so there are effectively 3 JSON-LD blocks. Title tag ('Electrical Testing Calculators | Zs, Fault Current, RCD') is good — keyword-rich and under 60 chars. Meta description (153 chars) is within limit. The page's URL in softwareApplicationSchema is wrong: hardcoded as '/electrical-testing-calculators' but the route is '/tools/electrical-testing-calculators'.

top_improvements.action: Correct the Table 41.2/41.3/41.4 citation. In A4:2026 Table 41.3(a) is the unified Zs limit table covering both 0.4s and 5s criteria, with Cmin=0.95 baked in. Remove the three-table split and reference Table 41.3(a) for TN systems (both final and distribution circuits). Also replace the erroneous 'new Section 530' claim with the correct reference to Reg 551.7.1 (new indents c and d for bidirectional energy flow). These two citation errors undermine the page's credibility claim of A4:2026 compliance.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a) facets (all MCB types); Reg 551.7.1 [note] 'Regulation 551.7.1 has been redrafted... indent (c) added... indent (d) added'; Reg 722.826.3.201 confirming A3:2024 (bidirectional) issued 31 July 2024.

top_improvements.action: Add a content block for Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 new requirement): domestic lighting circuits now need 30mA RCD additional protection. This is a high-traffic EICR failure point since many pre-A4 installations lack RCD-protected lighting. A short section or FAQ entry explaining which circuits this affects and how the calculator flags non-compliant boards would make the page definitively best-in-class for EICR professionals.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 411.3.4 [requirement]: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

top_improvements.action: Add a direct answer block (2-3 sentences) immediately under the H1, before the CTA button. Searchers and featured-snippet algorithms need to see the answer first. Suggested lead: 'Elec-Mate provides 70 electrical calculators covering Zs, prospective fault current, RCD trip thresholds, volt drop, cable sizing, and more — all referenced against BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. Enter your measured values and get instant pass/fail results with the correct BS 7671 table values applied.' Then place the CTA. Also fix the softwareApplicationSchema URL: change to https://www.elec-mate.com/tools/electrical-testing-calculators.
top_improvements.grounded_in: SEO best practice: answer-first structure for featured-snippet eligibility; schema URL error noted in code review (line 57 of the file).

top_improvements.action: Add the GN3 Type AC 40ms nuance to the RCD Testing section: state that for Type AC devices the 40ms test current is 5×IΔn (150mA for a 30mA device) unless the manufacturer has declared 250mA as the test current, in which case the 250mA figure takes precedence. This is a real on-site confusion point and citing GN3 Reg 5.6 elevates E-E-A-T. The calculator should accept a manufacturer-declared test current field.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 5.6 [requirement]: 'The manufacturer may select a current of 250 mA for the Type AC 40 ms tripping test instead of 5×IΔn.'

top_improvements.action: Add Reg 421.1.7 AFDD content: a short paragraph explaining that A4:2026 recommends (not mandates) AFDDs in AC final circuits to mitigate arc-fault fire risk, and that a correctly designed installation should consider AFDD fit-out — particularly relevant to EICR C3 observation practice. Link to any dedicated AFDD calculator or guide page. This captures growing search volume for 'AFDD requirement 2026' while accurately reflecting the recommendatory wording.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 421.1.7 [note]: 'The text of Regulation 421.1.7 uses the term recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... advisoryrather than mandatory.'

confirmed_issues.claim: GN3 provides guidance on appropriate sampling rates. For a large commercial installation with hundreds of circuits, a sampling rate of 10% to 25% may be appropriate.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The only RAG facet for GN3 Ch 8 Reg 8.2 sampling is: 'Sampling shall be documented, justified by risk assessment, and the details included in the Schedule of Inspections and Schedule of Test Results.' No numeric percentage figures (10%, 25%, or any range) appear in any indexed GN3 facet. Three separate targeted queries combining 'sampling' with 'percentage', 'ten', 'twenty five', and 'percent' all returned zero rows. The specific 10%–25% range is not grounded in the available RAG and must be treated as unsupported numeric specificity — it should either be removed or qualified as 'varies by risk assessment' without citing GN3 for the numeric range.

confirmed_issues.claim: For domestic properties: test every circuit. Sampling is not appropriate.
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Ch 8 Reg 8.2 [scope] states sampling applies 'for groups of similar circuits not all are tested if an appropriate sampling plan is justified' — it makes no domestic/dwelling carve-out. No RAG facet (across all three document types: bs7671, gn3, osg) explicitly prohibits or discourages sampling for domestic installations. Targeted queries for 'domestic dwelling all circuits test every' and 'domestic sampling' returned zero results. The claim asserts a prohibition not present anywhere in the indexed corpus. The RAG rather implies sampling is universally available when risk-assessed and documented, with no property-type restriction indexed.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 mandates RCD protection (≤30 mA) for domestic lighting circuits under Reg 411.3.4. The page lists RCDs/RCBOs in the consumer unit as part of what the EICR covers, but never explains that absence of RCD on a domestic lighting circuit is now a codeable defect (C2/C3 depending on age). This is a high-value insight for electricians writing the report.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.4 · [acceptance_criterion]: 'An installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.' Also [OSG] Ch 7 · Reg 7.2.5: 'An RCD shall be provided for lighting circuits in domestic (household) premises.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 mandates AFDDs on final circuits to socket-outlets ≤32 A in HMOs, Higher Risk Residential Buildings, purpose-built student accommodation, and care homes. The page covers HMOs as a property type for limitations examples but never mentions that an inspector must check/note AFDD presence in these premises. This is a direct gap in the HMO section and the 'what EICR covers' list.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.4.2: 'Regulation 421.1.7 requires protection of final circuits supplying socket-outlets with rated current not exceeding 32 A using Arc Fault Detection Devices (AFDD) in Higher Risk Residential Buildings, Houses in Multiple Occupation, Purpose-built student accommodation and Care homes.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 8th/9th Ed defines FI as 'Further investigation required without delay' (OSG Reg 7.28) — stronger than the page's softer framing of 'arrange the further investigation promptly.' The page underplays the urgency signalled by FI.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Ch 7 · Reg 7.28: 'FI is the EICR code meaning Further investigation required without delay. It is applied where conditions observed may present danger and require additional investigation to determine remedial action.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 Chapter 8 on sampling states sampling must be 'justified by risk assessment' and documented. The page gives percentage ranges (10–25%) without explaining the risk-assessment justification requirement, which is the actual GN3 obligation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Ch 8 · Reg 8.2: 'Sampling shall be documented, justified by risk assessment, and the details included in the Schedule of Inspections and Schedule of Test Results.'
