confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 631.2 covers the requirement for the Minor Works certificate — the page cites this regulation twice
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh SQL `select ... where context_prefix ilike '%Reg 631%'` returned zero rows across all document types (bs7671, gn3, osg). The correct A4:2026 regulation governing Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificates is Reg 644.4.201 (Part 6, Chapter 64), which is densely represented in the RAG: 'Regulation 644.4.201 defines a Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate as the certificate based on the model given in Appendix 6 which may be provided for each circuit added to or altered where the work does not include provision of a new circuit or replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit.' The reg number 631.2 does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 — it appears to be a stale or invented citation. The page should cite Reg 644.4.201 instead.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 411.3.3 risk assessment: when adding a socket to a non-domestic circuit, a documented risk assessment may be required and must be attached to the certificate. The practical_work_intelligence table flags 'failing to attach required risk assessment under Regulation 411.3.3' as the top common mistake on the cert — the page's 'Common Mistakes' section completely omits this.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3 / 411.4.4 facets; practical_work_intelligence 'Use and completion of the Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 — domestic lighting circuits now require 30 mA RCD additional protection. Any minor works on a domestic lighting circuit (extending a circuit, adding a light point) must confirm compliance with 411.3.4, and any deficiency should be coded C2/C3 on the cert. Not mentioned anywhere on the page.

The EIC may be issued as an alternative to multiple Minor Works Certificates (Reg 120.3). The page covers the EIC vs EWC choice but never mentions this legitimate alternative, which is directly relevant to contractors doing multiple additions on the same visit.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 120.3: 'The Electrical Installation Certificate may be used to certify multiple additions, or alterations … as an alternative to issuing multiple Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificates.'

content_gaps.missing: The obligation to record defects found in the existing installation on the Minor Works certificate. practical_work_intelligence notes 'not recording defects found in existing installation' as a common mistake — the page's common-mistakes section does not cover this.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: 'Use and completion of the Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate … to record any defects found in the existing installation'

confirmed_issues.claim: Options for Band I/II segregation include 'minimum separation distance (typically 300 mm)'
confirmed_issues.evidence: OSG facet (Reg 7.4.1) lists exactly five permitted methods for Band I / Band II co-location: (a) every cable insulated for highest voltage, (b) each conductor insulated for highest voltage, (c) separate compartments, (d) tray partition, (e) earthed metal screen on a multicore cable. A full-text search for '300 mm separation power data' and a keyword search for 'segregation Band I Band II separation distance' both returned zero rows. No metric distance appears anywhere in the Reg 528.1 / OSG 7.4.1 corpus. The '300 mm' figure is not one of the five permitted methods and is unsupported by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Table 4C2 provides correction factors for different installation methods; cables on open tray generally have higher ratings than cables in enclosed tray or trunking
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two targeted searches ('Table 4C2' full-text and content ILIKE '%4C2%') both returned zero rows — Table 4C2 does not appear anywhere in the RAG corpus. The RAG instead uses Table 4D1A with installation Method C ('clipped direct to non-metallic surface or cable tray') for open-tray ratings, returning Iz values for 1.0 mm² through 25 mm² T+E cables. Table 4C2 in BS 7671 Appendix 4 covers grouping correction factors for cables in conduit/duct/trunking, not open-tray correction factors. The citation is a confirmed mis-reference.

confirmed_issues.claim: Metal cable tray must be earthed as an exposed-conductive-part in accordance with BS 7671
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG contains explicit definitional facets for both terms. Exposed-conductive-part: 'a conductive part of equipment that can be touched and is not normally live but may become live under fault conditions' (Reg 411.5.1, 411.3.2.5). Extraneous-conductive-part: 'conductive parts not forming part of the electrical installation that can introduce a dangerous potential difference; examples include metallic water pipes, metallic gas pipes, other metallic pipework and ducting, central heating and air conditioning systems, and exposed metallic structural parts of the building.' Metal cable tray is structural metalwork not forming part of the electrical installation, which places it squarely within the extraneous-conductive-part definition. Describing it as an exposed-conductive-part conflates the two defined terms and is factually incorrect under BS 7671:2018+A4:2026.

content_gaps.missing: BS 7671 Reg 528.1 segregation methods are more nuanced than the page states: permitted methods include (a) separate containment, (b) insulation to highest voltage, (c) earthed partition, (d) MIMS cable, (e) earthed screen in multicore — the page only cites separate trays, divider, and '300 mm separation'. The earthed screen / MIMS options are omitted entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 528.1 facets and [GN3] Reg 4.8.5: 'partitioned or be wired in mineral-insulated metal-sheathed cables'; [OSG] Reg 7.4.1 condition (e): earthed metal screen between Band I and Band II in multicore cable

content_gaps.missing: Emergency lighting segregation on cable tray requires a continuous metal partition throughout the run (not just separation) — a common tray design scenario the page omits entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: 'Installation of emergency escape lighting cables within a common containment system using a permanent metal partition to segregate the emergency lighting circuits... failing to make partition continuous for whole run' is listed as a common mistake

content_gaps.missing: Table 4A1/4A2 (Reg 521.2) is the correct BS 7671 reference for selecting installation method; the page cites 'Table 4C2' for tray correction factors which is a mis-reference. Best-in-class content would correctly cite Appendix 4 installation method tables.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 521.2: 'installation method of a wiring system... shall be in accordance with Table 4A2 of Appendix 4'; Reg 521.201: 'shall use Table 4A1 of Appendix 4'

content_gaps.missing: Distinction between exposed-conductive-part (ECP) and extraneous-conductive-part is important for tray earthing — the page incorrectly calls tray an 'exposed-conductive-part'. Tray is an extraneous-conductive-part and the bonding requirements differ.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1 and Reg 415.2.1: 'extraneous-conductive-parts (such as exposed metalwork of plumbing, gas pipes, structural steelwork)' — cable tray fits this definition, not exposed-conductive-part

seo.notes: The page leads with keyTakeaways above the fold — this serves as an answer-first block. FAQ section (6 questions) is present and will render with FAQ schema via ToolTemplate. Internal links: 6 SEOInternalLink components (cable-sizing-calculator, trunking-fill-calculator, conduit-fill-calculator, voltage-drop-calculator, electrical-load-schedule, eic-certificate). No author or E-E-A-T attribution (no byline, credential, or reviewer name). Badge reads 'BS EN 61537 Compliant' which is the product standard, not BS 7671 — slightly misleading for an audience searching BS 7671 content. Hero subtitle correctly names 50+ calculators but the features section footer says 70+ — inconsistent claim. Title tag is well-formed with primary keyword and action phrase. Page lacks a structured 'Who is this for' or trust signal beyond the badge.

top_improvements.action: Fix the 'Table 4C2' citation in the tray types section — replace with the correct reference to BS 7671 Appendix 4 Table 4A1/4A2 (installation method selection) and note that current-carrying capacity for cables on tray is determined by the installation method reference number from these tables. This is a direct inaccuracy that an inspector or designer would catch.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 521.2 / Reg 521.201: Table 4A2 and Table 4A1 govern installation method selection; Table 4C2 is for grouped cables in conduit/trunking, not open tray

top_improvements.action: Correct the earthing terminology in the FAQ: cable tray is an extraneous-conductive-part, not an exposed-conductive-part. The earthing obligation flows from Reg 411.3.1 / 415.2.1 (bonding of extraneous-conductive-parts). Update the FAQ answer to use the correct BS 7671 defined terms and remove 'bonded to the main earthing terminal' — supplementary bonding requirements depend on whether the tray introduces a potential, per Reg 415.2.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1 definition: extraneous-conductive-parts include structural metalwork; Reg 415.2.1 governs supplementary bonding of simultaneously accessible conductive parts

top_improvements.action: Expand the Band I/II segregation FAQ to include all BS 7671 Reg 528.1 permitted methods: (a) separate containment, (b) insulation to highest voltage present, (c) earthed partition/divider, (d) MIMS cable, (e) earthed metal screen in multicore — not just 'separate trays, divider, or 300 mm gap'. Remove the unverified '300 mm' figure or qualify it as a common industry practice rather than a BS 7671 requirement. Add the GN3 note that partitioning or MIMS is the regulated approach.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] Reg 4.8.5: 'partitioned or mineral-insulated'; [OSG] Reg 7.4.1 conditions (b)(e); [BS 7671] Reg 528.1 methods (a)-(f)

top_improvements.action: Add a content section or FAQ entry on fire stopping / compartmentation: cable tray penetrating fire compartment walls must be sealed per Reg 527. This is a significant practical consideration in commercial/industrial tray design and a common inspection finding that would make the page best-in-class for the topic.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 527.2.4 sealing arrangements for wiring systems penetrating building elements

top_improvements.action: Add a practical tip on emergency lighting segregation: where emergency lighting circuits share tray with other circuits, a continuous metal partition is required throughout the entire run — not just at the tray ends. Common mistake is using temporary or non-continuous fixings for the partition.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: 'Installation of emergency escape lighting cables within a common containment system... permanent metal partition... common mistake: failing to make partition continuous for whole run'

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 421.1.201 applicable since 1 January 2016
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix '%Reg 421.1.201%' returned 6 facets covering scope, requirements (BS EN 61439-3, non-combustible enclosure), exception and definition note. None contain any effective date or reference to '1 January 2016'. A full FTS query on 'consumer unit non-combustible 2016 January effective date' returned zero rows. The date is not grounded in any bs7671_facets facet and must be treated as unverified/unsupported by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 514.9.1 requires a durable circuit chart or schedule at or near each distribution board
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix '%Reg 514.9.1%' returned 6 facets. Every facet describes only (a) that the regulation exists in Part 5 Chapter 51, (b) that A4:2026 added a domestic exception, and (c) guidance on applying that exception. The verbatim 'durable circuit chart' or 'schedule' requirement text is absent from all returned content. FTS query on 'durable circuit chart schedule distribution board' returned zero rows. The core requirement text is ungrounded, and the page's blanket statement ignores the A4:2026 domestic exception confirmed in the facets — making the page's statement both unverified in wording and partially misleading.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 514.12 — RCD quarterly test notice required (page states this as blanket requirement without domestic exception)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix '%Reg 514.12%' returned 6 facets. These confirm Reg 514.12 covers 'Notices: periodic inspection and testing' and that A4:2026 explicitly added an exception for domestic (household) premises 'in certain situations'. FTS query on 'RCD quarterly test notice periodic inspection' returned zero rows — the RAG does not ground 514.12 as specifically an RCD quarterly test notice (it covers periodic inspection notices more broadly). The page's blanket attribution of an RCD quarterly-test-notice requirement to 514.12, without mentioning the A4:2026 domestic exception, is confirmed misleading for domestic premises contexts.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.1.2 cited for absence of main protective bonding to gas, water, oil services as a common C2 observation
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix '%Reg 411.3.1.2%' returned 6 facets — all unambiguously confirm this regulation covers maximum disconnection times (Table 41.1) for socket-outlet final circuits up to 63 A and fixed-load circuits up to 32 A. It has no connection to main protective bonding. Main protective bonding to extraneous-conductive-parts (gas, water, oil) is covered by Reg 411.3.1.1 and sizing by Reg 544.1 — not 411.3.1.2. The page mis-cites the regulation.

confirmed_issues.claim: Main protective bonding conductors: minimum 10mm² for PME (TN-C-S), 6mm² for TN-S
confirmed_issues.evidence: FTS and direct-reg queries for 'protective bonding conductor cross-sectional area PME TN-S 10mm 6mm' and related Reg 544.1 returned ThrottlerException errors after the 411.3.1.2 result, meaning no supporting facet was returned at any point in this session. The auditor's original finding stands: no facet grounds these specific CSA values. Cannot confirm from RAG. Additionally, the page's reg citation (411.3.1.2) for bonding is itself wrong per claim 4, compounding the issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: 30mA RCDs required for socket outlet circuits up to 32A
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix '%Reg 411.3.1.2%' (the reg the page cites in this context) confirms that regulation covers disconnection times only — not RCD additional-protection requirements. FTS query on 'RCD additional protection socket outlet 30mA domestic' (auditor's original query) returned no rows. No facet in this session grounds the 30mA/32A socket-outlet RCD requirement under the cited regulation. The requirement itself may be correct substantively (Reg 415.1 / 411.3.3 area) but the page's regulatory citation is ungrounded and likely wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD protection required for domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4) — page omits this A4:2026 requirement entirely
confirmed_issues.evidence: The auditor's own RAG run confirmed Reg 411.3.4 facet returns: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' This is a confirmed A4:2026 addition. The page on EICR schedule of inspections omits it entirely — inspectors relying on the page would not know to check for or flag absence of RCD protection on domestic lighting circuits. This is a genuine content gap confirmed by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Typical EICR cost £135–£500, £248 average North West/Yorkshire, domestic 2.5 hours, commercial 4+ hours
confirmed_issues.evidence: The auditor's original query on practical_work_intelligence for 'EICR periodic inspection domestic duration time' returned no results. No session query returned any facet from practical_work_intelligence supporting these specific cost or duration figures. The figures are ungrounded in the RAG and should not appear without a sourced basis. Per grounding rules, unverified practical claims from practical_work_intelligence with zero supporting rows = confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 A4:2026 requirement: RCD ≤30mA additional protection mandatory for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. This is a new A4:2026 requirement that changes what must be checked during inspection of domestic lighting circuits and should be listed as a C2 observation when absent.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 + [GN3] Reg 5.12: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 514.12 A4:2026 domestic exception: the requirement for a periodic inspection notice (often described as the RCD quarterly test notice) now has an exception for domestic premises in certain situations. The page states this as a blanket requirement. Inspectors need to know the exception exists to code observations correctly.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 514.12: 'now contains an exception specifically for domestic (household) premises in certain situations. The amendment introduced by A4:2026 adds or clarifies that some notice provisions do not apply to domestic premises where the stated conditions are met.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 514.9.1 A4:2026 domestic exception: the circuit chart requirement also has a domestic exception introduced by A4:2026. The page cites 514.9.1 as a blanket requirement without acknowledging the amendment.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 514.9.1: 'Regulation 514.9.1 has been amended in the A4:2026 amendment to include an exception for domestic premises.'

content_gaps.missing: Correct regulation for main protective bonding: the page cites Reg 411.3.1.2 for bonding requirements but the RAG shows that reg covers disconnection times. The bonding regulation (likely 411.3.1.1 / 544.1) should be cited instead — this is a factual error that could mislead inspectors writing observation citations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1.2: covers Table 41.1 maximum disconnection times for socket-outlet circuits up to 63A and fixed-load circuits up to 32A — not bonding.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 559.4.1 requires that the line conductor connects to the centre contact of a bayonet or Edison screw lamp holder.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix ilike '%559.4%' returns multiple facets for Reg 559.4.1, all describing thermal effects of radiant and convected energy on luminaires — e.g. '[BS 7671] Reg 559.4.1 General [scope]: This regulation applies to the selection and erection of a luminaire and requires that the thermal effects of radiant and convected energy on the surroundings shall be taken into account.' There is no mention of lampholder polarity or centre-contact wiring. The lampholder outer-contact-to-neutral requirement is found at Reg 559.5.1.206: '[BS 7671] Reg 559.5.1.206 [requirement]: the outer contact of every Edison screw or single centre bayonet cap type lampholder shall be connected to the neutral conductor' for TN/TT circuits. Additionally, Reg 643.5.201 / 643.6(b) governs polarity verification of lampholders during testing. The page cites entirely the wrong regulation number.

confirmed_issues.claim: E14 and E27 lampholders are excepted from the outer-contact-to-neutral rule.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG explicitly confirms the E14/E27 exception in multiple facets. Reg 559.5.1.206 [exception]: 'The requirement to connect the outer contact of every Edison screw or single centre bayonet cap type lampholder to the neutral conductor does not apply to E14 and E27 lampholders that comply with BS EN 60238.' Reg 643.5.201 [exception]: 'the requirement that centre contact bayonet and Edison screw lampholders have the outer or screwed contacts connected to the neutral conductor does not apply to E14 and E27 lampholders to BS EN 60238.' A general facet also states: 'E14 and E27 lampholders to BS EN 60238 are excepted from the requirement.' The page omits this exception entirely, making its presentation of the rule incomplete and potentially misleading.

content_gaps.missing: The E14/E27 lampholder exception to the outer-contact-to-neutral rule (Reg 559.5.1.206 / 643.6(b)) is entirely absent. A best-in-class guide must note this to avoid misleading readers about all bayonet/ES fittings.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.5.201 / 559.5.1.206: 'except for E14 and E27 lampholders to BS EN 60238 … centre contact bayonet and Edison screw lampholders shall have the outer or screwed contacts connected to the neutral conductor.'

content_gaps.missing: Correct regulation citation for the lampholder polarity rule. The page cites Reg 559.4.1 (thermal effects of luminaires) — the actual rule is Reg 559.5.1.206 for the wiring requirement and Reg 643.6(b) / 643.5.201 for the test verification.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 559.5.1.206 and Reg 643.5.201 context_prefix: 'Lampholder centre contact connection for earthed neutral circuits (E14/E27 exception)'

content_gaps.missing: The OSG Test Method 1 temporary shorting-link technique for lighting circuit polarity verification (Figure 10.3.4) is not mentioned. This is a concrete GN3/OSG practical procedure that differentiates a thorough guide.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 10.3.4: 'a temporary shorting link as illustrated (Figure 10.3.4) may be used to aid the test procedure for single-pole switching arrangements during polarity tests on lighting circuits.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the Reg 132.14.2 prohibition on unlinked switches/circuit-breakers in earthed neutral conductors (with the linked-device exception). This is the companion regulation to 132.14.1 and a gap for a complete polarity guide.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 132.14.2: 'No switch or circuit-breaker, except where linked … shall be inserted in an earthed neutral conductor.' Linked device 'shall be arranged to break all the related line conductors.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Page says 'R1+R2 must not exceed approximately 1.02 ohms (before temperature correction)' for a B32 circuit, implying the site-measured limit equals 1.37 ohm; the GN3 0.80 factor and the correct 1.10 ohm cold-measured site limit are entirely absent.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG row at context_prefix 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Part 4 Chapter 41 Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a) Type B MCB 230V' states explicitly: 'Maximum earth fault loop impedance Zs for a Type B MCB … rated 32 A at 230 V is 1.37 ohm … Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.10 ohm.' A separate facet at Reg 253.0 confirms the 0.8 factor is a recognised temperature-correction method. The page's stated figure of 1.02 ohm appears nowhere in the RAG and does not match either the 1.37 ohm table value or the 1.10 ohm site limit (1.37 × 0.80 = 1.096 ≈ 1.10 ohm). The page's 'before temperature correction' framing is therefore misleading — site testers are required to apply the 0.80 factor and compare against 1.10 ohm, not 1.37 ohm. Both the correct site limit and the GN3 0.80 factor are absent from the page, which the RAG clearly shows are material and mandatory considerations.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 0.80 site-test factor: the page gives the tabulated Zs limit (1.37 Ω for B32) but never states that site-measured values must not exceed the 0.80-corrected limit (1.10 Ω for B32). This is a critical omission — site testers measuring Zs must use the lower 0.80 factor limit, not the tabulated value.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Table 41.3(a) context_prefix: 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.10 ohm' for Type B 32A MCB.

content_gaps.missing: Temperature correction procedure for R1+R2 and Zs: the page mentions temperature correction only briefly in one FAQ answer. GN3 Reg 1.08 requires the Cr correction factor when conductor temperature differs from 20°C, and GN3 Reg 5.78 specifies that Ri and Ro must be at operating temperature. A best-in-class page should explain how to apply the correction (multiply cold resistance by ~1.20 for 70°C conductors).
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 1.08: 'The resistance correction factor Cr shall be applied where conductor operating temperature differs from 20°C.' GN3 Reg 5.78: 'Ri and Ro are temperature-dependent.'

content_gaps.missing: The distinction between Test Method 1 (long lead / bridge at DB) and Test Method 2 (wander lead, separate R2 measurement) from BS 7671 Reg 643.2.1 is not explained. The page only covers Method 1. Method 2 (measuring R2 alone using a wander lead from the DB earth bar) is important for circuits where Method 1 is impractical.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: 'Continuity testing of protective conductors including Test method 1 and Test method 2 instructions' — keywords: 'Test method 1, Test method 2, R1+R2, polarity checks'.

content_gaps.missing: Polarity checks performed during continuity testing: the practical intelligence data records that 'polarity checks [are] to be performed during continuity testing'. The page does not mention this combined benefit of the continuity test procedure.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 612.9 requires RCDs to be tested at the time of installation and periodically thereafter.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns zero facets for any Reg 612.x. The governing RCD testing regulation in A4:2026 is in Chapter 64: Reg 643.3 (facet: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3 · [requirement] · RCD testing — alternating current test at rated residual operating current') and Reg 643.7.3.201 (facet confirms RCD effectiveness verification sits in Chapter 64). Reg 612.9 does not exist in the A4:2026 RAG. The correct citation is Reg 643.3 / 643.7.3.201.

confirmed_issues.claim: At 5 times rated current (150mA for a 30mA RCD), it must trip within 40ms.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns zero results for a 40ms trip criterion. Reg 643.7.3.201 facet states: 'NOTE: Regardless of RCD Type, effectiveness is deemed to have been verified where an RCD disconnects within the time stated below with an alternating current test at rated residual operating current (An): for general non-delay type, 300 ms maximum.' A4:2026 replaced multi-current-level testing (including the 5× IΔn criterion formerly in Table 3A, Appendix 3) with a single AC test at IΔn. The 40ms figure and the 5× IΔn test are no longer BS 7671 verification criteria post-A4.

confirmed_issues.claim: The half-rated current test — the RCD must NOT cause the RCD to trip within 2 seconds.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns zero facets for any half-rated (0.5×IΔn) test criterion or a 2-second pass/fail limit. Reg 643.3 facets confirm A4:2026 moved to a single alternating current test at IΔn for all RCD types. The 0.5×IΔn non-trip test and its 2-second limit derived from the former Appendix 3 Table 3A, which A4:2026 deleted. No supporting facet exists for this claim; it reflects pre-A4 practice only.

confirmed_issues.claim: S-type RCDs must not trip before 130ms and must trip within 500ms at rated current; at 5× rated current must not trip before 50ms and trip within 200ms.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns only one GN3 facet for S-type RCDs: '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.32 · [definition] · Non-delay versus time-delay (S-delay) RCD instruments' — which defines the instrument category distinction but provides no numeric trip-time limits. No facet in bs7671_facets contains the figures 130ms, 500ms, 50ms, or 200ms for S-type verification. These values came from the former Appendix 3 Table 3A, which was deleted in A4:2026. They are not a current BS 7671 verification criterion in the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671:2018+A2:2022 introduced mandatory SPD requirements for certain installation types.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for SPD mandatory requirements (Reg 443.4, 534.4.1.3, 534.4.1.4) are all attributed solely to BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. No facet attributes the SPD mandate to A2:2022 as the introduction point. Reg 443.4 facets state the transient overvoltage protection requirements as current A4:2026 obligations with no amendment-history note crediting A2. The RAG cannot confirm the A2:2022 attribution, making the page claim unverifiable from the current standard — and potentially incorrect if SPD requirements were in fact introduced or substantially changed by A4 rather than A2.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page states RCD testing falls under 'BS 7671 Chapter 61' (implied by citing Reg 612.9).
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns zero facets for any Reg 612.x. Querying context_prefix ilike '%Reg 612%' returns an empty result set. All Part 6 inspection and testing facets are in Chapter 64 (Reg 643.x). The facet '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3' and '[BS 7671] · ... · Reg 643.7.3.201' are the governing regulations. Chapter 61 / 612.x numbering is stale pre-A4 numbering not present anywhere in the A4:2026 RAG.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 changed RCD testing: Appendix 3 Table 3A (half-rated/5× time criteria) has been DELETED. The current test requirement (Reg 643.3) is a single AC test at IΔn. The page presents the old Table 3A framework as current BS 7671 requirements without noting A4:2026 removed it.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3 · [exception] · 'Table 3A (Time/current performance criteria for RCDs) in Appendix 3 has been deleted. Therefore, the previous requirement to test according to that table is no longer applicable.'

content_gaps.missing: Correct regulation number for RCD testing is Reg 643.3 and 643.7.3.201 (Chapter 64, Part 6), not Reg 612.9 (Chapter 61). This is cited 7+ times across the page and FAQs.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3 · [requirement] · 'RCD testing — alternating current test at rated residual operating current'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 new requirement: Reg 411.3.4 now mandates RCD ≤30mA protection for ALL AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. The page only mentions 411.3.3 (socket outlets). This is a major EICR/inspection implication not flagged anywhere on the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4 · [acceptance_criterion] · 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 643.3 A4:2026 states the test shall use an alternating current waveform 'regardless of RCD Type (AC, A, F, B etc.)'. The page focuses only on general type and S-type; it omits Type A, F, and B RCDs and the A4 clarification that AC test applies to all types.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3 · [condition] · 'Condition — RCD Type does not affect choice of AC test at IΔn'

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 705.512.2 requires that no exposed metalwork is accessible to livestock
confirmed_issues.evidence: All 19 RAG facets returned for Reg 705.512.2 cover IP ratings (minimum IP44 under normal conditions), protection of socket-outlets where external influences exceed AD4/AE3/AG1, and protection against corrosion in dairies and cattle sheds. Not one facet mentions exposed metalwork or livestock accessibility. That requirement belongs to Reg 705.537.2, which the RAG explicitly states: 'Devices for isolation and switching and devices for emergency stopping or emergency switching shall not be erected where they are accessible to livestock.' The page misattributes a 705.537.2 concept to 705.512.2.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 705.411.1 requires all socket outlet circuits in agricultural premises to be protected by 30 mA RCDs — mandatory with no exceptions
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 705.411.1 confirm three tiers: (a) circuits ≤32 A: RCD per Reg 415.1.1 (which specifies ≤30 mA); (b) circuits >32 A: RCD with rated residual operating current ≤100 mA; (c) all other circuits: ≤300 mA. An explicit example facet states: 'A final circuit supplying 63 A socket-outlets (rated current > 32 A) shall be provided with an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 100 mA.' The page's blanket '30 mA for all socket outlets with no exceptions' contradicts (b): large-current (>32 A) socket circuits require ≤100 mA, not ≤30 mA.

confirmed_issues.claim: PEN conductor prohibition on agricultural sites is not mentioned by the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet for Reg 705.411.4 states verbatim: 'A PEN conductor shall not be used within electrical installations of agricultural and horticultural premises. This is a direct prohibition applying to the use of a combined protective and neutral conductor (PEN) inside such premises.' This is a mandatory normative requirement (uses 'shall'). The page discusses TT vs TN-C-S earthing but according to the auditor does not mention this specific prohibition. A mandatory ban on PEN conductors is a material omission.

confirmed_issues.claim: PME earthing not recommended without a metal floor grid in livestock buildings — this nuance is absent from the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet for Reg 705.411.4 NOTE 2 states explicitly: 'Unless a metal grid is laid in the floor, the use of a PME earthing facility as the means of earthing for the electrical installation is not recommended.' A further facet elaborates: 'PME may be used but is discouraged where the floor lacks a metal grid' to mitigate equipotential and touch-voltage issues. The auditor confirms the page discusses TT vs TN-C-S in general terms only and omits this specific regulatory note entirely. This is a material regulatory nuance present in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 and absent from the page.

content_gaps.missing: PEN conductor prohibition (Reg 705.411.4): mandatory ban on PEN conductors within agricultural and horticultural premises — a hard regulatory requirement completely absent from the page
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 705.411.4: 'A PEN conductor shall not be used within electrical installations of agricultural and horticultural premises.'

content_gaps.missing: PME without floor grid warning (Reg 705.411.4 NOTE 2): PME earthing not recommended unless a metal grid is laid in the floor — the earthing section discusses TT vs TN-C-S but never flags this NOTE

content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 705.522.16: 'For locations where livestock is kept, the external influences shall be classified AF4... conduits shall have protection against corrosion of at least Class 2 (medium) [indoors] ... Class 4 (high) [outdoors]... degree of protection against impact of 5 J.'

content_gaps.missing: RCD threshold for >32 A socket circuits: the page says 30 mA is mandatory for ALL socket outlets, but Reg 705.411.1(b) permits up to 100 mA for circuits supplying socket-outlets rated >32 A
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 705.411.1(b): 'For final circuits supplying socket-outlets with rated current more than 32 A, an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 100 mA shall be provided.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Consumer unit replacement is listed as an example of work requiring a Minor Works certificate. The page states 'most scheme providers accept a Minor Works certificate for each circuit' and presents it as an acceptable option.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple facets from bs7671_facets directly contradict the page. Reg 644.4 facet [condition]: 'The permission to provide a Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate applies only where the work does not include ... replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit — replacement of those items removes eligibility for the Minor Works Certificate alternative.' Reg 644.4 facet [example]: 'Replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit is not eligible for a Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate under 644.4.201; such work requires an Electrical Installation Certificate.' Reg 644.4.201 facets confirm the EIC requirement from three angles: (a) the regulation explicitly covers 'replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit' as a trigger for an EIC, (b) 'An Electrical Installation Certificate ... shall be issued ... upon completion of the verification ... for ... the replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit.' Presenting consumer unit replacement as acceptable for a Minor Works certificate is directly contradicted by the A4:2026 RAG at Reg 644.4 and Reg 644.4.201. The page is wrong.

content_gaps.missing: Consumer unit replacement mandates an EIC under Reg 644.4.201 and Reg 120.3 — this is not ambiguous or scheme-dependent. The page misrepresents it as a grey area where Minor Works is often acceptable, which contradicts the regulation text. The correction should clearly state that an EIC is the required certificate for consumer unit replacement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 644.4.201 / Reg 120.3: 'The Electrical Installation Certificate is to be used when a consumer unit or distribution board is replaced.' and 'replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit is explicitly listed in Regulation 644.4.201 as an instance where … an Electrical Installation Certificate shall be issued.'

content_gaps.missing: The page does not mention that an EIC can alternatively certify multiple circuit additions/alterations on one job (Reg 120.3), avoiding the need for multiple Minor Works certificates. This is practically useful and would improve the FAQ answer on 'how many Minor Works certs for multiple jobs'.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 120.3: 'The Electrical Installation Certificate may be used to certify where there are multiple additions, or alterations, or remedial works to the existing installation which do not extend to new circuits, as an alternative to issuing multiple Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificates.'

content_gaps.missing: Certificates (EIC and Minor Works) may be issued in electronic form under Reg 644.4.202, provided authenticity and integrity are verified by a reliable process. This is directly relevant to Elec-Mate's digital certificate proposition and is not cited anywhere on the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 644.4.202: 'Electrical Installation Certificates and Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificates may be produced in any written or electronic form … their authenticity and integrity shall be verified by a reliable process or method.'

content_gaps.missing: The page does not mention the recommended next inspection interval that must be recorded on the EIC (Reg 644.4 requirement). This is a common oversight field and a useful practical point for the 'When EIC Is Required' section.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 644.4: 'the recommendation for the interval between initial verification and the first periodic inspection shall be recorded on the Certificate issued by the person or persons responsible for the design, construction and verification of the installation.'

confirmed_issues.claim: The four main schemes for electrical work in England and Wales are NICEIC, NAPIT, ELECSA, and BRE Certification.
confirmed_issues.evidence: All 8 PWI rows returned for scheme-operator queries list the four operators as NICEIC, NAPIT, STROMA, ELECSA — consistently and without exception. One row states explicitly: 'Scheme operator examples and their role: Examples of current scheme operators include: NICEIC, NAPIT, STROMA, ELECSA.' BRE does not appear in any PWI row. The page substitutes BRE for STROMA, which is a direct factual inversion contradicted by the RAG corpus.

confirmed_issues.claim: Registered businesses must employ fully qualified electricians as set by the Electrotechnical Assessment Specification (EAS) — page omits EAS entirely.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple PWI rows directly state the EAS requirement. A representative row: 'Complying with Competent Persons Scheme requirements: organisations registered with scheme operators (NICEIC, NAPIT, STROMA, ELECSA) must employ fully qualified electricians as set by the Electrotechnical Assessment Specification (EAS) and are subject to regular inspections by the scheme operator.' The EAS is the formal qualification standard underpinning all scheme eligibility and is consistently present in the RAG. A page that omits EAS while describing scheme requirements is missing a RAG-supported material fact.

content_gaps.missing: STROMA is the actual fourth major scheme operator (alongside NICEIC, NAPIT, ELECSA) — consistently listed in practical_work_intelligence. The page replaces it with BRE, which is not cited anywhere in the RAG as a main scheme peer. STROMA should replace or join BRE in the schemes-compared section and FAQ.

confirmed_issues.claim: Minimum 1 lux illumination on escape routes confirmed during commissioning test
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG facets in either bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence contain the numeric value '1 lux' or any lux threshold for escape routes. bs7671_facets confirms Reg 560.9 requires emergency lighting to comply with BS EN 1838 and BS 5266-1 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 — Chapter 56 · Reg 560.8.5 · [reference] · Emergency lighting performance standard BS EN 1838'), but the clause text of BS EN 1838 containing the numeric floor is not in the corpus. A PWI commissioning facet lists 'not verifying lux levels' as a common mistake and a design facet notes 'insufficient spacing causing non-compliant lux levels', confirming lux verification is required practice, but no facet states or supports the specific 1 lux figure. The page asserts a specific numeric threshold that cannot be grounded in the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: After annual test, mains power restored and system left to recharge for at least 24 hours before building is occupied
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple PWI facets confirm that recharge must occur after a full-duration annual discharge test and must be recorded: one facet (primary_topic: 'Annual full duration and battery discharge testing...') lists 'Not recording recharge behaviour' as a common mistake; another (primary_topic: 'Annual full-duration discharge test...') states 'confirm charging arrangements return batteries to service afterwards'. However, no facet in either bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence states a '24 hours' recharge period. The specific figure is not in the RAG corpus and therefore cannot be grounded. The page presents an unsupported numeric claim.

confirmed_issues.claim: System types include maintained, non-maintained, and sustained luminaires
confirmed_issues.evidence: The terms 'maintained' and 'non-maintained' are well-supported in the RAG: multiple PWI facets use them as standard classification vocabulary (e.g. keywords: ['non-maintained', 'local final circuit failure', 'emergency operation', 'luminaires'] and 'maintained', 'non-maintained' annotation facets). However, 'sustained' as a named emergency luminaire mode type does not appear in any bs7671_facets or PWI facet in an emergency lighting context — the word appears only in unrelated topics (burn injuries, load shedding). The three-way taxonomy including 'sustained' as a distinct type cannot be confirmed from the RAG. The claim is plausible from BS 5266-1 but is ungrounded.

confirmed_issues.claim: 3-hour duration required for sleeping accommodation (hotels, hospitals, care homes) and premises where phased evacuation is needed
confirmed_issues.evidence: No facet in bs7671_facets links a 3-hour rated duration to sleeping accommodation, hotels, hospitals, care homes, or phased evacuation. Two PWI facets record typical_duration_minutes of 180 for annual test procedures (e.g. 'Annual full duration and battery discharge testing... to verify the battery meets declared duration') but this describes the test procedure time, not a premises-type-specific design duration requirement. No RAG facet maps the 3-hour rated duration to specific premises types. The premises-type mapping comes from BS 5266-1 Table 1, which is not reproduced in the corpus.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005 requires the Responsible Person to provide and maintain emergency lighting
confirmed_issues.evidence: No facet in bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence mentions the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005, its article numbers, or the statutory 'Responsible Person' duty under that Order. The phrase 'responsible person' does appear in bs7671_facets but only in the BS 7671 sense of the designer/installer (e.g. '[BS 7671] · Reg 422.1 · [procedure] · Procedure — record keeping for fire safety design documentation': 'ensuring it is provided to the responsible person, client, or enforcing authority'). The FSO 2005 is a fire-safety statute outside the BS 7671/BS 5266 scope of both RAG tables and is entirely absent from the corpus.

confirmed_issues.claim: Building Regulations Approved Document B requires emergency lighting in new buildings, referencing BS 5266-1
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two PWI facets do co-cite Approved Document B alongside BS 5266 in an emergency lighting context: primary_topic 'Emergency escape lighting guidance... referencing the Electrician's Guide to Emergency Lighting which must be read in conjunction with Approved Document B and BS 5266', with keywords ['emergency lighting', 'BS 5266', 'Approved Document B', 'functional test']. This confirms ADB and BS 5266 are associated in the RAG, but the facets describe a practitioner reference guide, not the legislative requirement itself. No facet states that ADB 'requires emergency lighting in new buildings' as a statutory mandate. The specific causal claim (ADB imposes the requirement; it references BS 5266-1) is not directly grounded in the RAG corpus — ADB is outside BS 7671/BS 5266 RAG scope.

content_gaps.missing: BS EN 50172:2004 (system of maintenance) — the page never cites BS EN 50172, which practical_work_intelligence explicitly references as the standard governing annual full-duration testing procedure ('Clause 7.2 of BS EN 50172:2004'). Best-in-class guides cite both BS 5266-1 and BS EN 50172.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: 'Annual full-duration discharge test... as part of routine maintenance required by Clause 7.2 of BS EN 50172:2004.'

content_gaps.missing: BS EN 1838 performance standard — BS 7671 Reg 560.9 (and Reg 110.1.3(b)) mandates emergency lighting systems comply with BS EN 1838. The page never mentions BS EN 1838, which sets the illuminance values (including the 1 lux floor for escape routes and 0.5 lux for open areas) that underpin the completion certificate acceptance criteria.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: '[BS 7671] Reg 560.8.5 — Emergency lighting systems shall comply with BS EN 1838. BS EN 1838 provides requirements for lighting applications intended to provide emergency escape lighting.' Also Reg 110.24: 'BS EN 1838:2024 Lighting applications - Emergency lighting.'

content_gaps.missing: Annex J log book — the page discusses 'fire safety logbook' generically but never names Annex J of BS 5266-1 as the model log book format, which is what an inspector or verifier expects to see cited on a professional guide.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: 'Annex J (informative) Emergency lighting log book: maintain the Annex J emergency lighting log book on-site to record routine functional tests, duration (discharge) tests, repairs, battery replacements and photometric verifications.'

content_gaps.missing: De-rating factors and photometric design data — the completion certificate validity section omits that the certificate is only valid when accompanied by photometric design data with de-rating factors applied (lumen depreciation, worst-case battery condition). Certificate is only valid when accompanied by current signed checklist and report, photometric design data in accepted formats.' confirmed_issues.claim: Scotland's equivalent body is SELECT (Scottish Electrical Charitable Training Trust).
confirmed_issues.evidence: Both RAG tables are silent on SELECT and SECTT specifically — one OSG facet mentions Scottish Building Regulations compliance via BS 7671 but says nothing about trade bodies. However, this claim contains a clear factual misidentification that is not a matter of RAG interpretation: SELECT and SECTT are two distinct Scottish organisations. SELECT (full title: SELECT — Promoting Electrical Safety) is the Scottish electrical contractors trade association, the Scottish equivalent of NICEIC/ECA. SECTT (Scottish Electrical Charitable Training Trust) is a separate body that administers apprentice training in Scotland. The page attributes SECTT's name to SELECT, conflating them. This is a confirmed factual error on the page regardless of RAG coverage.

content_gaps.missing: The page lacks a bridging section connecting pay/career progression to BS 7671 competence requirements. bs7671_facets (Reg 641.6, Reg 653.5) confirms that inspection, testing, and certification work requires skilled persons competent in that work — this is the regulatory backbone for why JIB Technician grade commands higher pay, and it would strengthen the page's authority.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 641.6 — verification may be a single skilled person or team, key condition is competence; Reg 653.5 — Report shall be compiled and signed by one or more skilled persons

content_gaps.missing: No mention that signing Electrical Installation Certificates requires the signatory to be a competent skilled person (Reg 644.5, Reg 653.5). This is directly relevant to the value of the Approved/Technician grade — and why employers pay more for electricians who can certify work independently.

confirmed_issues.claim: GN3 (Reg 5.78) confirms that Zs shall be corrected to conductor normal operating temperature before comparison
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for GN3 Reg 5.78 return content about reduced cross-sectional area protective conductors and the Regulation 543.1.3 heating equation — not about correcting measured Zs to operating temperature. Specifically: 'Where reduced csa protective conductors are used, maximum EFLIs may need to be further reduced... Compliance with the specified equation in 543.1.3 is mandatory.' The temperature-correction-of-Zs principle is confirmed by a different GN3 regulation: GN3 Reg 5.78 condition facet states 'Calculations of prospective earth fault current and selection of operating time t shall use Zs measured or corrected to conductor normal operating temperature (e.g. 70°C)' — however this temperature-correction facet also appears under the Reg 5.78 context_prefix, so the principle IS confirmed under GN3 5.78. On re-examination the [GN3] Reg 5.78 [condition] facet does state the temperature correction requirement. The page citation of GN3 Reg 5.78 for this principle is therefore supported by the RAG. Verdict: false_alarm.

content_gaps.missing: The GN3 0.80 site-test factor (measuring Zs with instruments at ambient and comparing directly against 80% of the tabulated value) is mentioned in passing as the '80% rule' but the page does not cite where this factor originates or its limitations. GN3 specifies the exact factor and the conditions under which it applies vs the full correction-factor method.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets (gn3) — GN3 Chapter 5 / Reg 5.9 EICR recording; GN3 temperature/correction methodology facets (returned no numeric facets, indicating GN3 Table B2 is a gap in the facet store but should be referenced)

content_gaps.missing: No mention of prospective fault current (Ipf) and its relationship to Zs verification. content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets (bs7671) — Reg 643.7.3.1: 'relevant impedances for earth fault loop assessment shall be measured or determined by alternative method when required'

content_gaps.missing: No coverage of TT systems and earth electrode resistance testing (Ra) as a parallel Zs verification path. The page briefly mentions TT but does not explain the Ra measurement or why the OCPD Zs limits are practically irrelevant for TT (RCD is mandatory).
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets (osg) — OSG Reg 1.3: 'obtain the typical maximum external earth fault loop impedance (Ze) from the distributor' and OSG Reg 2.2 which notes Ze for TT relates to earth electrode

confirmed_issues.claim: Installation of new metal consumer unit described as 'AMD3 type per BS 7671 Amendment 4'
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms metal consumer units are required (OSG Reg 2.2.5 facets: 'Steel, as an example of a ferrous metal, is acceptable and deemed to satisfy the requirement to be a non-combustible material for consumer unit enclosures in domestic premises'). However, the page's attribution is wrong on two counts. First, 'AMD3' is a product-standard shorthand for the change introduced by the 3rd Amendment to the 17th edition (BS 7671:2008+A3:2015) — not a BS 7671:2018 Amendment 4 concept. Second, the RAG amendment-chronology facet ([BS 7671] Reg 433.1 APPENDIX) explicitly names the 18th edition amendment sequence as A1:2020, A2:2022, A3:2024, A4:2026. No retrieved RAG facet links the metal CU non-combustible requirement to Amendment 4 (2026); the OSG 2.2.5 facets carry no amendment attribution. Attributing the metal CU requirement to 'BS 7671 Amendment 4' is factually incorrect — it originated in BS 7671:2008+A3:2015 (17th edition). The RAG supports metal CUs being required but does not support the Amendment 4 attribution. This is a confirmed factual accuracy error on the page.

content_gaps.missing: The metal consumer unit requirement in domestic premises stems from Amendment 3 (2015), not Amendment 4:2026. The page says 'AMD3 type per BS 7671 Amendment 4' which conflates two separate amendments. A best-in-class page should clarify: the metal enclosure rule came with AMD3 (2015, Regulation 421.1.201), while A4:2026 introduces further changes such as AFDD requirements.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 — AFDD requirement added by A4:2026; metal CU rule predates A4

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced Reg 421.1.7 requiring AFDDs on certain socket-outlet final circuits in new domestic installations. A method statement for domestic rewire or CU change post-A4 must now consider AFDD specification and installation. The page makes no mention of AFDDs anywhere, which is a significant gap for work carried out under the current (A4:2026) edition.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 — 'arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)' for socket-outlet final circuits

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 added Reg 411.3.4 requiring RCD protection (≤30 mA) on all AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. A domestic rewire or CU change method statement written post-A4 must include this as a design and verification step. The page omits this entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 — 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires on the consumer unit ... protected by an RCD rated ≤30 mA'

content_gaps.missing: The OSG safe isolation minimum sequence (Reg 12.5) specifies three mandatory stages: (1) locate/positively identify correct isolation point, (2) check condition of voltage indicating device, (3) confirm VID functioning on known live source. confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 826.1.4 of BS 7671 requires that overload and short-circuit currents must be determined at every point where protection is installed
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG definitively contradicts this. Reg 826.1.4 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · Part 8 · Chapter 82 · Reg 826.1.4 · Protection against transient overvoltages') covers protection against switching/transient overvoltages in a Private Electrical Installation (PEI) and the consideration of SPDs — nothing about overload or short-circuit current determination. The overload/short-circuit determination requirement is at Reg 826.1.2.1 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · Part 8 · Chapter 82 · Reg 826.1.2.1 · Magnitude of · [requirement] · Determine overload and short-circuit currents at every PEI protective device'). The page cites the wrong regulation number and describes content that belongs to a different regulation entirely.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 133.2 of BS 7671 requires that conductor cross-sectional area is determined considering maximum permissible temperature, particularly when cables pass through thermal insulation
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG returns no facet for 'Reg 133.2' with any content resembling conductor CSA or thermal insulation. The only Reg 133.x facets present are all for Reg 133.1.3, which is about recording certain equipment usages on Part 6 electrical certification — entirely unrelated. Conductor CSA and thermal insulation correction content appears in the RAG under OSG Reg 2.6 (Tables F4(i)/F5(i)/F6 for thermal insulation corrections) and Reg 622.85 (verify conductor sizing against correction and grouping factors, including thermal insulation, per Section 523). Reg 133.2 as described on the page does not correspond to any facet in bs7671_facets, confirming this is a wrong regulation citation.

confirmed_issues.claim: Consumer units are required to be metal-enclosed since January 2016 under Amendment 4 to BS 7671
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG results returned for any query on metal enclosure, consumer unit fire containment, or non-combustible material (queries: 'metal enclosure consumer unit fire containment', 'consumer unit enclosure non-combustible material 2016'). The RAG cannot verify the January 2016 date or the amendment attribution. Critically, the edition label is wrong: the metal-enclosure requirement for consumer units came into effect via the 17th Edition Amendment 3 (AMD3:2015, effective January 2016), not 'Amendment 4 to BS 7671'. In the current context (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026), 'Amendment 4' refers to the 2026 update — a completely different amendment. The page conflates 17th Edition amendment numbering with 18th Edition amendment numbering, which is misleading to readers and unverifiable from the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026): AFDD recommendation for AC final circuits. A consumer unit noise/safety page is a natural home to mention that modern RCBO consumer units can now also include AFDDs, which detect arc faults (a source of dangerous buzzing/arcing) and are now recommended under BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. This is the single most important A4 addition for consumer unit content.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 421.1.7 · 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)… to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents… introduced by Amendment A4:2026'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026): Mandatory RCD ≤ 30 mA additional protection for domestic lighting circuits. confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.411.4.1 — where the installation is supplied by a PME (TN-C-S) earthing system, the protective conductor for the EV charging circuit must not be connected to the PME earth terminal; a separate earth electrode (TT earthing rod) must be installed and the circuit protected by a 30mA RCD.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms the core PME prohibition via Reg 722.411.4 [TNsystem] facets: 'A PME earthing facility shall not be used as the means of earthing for the protective conductor contact(s) of a charging point located outdoors … unless one of the alternative methods listed in Regulation 722.411.4 (b) to (e) is used.' However, the page collapses five alternatives into one mandatory path ('TT rod + 30mA RCD'). Method (b) is an earth electrode, (c) is a voltage-monitoring disconnect device (>70 V in ≤5 s), (d) is a voltage-window device (207–253 V), (e) is an equivalent device. NOTE 3 explicitly warns: 'Creating a TT earthing system … may not be an appropriate solution due to the inability to provide sufficient separation from buried metalwork connected to the supply PEN conductor.' The 30mA RCD is shown on the secondary of a separated system in Figure A722, not as the sole general solution. Additionally, indent (a) of Reg 722.411.4.1 was deleted in A4:2026 and the regulation now adds indent (iv) as an alternative. The page's framing that a TT rod 'must be installed' is a confirmed misrepresentation of the multi-option framework and contradicts NOTE 3.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.531.3.101 requires that each charging point be supplied by a dedicated circuit, individually protected by an overcurrent protective device.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Every RAG facet for Reg 722.531.3.101 confirms it is about transformer proximity siting and a one-charging-point-per-transformer limit, not dedicated circuit overcurrent protection. Context_prefix: '[BS 7671] … Reg 722.531.3.101 · [requirement] · Placement of transformer for charging installation — close as possible'. Content: 'Regulation 722.531.3.101 requires that the transformer supplying the charging installation should be placed as close as possible to the transformer.' NOTE 2: 'Only one charging point may be supplied by each transformer.' The dedicated-circuit requirement is real (confirmed by practical_work_intelligence: 'Electric vehicle charging should be from a single socket-outlet via a dedicated circuit … conforming to BS 7671 Section 722'), but the attribution of that requirement to Reg 722.531.3.101 is a clear misattribution.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.311.201 is cited by the RAG as a significant A4:2026 addition covering load curtailment for maximum demand — the page discusses load management in general terms but never cites Reg 722.311.201 by number, missing an opportunity to demonstrate regulatory authority for smart charging/CT clamp documentation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.311.201 — multiple facets confirm load curtailment (automatic or manual) may be taken into account for maximum demand determination

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.531.3.101 concerns transformer proximity siting for EV charging installations (one charging point per transformer) — a real requirement the page does not mention at all, relevant for commercial/multi-point installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.531.3.101: 'Only one charging point may be supplied by each transformer, see Regulation 722.413.1.2'

confirmed_issues.claim: LSZH variant (J-H(St)H) required for escape routes, communal areas and Section 422 high-risk locations under BS 7671
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on 'Section 422 fire risk wiring location cable' and 'LSZH low smoke zero halogen cable escape route fire Section 422' both returned empty. A direct context_prefix ilike '%422%' query returned Reg 422.2.1 facets, which confirm only that cables must satisfy CPR reaction-to-fire requirements (Regs 422.2.1 and 527.1.3). Crucially, one facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 2 · Reg 253.0') explicitly states: 'In the UK, MHCLG has not exercised the powers under the Construction Products Regulation to determine levels of performance required for particular installations in respect of reaction to fire for cables. Guidance may be given elsewhere.' The page's claim that LSZH is specifically 'required' under Section 422 for escape routes overstates what the RAG supports — the RAG confirms CPR compliance is required but does not mandate LSZH as the cable type; no performance level has been set by regulation in the UK. The specific LSZH requirement is ungrounded in the RAG and the RAG actively notes no mandatory level has been set.

content_gaps.missing: The page references 'Section 528' generically but never mentions Reg 444.6.1, which is the companion regulation governing Band I/Band II sharing of cable management — a requirement UK inspectors check directly alongside Reg 528.1. Citing both would make the BS 7671 coverage authoritative.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 444.6.1: 'Cables at voltage Band II and Band I which share the same cable management system or the same route shall be installed according to the requirements of Regulations … installers shall use segregation methods such as separate conduits, sealed partitions, screened metallic enclosures or segregated compartments within trunking.'

content_gaps.missing: OSG Reg 7.4.1 specifies that when Band I and Band II circuits are in the same multicore cable, an earthed metal screen equivalent to the largest Band II circuit is required. The page only discusses trunking/conduit approaches and misses this specific OSG method entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG 9th Ed Reg 7.4.1: 'When Band I and Band II circuits are contained within the same multicore cable, they shall be separated by an earthed metal screen whose current-carrying capacity is equivalent to the largest Band II circuit.'

content_gaps.missing: The page does not address A4:2026-specific additions (AFDD Reg 421.1.7 for arc protection, RCD requirements on domestic lighting circuits Reg 411.3.4) which are relevant to the mains side of any building-automation project in 2026 and would strengthen E-E-A-T for A4:2026 currency.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: A4:2026 AFDD (421.1.7) and RCD domestic lighting (411.3.4) — queries returned empty, confirming these topics exist in the standard but are absent from the page.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of fault-protection requirements triggered when ELV and LV services run in proximity — Reg 528.3.4 requires fault protection per Section 411 where electrical services are in close proximity. This is relevant whenever KNX bus runs parallel to mains in a shared cable route.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 528.3.4: 'fault protection shall be afforded in accordance with the requirements of Section 411' when electrical and non-electrical services (or differently-rated electrical services) are in close proximity.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.1.2 requires main protective bonding to gas, water, and oil pipework on TN-C-S systems
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG unambiguously contradicts this. All six bs7671_facets rows for Reg 411.3.1.2 confirm it is exclusively about maximum disconnection times in Table 41.1 for final circuits (socket-outlet circuits ≤63 A and fixed-load circuits ≤32 A) — no mention of protective bonding whatsoever. The correct regulation is Reg 544.1.2, which the RAG confirms in six separate facets: 'The main protective bonding connection to any extraneous-conductive-part such as gas, water or other metallic pipework or service shall be made as near as practicable to the point of entry of that part into the premises.' The page cites the wrong regulation number — 411.3.1.2 instead of 544.1.2.

content_gaps.missing: Correct regulation citation for main protective bonding: the page cites Reg 411.3.1.2 but the correct regulation is Reg 544.1.2 (connection location for main bonding conductors to extraneous-conductive-parts).

No mention of the A4:2026 TN-C-S (PME) earthing update. BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 introduced specific new requirements and renaming conventions around TN-C-S/PNB systems — the page predates this and does not flag that the standard has been updated.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 — A4 amendments noted in context_prefix facets]

content_gaps.missing: No coverage of AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) under Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026). A loose neutral with arcing at the terminal is precisely the scenario AFDDs are designed to detect, and the page misses the opportunity to reference this as a Code C3 upgrade recommendation on an EICR.

The GN3/PWI data confirms open PEN diagnosis should use a CAT-rated multimeter and involves cordon-off procedures. The page's diagnosis section omits the CAT rating requirement for test equipment used near a potentially live PEN — a safety gap for practicing electricians.
content_gaps.rag_source: [practical_work_intelligence · 'Fault diagnosis scenario for a suspected open PEN conductor in a TN-C-S supply' · typical_duration_minutes: 120]

confirmed_issues.claim: Current ratings (Method C clipped direct, single-phase): 16mm² Cu=87A, 25mm² Cu=114A, 35mm² Cu=141A, 50mm² Cu=168A; 16mm² Al=73A, 25mm² Al=96A, 35mm² Al=119A, 50mm² Al=142A
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG contains explicit tabulated values for two copper Method C tables that directly contradict the page. Table 4D5A (XLPE 90°C T+E, Method C, 30°C ambient): 16mm²=94A, 25mm²=125A, 35mm²=156A, 50mm²=188A. Table 4D1A (PVC 70°C T+E, Method C, 30°C ambient): 16mm²=76A, 25mm²=101A, 35mm²=125A, 50mm²=151A. The page's claimed copper values (87/114/141/168A) match neither table. They are neither the PVC 70°C nor the XLPE 90°C Method C figures from Appendix 4. No aluminium 4E4A rows exist in the RAG to check the aluminium column, but the copper column alone is demonstrably wrong against RAG evidence. The page appears to present figures from an unidentified or incorrectly cited table source.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced Reg 421.1.7 recommending AFDDs on AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk from arc fault currents. This is a major A4 addition that a student studying for the 2365 (aligned to BS 7671:2018+A4:2026) must know. The page never mentions AFDDs at all.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Ch.42 · Reg 421.1.7 · 'Regulation 421.1.7 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' (A4 insertion)

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced Reg 411.3.4 requiring RCD ≤30 mA additional protection on all AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. This is a mandatory new rule that directly affects consumer unit design and I&T — core 2365 Level 3 content — yet the page never mentions it.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Ch.41 · Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' (A4 change)

content_gaps.missing: The GN3 0.80 site-test derating factor applied to Zs limits during periodic inspection is absent. This is a common exam topic and real-world gotcha: design Zs values in the OSG/Appendix 3 must be multiplied by 0.8 when comparing to measured Ze on site, because instrument and thermal tolerance means measured Zs can read up to 25% higher than the true value.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] 9th Ed:2022 (A4) — inspection and testing chapters reference site-test Zs acceptance values and the use of the 0.8 factor against tabulated Zs limits.

content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets query for 'Appendix 4 cable sizing correction factors' returned empty — but the page's own claim references this content.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDD covered by 'Regulation 421.1 of BS 7671:2018+A2:2022' which 'recommends use in AC single-phase circuits supplying socket outlets rated up to 32A in residential premises'
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 421.1.7 are definitive on three points. (1) The sub-regulation is 421.1.7 — not bare '421.1'. Context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7 · [reference] · Introduced by Amendment A4:2026'. (2) The amendment is A4:2026, not A2:2022. (3) The scope facet states explicitly: 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends AFDD installation specifically in AC final circuits of a fixed installation' — no restriction to single-phase, no restriction to socket outlets, no 32 A cap. The page's regulation number, amendment citation, and scope description are all wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDD 'Recommended by BS 7671 Regulation 421.1 for socket circuits up to 32A in dwellings with sleeping accommodation' (section body, FAQ also attributes this to A2:2022)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Same RAG evidence as claim 1. The scope facet for Reg 421.1.7 reads: 'the recommendation is directed at alternating-current (AC) final circuits that are part of a fixed electrical installation, rather than other types of circuits or portable equipment.' No mention of socket-circuit-only scope, no 32 A threshold, no dwelling/sleeping-accommodation restriction in the facet content. The correct regulation is 421.1.7 (A4:2026), not 421.1 (A2:2022).

confirmed_issues.claim: PME has strict bonding requirements under 'Regulation 544.1'
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 544.1 exists in the RAG but its content is: bypass/substitute bonding conductors where supply live conductors exceed 35 mm² (sized per Table 54.8) — it has no PME-specific content. PME bonding conductor sizing is governed by Reg 544.11, which the RAG records explicitly: 'Where PME conditions apply…the main protective bonding conductor shall be selected in accordance with the PEN conductor of the supply and Table 54.8.' Citing Reg 544.1 for PME bonding is incorrect; the correct regulation is 544.11.

confirmed_issues.claim: The page makes no mention of AFDD requirement for HMOs despite citing BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 throughout
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.2 · [requirement]' contains: 'Regulation 421.1.7 requires protection of final circuits supplying socket-outlets with rated current not exceeding 32 A using Arc Fault Detection Devices (AFDD) in Higher Risk Residential Buildings, Houses in Multiple Occupation, Purpose-built student accommodation and Care homes. The requirement is mandatory for those named premises types.' A second facet under the same context_prefix confirms '[scope] · types of premises defined for mandatory AFDDs: Higher Risk Residential Buildings, Houses in Multiple Occupation (HMO), Purpose-built student accommodation, and Care homes.' A4:2026 redrafted 421.1.7 to make AFDD mandatory (not merely recommended) for HMOs. A page advising electricians on HMO compliance while citing BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 and omitting this obligation is materially incomplete.

confirmed_issues.claim: The page does not mention Regulation 411.3.4 (30mA RCD on lighting circuits in domestic premises)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns eight facets confirming Reg 411.3.4. Key facets: '[requirement] · Additional protection by an RCD shall be provided' — 'Regulation 411.3.4 requires that, within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided. The regulation uses the imperative shall, creating a mandatory obligation.' '[requirement] · RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA' — 'shall be provided by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA.' '[scope] · Application within domestic (household) premises' — the requirement applies to premises used as dwellings. HMOs are dwellings and therefore domestic premises under BS 7671. A page on HMO electrical compliance citing A4:2026 that omits this new mandatory lighting-circuit RCD obligation is missing a directly applicable A4:2026 addition.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD mandatory requirement for HMOs under Reg 421.1.7 / A4:2026. The RAG facet (context_prefix Reg 411.4.2) explicitly states AFDD is mandatory for final circuits supplying socket-outlets ≤32A in Houses in Multiple Occupation. The page has zero coverage of this — AFDD appears only as a related-page link. This is the most significant accuracy gap for a guide claiming A4:2026 compliance.

confirmed_issues.claim: Part P applies to Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland under their own separate building regulations with equivalent requirements
confirmed_issues.evidence: The OSG facets positively contradict this. Key facets: (1) '[OSG] Reg 1.2.1 — Geographic applicability: England approved documents location' — 'the MHCLG approved documents referred to apply to England. Installers working in other UK nations should consult the equivalent publishing authority for Wales, Scotland or Northern Ireland.' (2) '[OSG] Reg 2.7 — Scottish Mandatory Standard 4.6' — Scotland uses its own Mandatory Standard 4.6, not Part P. (3) '[OSG] Reg 1.2.3 — Application of Building Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2000' — NI has its own separate instrument. (4) '[OSG] Transfer of power to make building regulations for Wales from 31 December 2011' — Welsh ministers are the competent authority. Part P is an England-only instrument; the other nations have equivalent but separate legislative instruments. The page claim that 'Part P applies to Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland' is factually wrong — Part P does not apply there. The OSG explicitly distinguishes them.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 AFDD requirement (Reg 421.1.7): Arc Fault Detection Devices are now required in domestic consumer unit replacements in certain circumstances under BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The page mentions consumer unit replacement as notifiable but says nothing about the new AFDD obligation that triggers when replacing a CU in a dwelling — a significant A4:2026 addition directly relevant to this page's audience.
