confirmed_issues.claim: The 18th Edition introduced AFDDs as a recommendation ('shall be considered'), not a mandatory requirement, under Section 421 for higher-risk premises with sleeping accommodation — the page's blanket 'recommendation only' framing is no longer accurate under A4:2026.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets at Reg 411.4.2 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.2 · [requirement] · AFDD protection for final circuits to socket-outlets in Higher Risk Residential Buildings') state: 'Regulation 421.1.7 requires protection of final circuits supplying socket-outlets ≤32 A using AFDDs in Higher Risk Residential Buildings, Houses in Multiple Occupation, Purpose-built student accommodation and Care homes. The requirement is mandatory for those named premises types.' A separate facet confirms: 'For all other premises... Regulation 421.1.7 recommends AFDDs... as a recommendation rather than a mandatory requirement.' The page (line 265–273) says 'recommendation (not a mandatory requirement) for AFDDs in certain higher-risk locations — specifically, premises with sleeping accommodation.' This is stale pre-A4:2026 framing. Under A4:2026, AFDDs are mandatory (not merely recommended) for HRRBs, HMOs, student accommodation and care homes.

confirmed_issues.claim: Part 6 specifies testing and certification requirements; 'Table 61' contains insulation resistance minimum values and Zs tables for maximum earth fault loop impedance.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Part 6 Inspection and Testing consistently use Chapter 64 numbering (Reg 641.x, 642.x, 643.x, 644.x) throughout. The insulation resistance table in A4:2026 is explicitly 'Table 64' — facet context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [test] · Table 64 test voltages and minimum insulation resistance values' states: 'Table 64 specifies minimum insulation resistance values and the DC test voltages... For circuits up to and including 500 V use 500 V DC with minimum 1.0 MΩ.' No facet references 'Table 61' anywhere in bs7671_facets. 'Table 61' is 17th-edition numbering (Chapter 61 was the pre-A4 location for testing regs). The page at line 212 explicitly names 'Table 61 (insulation resistance minimum values)' — this is stale numbering that should be Table 64 in the current A4:2026 standard.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD protection required for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises (A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4) — this requirement is completely absent from the page.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets at Reg 411.3.4 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4 · [requirement]') confirm: 'Regulation 411.3.4 requires that, within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' The wording uses the mandatory 'shall'. This is a real omission: a mandatory A4:2026 requirement that the page — which presents itself as the authoritative guide to the 18th Edition including A4:2026 — does not mention at all.

confirmed_issues.claim: FI also makes the overall assessment Unsatisfactory — no exceptions
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 3.11 acceptance criterion facet (retrieved via tsv query on 'GN3 acceptance criterion unsatisfactory') states verbatim: 'An overall assessment of satisfactory on the EICR is not permitted where any observation has been coded C1 or C2. Presence of any C1 or C2 code shall result in an unsatisfactory overall assessment.' FI is not mentioned. No other GN3 3.11 facet (15 retrieved) extends the Unsatisfactory rule to FI. OSG Reg 7.28 facets confirm FI is a valid outcome on inspection schedule line-items but make no connection between FI and the EICR overall assessment box. The claim that FI forces Unsatisfactory 'with no exceptions' cannot be grounded from the RAG; the RAG only grounds C1 and C2 as forcing that outcome.

confirmed_issues.claim: Absence of main protective bonding to gas, water, or oil services — Regulation 411.3.1.2
confirmed_issues.evidence: Six facets were returned for Reg 411.3.1.2. Every one concerns maximum ADS disconnection times from Table 41.1 — specifically, which final circuits those times apply to (socket-outlet circuits up to 63 A, fixed-load circuits up to 32 A). None mentions main protective bonding. The main bonding obligation to incoming metallic services (gas, water, oil) is in Reg 411.3.1.1 and Chapter 54 (Reg 544.1 / Reg 545.3), confirmed by a separate facet: '[BS 7671] Part 5 Chapter 54 Reg 545.3 — Main protective bonding conductors connect main earthing terminal to incoming metallic services (water, gas, oil, sewage)'. Citing Reg 411.3.1.2 for a main-bonding observation is a wrong regulation number; it should be 411.3.1.1 (or 544.1).

confirmed_issues.claim: SPD absence is C3 under Regulation 443.4.1 for existing installations
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 443.4 is fully grounded in the RAG (six facets returned): it sets four named criteria (a)–(d) for mandatory SPD provision, a risk-assessment route for other cases, and an exception for single dwellings. However: (1) the RAG uses lettered sub-clauses 443.4(a)–(d), never a numbered sub-clause '443.4.1', so '443.4.1' as cited on the page does not correspond to any returned facet; (2) no RAG facet assigns a C3 classification to SPD absence — no EICR observation code appears in any 443.4 facet; (3) the single-dwelling exception facet means SPD absence in a domestic EICR is not automatically a defect at all, let alone a fixed C3. The C3 classification and '443.4.1' sub-clause numbering are ungrounded.

confirmed_issues.claim: Supplementary bonding in bathrooms not required if Regulation 701.415.2 conditions met (all circuits RCD protected + main bonding effective)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Six facets were returned for Reg 701.415.2. They detail the requirement: local supplementary protective equipotential bonding shall connect protective conductor terminals of each circuit supplying Class I and II equipment to accessible extraneous-conductive-parts (metallic pipes, central heating, structural parts). No returned facet mentions any exception based on RCD protection of circuits or main bonding being effective. The exception the page describes (widely cited in practice as deriving from BS 7671 Reg 701.415.2 Note or a related GN8 passage) is not grounded in any of the 701.415.2 facets in the RAG, making the claim unverifiable from the available source.

confirmed_issues.claim: Mixed wiring colours (old/new) without warning notice at DB — Regulation 514.14
confirmed_issues.evidence: Five facets were returned for Reg 514.14. They all confirm it has been deleted from BS 7671:2018+A4:2026: '[BS 7671] Reg 514.14 [note] Regulation 514.14 deleted' and '[BS 7671] Reg 514.14 [note] Implication: no current regulatory requirement at 514.14'. The OSG Reg 6.12 facets further confirm: 'The requirement for such a warning notice was deleted by BS 7671:2018+A2:2022' and 'the guidance uses permissive language (may) rather than a mandatory shall'. The page presenting a mixed-colour warning notice absence as an EICR observation under Reg 514.14 cites a deleted regulation and misrepresents the current status — the notice is now permissive guidance (OSG 6.12), not a regulatory requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671 requires RCD protection where the risk of electric shock is increased. Storage heater circuits should have appropriate overcurrent protection matched to the cable rating and heater load.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 411.3.3 (bs7671, Part 4 Chapter 41) confirm unambiguously that 411.3.3 'applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A' and that 'socket-outlets with a rated current greater than 32 A are not covered.' Storage heaters are hardwired fixed appliances, not socket-outlets — citing 411.3.3 for their RCD obligation is a misapplication. The correct regulation is Reg 753.415.1 (Part 7, Chapter 75), which RAG confirms imposes a mandatory RCD additional-protection requirement on 'circuits supplying heating units' and expressly prohibits time-delayed RCDs on those circuits. No RAG facet links 411.3.3 to fixed heating circuits. The regulation number on the page is wrong.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 753.515.1 (Chapter 75 — Electric Heating Systems): requires electric heating systems to be selected and erected to avoid harmful influence on other electrical and non-electrical installations. confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.1.2 requires main protective bonding to be applied to extraneous-conductive-parts as part of the ADS protective measure
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG is unambiguous. Reg 411.3.1.2 facets (all 8 returned) consistently describe disconnection time requirements: 'Regulation 411.3.1.2 establishes which categories of final circuits the maximum disconnection times in Table 41.1 shall apply to.' The bonding mandate for extraneous-conductive-parts sits exclusively in Reg 411.3.1.1: 'extraneous-conductive-parts liable to introduce a dangerous potential difference shall be connected to the main earthing terminal by protective bonding conductors complying with Chapter 54.' The page's attribution of the bonding requirement to 411.3.1.2 is directly contradicted by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Key Takeaway cites Regulation 411.3.1.2 for main protective bonding requirement
confirmed_issues.evidence: All Reg 411.3.1.2 facets in the RAG define it as a disconnection-time scoping rule (Table 41.1 for socket-outlet circuits up to 63 A and fixed-load circuits up to 32 A), not a bonding rule. Reg 411.3.1.1 facet explicitly states the bonding mandate: 'In each consumer's installation within a building, extraneous-conductive-parts liable to introduce a dangerous potential difference shall be connected to the main earthing terminal by protective bonding conductors complying with Chapter 54.' The Key Takeaway's citation of 411.3.1.2 for bonding is incorrect per the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Non-PME TN-S and TT: bonding CSA is half the size of the earthing conductor, minimum 6 mm²
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms the half-earthing-conductor rule for non-PME in Reg 544.11: 'Except where PME conditions apply, a main protective bonding conductor shall have a cross-sectional area not less than half the cross-sectional area required for the earthing conductor of the installation.' This part is correctly stated. However, the 6 mm² minimum in Reg 544.11 is stated only for multi-building installations ('For installations serving more than one building the cross-sectional area ... shall be not less than 6 mm²'). No RAG facet confirms a 6 mm² absolute minimum for a single-building non-PME (TN-S or TT) installation. The page conflates the multi-building 6 mm² floor with the single-building rule, which the RAG does not support.

confirmed_issues.claim: Bonding conductor minimum 6 mm² in all cases for protective bonding
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG's Reg 544.11 facets confine the 6 mm² minimum to two specific contexts: (a) installations serving more than one building (non-PME) and (b) PME street furniture where PEN is up to 10 mm² (Reg 714.411.203). No facet states a universal 6 mm² floor for all main protective bonding conductors. For a single-building non-PME installation the rule is half the earthing conductor CSA, which could be less than 6 mm² in theory. The page's 'minimum 6 mm² in all cases' is not grounded by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page cites Reg 411.3.1.1 as defining the protective measure of ADS, and separately Reg 411.3.1.2 for the bonding requirement
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG is definitive on the split: Reg 411.3.1.1 covers both (a) connection of exposed-conductive-parts to protective conductors and (b) the bonding mandate for extraneous-conductive-parts to the MET. Reg 411.3.1.2 covers only Table 41.1 disconnection time applicability. The page's assignment of the bonding mandate to 411.3.1.2 is directly contradicted. One Part 7 special-location cross-reference (Reg 551.7.3) does cite 411.3.1.2 alongside 543.3 in an accessibility context, but this does not alter the primary regulation structure. The page's attribution is wrong per the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: The correct regulation number for main protective bonding (Reg 411.3.1.1) is never stated. The page uses 411.3.1.2 throughout but the RAG shows 411.3.1.1 is the bonding regulation. Every reg-number reference in the page and key-takeaways must be corrected.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1.1: extraneous-conductive-parts definition and bonding mandate (multiple facets)

content_gaps.missing: The requirement to check with the local DNO before finalising bonding conductor size on PME — the RAG explicitly states 'the installer shall check with the local distribution network operator because local distributor's network conditions may require a larger conductor than the table minimums.' This is absent from the sizing and remedial sections.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 544.11 · Except · [procedure]: 'Check with local distributor for larger bonding conductor requirement'

content_gaps.missing: Central heating and air conditioning systems are listed in the RAG as extraneous-conductive-parts requiring bonding, but the page omits them entirely. The page only lists gas, water, oil, structural steel and lightning protection.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.1.1: 'Examples of extraneous-conductive-parts that shall be bonded include: (d) central heating and air conditioning systems'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 inspection criterion: bonding connections must be 'free from significant corrosion, securely fastened, continuous and accessible for testing' — the page's inspection checklist does not include the corrosion/condition check as a distinct pass/fail criterion.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 1.2.2: Acceptable condition of bonding connections for inspection pass

confirmed_issues.claim: An EICR is required for commercial premises under the Electricity at Work Regulations 1989, typically every 5 years or at change of tenancy.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG actively contradicts the fixed-interval framing. GN3 Reg 2.4 ([GN3] Chapter 2, Reg 2.4) states: 'The time intervals between the recommended dates of periodic inspections need consideration. The person or persons responsible for specifying inspection intervals shall consider appropriate time periods between recommended dates rather than applying a fixed interval without assessment.' GN3 Reg 3.5 reinforces: 'Periodic inspection and testing intervals shall be determined by the maintenance plan established by the person responsible for the electrical installation. The intervals are not fixed by this guidance text.' BS 7671 Reg 644.4 only requires the recommended interval to be recorded on the Certificate — it does not fix a 5-year value. No facet in bs7671_facets returns any '5 years' figure for commercial premises or a 'change of tenancy' trigger. EAWR 1989 is referenced in the RAG only via GN3 Reg 2.5 (maintenance records / Reg 4(2)) and Reg 16 (competence); neither mandates a 5-year EICR cycle. The page presents a risk-assessed guidance figure as a statutory rule, which is factually incorrect.

content_gaps.missing: RCD 30mA protection for all socket outlets ≤32A is a mandatory BS 7671 requirement (Reg 411.3.3(b)) for commercial installations. The page's 'Regulations and Certification' section omits this entirely — a new commercial fit-out that does not include this will fail inspection. This is a critical practical point for any electrician reading the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3(b): additional RCD ≤30 mA for all socket-outlets ≤32 A — mandatory requirement.

content_gaps.missing: SPD (Surge Protection Device) requirement for new commercial installations. A4:2026 and BS 7671 (Appendix 16, Type 1/2 SPD guidance) require SPD consideration at distribution boards. practical_work_intelligence has a dedicated SPD installation entry. The page does not mention SPDs at all despite covering distribution board design.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: 'Selection and installation of a surge protection device (SPD) at a consumer unit… Install a Type 2 SPD on a DIN rail adjacent to the consumer unit… BS 7671:2018 Appendix 16.'

content_gaps.missing: The EICR '5-year' interval for commercial premises is presented as a fixed rule. GN3 explicitly states intervals require 'careful consideration' based on risk, previous findings, and usage. The page should caveat this as a starting-point recommendation, not a statutory fixed period — a surveyor could legitimately recommend 1 or 3 years.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 2.4: 'The time intervals between the recommended dates of periodic inspections need careful consideration… it does not itself impose statutory intervals.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 644.4 requires the recommended interval to the first periodic inspection to be recorded on the EIC at handover. The page does not mention this certificate obligation despite covering EIC issuance — an important practical point for electricians completing commercial fit-outs.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 644.4: 'The recommendation for the interval between initial verification and the first periodic inspection shall be recorded on the Certificate.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 442 of BS 7671 is a notable omission — the page covers HV electrical work in the UK without citing Section 442 (temporary overvoltages from HV faults), which the RAG confirms is directly applicable
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets actively confirm Section 442 is the core BS 7671 requirement for HV-fed LV installations. bs7671_facets Reg 442.2.1 states power frequency fault voltage Uc 'shall not exceed a dangerous level' for LV installations exposed to HV earth faults. Reg 442.2.2 sets explicit stress-voltage limits (Up+250V for long disconnection times; Up+1200V for <5 s disconnection). The Section 442 scope facet explicitly names 'a fault between the high voltage system and Earth in the transformer substation that supplies the low voltage installation' as a covered scenario. GN3 facets at Reg 2.36 and Reg 2.6.21 require verification of LV installations against temporary overvoltages from HV faults, referencing Section 442 as the substantive requirement. practical_work_intelligence returns six Section 442 substation facets confirming the regulation governs consumer-owned substation design at 11kV/33kV. A UK guide on HV electrical work that omits Section 442 is missing directly applicable BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 content confirmed by RAG.

content_gaps.missing: BS 7671 Section 442 / Regs 442.2.1 and 442.2.2: Temporary overvoltages due to HV system faults affecting LV installations. The RAG confirms these regs set magnitude and duration limits for overvoltages at 11 kV consumer substations — directly relevant to anyone designing or maintaining HV consumer installations. The page covers the HV system itself but never explains the BS 7671 interface requirements (what the LV side must do when a fault occurs on the 11 kV side).
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 facet: [GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.36 · 'temporary overvoltages arising from a fault in the HV system (typically 11 kV) that supplies the substation feeding the LV installation' + bs7671_facets Reg 442.2.1/442.2.2 magnitude and duration requirements.

content_gaps.missing: Section 442 earthing and SPD requirements for LV installations supplied from HV consumer substations (Reg 442.2.3 and Section 534 SPD selection). The practical_work_intelligence facet on substation design cites 'insufficient earthing provision' and 'inadequate record of protection settings' as common mistakes — the page gives no BS 7671-grounded earthing guidance for the LV side.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence facet: 'Design and installation considerations for substations and transformer systems supplying installations at 33 kV or 11 kV ... summarising Section 442 responsibilities ... common_mistakes: insufficient earthing provision, inadequate record of protection settings'. bs7671_facets Reg 442.2.3 requires SPD selection per Section 534.

content_gaps.missing: SPD (surge protective device) requirements under Section 534 / Chapter 443 for LV distribution fed from HV systems. HV faults produce transient overvoltages that propagate downstream without significant attenuation. Page has no mention of SPD considerations for HV-connected sites.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets [BS 7671] Reg 442.2.3 [requirement]: 'Where protection against overvoltages is by the use of surge protective devices (SPDs), they shall be selected and erected in accordance with Section 534' + note: 'transient overvoltages transmitted by the supply distribution system are not significantly attenuated downstream'.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 verification procedure for LV installations at HV-fed substations — the inspector's checklist obligation to verify protection against temporary overvoltages. Any EICR on a site with an 11 kV consumer substation must address Reg 2.36 (GN3) / Section 442 (BS 7671). This would be highly relevant for the target audience (electricians working on large commercial/industrial sites).
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 facet [GN3] · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.36 · [scope]: 'This section concerns verification of protection of LV installations against temporary overvoltages due to faults in the HV or LV system. The Guidance Note refers to Section 442 of BS 7671 for the substantive requirements.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Minimum acceptable insulation resistance for 230V circuits is 1MΩ at 500V DC (BS 7671 Table 61)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG is unambiguous: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 643.3.2 references Table 64 (not Table 61) for all insulation resistance test voltages and minima. Multiple facets confirm 'Circuits up to and including 500V (excluding SELV/PELV) = 1.0 MΩ at 500V DC per Table 64'. Table 61 numbering does not appear anywhere in the A4:2026 facets. The 1MΩ/500V value is correct but the table citation 'Table 61' is stale 17th/early-18th edition numbering and must be corrected to 'Table 64'.

confirmed_issues.claim: Tests RCD trip time at 50%, 100%, and 500% of the rated residual operating current (IΔn)
confirmed_issues.evidence: OSG Reg 11.3 explicitly states: 'With a residual current flowing equivalent to 50% of the rated tripping current of the RCD, the device shall not open.' This is a no-trip (non-operating) verification, not a timed trip test. GN3 Reg 4.2 confirms the test levels as '0.5 x IΔn, IΔn, and 5 x IΔn' but the 0.5x level is explicitly a no-trip check. Listing 50% as a standard trip-time test level alongside 100% and 500% misrepresents the test regime — 50% is a pass/no-trip pre-check, not a timed operation test.

confirmed_issues.claim: Temperature compensation allows correction of measured Zs values to the standard reference temperature (20°C)
confirmed_issues.evidence: OSG Reg 1.08 / Table B8 facets are explicit and consistent: the reference temperature for site Zs measurement comparison (Tables B1–B6) is 10°C, not 20°C. OSG states 'the appropriate ambient correction factor from Table B8 shall be applied … if the ambient temperature is other than 10°C'. A separate facet notes 'Table B8 corrects from 10°C and Table 12 corrects from 20°C' — confirming 20°C is a different table for a different context. The page stating 20°C as the standard reference is incorrect per the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Test voltage is 250V DC for SELV/PELV circuits; minimum acceptable insulation resistance remains 1MΩ at 250V
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Table 64 (Reg 643.3.2) is unambiguous: 'SELV and PELV circuits: 250V DC with minimum 0.5 MΩ'. Multiple facets repeat this numeric minimum. The page's claim of 1MΩ at 250V for SELV/PELV is directly contradicted — the correct minimum is 0.5 MΩ (500kΩ), half of what the page states.

content_gaps.missing: Table 64 (not Table 61) is the correct A4:2026 insulation resistance reference — the page uses the pre-A4 'Table 61' numbering which will mislead practitioners. The correct citation is Table 64 per Reg 643.3.2.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.3.2 and Table 64 facets

content_gaps.missing: SELV/PELV minimum insulation resistance is 0.5MΩ at 250V DC (not 1MΩ) — an important distinction for circuits with electronic components that the page incorrectly states.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Table 64 (Reg 643.3.2 acceptance_criterion facet)

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 new requirement: Reg 411.3.4 mandates RCD (≤30mA) additional protection for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. An MFT buying guide should explain that modern EICR/EIC work will encounter this new requirement, and the MFT must test the RCD protecting lighting circuits.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page implies standard CPC connection on ring-circuit spurs with no special mention of high-integrity CPC requirement.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple bs7671_facets explicitly mandate high-integrity CPC on ring spurs. OSG Reg 7.5.3 (osg facet): 'Where a ring final circuit has spurs provided from the ring to supply additional socket-outlets, those spurs require high integrity protective conductor connections. The protective conductor connection method for spurs shall be of equal integrity to maintain continuity of the CPC.' BS7671 Reg 543.7.2.201 (bs7671 facet): 'Spurs, if provided, require high integrity protective conductor connections complying with Regulation 543.7.1.' A second BS7671 facet (Reg 543.7.2.201 condition): 'Where a ring final circuit has spurs provided from it, those spurs require high integrity protective conductor connections complying with the requirements of Regulation 543.7.1. Spurs are not exempt from the high integrity requirement.' The page's failure to mention this is a confirmed content omission — the RAG clearly and repeatedly grounds this as a mandatory requirement, not optional guidance.

content_gaps.missing: High-integrity CPC connections required for spur CPCs on ring final circuits. The page never mentions this requirement at all — it only discusses cable CSA, not CPC connection integrity.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 7 · Reg 7.5.3: 'Where a ring final circuit has spurs provided from the ring to supply additional socket-outlets, those spurs require high integrity protective conductor connections. The protective conductor connection method for spurs shall be of equal integrity to maintain continuity of the CPC.'

content_gaps.missing: 30mA RCD additional protection requirement for all socket-outlets up to 32A — the page mentions RCD testing in the certification section but never says the new spur socket itself must be RCD-protected, nor that a risk assessment is required to omit it.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 3.3: 'Generally, and unless an exception applies, all socket-outlets up to and including 32A shall be provided with additional protection by a 30mA RCD.' [OSG] Reg 7.2.5 requires written risk assessment to omit RCD protection.

content_gaps.missing: Obligation under Reg 644.1.2 to record any discovered defects in the existing installation on the Minor Works Certificate — the certification section describes the MWC but omits this duty to document pre-existing defects.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 Reg 644.1.2: 'the person responsible shall record discovered defects in the existing installation on that certificate.'

content_gaps.missing: Minor Works Certificate full title is 'Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate' (MEIWC). Page uses 'Minor Works Certificate' throughout — acceptable abbreviation but the formal name is needed for SEO accuracy.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 Reg 120.3 and [GN3] Reg 1.4 both use the full title 'Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate'.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 411.3.3 requires RCD protection for all socket outlets up to 32A and all final circuits in new domestic installations
confirmed_issues.evidence: Three bs7671_facets rows for Reg 411.3.3 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3') confirm the regulation is scoped exclusively to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32A. The RAG states verbatim: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception in the Regulation is met.' Separately, Reg 411.3.4 (six facets returned) mandates additional RCD protection (≤30 mA) specifically for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises — a wholly separate regulation. The page conflates these two distinct regulations by extending 411.3.3's scope to 'all final circuits', which the RAG directly contradicts.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Section 631 sets out the requirements for periodic inspection and testing
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero rows were returned for context_prefix matching 'Section 631', 'Reg 631', or 'Chapter 63x' in bs7671_facets — the numbering does not exist in the A4:2026 RAG. The RAG places periodic inspection and testing in Part 6, Chapter 65, Regs 651–653 (confirmed by seven returned facets under '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 65 · Reg 653.1'). 'Section 631' is stale 17th-edition numbering, not valid in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Modern consumer units must be fitted with RCDs or RCBOs per Reg 411.3.3 (implying this covers all circuits including lighting)
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG is explicit that Reg 411.3.3 applies only to socket-outlets ≤32A. The A4:2026 addition — Reg 411.3.4 — separately requires additional RCD protection (≤30 mA) for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4'). Attributing the all-circuits or lighting-circuit requirement to 411.3.3 alone is directly contradicted by the RAG; the page omits 411.3.4 entirely, which is a new A4:2026 requirement.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — mandatory RCD protection (≤30 mA) for ALL domestic lighting circuits is entirely absent. This is a significant A4:2026 addition that directly affects every consumer unit upgrade job in Swindon.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Table 61 (Table 61.1 in the 18th Edition) sets out the minimum insulation resistance values
confirmed_issues.evidence: Every relevant facet in bs7671_facets consistently cites Table 64 in Chapter 64 for insulation resistance test voltages and minima. Reg 643.3.2 facet: 'insulation resistance measured with the test voltages indicated in Table 64 shall be considered satisfactory…not less than the appropriate value given in Table 64.' A search for 'Table 61' in both context_prefix and content returns zero rows across the entire bs7671_facets table. 'Table 61' is stale 17th-edition numbering; the correct A4:2026 reference is Table 64.

confirmed_issues.claim: Where equipment is connected that could influence or be damaged by the test, a 250V DC test is used; simply disconnect all electronic equipment before testing
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 643.3.3 facets are unambiguous: the procedure is a mandatory two-stage sequence — (1) 'the test shall be applied prior to the connection of such equipment, in accordance with Table 64' and (2) 'Following connection of the equipment, a test at 250 V DC shall be applied between live conductors and the protective conductor connected to the earthing arrangement.' A dedicated facet states the sequence explicitly: '[procedure] Sequence: pre-connection tests then post-connection 250 V DC test.' The acceptance criterion facet confirms the post-connection 250 V DC test must yield at least 1 MΩ. The page's simple 'disconnect everything' advice omits the mandatory post-connection 250 V DC verification stage, which is a normative requirement of A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: SELV/PELV minimum insulation resistance is 0.5 MΩ (stated unconditionally throughout)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Table 64 / Reg 643.3.2 facets confirm the general SELV/PELV minimum is 0.5 MΩ at 250 V DC. However, GN3 Reg 4.8 acceptance_criterion facet states: 'When SELV and PELV circuits that include equipment such as SPDs are insulation resistance tested at 250 V DC, the measured insulation resistance shall be at least 1 MΩ.' A second GN3 Reg 4.8 facet ([requirement]) confirms: 'A test voltage of 250 V DC shall be used for insulation resistance testing of SELV and PELV circuits where equipment such as SPDs may influence test results…the insulation resistance is required to be at least 1 MΩ.' Stating 0.5 MΩ unconditionally omits this mandatory 1 MΩ exception for SELV/PELV circuits containing SPDs.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 643.3.3 mandatory two-stage procedure: test cables at standard voltage before connecting sensitive equipment, then mandatory 250V DC post-connection test with 1 MΩ minimum. GN3 2.24 illustrates this with a figure. The page only says 'disconnect everything' and misses this normative A4:2026 requirement entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.3.3 [procedure] + [acceptance_criterion] 'post-connection 250 V DC insulation minimum 1 MΩ'; [GN3] Reg 2.24 'Sequence: test wiring at 500 V, then connect equipment, then 250 V test'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 nuance: SELV/PELV circuits containing SPDs must achieve 1 MΩ (not 0.5 MΩ) at 250V DC. Page unconditionally states 0.5 MΩ minimum for all SELV/PELV without this exception.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 4.8 [acceptance_criterion]: '1 MΩ minimum insulation for SELV and PELV tested at 250 V DC' where SPDs are present

content_gaps.missing: Table citation should be Table 64 (Chapter 64, BS 7671:2018+A4:2026), not 'Table 61' or 'Table 61.1 in the 18th Edition.' A4:2026 renumbered Chapter 61 to Chapter 64. The stale reference could mislead readers consulting the current standard.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.3.2 [reference]: 'Table 64 reference for insulation test voltages and minima' and [test]: 'Table 64 specifies minimum insulation resistance values'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 change note: Reg 643.3 was redrafted in A4:2026. The page makes no mention of this amendment, which is a significant update for electricians updating their knowledge from 18th Edition to current standard.

The redraft clarifies requirements for testing insulation resistance where connected equipment is likely to influence the verification test or be damaged by the test.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Equipment in food processing wash-down areas requires IP69K as a minimum. IP69K certifies protection against high-pressure, high-temperature water jets: test conditions are 80°C water at 80 bar pressure, flow rate of 14–16 litres per minute, nozzle at 100–150mm distance, applied from all angles over a 30-second duration.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG (bs7671_facets + PWI) contains no row for IP69K or IPX9. The PWI row on water ingress protection covers IPX4, IPX5 and IPX7 only (keywords: ['IPX4','IPX5','IPX7','water ingress','splash proof','jet wash','temporary immersion','enclosure testing']). The original auditor's claim that PWI returns IPX9 as the IEC designation for high-temperature/pressure jets was not reproducible — no IPX9 row exists. More critically, IP69K is a DIN 40050-9/ISO 20653 designation (used in automotive and food industries) whereas IEC 60529 uses IPX9K. The page presents IP69K test numerics (80°C, 80 bar, 14–16 L/min, 100–150 mm, 30 s) as if they are a BS 7671 minimum requirement for food processing wash-down areas, but the RAG provides no grounding for this mandatory minimum. The claim is outside RAG scope and cannot be confirmed as a BS 7671 requirement.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Reg 421.1.7, A4:2026 addition): the page has no mention of arc fault detection devices, which BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 now recommends for AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk from arc faults. Food factory environments with extensive PVC cable runs, vibration, and vermin damage have elevated arc fault risk — this is a best-in-class addition.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7 — recommends AFDD installation on AC final circuits to mitigate risk of fire due to arc fault currents. Wording is recommendatory not mandatory.'

content_gaps.missing: Correct IEC 60529 IPX9 designation alongside IP69K: the RAG uses IPX9 consistently as the IEC code for high-temperature/high-pressure jet protection. The page exclusively uses 'IP69K' (the older DIN 40050-9 designation). Best-in-class content should cite both: IP69K (DIN/ISO) = IPX9 (IEC 60529) and explain the equivalence.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: 'There is also an IPX9, which relates to high-temperature and high-pressure water jets where the temperature of the jet exceeds 80°C'; keywords include IPX9, IPX5, IPX6 — not IP69K.

content_gaps.missing: Cable grouping and current-carrying capacity derating in high-ambient-temperature food factory environments: BS 7671 Reg 523.4 requires applying group rating factors and lowest-maximum-operating-temperature rules for grouped cables. Food factories can have elevated ambient temperatures near ovens, dryers, or pasteurisers — a practical gap not addressed.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 52 · Reg 523.4 — groups containing cables with different maximum operating temperatures must base capacity on the lowest maximum operating temperature together with the appropriate group rating factor.'

content_gaps.missing: SPD (surge protection device) requirements under A4:2026 Reg 534: industrial food processing plants with large motor loads, VFDs, and switching transients have elevated surge risk. BS 7671 Reg 534.4.1.6 requires consideration of SPDs to protect from switching and other external overvoltage sources — omitted entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 534.4.1.6 — consideration shall be given to the provision of SPDs to protect from other sources, such as switching and other external overvoltages.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Body text (AI Pricing section) lists only C1, C2, C3 — omits FI code
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 3.11 (context_prefix: '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 3 · Reg 3.11 · … · Purpose of coding observations on EICR Section K') states: 'The purpose of coding each observation in Section K of the EICR with C1, C2, C3 or FI is to clearly identify the severity of a defect.' GN3 Reg 1.2.2 explicitly defines all four codes: 'C1 (danger present), C2 (potentially dangerous), C3 (improvement recommended) and FI (further investigation required). These codes are to be used as the outcomes field.' GN3 Reg 5.9 shows FI on the example EICR form alongside C3 with its own definition: 'FI on an EICR denotes Further investigation is advised.' The page text 'you record defects with observation codes (C1, C2, C3)' is technically incomplete — GN3 mandates four codes, not three. The omission of FI is a genuine regulatory inaccuracy.

content_gaps.missing: The body text in the AI Pricing section omits the FI (Further Investigation) observation code. GN3 mandates four codes: C1, C2, C3, and FI. confirmed_issues.claim: Fill ratio must not exceed approximately 40% (to maintain cable current ratings through adequate ventilation)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Exhaustive RAG search across bs7671_facets for 'cable tray basket fill ratio ventilation', 'grouping correction factor cable current rating', and 'cable tray perforated basket installation method' returned zero facets containing any fill-ratio threshold for cable basket or tray. Appendix 4 grouping/derating facets (Cg, Ca) confirm correction factors apply to grouped cables but no 40% figure appears anywhere in the RAG. The 40% figure is a manufacturer-derived convention, not a BS 7671 or OSG prescribed threshold. The page presents it with implied regulatory backing it does not have from the RAG — confirmed unverifiable.

confirmed_issues.claim: Cable basket is a metallic extraneous conductive part under BS 7671 and must be bonded
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet specifically classifies metallic cable basket or tray as an 'extraneous-conductive-part'. The extraneous-conductive-part bonding facets returned (Reg 702.415.2, Reg 714.411.3.1, GN3 Reg 3.3) refer to pipework, handrails, and frames within special locations — not cable containment. However, Reg 444.5.3.1 (returned explicitly) states: 'Metallic containment shall be connected to the equipotential bonding network' — covering trunking, conduit, ducting and other metallic enclosures. The bonding obligation itself is RAG-confirmed via 444.5.3.1, but the specific legal framing as 'extraneous conductive part' is not supported by any returned facet. Cable basket is more accurately an exposed-conductive-part or metallic containment under 444.5.3.1, not an extraneous-conductive-part. The page's classification is therefore incorrect in its specific framing, even though the bonding conclusion is correct.

confirmed_issues.claim: Support spacing for horizontal runs: 1500mm maximum for Cablofil CF54 with standard cable loading
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 521.10.202 is confirmed in the RAG as the regulation 'giving requirements for the methods of support of wiring systems' (multiple facets returned). However, no RAG facet — across bs7671, gn3, or osg document types — contains any specific 1500mm horizontal spacing figure for cable basket or tray. OSG Reg 3.76 facets returned are index entries for unrelated topics (supply frequency, SPDs, spurs, stud walls) and contain no support-spacing data. The 1500mm figure is a Cablofil product datasheet specification, not a BS 7671 prescriptive value. The page presents it without making that distinction clear.

confirmed_issues.claim: At bends, tees, and changes of direction, supports within 300mm are required
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 521.10.202 facets confirm support requirements exist in BS 7671, but no RAG facet contains a specific 300mm at-fittings figure for cable basket. The OSG Reg 3.76 query returned index entries entirely unrelated to support spacing. No bs7671, gn3, or osg facet mentions a 300mm proximity-to-fittings rule. This is a manufacturer recommendation (common in Cablofil and similar datasheets), not a verbatim BS 7671 or OSG requirement. The page implies regulatory backing that the RAG does not support.

confirmed_issues.claim: Cable basket typically costs 20-40% more per metre than equivalent perforated steel cable tray
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two separate PWI queries ('cable tray basket cost installation labour' and 'wire basket cost price labour saving tray') returned zero rows containing any cable basket or tray cost comparison data. The practical_work_intelligence table has no pricing or material-cost rows for cable basket vs perforated steel tray. The 20-40% cost premium figure cannot be grounded or contradicted from the RAG; it is entirely unverifiable against the permitted sources of truth.

confirmed_issues.claim: Labour saving on-site: installation labour reduced by 15-30% on complex commercial routes vs pressed steel tray
confirmed_issues.evidence: Both PWI queries for cable basket/tray installation labour returned no rows with labour-saving comparison data. The one relevant PWI row found ('Rapid installation of fast-coupling wire basket cable management', 150 minutes) records installation duration and notes 'insufficient ventilation allowance' and 'omitting safety edge protectors' as common mistakes, but contains no percentage labour-saving claim versus pressed steel tray and no comparative figure. The 15-30% saving is commercially unverifiable from the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Cable segregation: minimum separation of 200mm between power and data cables where runs share a space
confirmed_issues.evidence: Regs 528.2, 444.4.2, 830.3.201 and OSG Reg 7.25 are all confirmed in the RAG as requiring segregation or separation of power and communications/data cables to avoid electromagnetic interference. However, no facet returns a specific 200mm distance figure. Reg 528.2 defines segregation as 'physical or electrical measures' without prescribing a metric. Reg 444.4.2 requires 'segregation distances or the use of separate containment' without specifying 200mm. The 200mm figure originates from CENELEC EN 50174 (structured cabling standard), not BS 7671. The page presents it as though it is a BS 7671 requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Vertical runs: support at maximum 1200mm centres for vertical cable basket
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 521.10.202 is confirmed in the RAG as governing support methods for wiring systems. Reg 522.15.1 (referenced in the original audit) was not returned in RAG queries. No facet — across bs7671, gn3, or osg — contains a 1200mm vertical spacing figure for cable basket. OSG Reg 3.76 queries returned only unrelated index topics. The 1200mm vertical centres figure is a manufacturer specification (standard for most wire basket systems), not a BS 7671 prescriptive value. The page presents it without making that distinction clear.

content_gaps.missing: BS 7671 Appendix 4 installation Method E/F distinction: open cable tray (perforated/wire basket) is Method E (free air) giving higher current ratings than Method C (clipped direct). The page never mentions installation reference method letters or that basket may permit Method E/F ratings — a critical sizing detail for electricians.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Appendix 4 Table 4D1A — 'Method C (clipped direct to non-metallic surface or cable tray)' with explicit Cg/Ca correction factor instructions. No Method E/F facet returned but the Appendix 4 distinction is well-established in the standard.

content_gaps.missing: Specific grouping derating factor values from BS 7671 Appendix 4 Table 4C1 (e.g. Cg = 0.80 for 3 cables touching). The page mentions 'apply grouping factors' in the FAQ but gives no example values, which is the practically useful detail for electricians sizing cables in basket.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Appendix 4 Table 4D1A with Cg correction factor references — grouping table values not returned but the requirement for Cg is confirmed.

content_gaps.missing: OSG site-test derating factor of 0.80 applied during inspection and testing of cable current ratings. Relevant for electricians certifying commercial cable basket installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: OSG Reg 12.513 — CPC adequacy per Section 543; the 0.80 site-test factor appears in GN3/OSG but no facet returned directly.

content_gaps.missing: The page does not reference BS 7671 Regulation 521 (selection of wiring systems) or Chapter 52 in the context of cable basket specifically — no citation of the relevant regulation for cable management selection.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: BS 7671 Reg 521.10.202 (via 411.9.3) — 'cables shall be correctly supported throughout their run in accordance with Regulation 521.10.202'.

confirmed_issues.claim: PAS 63100 is a required reference standard for domestic battery installations
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 570.6.7.203 [reference] facet (bs7671, 2018+A4:2026, Chapter 57): 'Stationary secondary batteries in dwellings shall be installed in a suitable location taking account of PAS 63100. Installers shall consult PAS 63100 standards in addition to manufacturer's instructions when selecting locations in dwellings.' OSG facet Reg 12.4.2 [example] corroborates: 'A domestic dwelling battery energy storage system installation shall be sited taking account of the manufacturer's instructions and PAS 63100.' The RAG unambiguously mandates PAS 63100 for domestic BESS. The auditor is correct that omitting any mention of it is a regulatory gap.

confirmed_issues.claim: Insulation resistance test: 500V DC, minimum 1 megohm — without caveat for connected battery electronics
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 643.3 [requirement] facet (bs7671, 2018+A4:2026, Chapter 64): 'Where equipment is connected and the equipment is likely to influence the insulation resistance verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used.' The [condition] facet confirms: 'The condition likely to influence or be damaged is the trigger for using the 250 V DC test.' Battery inverter electronics are exactly such connected equipment. Presenting 500V/1MΩ as the test specification without the 250V caveat for a battery installation with connected inverter electronics misrepresents A4:2026 Reg 643.3. The 250V requirement is mandatory when inverter electronics are connected, and its omission is a confirmed issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems must be consulted for testing
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 1.7 [requirement] facet (gn3, GN3 9th Ed:2022 A4, Chapter 1): 'For testing regimes, acceptance criteria and particular safety tests relating to Electrical Energy Storage Systems, GN3 requires that the tester shall refer to the IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems to determine applicable tests (for example battery isolation, DC earthing arrangements and BMS considerations).' This is an explicit mandatory GN3 requirement. A battery storage installation guide that covers testing without referencing this mandatory consultation is a confirmed regulatory gap.

content_gaps.missing: PAS 63100 is not mentioned anywhere. Reg 570.6.7.203 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 explicitly requires domestic BESS installations to be sited taking account of PAS 63100. This is a mandatory regulatory reference that directly governs ventilation, clearances and fire separation for the installations this page describes.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 Reg 570.6.7.203; OSG 12.4.2 — 'Stationary secondary batteries in dwellings shall be installed in a suitable location taking account of manufacturer's instructions and PAS 63100.'

content_gaps.missing: IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems is not mentioned in the testing section. GN3 Reg 1.7 classifies EESS as requiring specialist knowledge and mandates the tester consult this Code for test procedures, DC earthing arrangements, and BMS considerations. Omitting this leaves the testing section incomplete for competent-person compliance.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 1.7: 'designers, installers and inspectors shall refer to the IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems for specific guidance on selection, installation, earthing, protection and testing of EESS.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 712.514.103 (inverter warning notice) wording is not cited. The page mentions labelling generically but does not reference the specific BS 7671 regulation requiring a warning notice on all inverters covering both AC and DC isolation. This reg is mandatory and the specific wording format (referencing DC residual energy) should appear.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 Reg 712.514.103: 'A warning notice shall be fixed to all inverters with words similar to the following: WARNING… [covering AC and DC sides]'

content_gaps.missing: Insulation resistance testing with connected battery electronics: the page states '500V DC, minimum 1 megohm' without noting the 250V DC alternative required by Reg 643.3 where electronics would be damaged by 500V. Battery inverters and BMS electronics virtually always require the 250V approach, making the unqualified 500V statement potentially misleading for this specific installation type.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 Reg 643.3: 'Where equipment is connected and likely to be damaged by standard test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test shall be used.'

top_improvements.action: Add PAS 63100 to the Safety Requirements section. Reg 570.6.7.203 explicitly requires domestic BESS installers to follow PAS 63100 for location, ventilation and fire separation. A single sentence ('Battery installations in dwellings must also comply with PAS 63100 — the publicly available specification for domestic energy storage, referenced directly by BS 7671 Reg 570.6.7.203') removes a regulatory gap that a competent inspector would flag.

Add the IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems to the Testing and Certification section. GN3 Reg 1.7 classifies EESS as requiring specialist knowledge and mandates referral to this Code for test procedures, DC earthing and BMS considerations. The testing checklist currently omits DC earth fault loop, BMS functional check, and capacity verification — all covered by the Code.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 1.7: 'designers, installers and inspectors shall refer to the IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems'

top_improvements.action: Qualify the insulation resistance test figure. Replace '500V DC, minimum 1 megohm' with: '500V DC for AC wiring with equipment disconnected; where battery inverter or BMS electronics are connected, use 250V DC per Reg 643.3 to avoid equipment damage.' This is the most technically incorrect statement on the page for the intended audience.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671 Reg 643.3: 'where equipment is connected and likely to be damaged by standard test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test shall be used'

top_improvements.action: Add Reg 712.514.103 to the Labelling section. The page mentions labels are required but does not cite the regulation or note that the wording must cover both AC and DC isolation (addressing residual DC energy from the battery). Adding the regulation reference and noting the dual AC/DC isolation requirement makes the page authoritative and citable.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671 Reg 712.514.103: 'A warning notice shall be fixed to all inverters… [covering both AC and DC sides]'

top_improvements.action: Reference BS EN 50549-1 in the anti-islanding / backup power section. The page mentions anti-islanding protection but not the operative standard (BS EN 50549-1) that governs voltage, frequency and loss-of-mains settings for inverters ≤16A per phase — the exact threshold that aligns with G98 domestic systems. This is what DNOs inspect and what commissioning engineers configure.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671 Reg 551.7.4: 'anti-islanding, voltage and frequency protection settings configured in accordance with BS EN 50549-1'

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR is documented in accordance with BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Section 631)
confirmed_issues.evidence: No 'Section 631' exists anywhere in the A4 RAG. The periodic inspection and EICR framework in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 sits in Chapter 65: Reg 651.1 (requirements for periodic inspection and testing), Reg 651.4 (action on dangerous conditions), and Reg 135.1 explicitly directs practitioners to 'Chapter 65' for periodic inspection. The facet at [BS 7671] Reg 135.1 states: 'periodic inspection and testing recommendations are to be carried out in accordance with Chapter 65.' The citation 'Section 631' appears to be stale pre-A4 numbering from an older edition and is incorrect against the live standard. This is a wrong reg citation on the page.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 621 establishes that installations must be periodically inspected at intervals suited to the property type
confirmed_issues.evidence: No 'Section 621' exists in the A4 RAG. The obligation to carry out periodic inspection is placed in Chapter 65 (Reg 651.1): 'Where required, periodic inspection and testing shall be carried out in accordance with Regulations.' Reg 651.1 explicitly notes that 'frequencies are determined by other Regulations, Codes of Practice, guidance or statutory requirements.' GN3 Regs 3.4, 3.5, and 2.4 cover inspection intervals and specify that intervals 'require careful consideration' rather than being fixed — there is no regulation numbered 621 in the current standard. This is a stale/incorrect reg citation analogous to the Section 631 error in claim 1.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDD (Reg 421.1.7) — page does not mention AFDD at all (omission flag)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 421.1.7 is fully confirmed in the A4:2026 RAG as a redrafted regulation that 'introduces mandatory AFDDs in specific premises and a wider recommendation for other premises' ([BS 7671] Reg 411.4.2 note). Critically, Reg 651.2(e) is explicitly cross-referenced in the AFDD inspection checklist alongside Regs 421.1.7 and 532.6, meaning AFDD operational confirmation is a named item in the BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 periodic inspection schedule. The RAG facet at [BS 7671] Reg 411.9.3 states: 'Regulations 421.1.7, 532.6 and 651.2(e) require confirmation that Arc Fault Detection Devices (AFDDs) are operational.' An EICR guide page that omits any mention of this A4:2026 addition is materially misleading for EICR inspectors assessing Nottingham properties, where older installations are precisely the context where this new requirement needs to be flagged.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — AFDD recommendation for AC final circuits. Relevant to EICR inspectors in Nottingham assessing older Victorian terraces: absence of AFDD is increasingly recorded as a C3 or FI on modern EICRs. The page should note that A4:2026 introduced Reg 421.1.7 recommending AFDDs, and that inspectors should reference this when completing the updated EICR model forms.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: [BS 7671] · Reg 421.1.7 · 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits... introduced by Amendment A4:2026'

content_gaps.missing: Updated EICR model forms under A4:2026 — Reg 133.1.3 now requires certain equipment usages (including AFDD, SPD) to be recorded explicitly on Part 6 certification. The page's 'What to Expect During an EICR' section describes the old report structure without mentioning the revised model forms that include new fields for SPD and AFDD presence.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: [BS 7671] · Reg 133.1.3 · 'where BS 7671 calls for the usage of particular equipment to be identified, that usage is explicitly entered on the certification associated with the work covered by Part 6'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 EICR observation code decision rule — GN3 Reg 3.11 specifies that C3 shall only be used where C1 and C2 do not apply (not merely preference). The page's observation code descriptions are accurate but omit this important professional rule about code selection hierarchy, which would add depth for electrician readers.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets (gn3): [GN3] · Reg 3.11 · 'The classification Improvement recommended (C3) shall be attributed only where C1 (Danger present) or C2 (potentially dangerous / non-compliant) do not apply. Practitioners shall not use C3 if evidence supports C1 or C2 classifications.'

content_gaps.missing: Ze measurement purpose and Zs derivation — GN3 Reg 2.29 explains that Ze is measured to determine the external component for calculating Zs (R1+R2 addition) for protective device disconnection verification. The page mentions 'Ze and Zs values' in live testing but does not explain the relationship or why both matter for an aging Nottingham property with a TN-C-S supply.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets (gn3): [GN3] · Reg 2.29 · 'Measuring Ze at the origin is intended to determine the external component used to calculate circuit earth fault loop impedance Zs by addition with internal conductor resistances (R1 + R2)'

confirmed_issues.claim: MEIWC is issued for minor work that does not include the provision of a new circuit; replacing a consumer unit on a like-for-like basis is cited as an example of MEIWC work
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 644.4.201 is covered by multiple bs7671_facets (document_type='bs7671', context_prefix includes '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · … Reg 644.4'). Every returned facet is unambiguous: 'a Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate … may be provided … where the work does not include the provision of a new circuit or replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit' and 'Replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit is not eligible for a Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate under 644.4.201; such work requires an Electrical Installation Certificate.' The page's claim that consumer unit replacement is an example of MEIWC work is directly and explicitly contradicted by the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced Reg 421.1.7 recommending AFDD installation on AC final circuits of fixed installations. The page never mentions AFDDs in its compliance context, even though the updated Appendix 6 model forms (which the page cites) now include mandatory AFDD recording fields. A best-in-class compliance guide should explain that EIC/EICR forms now require AFDD and SPD details.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 [reference]: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs).' Also Reg 722.826.3.201: 'Appendix 6 Model forms...amended to include fields for recording the details of SPDs and AFDDs.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced Reg 411.3.4 requiring additional RCD protection (≤30 mA) on AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. The page makes no mention of this significant new requirement, which directly affects what must be recorded and certified on an EIC for any domestic installation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 [requirement]: 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.'

content_gaps.missing: Appendix 6 in A4:2026 was significantly revised: the schedule of inspections for initial verification was simplified, SPD/AFDD fields were added, and a new inspection checklist was introduced (though not mandatory to include with the certificate). None of these A4:2026-specific changes to the model forms are mentioned on the page despite directly affecting the certificates it describes.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201 [note]: 'Appendix 6 schedule of inspections has been simplified for initial verification. A new example checklist of items requiring inspection during initial verification has been added to Appendix 6.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 514.12 now includes an exception for domestic (household) premises regarding periodic inspection and testing notices — a nuance absent from the page's discussion of EICR intervals.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 514.12 [note]: 'BS 7671 Regulation 514.12 now contains an exception specifically for domestic (household) premises in certain situations. The amendment introduced by A4:2026 adds or clarifies that some notice provisions do not apply to domestic premises where the stated conditions are met.'

confirmed_issues.claim: The current standard is BS 7671:2018+A3:2024 (stated in key takeaways, FAQs, CTA, and hero subtitle)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG is unambiguous that A4:2026 is the current edition. Facet at context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 65 · Reg 653.2 · [note] · Editions history and Amendment 4 issued April 2026' states: 'Amendment 4 was issued April 2026 (Orange Cover).' A second facet at Reg 722.826.3.201 states: 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 was issued on 15th April 2026 and may be implemented immediately. BS 7671:2018+A2:2022+Corrigendum (May 2023)+A3:2024 remains current but will be withdrawn on 15th October 2026.' The page body stating A3:2024 is the current edition is directly contradicted. The internal contradiction noted by the auditor (meta description says A4:2026, body says A3:2024) is also confirmed.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4 to BS 7671:2018 is 'expected in 2026' (future tense, FAQ)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet at Reg 722.826.3.201 explicitly states: 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 was issued on 15th April 2026 and may be implemented immediately.' A second facet at Reg 653.2 confirms: 'Amendment 4 was issued April 2026 (Orange Cover).' A4:2026 is an already-published standard, not an expected future amendment. The page's future-tense FAQ is directly contradicted.

confirmed_issues.claim: Introduction of AFDDs per Regulation 421.1 in the 18th Edition (wrong regulation number, wrong chapter if page implies Chapter 41, and mandatory framing)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns six facets all under context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7'. The facet explicitly headed '[note] · Wording is recommendatory not mandatory' states: 'The text of Regulation 421.1.7 uses the term recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... the regulation's wording is advisory rather than mandatory; it does not use shall or other mandatory phrasing.' The correct citation is Reg 421.1.7 in Chapter 42, not Reg 421.1 or Chapter 41. The page has two confirmed errors: wrong regulation number (421.1 vs 421.1.7) and mandatory framing where the standard uses advisory language.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 3 (A3:2024) added new Section 530 requirements for bidirectional and unidirectional protective/switching devices
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms A3:2024 did introduce bidirectional/unidirectional device provisions, but they are located in Chapter 72 (Reg 722.826.3.201), not Section 530. Facet at '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 — Special Installations or Locations · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201' states: 'Amendment A3:2024 provides for the inclusion of two new definitions and one new Regulation relating to the use of bidirectional and unidirectional devices.' Section 530 facets that do exist (Reg 530.3.5, 530.3.201) concern protection-only equipment not being used for functional switching — no bidirectional/unidirectional device content appears there. The page's 'Section 530' attribution is contradicted by the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — RCD protection not exceeding 30mA now required for domestic lighting circuits. This is a major A4:2026 addition entirely absent from the page, which would be highly relevant to the '18th Edition amendments' and 'looking ahead' sections.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4: 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA' in domestic premises. GN3 facet confirms scope to household premises.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 is already published (not merely 'expected'). The page's 'Looking Ahead' section and FAQ treat A4 as future, but RAG confirms it is current. The page should describe what A4:2026 actually changed — including Reg 411.3.4, new Reg 411.6.5, new Regulation group 419, deletion of 433.1, and RCD adjustability rules (531.3.4.201).
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.6.5 — 'inserted'; Reg 419 group inserted; Reg 433.1 deleted; Reg 531.3.4.201 RCD adjustment key/tool requirement.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD regulation number precision: the page says 'Regulation 421.1' but the correct citation is Reg 421.1.7 in Chapter 42. The page should also note the recommendatory (not mandatory) nature of the wording in A4:2026.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7 · [note] · 'Wording is recommendatory not mandatory (recommending)'

content_gaps.missing: RCD adjustability requirement (Reg 531.3.4.201): A4:2026 requires that any adjustment of an RCD's rated residual operating current requires a key or tool and provides a visible change indicator. This is a new practical requirement electricians fitting adjustable RCDs in consumer units need to know.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 53 · Reg 531.3.4.201: 'any attempt to change its rated residual operating current (Ign) requires a key or tool and after adjustment provides a readily visible change.'

confirmed_issues.claim: 2.5mm² T&E clipped direct (Method C) has Iz = 27A; Method A has Iz = 20A per conductor (Table 4D5A)
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG directly contradicts the 27A Method C figure. Table 4D5A facet states: 'Maximum current-carrying capacity (Iz) for 2.5 mm² XLPE-insulated 90°C twin and earth cable installed Method C (clipped direct) is 30 A. Per BS 7671 Table 4D5A, ambient 30°C.' Table 4D1A (standard PVC 70°C T&E) gives 24A for 2.5mm² Method C — neither matches the page's 27A claim. The page either cites the wrong table row or has transposed a 3-phase SWA value (Table 4D4A gives 27A for 2.5mm² Method C in a 3-core XLPE SWA cable). No Method A facets exist in the RAG corpus to verify the 20A figure, but the Method C claim of 27A is clearly wrong per the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: 4mm² T&E Method A has Iz = 27A (Table 4D5A)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Table 4D5A facets show 4mm² XLPE 90°C T&E Method C = 40A. Table 4D1A shows 4mm² PVC 70°C T&E Method C = 32A. No Method A facets for T&E cable are present in bs7671_facets, so the 27A cannot be positively confirmed from RAG alone — however, 27A is substantially lower than the Method C rating for 4mm² in any T&E table (32A or 40A), and Method A (enclosed in insulated wall) is typically lower than Method C but not that much lower for 4mm². The value 27A for 4mm² Method A does not match typical BS 7671 figures for any T&E insulation type, and the same numeral (27A) appears incorrectly in claim 8 for 2.5mm² Method C, suggesting a systematic table-reading error on the page. Flagged as confirmed issue on the basis of internal inconsistency and contradiction with adjacent RAG evidence.

confirmed_issues.claim: Cables should be suspended at a minimum height of 5.8 metres where vehicles pass beneath
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet Reg 708.521.7.3 (bs7671_facets, document_type=bs7671, context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 708.521.7.3 · Minimum height 6 m for overhead conductors in areas with vehicle movement') states verbatim: 'Every overhead conductor shall be at a height above ground of not less than 6 m in all areas subject to the movement of vehicles.' The same value is confirmed by two additional facets (Reg 709.521.1.8 and a second Reg 708.521.7.3 requirement facet). The page's figure of 5.8 m is 200 mm below the code minimum and is directly contradicted.

confirmed_issues.claim: 3 metres minimum for pedestrian areas only (overhead cables)

The same 3.5 m figure is returned by Reg 709.521.1.8 facets. The page's figure of 3 m is 500 mm below the mandatory minimum and is directly contradicted.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 132.15 states that 'live working is not permitted unless it is unreasonable in all circumstances for the conductor to be made dead.'
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix ilike '%Reg 132.15%' returns 8 facets, all confirming Reg 132.15.201 is exclusively about provision of isolation means: 'It requires provision of means so that all voltage may be cut off from every installation, from every circuit thereof and from all equipment.' There is zero content in any Reg 132.15 facet about live working or the dead-conductor exception. Separately, a full-text search for 'live working dead conductor unreasonable' returns one result: GN3 Reg 4.9, which explicitly identifies the 'unreasonable in all the circumstances for it to be dead' language as coming from Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 Regulation 14(a), not from BS 7671. The page incorrectly attributes an EaWR Reg 14(a) exception to 'BS 7671 Regulation 132.15' — a confirmed misattribution. The regulation number and the source statute are both wrong.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 addition Reg 411.3.4 — RCDs required on domestic lighting circuits (30 mA maximum). The page's BS 7671 introduction section mentions 'protection against electric shock' but omits this specific A4:2026 rule, which first-year apprentices encounter constantly on domestic work.

content_gaps.missing: Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 Regulation 14 as the actual legal source for the live-working prohibition and exception. content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 4.9: 'Regulation 14(a): Work on or so near live conductors is permitted where it is unreasonable in all the circumstances for it to be dead.'

This is a key piece of Year 1 testing knowledge.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 132.15.201 — what it actually covers (means to cut off all voltage from every installation, circuit, and equipment). confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 530.3.201 requires that switching devices installed in installations with bidirectional power flow must be rated for bidirectional operation or otherwise proven suitable
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG verbatim from context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 530.3.201 · [requirement] · Selection shall take account of unidirectional or bidirectional devices': 'Selection and erection of equipment for protection shall take account of appropriate use of either a unidirectional protective device or a bidirectional protective device. The installer or designer shall determine which directional characteristic is appropriate for the application when selecting protective devices.' A separate facet ([condition] · Appropriate use depends on application and device marking) confirms the obligation is conditional: 'where a device is marked as unidirectional, the installation shall respect the marked orientation.' The regulation mandates appropriate consideration and correct orientation of marked unidirectional devices — it does NOT categorically require bidirectional rating wherever bidirectional flow exists. A correctly oriented unidirectional device remains compliant. The page's framing 'must be rated for bidirectional operation' is an overstatement unsupported by the RAG text.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4's primary addition is Regulation 530.3.201; it also includes some minor editorial corrections
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms A4:2026 introduced substantially more than 530.3.201 and minor editorial corrections. Confirmed new/modified provisions in the RAG: (1) Reg 411.6.5 inserted plus new Regulation group 419 in Chapter 41 (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.6.5 · [reference] · Insertion of Regulation 411.6.5 into BS 7671 A4:2026'); (2) Reg 421.1.7 inserted recommending AFDD installation in AC final circuits (Chapter 42, '[reference] · Introduced by Amendment A4:2026'); (3) Reg 444.4.6 covering PNB/PME earthing and TN-C-S public distribution network (Chapter 44); (4) Reg 514.12.2 modified — RCD notice with new domestic premises exception; (5) Chapter 41 described as containing 'significant changes'; (6) Part 2 definitions 'expanded and modified'; (7) Changes to plug/socket-outlet requirements; (8) SELV/PELV introduction at 703.414.3. Describing 530.3.201 as the 'primary addition' with only 'minor editorial corrections' is clearly contradicted by the RAG, which shows at least six substantive regulation changes across multiple chapters.

confirmed_issues.claim: The timeline row reads '2026: Amendment 4 expected' while also listing '2024: Amendment 4 (A4:2026)' — self-contradictory copy
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet at context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201 · [reference] · Amendment 4:2026 issuance and withdrawal timeline' confirms: 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 was issued on 15th April 2026 and may be implemented immediately. BS 7671:2018+A2:2022+Corrigendum (May 2023)+A3:2024 remains current but will be withdrawn on 15th October 2026.' A4:2026 is a published standard issued April 2026. Any page timeline simultaneously showing '2024: Amendment 4 (A4:2026)' and '2026: Amendment 4 expected' is self-contradictory: the amendment cannot be both already published (with a 2026 year-suffix confirming its identity) and simultaneously 'expected'. This is a confirmed drafting error on the page.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 530.3.201 actual wording: the regulation requires designers to 'take account of appropriate use of either a unidirectional protective device or a bidirectional protective device' — a nuanced selection duty, not an absolute mandate for bidirectional devices wherever reverse flow exists. The page never quotes or paraphrases the actual regulatory text, which would be the most valuable content for practitioners.

confirmed_issues.claim: Zs validation against maximum values from Tables 41.2, 41.3, and 41.4
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms Tables 41.2 (BS 88-2 fuses, Reg 411.4.201), 41.3 (MCBs 0.4 s and 5 s, Reg 411.4.202), and 41.4 (Reg 411.4.203 Note 2) all exist as named Zs compliance tables. However the RAG equally confirms Table 41.5 (maximum Zs for RCDs to BS EN 61008-1/61009-1, Reg 411.5.3 acceptance criterion) and Table 41.6 (Zs values not to be exceeded, Reg 411.8.3) are mandatory for other protective device types. Naming only 41.2/41.3/41.4 as the Zs validation source omits the RCD table (41.5) and the general table (41.6) — making the stated set factually incomplete. The RAG facet for Reg 411.4.204(a) also confirms a GN3 0.80 site-test factor applies to cold-measured Zs limits, which the page claim does not mention. The auditor's concern is confirmed: 41.2–41.4 alone does not cover all device types.

The page does not mention that its certificate forms include the new SPD/AFDD recording fields, which is a concrete A4:2026 differentiator that would reassure inspectors and scheme providers.
