confirmed_issues.claim: Insulation resistance minimum values: 1 megohm for SELV and PELV circuits
confirmed_issues.evidence: BS 7671 Reg 643.3.2 facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3.2 · SELV and PELV circuits insulation minimum 0.5 MΩ at 250 V DC') states verbatim: 'For SELV and PELV circuits the insulation resistance measured at a test voltage of 250 V DC shall be not less than 0.5 MΩ.' The page claims 1 MΩ for SELV/PELV — this is directly contradicted. The correct minimum is 0.5 MΩ at 250 V DC, not 1 MΩ.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD test: both no-trip at 50% rated current and trip at 100% rated current should be recorded
confirmed_issues.evidence: Under A4:2026, Appendix 3 Table 3A (the x0.5/x1/x5 IΔn multi-step test sequence including the 50% no-trip check) has been deleted. Facets confirm this explicitly: '[BS 7671] Reg 643.3 [exception] — Table 3A (Time/current performance criteria for RCDs) in Appendix 3 has been deleted. Therefore, the previous requirement to test according to that table is no longer applicable; instead the single alternating current test at IΔn is used for verification regardless of type.' The OSG facet confirms a 100% IΔn trip-time test (< 300 ms) is still required, but no RAG facet supports recording a separate 50% no-trip test as a current A4 requirement. The page's two-test description reflects the superseded pre-A4 regime.

content_gaps.missing: RCD protection now mandatory for domestic lighting circuits under Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026). Page discusses RCD testing and consumer unit replacement but never mentions that every lighting circuit in domestic premises must be RCD-protected (≤30 mA). This is a new A4 requirement that directly affects the EIC checklist and schedule of inspections at handover.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' Confirmed in GN3 Ch5 Reg 5.12.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) requirements under Reg 421.1.7 and their documentation at handover. The page mentions AFDDs only in the warranty/product information list, never as a notifiable installation change or schedule item. A4:2026 introduced mandatory AFDD requirements for certain socket-outlet circuits; the EIC schedule of inspections must now capture AFDD presence/compliance.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 421.1.7 and Reg 538.4: 'Chapter 53 includes a section on Arc Fault Detection Devices (AFDDs)... AFDD selection, application and erection guidance is provided where required to reduce risk of fire from arc faults.' Also Reg 722.421.1.7.201 for EV charging circuits.

content_gaps.missing: Correct insulation resistance minimum for SELV/PELV is 0.5 MΩ (not 1 MΩ as stated). Table 64 (Reg 643.3.2) specifies 0.5 MΩ at 250V DC for SELV/PELV — this is a factual error in the page that needs correcting.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Table 64 (Reg 643.3.2): 'SELV/PELV = 0.5 MΩ (at 250 V DC); Circuits up to and including 500 V (excluding SELV/PELV) = 1.0 MΩ (at 500 V DC).'

content_gaps.missing: The page instructs users to apply a 0.8 site correction factor when comparing measured Zs against BS 7671 tabulated maximum values (GN3 guidance). Measured Zs values on site must be multiplied by 1/0.8 = 1.25 before comparing with table values, or equivalently the table values reduced by 0.8. The page mentions Zs recording without this critical verification step.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 and practical_work_intelligence facets for Zs documentation: 'comparing with the designer's calculated values and BS 7671/IEE On-Site Guide figures' — the 0.8 factor is standard GN3 guidance for site measurements.

confirmed_issues.claim: S-type RCD at 5× rated current must not trip before 50ms and must trip within 200ms.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facets (bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence) return the S-type 5×IΔn timing window (50ms minimum / 200ms maximum). Searches for '50 ms' combined with '200 ms' and 'S type selective five times trip' returned zero results. The 200ms figures that did appear relate to portable generator RCDs (OSG 2.4.2) — an entirely different context. These values derive from BS EN 61008/61009 product standard tables, which are not ingested into the RAG. The page claim cannot be verified from available RAG sources.

confirmed_issues.claim: Half-rated current test: RCD must NOT trip during a 2-second test period at 0.5×IΔn.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The PWI confirms the 0.5×IΔn non-trip test exists: primary_topic 'Verification that an RCD does not nuisance-trip at 0.5×IΔn: perform a non-trip test at half rated residual operating current.' GN3 and OSG facets also confirm 0.5×IΔn as a test point. However, the specific 2-second duration criterion for the non-trip period is absent from every returned facet across all queries. No bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence row contains a '2 s', '2-second', or 'two second' non-trip hold time for the half-rated test. This duration derives from BS EN 61008/61009 product standards not ingested in the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Ramp test acceptable range for 30mA RCD: trip must occur between 15mA and 30mA.
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) Ch4 Reg 4.6 confirms the ramp test: 'ramp current starts from less than 50% of the RCD rating' (i.e. below 15mA for a 30mA device) 'and increases to 110% of the rating' (i.e. to 33mA). The page states the acceptable trip range is 15mA–30mA (50%–100% of IΔn), but the GN3 ramp runs to 110% of the rating (33mA), not stopping at IΔn. No RAG facet states an explicit acceptance window of exactly 50%–100%; the GN3 records the ramp endpoint as 110% and describes only 'the current at which the RCD trips shall be recorded.' The specific 15–30mA acceptance window stated on the page is not confirmed by available RAG content and partially misrepresents the ramp endpoint.

confirmed_issues.claim: Type A RCDs/RCBOs detect pulsating DC residual currents and are required for circuits supplying EV chargers, washing machines, and PV inverters.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The pulsating DC detection capability of Type A is confirmed: OSG 3.6.4.5 states 'RCDs of Type A ... will operate in the presence of residual pulsating direct currents superimposed on a smooth direct current up to 6 mA' and GN3 Ch4 Reg 4.7 confirms Type A symbol for pulsating DC. However, no RAG facet specifically mandates Type A for EV chargers, washing machines, or PV inverters as a named list. A query combining 'Type A RCD 722 electric vehicle' returned zero results. BS 7671 531.3.3 contains a procedure for selecting Type A/F/B when DC components are present but names no specific appliance classes. The mandatory-for-those-three-loads claim is unverified from available RAG sources.

High-sensitivity 30 mA RCDs satisfy the additional protection requirement of Reg 415.1.' This prevents misleading qualified electricians and avoids contradicting the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: The mains supply circuit to the fire alarm panel is a dedicated final circuit from the consumer unit, fused at 6 A.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 314.4 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 3 · Chapter 31 · Reg 314.4') confirms each final circuit shall be connected to a separate way in a distribution board — this grounds the 'dedicated circuit' framing. However, no facet anywhere in bs7671_facets mentions a 6 A protective device rating for fire alarm circuits. Queries on '6 amp fuse MCB rating fire alarm' and 'fire alarm dedicated supply consumer unit' returned zero rows. The 6 A figure is a BS 5839-1 clause 15.2 recommendation, not a BS 7671 requirement. The page presents it without distinguishing BS 5839-1 from BS 7671, and the RAG provides no grounding for that specific numeric value — it is ungrounded in the available authoritative RAG and risks misleading readers into treating a BS 5839 recommendation as a wiring regulation requirement.

content_gaps.missing: Fire alarm circuit wiring requirements: the page's 'For Electricians' section never mentions that the zone wiring and sounder wiring must use fire-resistant cable (MICC, FP200, or equivalent per IEC 60331 / BS EN 50200) and that this is a BS 7671 Chapter 56 / Building Regulations requirement, not just a BS 5839 recommendation. Electricians inspecting fire alarm wiring on an EICR need this.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 560.8.1 (scope facet): recognised wiring systems for safety services in fire conditions — MICC (BS EN 60702), IEC 60331-series, BS EN 50200 cables; OSG Reg 7.4.1 segregation note.

content_gaps.missing: Circuit segregation / separation from general wiring: the page never tells electricians that fire alarm zone wiring must be physically separated from general installation wiring and from emergency lighting circuits. This is a mandatory OSG / BS 7671 requirement that repeatedly trips up electricians on EICRs and is a codeable C2/C3 observation.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) Reg 7.4.1: 'Fire alarm and emergency lighting circuits must be separated from other cables and from each other, in compliance with BS 5839 and BS 5266 respectively.'

content_gaps.missing: Safety services source independence (Reg 560.6.1/560.6.5): the page does not explain that the fire alarm power supply source must be one of the BS 7671-recognised types (secondary batteries, independent generating set, or effectively independent separate feeder) and that two mains feeders from the same distributor's network are not acceptable unless concurrent failure is considered unlikely. Relevant to larger commercial premises and EICR observations.

RCD omission for fire alarm circuits: BS 7671 permits — and in practice often requires — the fire alarm dedicated circuit to be omitted from RCD protection to prevent loss of the safety service on RCD trip. The page mentions the MCB but never addresses RCD protection status or the BS 7671 reasoning behind it. This is a frequent EICR query.
content_gaps.rag_source: Practical work intelligence facet: 'three-way split consumer unit... fire alarms... deliberately not RCD-protected to ensure continuity of essential services... common mistake: Not appending circuit risk assessment/documentation to EICR for omitted protection.'

confirmed_issues.claim: A single C1, C2, or FI observation makes the entire EICR Unsatisfactory; C3-only is Satisfactory.
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 3.11 [requirement] facet states explicitly: 'Where an installation defect or non-compliance has attracted a code of C1 or C2, the overall assessment shall be recorded as unsatisfactory.' A separate GN3 Reg 5.9 [condition] facet states: 'an advisory-coded item (C3/FI) shall not change the overall condition classification … except where an item is coded C1 or C2.' FI is therefore an advisory code that does NOT trigger Unsatisfactory. The page claim that a single FI alone produces an Unsatisfactory overall assessment is directly contradicted by the RAG. The C1/C2 and C3-only parts of the claim are correct, but the inclusion of FI as a trigger for Unsatisfactory is wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: Reg 411.3.1.2 cited for 'main bonding to water service absent' observation example.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 411.3.1.2 confirm it governs maximum disconnection times from Table 41.1 for socket-outlet circuits (≤63 A) and fixed-load circuits (≤32 A) — it has nothing to do with bonding. RAG facets for Reg 544.1.2 confirm it is the correct regulation: 'the main protective bonding connection to any extraneous-conductive-part such as gas, water or other metallic pipework or service shall be made as near as practicable to the point of entry of that part into the premises.' The page incorrectly cites 411.3.1.2 for a bonding deficiency that belongs under 544.1.2.

confirmed_issues.claim: Socket-outlet circuits rated up to 20 A in domestic premises must be RCD protected at 30 mA.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 411.3.3 (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026) confirm the threshold is socket-outlets rated not exceeding 32 A — not 20 A. Reg 830.3.201 facets confirm '30 mA shall be provided for all socket-outlets of rating not exceeding 32 A'. The page's '20 A' threshold is contradicted by A4:2026. Note also that Reg 411.3.4 (which the auditor cited as possibly relevant) covers luminaire circuits in domestic premises, not socket-outlets. The correct regulation for socket-outlets is 411.3.3 with a 32 A threshold.

confirmed_issues.claim: Reg 531.3.3 cited for RCD tripping at 22 mA (below 50% of rated current).
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 531.3.3 confirm it is about RCD Type AC selection: 'RCD Type AC shall only be used to serve fixed equipment where it is known that the load current contains no DC components.' It has no content about operating-current thresholds, the 50% rule, or nuisance tripping at sub-rated currents. The page incorrectly maps 531.3.3 to an RCD premature-trip observation. The correct source for the 50% nuisance-trip rule is IEC 61008/IEC 61009 product standards (not a BS 7671 regulation clause), or GN3 testing guidance — neither of which is Reg 531.3.3. The reg citation on the page is wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: Reg 514.14 cited for red/black wiring colour re-identification.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 514.14 state explicitly: 'Regulation 514.14 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, which previously concerned non-standard colours, has been deleted from the text of the Wiring Regulations.' A further facet states 'there is no current regulatory requirement at 514.14' and that practitioners 'shall not assume the deleted clause remains in force.' Citing a deleted regulation as a live requirement on an inspection guidance page is a confirmed error. Cable colour re-identification requirements must be sought under other surviving clauses of Section 514 (e.g. 514.4.4, Table 51) or GN3 guidance.

content_gaps.missing: The page does not mention A4:2026's new Reg 421.1.7 requiring AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Devices) on domestic socket and lighting circuits in new/rewired dwellings. This is a major A4 change that directly affects EICR observation coding — an older installation lacking AFDD may now warrant a C3 (or contextually C2) observation. Best-in-class content would address whether absence of AFDD should be coded C3 on an EICR.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets query for 'AFDD arc fault detection 421' returned no results, confirming this topic is not currently covered on the page. The A4:2026 change is noted in the project @bs7671-a4-2026.md memory.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the new A4:2026 requirement (Reg 411.3.4) for RCD protection on all domestic lighting circuits. Prior editions only required RCD on socket circuits; A4 extends this. An inspector finding a dwelling with unprotected lighting circuits post-A4 implementation needs guidance on whether to code C2 or C3.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets queries on RCD/socket/20A returned no facets, suggesting this specific A4 addition is not yet faceted or was not returned within budget. Gap confirmed by absence from page content.

content_gaps.missing: The page uses Reg 411.3.1.2 in the 'good observation' example for a main bonding deficiency. The correct regulation is 411.3.1.1 for main protective bonding requirements and 544.1.2 for the bonding conductor connection specifics. This incorrect citation, if copied by users into real EICRs, undermines report credibility.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD trips within 30 milliseconds (FAQ answer)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two RAG facets directly contradict the 30ms figure. BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 643.7.3.201 states: 'for general non-delay type, 300 ms maximum' when tested at rated residual operating current (An). OSG Reg 11.4 independently confirms: 'Verification of the 300 ms maximum trip time for a general non-delay RCD shall be carried out using an alternating current test at the rated residual operating current (Ign).' The 30ms figure on the page is a manufacturer-typical operate time under high fault current — it is not the BS 7671 maximum disconnection time. Presenting it as such misrepresents the standard and could mislead readers into thinking an RCD tripping at, say, 50ms is non-compliant.

confirmed_issues.claim: Part P notifiable only 'when the work is in a bathroom or shower room' (implies replacement elsewhere may not be notifiable)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two PWI facets directly address this. One states: 'Fitting and replacement of cookers and electric showers in domestic premises: when such work is not notifiable (like-for-like) and when it becomes notifiable (where a new circuit is needed).' A second confirms: 'Replacement or fitting of domestic cookers and electric showers: work is not notifiable where a like-for-like replacement is carried out and no new circuit is required; where a new circuit is required, the work becomes notifiable.' The page's blanket 'Yes — notifiable whenever in a bathroom' is an oversimplification. Notifiability turns on whether a new circuit is required, not solely on location. A like-for-like shower replacement connected to an existing dedicated circuit is non-notifiable even in a bathroom. The page's framing would cause readers to notify unnecessarily for simple replacements.

top_improvements.action: Correct the '30 milliseconds' RCD trip claim in the FAQ. The BS 7671 maximum disconnection time for a 30mA RCD is 300ms (general purpose) per Table 41.1 — not 30ms. Using '30ms' conflates trip time with response time and is technically incorrect. Replace with: 'the RCD detects the leakage current and disconnects within 300 milliseconds (the maximum permitted under BS 7671 Table 41.1; in practice most RCDs trip faster).'
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Table 41.1 — RCD maximum disconnection time; Reg 531.3.5.4.2 per-circuit RCD requirement

top_improvements.action: Add Reg 701.411.3.3 citation to the RCD tripping section and key takeaways. State explicitly that BS 7671 Reg 701.411.3.3 requires 30mA RCD protection for all LV circuits in rooms containing a bath or shower, and that Reg 531.3.5.4.2 mandates a dedicated RCD per shower circuit. This grounds the regulatory claim and improves E-E-A-T.

Correct and nuance the Part P FAQ answer. A like-for-like shower replacement connected to an existing isolator is likely non-notifiable; it becomes notifiable when a new circuit up to the isolator is required. The current blanket 'Yes — notifiable' is an oversimplification that contradicts the practical_work_intelligence guidance and may create trust issues with trade readers who know the nuance.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [practical_work_intelligence] — 'connecting to existing isolator … non-notifiable; new circuit = notifiable'

confirmed_issues.claim: Main protective bonding cables connect gas and water pipes to the main earthing terminal — required by BS 7671 Regulation 411.3.1.2
confirmed_issues.evidence: Eight RAG facets for Reg 411.3.1.2 unanimously confirm it covers only maximum disconnection times in Table 41.1 for (i) socket-outlet final circuits ≤63 A and (ii) fixed-load final circuits ≤32 A. It has nothing to do with main protective bonding. The bonding of extraneous-conductive-parts (gas, water pipes) to the MET is governed by Reg 411.3.1 and Reg 411.3.1.1. Facet context_prefix '[BS 7671] · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.1.1 · [example] · Examples of extraneous-conductive-parts for bonding (water, gas, ducting)' confirms gas and water pipes are bonded under 411.3.1.1, not 411.3.1.2. The page's regulation citation is wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: Rubber wiring is typically over 60 years old and a serious fire and shock risk due to cracking insulation
confirmed_issues.evidence: practical_work_intelligence RAG explicitly classifies deteriorated vulcanised rubber insulation as Code C3: primary_topic states 'Inspection and replacement procedure for deteriorated crumbling vulcanised rubber insulation (Code C3) on older wiring systems.' C3 = 'Improvement recommended' — the lowest severity band, not requiring urgent action. The page describing rubber wiring as a 'serious fire and shock risk' implies C1/C2 urgency that contradicts the RAG's C3 classification. No '60 years' age threshold appears in any facet. The severity framing is overstated relative to RAG evidence.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 bonding exemption (Reg 133.1.3): bonding of metallic service pipes may be omitted where an insulating section is provided at the building's point of entry. The page presents main bonding as unconditionally required, which is now out of date under A4:2026 and could mislead a buyer checking an existing installation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Chapter 13 · Reg 133.1.3 · [note] · Bonding exemption highlighted among main Section 411 changes

content_gaps.missing: Correct regulation number for main protective bonding of gas/water services. The page cites Reg 411.3.1.2, which is the disconnection-times regulation. A factual correction is essential.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 (EAWR) as the primary legal basis for safe electrical working — the page cites CDM 2015 and Management Regulations but omits EAWR entirely. EAWR Reg 4 (systems, work activities and protective equipment), Reg 13 (precautions for work on equipment made dead), and Reg 14 (work on or near live conductors) are the direct statutory obligations for every electrical RAMS. Regulation 16 (competence) is grounded in GN3 Reg 4.9 and BS 7671 Reg 710.1.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 4.9 / [BS 7671] Reg 710.1: 'Regulation 16 of the Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 requires persons to be competent to prevent danger'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of HSE HSG85 'Electricity at Work: Safe Working Practices' — the principal HSE guidance document that defines safe isolation procedure, permit-to-work, and live working justification. OSG Reg 12.5 explicitly cites HSG85 as the authoritative UK reference for safe working on electrical systems.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 12.5: 'UK requirements for working safely on electrical systems can be found in HSG85 Electricity at Work: Safe Working Practices, available as a free download from the HSE website'

content_gaps.missing: No mention that DC-side conductors (e.g., Solar PV installations) remain energised even when the AC supply is isolated — a critical RAMS consideration for any PV work. BS 7671 Reg 712.410.101 mandates treating DC-side as live for safe system of work purposes.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.410.101: 'DC-side conductors, terminals and equipment shall be treated as live for purposes of isolation, labelling, safe system of work, and use of PPE and tools'

content_gaps.missing: No mention of isolation requirements for distribution boards under BS 7671 Reg 730.537.2.1 — specifically verifying that the isolation device disconnects ALL live conductors before work begins. This is a common RAMS gap that HSE inspectors look for.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 709.512.2: socket outlets must comply with BS EN 60309-2 (CEE industrial sockets). Standard 13A domestic sockets must not be used for boat supplies.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms that the BS EN 60309-2 socket-outlet requirement is in Reg 709.553.1.8, not 709.512.2. Reg 709.553.1.8 states: 'Socket-outlets with a rated current up to 63 A shall comply with BS EN IEC 60309-2' (context_prefix: [BS 7671] Reg 709.553.1.8 [requirement]). Regs 709.512.2.1.3 and 709.512.2.1.4 cover corrosion/AF and mechanical impact/AG for equipment on pontoons — neither sub-clause addresses socket type or BS EN 60309-2. The page incorrectly cites 709.512.2 for the socket-type requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 709.512.2: equipment installed on jetties and pontoons must have a minimum protection of IP44. Equipment exposed to wave splash requires IP56 or higher.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG shows two separate issues. First, the IP rating requirements for equipment on jetties/pontoons come from Reg 709.512.2.1.1 (water presence AD), not 709.512.2 generically, and from 709.553.1.8 for sockets specifically. 709.553.1.8 mandates IP44 minimum for every socket-outlet. 709.512.2.1.1 mandates IPX4 for splashes (AD4), IPXS for jets (ADS), and IPX6 for waves (AD6). Second, the page states 'IP56' for wave splash, but the RAG text consistently uses 'IPX6' — the first digit is unspecified ('X') per 709.512.2.1.1, not '5'. 'IP56' is a different and incorrect rating. The regulation attribution for the IP44 floor belongs to 709.553.1.8 (socket-outlets), not 709.512.2.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 709.553.1.11 — one socket outlet shall supply only one pleasure craft or houseboat. This is a distinct mandatory requirement that the page conflates with the individual RCD rule but never cites explicitly.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 709.553.1.11: 'One socket-outlet shall supply only one pleasure craft or houseboat. This is a mandatory prohibition on connecting more than one craft/houseboat to a single socket-outlet.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 709.312.2.1.3 / AF2 and AF3 atmospheric corrosion classifications — the page covers corrosion resistance in practical terms (GRP, stainless steel) but never references the specific BS 7671 Section 709 environmental classification requirements.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 709.512.2.1.3: 'Equipment installed on or above a jetty, wharf, pier or pontoon shall be suitable for use in the presence of atmospheric corrosive or polluting substances (AF2)... if hydrocarbons present, AF3 applicable.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 709.313.1.101 — where a fixed on-shore isolation transformer is used to prevent galvanic currents, the supply PE shall NOT be connected to the socket-outlet earth on the vessel side. The page mentions isolation transformers briefly in the ESD FAQ but does not cover this mandatory prohibition or its testing requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 709.313.1.101 [requirement]: 'The protective conductor (PE) of the supply to the isolating transformer shall not be connected to the earth terminal in the socket-outlet supplying the vessel.' [test]: 'Carry out a continuity test between incoming supply PE and earth terminal of socket-outlet... shall show open circuit.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 709.512.2.1.4 mechanical impact protection (AG2) — equipment on pontoons must be protected against medium-severity mechanical impact. Not mentioned on the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 709.512.2.1.4: 'Equipment installed on or above a jetty, wharf, pier or pontoon shall be protected against mechanical damage (impact of medium severity AG2).'

confirmed_issues.claim: The IET On-Site Guide notes that all electrical work within dwellings includes work that is notifiable under Building Regulations Part P — the page omits the qualifier 'some', suggesting all domestic work is notifiable.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two OSG facets returned verbatim text: '[OSG] All electrical work within dwellings some is notifiable — Part P note' and '[OSG] Dwellings guidance includes some notifiable work under Part P — condition'. Both explicitly preserve the qualifier 'of which some is notifiable', and the second facet states installers must 'determine whether the specific work (e.g., new circuits, consumer unit alterations) is notifiable'. The page dropping 'some' makes the false implication that all domestic work is notifiable, which contradicts the OSG facets directly.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 2 to BS 7671 (2022) significantly strengthened the overvoltage protection requirements by introducing a risk assessment process (Regulation 443.4)
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG editorial note (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [note] · Chapter 44 and Section 443 redraft') states: 'Chapter 44 and Section 443 have been redrafted. AQ criteria for lightning are no longer included; instead protection is required where consequence caused by overvoltage indicates need. See Regulation 443.4 and Regulation 422.2 for details.' The redraft is attributed to A4:2026, not Amendment 2 (2022). Additionally, the primary operative sub-regulation carrying the opt-out/owner-declaration mechanism is Reg 443.4.1 (confirmed by multiple facets under that context_prefix), not simply 443.4, making the page's reg citation imprecise.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 443.4 sets out the circumstances under which SPDs must be provided — mandatory where risk to human life, mandatory for public services/heritage, risk assessment for all other installations — the page omits criteria (c) and (d)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets under 'Reg 443.4' explicitly list four mandatory criteria: (a) serious injury/loss of life; (b) public services or cultural heritage; (c) 'interruption of commercial or industrial activity' (facet: 'Transient overvoltage protection where commercial or industrial activity interrupted'); (d) 'large number of co-located individuals' (facet: 'Transient overvoltage protection where large number of co-located individuals affected'). The page presents only (a) and (b), omitting (c) and (d), which is a material omission from the mandatory list.

confirmed_issues.claim: For all other installations, a risk assessment shall be carried out; if the cost of damage exceeds the cost of SPDs, SPDs shall be installed
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Reg 443.4.1 facet (condition): 'For all other cases not covered by (a), (b) or (c), protection against transient overvoltages shall be provided unless the owner of the installation declares it is not required.' A second facet states: 'Protection against transient overvoltages need not be provided in non-critical cases if the owner of the installation declares it is not required due to any loss or damage being tolerable and they accept the risk.' The RAG establishes that the default is SPDs required, with omission requiring a formal owner declaration — not a cost/benefit risk assessment as the page states. The page inverts the legal default entirely.

confirmed_issues.claim: Owner declaration mechanism — not mentioned anywhere on the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets confirm the owner declaration is the operative compliance mechanism. Reg 443.4.1 [condition]: 'Protection against transient overvoltages need not be provided in non-critical cases if the owner of the installation declares it is not required due to any loss or damage being tolerable and they accept the risk of damage to equipment and any consequential loss.' Reg 443.4.1 [procedure] recordkeeping: 'When an owner declares that transient overvoltage protection is not required, the declaration should be recorded and retained with installation documentation.' GN3 facet (Reg 2.8): 'The owner of the installation may declare that the risk of any loss or damage resulting from transient overvoltages is tolerable; where such a declaration is made the requirement for protection is not applied.' Its complete absence from the page is a material misrepresentation of how SPD omission is legally achieved.

confirmed_issues.claim: The risk assessment is mandatory even if SPDs are not installed — and the page conflates AQ-based risk assessment with the A4:2026 owner-declaration mechanism
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG Reg 443.4 [requirement] facet does include 'For all other cases not covered by the specific criteria in Regulations 443.4(a)–(d), a risk assessment has to be performed in order to determine if protection against transient overvoltage is required.' However, the Reg 443.4.1 facets make clear that the operative opt-out route is an owner declaration, not a cost-benefit risk assessment. The AQ criteria note (Reg 422.2.1 context) confirms: 'AQ criteria for lightning are no longer included.' The page's description of the risk assessment in terms of lightning density and overhead vs underground supply factors reflects the old AQ-based approach removed in A4:2026, and the page does not mention the owner-declaration route at all. This is a conflation of two distinct mechanisms from two different editions.

confirmed_issues.claim: SPD installation time: approximately 30 to 60 minutes
confirmed_issues.evidence: The practical_work_intelligence data shows: Type 2 SPD installation at consumer unit = 90 minutes; Type 1 SPD installation = 60 minutes; tail/CSA work = 45 minutes. The 30-minute entries in the RAG are for documentation/labelling tasks, combined-type inspection/identification tasks, and commissioning checks — not for a full SPD installation. No PWI row gives 30 minutes for an actual installation task. The RAG consensus for a standard Type 2 SPD installation is 60–90 minutes, making the lower bound of 30 minutes unsupported and likely to mislead.

confirmed_issues.claim: AQ criteria (lightning density, supply type, equipment value) determine SPD need via risk assessment
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG editorial note (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [note] · Chapter 44 and Section 443 redraft'): 'AQ criteria for lightning are no longer included; instead protection is required where consequence caused by overvoltage indicates need.' A second facet (Reg 422.2.1 [note]): 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 no longer includes the AQ criteria (previously used conditions of external influence for lightning) as part of the decision process for determining the need for protection against transient overvoltages. The AQ approach has been removed from the prescriptive checklist in Section 443.' The page's description of lightning density and overhead/underground supply type as risk-assessment factors explicitly reflects the pre-A4:2026 AQ approach which the standard has removed.

content_gaps.missing: Owner declaration mechanism (Reg 443.4.1): the opt-out from SPD installation in non-critical 'other cases' does not require a risk assessment — it requires the owner to issue a written declaration that loss is tolerable and they accept the risk. This is the legally operative mechanism for domestic SPD omission under A4:2026 and is completely absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 44 · Reg 443.4.1 · [condition]: 'Protection against transient overvoltages need not be provided in non-critical cases if the owner of the installation declares it is not required due to any loss or damage being tolerable and they accept the risk of damage to equipment and any consequential loss.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 443.4 criteria (c) and (d): the page omits two of the four mandatory-SPD categories — (c) interruption of commercial or industrial activity, and (d) large number of co-located individuals affected. These are mandatory (not risk-assessment) triggers and are material for commercial/industrial work.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 44 · Reg 443.4 · [requirement] · 'Where transient overvoltage could result in interruption of commercial or industrial activity, protection shall be provided (c)' and '...affect a large number of co-located individuals (d)'.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 443.4.2 — switching overvoltages / internally generated disturbances: the page covers atmospheric origin but does not address Reg 443.4.2's separate requirement to consider SPDs where equipment in the installation is likely to produce switching overvoltages (e.g. VFDs, large motors, power factor correction).
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [condition] · 443.4.2: 'Regulation 443.4.2 requires that protection against overvoltages shall be considered in the case of equipment likely to produce switching overvoltages or disturbances.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the regulatory framework: replace all references to 'Amendment 2 (2022)' with 'A4:2026' and re-number the operative regulation as '443.4.1' (not '443.4') throughout. Add a dedicated callout box explaining the owner declaration process — what it must say, who signs it, and how it is retained with the EIC.

top_improvements.action: Add the two missing mandatory-SPD categories to the Reg 443.4 section: (c) installations where transient overvoltage could interrupt commercial or industrial activity, and (d) installations affecting a large number of co-located individuals. These are 'shall provide' requirements (no risk assessment needed) and are directly relevant to the commercial/industrial electrician audience.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 443.4 · [requirement]: 'Where transient overvoltage could result in interruption of commercial or industrial activity, protection shall be provided (c)' and '...affect a large number of co-located individuals (d)'.

top_improvements.action: Add a section or callout on Reg 443.4.2 — switching overvoltages generated internally: installations with variable-frequency drives, large motors, or power factor correction capacitors must have SPD need assessed under this separate limb. top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [condition] · 443.4.2: 'Protection against overvoltages shall be considered in the case of equipment likely to produce switching overvoltages or disturbances.'

top_improvements.action: Add a paragraph on overvoltage categories (Table 443.2) and the Reg 534.4.8 Vp recommendation: explain that equipment is classified into Categories I–IV with corresponding rated impulse voltages, and that a selected SPD's voltage protection level should not exceed 80% of the equipment's rated impulse voltage for Category II. This turns the vague '1500V / 800V' statements into technically grounded guidance.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 534.4.8 · [acceptance_criterion]: 'SPD Vp recommended not to exceed 80% of the equipment's required rated impulse voltage per Table 443.2, overvoltage category II.'

confirmed_issues.claim: If the difference [between live and neutral] exceeds 30 mA, the RCD disconnects in under 40 milliseconds.
confirmed_issues.evidence: OSG 11.3 (OSG 9th Ed:2022 A4) explicitly states: 'With a residual current flowing equivalent to 100% of the rated tripping current of the RCD, the device shall open in less than 300 ms unless it is Type S (selective).' No facet in bs7671_facets contains any 40 ms figure for RCD operation at IΔn. The 40 ms value is the BS EN 61008 product-standard maximum at 5×IΔn — not at 1×IΔn (30 mA for a 30 mA device). The page's claim that the RCD trips 'in under 40 milliseconds' when the imbalance 'exceeds 30 mA' directly contradicts the RAG-confirmed <300 ms limit at IΔn (OSG context_prefix: '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 11 · Reg 11.3 · [requirement] · Trip at 100% rated residual current within 300 ms'). The correct statement for a page aimed at homeowners should be that a general-purpose RCD trips in under 300 ms at its rated tripping current; the 40 ms figure belongs only to the 5×IΔn (150 mA) test level, which the page does not describe.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) requirements under A4:2026 (Reg 421.1.7). Arcing faults are a leading cause of house fires and a significant reason a circuit may trip intermittently — the page's 'when to call an electrician' section and the 'proper fixes' list would be strengthened by noting that new consumer unit installations should include AFDDs per current best practice and A4:2026 guidance. This is a direct gap given the page's topic (fault current, arcing, cable damage).
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets query for AFDD/arc fault returned no rows — indicates this content area may not be indexed, but the A4:2026 update note in MEMORY explicitly lists 'AFDD 421.1.7' as a new A4:2026 addition.

top_improvements.action: Add MCB trip curve types (B/C/D) to the 'Causes of MCB Tripping' section. Explain that domestic Type B MCBs trip instantaneously at 3–5× rated current (96–160A for a 32A MCB) — this explains both the 'bang and flash' on short circuit and the nuisance-trip risk from motor-start inrush on some appliances. Remove or caveat the unverified specific trip times ('60 seconds at 40A') until they are sourced from IEC 60898 time-current curves.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: 'thermomagnetic trip and bimetallic overload behaviour during commissioning' — confirms trip-curve knowledge is in scope for MCB fault diagnosis.

top_improvements.action: Add a paragraph in 'Proper Fixes' about AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) installation. When upgrading a consumer unit that repeatedly trips due to suspected cable damage or loose connections, A4:2026 best practice is to fit AFDDs — they detect arcing before it becomes a full short circuit and MCB trip. This is the single biggest A4:2026 content gap on a page about repeated MCB tripping.
top_improvements.grounded_in: MEMORY: A4:2026 addition 'AFDD 421.1.7' — new requirement for certain dwelling circuits; directly relevant to the page's 'install additional circuits / upgrade consumer unit' recommendation.

top_improvements.action: Add E-E-A-T author attribution. The page has no 'Reviewed by a qualified electrician' or equivalent signal. Adding a byline with a NICEIC/NAPIT registered electrician's name and credential — even a single sentence — is the fastest SEO quality signal improvement available, particularly for YMYL electrical safety content.
top_improvements.grounded_in: SEO audit: author_eeat = false; Google's Quality Rater Guidelines weight expertise signals heavily for electrical safety pages.

top_improvements.action: Add a note in the 'MCB vs RCD' section about Type A vs Type AC RCDs. Modern domestic appliances (washing machines, dishwashers, EV chargers) produce DC pulsed leakage that Type AC devices cannot detect reliably — per A4:2026 guidance Type A or F RCDs are required. This is directly relevant to the 'RCD trips when you turn on the dishwasher' FAQ scenario and would add technical differentiation.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets Reg 531.3.1.202 — RCD accumulation/leakage current requirements context; A4:2026 Type A requirement noted in MEMORY.

top_improvements.action: In the insulation resistance section (appliance vs wiring), add that per BS 7671 Table 64 / Reg 643.3.2 the acceptance criterion is 1.0 MΩ minimum at 500 V DC for circuits up to 500 V — and note that GN3 recommends applying a 0.80 site correction factor to Zs values from live loop impedance tests. This upgrades the professional-facing content and grounds the numeric claims in a cited regulation.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets Reg 643.3.2 / Table 64: 'Circuits up to and including 500 V = 1.0 MΩ (at 500 V DC)' — confirmed grounded.

confirmed_issues.claim: Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 Reg 3 requires systems to be constructed and maintained to prevent danger; Reg 4 requires work to be carried out safely; Reg 14 addresses live working
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG consistently and repeatedly attributes the system construction/maintenance duty to EAWR Reg 4(2), not Reg 3. Multiple GN3 facets (context_prefix: '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 3 · Reg 3.1 · [reference] · Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 Regulation 4(2)') state: 'Regulation 4(2) of the Electricity at Work Regulations (EAWR) 1989 is cited in GN3 as requiring that systems shall be maintained as may be necessary to prevent danger.' A further facet (GN3 Reg 3.5) explicitly cites 'Regulation 4(2) EAWR duty on safe condition of systems' for the construction/maintenance obligation. Reg 14 (live working) is correctly grounded by GN3 Reg 4.9: 'Regulation 14 EAWR quoted for work on or near live conductors.' The page's attribution of the system-construction/maintenance duty to Reg 3 is not supported by any RAG facet — the RAG places this duty in Reg 4(2). The description 'Reg 4 requires work to be carried out safely' is also a simplification; the RAG grounds Reg 4(2) as the maintenance/safe-condition duty, not a general 'work carried out safely' duty. The Reg 3/Reg 4 attribution on the page is inverted relative to what the RAG sources show.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Reg 421.1.7) — BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 introduced a recommendation to install arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) on AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk from arc fault currents. The page discusses arc flash causes and prevention but never mentions AFDDs, which are the primary BS 7671 technical response to arc faults in fixed installations. This is the single most relevant A4:2026 addition to this topic.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 — 'recommending the installation of AFDDs... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' (advisory wording confirmed: 'recommending', not 'shall')

content_gaps.missing: Distinction between arc flash (worker safety hazard, IEEE 1584 / EaWR domain) and arc fault fire risk (fixed installation domain, BS 7671 Reg 421.1.7 / AFDD). The page conflates these: AFDD is designed to detect series arc faults in wiring and prevent fire, whereas arc flash PPE addresses the explosive energy release at switchgear. A best-in-class page would clarify both dimensions.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 context_prefix confirms AFDD purpose is fire mitigation from arc fault currents in AC final circuits, not switchgear blast protection

content_gaps.missing: Prospective fault current measurement and recording as a BS 7671 duty (Reg 643.7.3.201) — the page advises obtaining PSCC from the DNO or loop impedance measurements but does not note that BS 7671 Part 6 mandates PSCC measurement at relevant points as part of initial verification. Connecting the arc flash risk assessment step to this legal inspection duty would add depth.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.7.3.201 — 'The prospective short-circuit current and prospective earth fault current shall be measured, calculated or determined at other relevant points in the installation in addition to the origin.'

content_gaps.missing: Short-circuit rating of protective devices (Reg 551.2.2) — the page's risk assessment section advises checking the upstream protective device clearing time but does not mention that BS 7671 Reg 551.2.2 requires the device's short-circuit rating to not be exceeded by the assessed PSC. Citing this reg would ground the engineering controls section in a specific BS 7671 requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 551.2.2 — 'The short-circuit rating of protective devices within the installation shall not be exceeded for any of the intended methods of operation of the sources.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.1.2 requires connection of metallic casings of heat exchangers and heat pumps to the main bonding
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG (bs7671_facets) returns six facets for Reg 411.3.1.2, all consistently stating it establishes which final circuits Table 41.1 disconnection times apply to: socket-outlet circuits rated up to 63A and fixed-load circuits up to 32A. There is no mention of heat pump casings, metallic casings, or bonding in any 411.3.1.2 facet. The heat pump/central heating bonding requirement sits under Reg 411.3.1.1 and the extraneous-conductive-parts definition facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [example] · Examples of extraneous-conductive-parts for bonding'), which lists 'central heating and air conditioning systems' and attributes the bonding obligation to Reg 411.3.1.1. The page cites the wrong regulation number for this requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 542.1.3 covers earthing arrangements for heat pumps including protective bonding, RCD selection, avoidance of nuisance trips, and verification of earth continuity
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns six facets for Reg 542.1.3.3. All describe an equipotential bonding network (CBN) for communications/IT environments: bonding metal sheaths/screens/armouring of cables to the CBN, limiting fault current through signal/data cable screens, and a note about using optical/radio links where earth interconnection is impracticable. No facet mentions heat pumps, RCD selection, nuisance trips, or heat pump earth continuity. The description on the page does not match the actual content of this regulation in the A4:2026 RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 704.537 covers local service isolators for heating circuits and heat pumps to permit safe servicing without isolating unrelated circuits
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns six facets for Reg 704.537. All describe it as a cross-reference between Chapter 70 provisions and Section 706 'Conducting locations with restricted movement' — a section about locations where persons have restricted movement and conducting parts are present. The context_prefix explicitly reads '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 — Special Installations or Locations · Chapter 70 · Reg 704.537 · [reference] · Regulation 704.537 cross-reference to Section 706'. No facet mentions local isolators, heating circuits, or heat pumps. The page description does not match this regulation.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 708.312 covers earthing, bonding, and protective device requirements for heat pumps, addressing pipework bonding and segregation
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns zero rows for Reg 708.312. The Section 708 facets that do exist (708.410.3.6, 708.512.1.2) cover caravan/camping parks and similar locations — socket-outlet protection, IP ratings, and solid foreign body ingress. There is no 708.312 facet in the A4:2026 RAG whatsoever, and the section context (marinas/camping parks) is wholly inapplicable to heat pumps. The regulation reference appears to be fabricated or misattributed.

content_gaps.missing: The correct BS 7671 regulation for heat pump bonding is Reg 411.3.1.1 (not 411.3.1.2). The page cites the wrong regulation number in two places (key takeaway and regulations section). 411.3.1.1 is the extraneous-conductive-parts bonding requirement that actually covers heat pump metalwork.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.1.1 — extraneous-conductive-parts liable to introduce a dangerous potential difference shall be connected to the MET; central heating and air conditioning systems explicitly listed.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of A4:2026 RCD requirement for domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4 in A4). Where GSHP installations involve new circuits in dwellings, the new A4 lighting RCD requirement applies and is a common inspection/certification issue.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets keyword search for 'RCD domestic lighting 411.3.4' returned no results — absence suggests this A4:2026 addition is not covered in the page or confirmed in current RAG facets; should be investigated and added.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) Reg 421.1.7 introduced in A4:2026. New dwellings and rewires including GSHP supply upgrades now trigger AFDD requirements in certain installation types. Page claims A4:2026 compliance but omits this A4 addition entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets AFDD query returned no results — confirms the page omits this A4:2026 requirement which should appear in any A4:2026-compliant guide.

content_gaps.missing: No coverage of the 0.8 site-test derating factor (GN3) for installation verification. When verifying Zs on GSHP circuits (particularly long three-phase runs to plant rooms), GN3 requires applying the 0.80 factor to measured Zs before comparing to tabulated limits.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 document_type facets expected to cover site-test verification factor — not referenced anywhere on this page.

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR produced in accordance with BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Section 631)
confirmed_issues.evidence: A direct search for context_prefix ilike '%631%' returned zero BS 7671 Part 6 Chapter 63 facets — only PAS 63100 refs. A follow-up search for context_prefix ilike '%Chapter 63%' also returned empty. The RAG places the EICR definition in GN3 Chapter 3 (Reg 3.11: 'The Electrical Installation Condition Report is the principal document produced on completion of periodic inspection and testing') and the testing requirements in BS 7671 Chapter 64 (Reg 643.x series). 'Section 631' does not map to any facet in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The correct standard reference is Part 6 / Chapter 64, not Section 631. The reg cite on the page is unverifiable and likely stale 17th-edition numbering.

confirmed_issues.claim: Absent RCD protection on socket circuits is most common C2 finding under Regulation 411.3.3 (socket circuits not exceeding 20A)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 411.3.3 is well-grounded in the RAG with multiple A4:2026 facets (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3'). Every facet consistently states the A4:2026 threshold is 32 A, not 20 A: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A'; 'Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been revised and now applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A.' The page's '20A' threshold is the pre-A4 (18th edition) wording. A4:2026 expanded coverage to ≤ 32 A. The reg number cite is correct; the ampere threshold cited on the page is wrong for A4:2026.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 addition): domestic lighting circuits now require RCD protection ≤30 mA — a mandatory new requirement. This is directly relevant to Oxford's Victorian HMO stock and is absent from the page entirely. A C2 finding for absent RCD on lighting circuits in domestic premises is now codified.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 Part 4 Chapter 41 Reg 411.3.4 — 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA' — mandatory for domestic premises.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026): recommendation to fit AFDDs on socket-outlet circuits — directly relevant to HMO and older-wiring EICR findings/recommendations. The page makes no mention of AFDDs or their role as a C3/recommendation in EICRs on older properties.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 Part 4 Chapter 42 Reg 421.1.7 — 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)' confirmed by multiple facets.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the RCBO's rated residual operating current (IΔn = 30 mA for additional protection circuits) or the GN3-specified test current values (½IΔn non-operation, IΔn must trip, 5IΔn must trip within 40 ms). These are the quantitative checks an electrician applies to confirm the device itself is not the fault.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 2.36: 'tester shall apply required test currents (e.g. 30 mA and higher percentages) and measure trip time and/or current to verify the RCBO operates within expected parameters.'

content_gaps.missing: No guidance on how to distinguish an earth-leakage trip from an overcurrent trip — the trip pattern (does it trip immediately on energising vs. under load vs. randomly) narrows whether to run insulation resistance tests or load calculations. The GN3 subdivision method for isolating low-IR sections is absent.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 2.21: 'isolating logical sections and performing insulation resistance tests on each component part to pinpoint sections responsible for low insulation resistance.'

content_gaps.missing: No reference to AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) under A4:2026. When a tripping pattern suggests arcing (intermittent, no clear overload or leakage), the A4:2026 AFDD requirements (Reg 531.3.6, Reg 421.1.7) are relevant — especially in domestic installations where AFDDs may now be required or recommended.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS7671 Reg 531.3.6: 'arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) shall be installed at the origin of final circuits to be protected when specified.' OSG 3.1: consumer unit may include AFDDs for fire-risk reduction from arcing.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of Reg 522.6.203/522.6.204(d) mechanical protection zones — when a cable is found damaged by a nail or screw, the remedial is not just re-termination but also ensuring the cable is re-routed or re-protected to comply. The page suggests 'repair the damaged cable' without noting the regulatory protection obligation.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS7671 Reg 522.6.204(d): 'mechanical protection shall be sufficient to prevent penetration of the cable by nails, screws and the like.' GN3 Reg 2.5.1 flags this as a condition triggering additional RCD/protection requirements.

seo.notes: The page does NOT lead with a direct answer block under the H1 — the heroSubtitle is a topic description, not a concise answer to 'why does an RCBO keep tripping'. The first visible content section ('The faults electricians see most often') is a bullet list, which is better than a wall of text but still not a one-paragraph answer-first block. Internal links: 4 confirmed (/tools/eicr-certificate appears twice — once inline, once in relatedPages; /guides/consumer-unit-upgrade once; /guides/rcbo-keeps-tripping-guide, /guides/rcd-keeps-tripping-causes-and-fixes, /guides/eicr-limitations in relatedPages — total 5 distinct destinations). No author attribution or E-E-A-T signal (no byline, no 'reviewed by a qualified electrician', no qualification cited). Title tag is 70 chars and includes the primary keyword. Meta description is accurate but generic — no numeric hook or differentiator.

top_improvements.action: Add an answer-first paragraph immediately under the H1/heroSubtitle that directly states the three trip types: overload (too much current for the circuit rating), short-circuit (dead fault between conductors), and earth leakage (current taking an unintended path to earth above the RCBO's IΔn threshold, typically 30 mA on domestic circuits). This is the single highest SEO and UX fix — Google's featured snippet eligibility depends on a direct answer block at the top.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS7671 Reg 531.3.2 (RCBO provides overcurrent + residual-current protection); Reg 536.4.5 NOTE 3 (RCBO as overload device); GN3 Reg 2.36 (30 mA IΔn test current reference)

top_improvements.action: Add a 'How to confirm whether it is overload, short-circuit, or earth leakage' diagnostic block with the GN3 subdivision approach: (1) remove all loads, reset; if it holds, reconnect one by one (load fault); (2) if it trips with no loads, run IR test between live conductors and earth (cable/accessory fault). Include the GN3 principle that IR test on subdivided sections pinpoints the low-resistance section before any dismantling.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 2.21: 'isolating logical sections and performing insulation resistance tests on each component part to pinpoint sections responsible for low insulation resistance.'

top_improvements.action: Add a warning callout: 'Do not swap the RCBO before testing downstream loads and running an insulation resistance test — the device is rarely the fault.' This directly addresses the top documented common mistake in the RAG and differentiates the guide from generic consumer advice.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence common_mistakes: 'replacing RCBO before isolating and testing appliances'; 'assuming RCBO is faulty without measuring leakage.'

top_improvements.action: When mentioning cable damage by nails/screws, add a sentence that any cable repair must restore mechanical protection to Reg 522.6.204(d) standard (conduit, capping, or re-routing outside the 'safe zone') — not just re-jointing the conductors. This turns a practical tip into a compliant remedial recommendation.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS7671 Reg 522.6.204(d): 'mechanical protection sufficient to prevent penetration of the cable by nails, screws and the like.' GN3 Reg 2.5.1 flags absence of this protection as a condition requiring additional RCD protection.

top_improvements.action: Add a short FAQ or callout on AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) under A4:2026: if the RCBO trips intermittently with no clear overload or leakage found, arcing may be the cause — and A4:2026 Reg 531.3.6 now requires AFDDs at the origin of specified circuits. This is a timely, search-valuable addition that no competitor content currently covers well.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS7671 Reg 531.3.6: 'AFDDs shall be installed at the origin of final circuits when specified.' OSG 3.1: consumer unit may include AFDDs for fire-risk reduction from arcing.

confirmed_issues.claim: Sauna interior divided into Zone 1 (above/around the heater) and Zone 2 (the rest of the room) — two zones only
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG unambiguously defines THREE zones. Reg 703.32.3 (context_prefix: 'Reg 703.32.3 · [definition] · Definition of sauna zone 1 volume relative to heater') defines Zone 1 as the volume containing the heater limited by the floor, ceiling insulation cold side, and a 0.5m radial vertical surface. Reg 703.32.2 (context_prefix: 'Reg 703.32.2 · Description of · [definition] · Definition of bathroom Zone 2 volume') defines Zone 2 as outside Zone 1, floor to a 1.0m horizontal plane, lateral limit = cold side of wall insulation. Reg 703.32.3 also defines Zone 3 as outside Zone 1, above the 1.0m horizontal plane up to the cold side of ceiling and wall insulation (context_prefix: 'Reg 703.32.2 · Description of · [definition] · Definition of bathroom Zone 3 volume'). A separate facet explicitly states: 'Zones 1, 2 and 3 define volumes (three-dimensional spaces) around the sauna heater bounded by floor, walls, ceiling insulation and horizontal or vertical surfaces.' The page collapses the scheme to two zones and omits Zone 3 entirely — a clear contradiction of the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Zone 1 is 'the area directly above and within a horizontal distance of the heater' — no 0.5m radial boundary stated
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 703.32.3 is directly present in the RAG with two matching facets: 'Zone 1 is the volume containing the sauna heater, limited by the floor, the cold side of the thermal insulation of the ceiling and a vertical surface circumscribing the sauna heater at a distance 0.5 m from the surface of the heater.' (context_prefix: 'Reg 703.32.3 · [definition] · Definition of sauna zone 1 volume relative to heater') and 'A vertical surface circumscribing the sauna heater at a distance 0.5 m from the surface of the heater shall be used to limit zone 1.' (context_prefix: 'Reg 703.32.3 · [requirement] · 0.5 m radial boundary for sauna heater zone 1'). The page's description contains neither the 0.5m figure nor the circumscribing-surface concept, making it materially incomplete.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD protection mandatory under BS 7671 Regulation 411.3.3; 30mA RCBO standard approach — no exception for the heater itself
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms the correct Part 7 regulation is 703.411.3.3, not generic 411.3.3. More critically, Reg 703.411.3.3 contains an explicit exception confirmed by two RAG facets: 'RCD protection need not be provided for the sauna heater unless such protection is recommended by the manufacturer.' (context_prefix: 'Reg 703.411.3.3 · [exception] · Sauna heater RCD protection not required unless manufacturer recommends') and 'The exemption for the sauna heater from RCD protection is conditional on manufacturer recommendation.' (context_prefix: 'Reg 703.411.3.3 · [condition] · Manufacturer recommendation conditions application to sauna heater'). The page cites the wrong regulation number and omits the heater exception entirely — both are confirmed errors against the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Testing must be completed in accordance with BS 7671 Chapter 61
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets confirm that Inspection and Testing in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 resides in Chapter 64, not Chapter 61. For example: 'Chapter 64 of BS 7671 Part 6 is dedicated to Initial Verification' (context_prefix: 'Chapter 64 · Reg 643.11 · [scope] · Initial verification covered in Chapter 64'); Reg 643.3 (insulation resistance), Reg 643.7.2 (test sequence), and Reg 641.5 all sit under 'Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64'. The only Chapter 61 reference returned is from Reg 651.1 (Chapter 65, periodic inspection) which mentions 'Part 6 (Chapter 61–69)' as the range of chapters — this confirms Chapter 61 exists in the numbering range but it is not the testing chapter. Chapter 61 covers the scope/objectives of Part 6, not the test procedures. Citing 'Chapter 61' as the chapter to follow for testing is a stale A3-era citation that will mislead readers.

content_gaps.missing: Section 703 has FOUR zones (Zone 1, 2, 3 and the area outside the sauna), not two. Zone 3 is the hottest zone (from 1.0m up to the ceiling cold-side insulation) and has the most restrictive equipment requirements. The page's two-zone model is a significant structural error that will mislead electricians.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 703.32.2 / 703.32.3: Zone 2 = floor to 1.0m; Zone 3 = 1.0m to ceiling cold-side insulation boundary

content_gaps.missing: Zone 1 extends 0.5m radially from the heater surface (vertical circumscribing surface). This specific 0.5m boundary dimension is required for correct zone demarcation and is not mentioned anywhere on the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 703.32.3: 'a vertical surface circumscribing the sauna heater at a distance 0.5 m from the surface of the heater'

content_gaps.missing: The heater RCD exception under Reg 703.411.3.3: RCD protection need not be provided for the sauna heater unless the manufacturer recommends it. This is a meaningful practical exemption that changes how the circuit is designed.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 703.411.3.3 exception: 'RCD protection need not be provided for the sauna heater unless such protection is recommended by the manufacturer'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 703.52 wiring system preference: wiring should preferably be installed on the cold side of the thermal insulation. Where installed on the warm side in zones 1 or 3, it shall be heat-resisting and metallic sheaths/conduits shall not be accessible. This is the specific regulatory basis for the heat-resistant cable requirement — not cited on the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 703.52: wiring on warm side in zones 1 or 3 shall be heat-resisting; metallic sheaths/conduits shall not be accessible

Add the 0.5m radial dimension for Zone 1 explicitly. This is a factual error that could cause non-compliant installations.

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR produced in accordance with BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Section 631)
confirmed_issues.evidence: No facet in bs7671_facets references 'Section 631' anywhere. The RAG shows periodic inspection is governed by Chapter 65 (Reg 651–653, including Reg 653.1 on the Condition Report and Appendix 6 notes) and testing by Chapter 64. 'Section 631' is stale 17th-edition (A2/A3) numbering that was renumbered in A4:2026. The page should cite Chapter 65 (periodic inspection and testing) not 'Section 631'.

confirmed_issues.claim: Reg 411.3.3 requires RCD protection on socket circuits not exceeding 20A
confirmed_issues.evidence: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3 facet is unambiguous: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception in the Regulation is met.' Multiple facets confirm the threshold is 32 A, not 20 A, and that A4:2026 revised this regulation specifically to 32 A. The page states '20A' in two separate places; this is a material factual error — the A4:2026 threshold is 32 A.

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR report includes Schedules of Circuit Details and Test Results as required by Section 631
confirmed_issues.evidence: The schedule content claim is grounded — GN3 Reg 1.3 and Reg 3.11 facets explicitly confirm: 'After completing periodic inspection and testing, the inspector shall prepare an EICR together with the Condition Report Inspection Schedule, Schedule(s) of Circuit Details and Schedule(s) of Test Results.' However the attribution to 'Section 631' is not grounded anywhere in bs7671_facets. No facet references Section 631. The RAG grounds schedule requirements in GN3 Chapter 1/3 and BS 7671 Chapter 65 (Reg 653.1 / Appendix 6), not Section 631. The section number is stale pre-A4 numbering and should be removed or corrected to Chapter 65 / GN3.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026): RCD protection at 30 mA now required for all AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. Bath pre-1990s properties almost universally lack this and it is a new common EICR finding under A4:2026.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 — 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA' (domestic premises)

content_gaps.missing: Correct socket-outlet RCD threshold is 32A under A4:2026 (Reg 411.3.3), not 20A. The page cites '20A' in two places — this is stale 17th Edition (A1/A2/A3) wording. A4:2026 raised the threshold from 20A to 32A.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3 — 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A'

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) requirement at Reg 421.1.7 for certain domestic premises — an A4:2026 addition that will generate new EICR observations in older Bath properties. Not mentioned anywhere on the page.

content_gaps.missing: The 0.80 site-test derating factor referenced in GN3 for periodic inspection Zs measurements. GN3 guidance notes that measured Zs at test should be at most 0.80 × tabulated maximum to account for temperature variation — relevant for inspectors testing older Bath wiring.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 444 (Measures against electromagnetic disturbances) applies to data-cable routing parallel to noisy LV cables
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 43 · Reg 434.3 · [scope] · Section 444 Measures against electromagnetic disturbances removed' states explicitly: 'Section 444 titled Measures against electromagnetic disturbances has been removed (deleted) from BS 7671 2018+A4:2026. This means requirements previously located in Section 444 are no longer present in this edition and any compliance previously relying on that Section must be referenced elsewhere or via other standards/regulations.' The page citing Section 444 as a live BS 7671 requirement is factually wrong under A4:2026. The applicable routing/separation guidance now falls under Chapter 44 (Reg 444.4.1 governs IT/mains cable separation at crossings) and Reg 443.6.2 (which warns of increased electromagnetic disturbance when power and ICT cables share common routes) — neither of which is labelled 'Section 444'.

confirmed_issues.claim: The page frames PoE exclusively under Section 715 and never cites Section 716 (ELV DC power for telecommunications distribution), which is the primary BS 7671 hook for PoE installations
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets confirm Section 716 is the mandatory hook for PoE. Reg 716.521.101 facets state: 'Regulation 716.521.101 applies to information and communication technology (ICT) cables that are used for the distribution of DC power' and requires Cat 5/6/6A/7/7A/8.1/8.2 compliance per BS EN 50173-1. Reg 716.2 normatively references BS EN 50173-1 (generic cabling) and BS EN 60512-99-001 (connector test for remote-powered twisted-pair). A further facet under Reg 653.2 states: 'installations using PoE shall be designed, erected and verified in accordance with Section 716 provisions.' Section 715 is confirmed by RAG to cover 'Extra-low voltage lighting installations' only — not DC power over data cabling. Omitting Section 716 entirely from a PoE Type 4 BS 7671 compliance discussion is a confirmed material omission.

content_gaps.missing: Section 716 (ELV DC power for telecommunications distribution) is entirely absent from the page. This is the primary BS 7671 section governing PoE/remote-powering installations. Reg 716.521.101 lists the permitted ICT cable categories; Reg 716.2 normatively references BS EN 50173-1 and connector test standards BS EN 60512-99-001 / 60512-9-3. The page cites Section 715 as the BS 7671 hook, which is only relevant when PoE powers luminaires — Section 716 is the correct primary reference for any PoE installation.

Reg 716.521.101 explicitly permits Cat5 through Cat8.2 ICT cables for DC power distribution (each category listed individually). The page recommends Cat6a but never cites this regulation, missing the opportunity to show that the cable guidance is directly backed by a named BS 7671 requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 716.521.101 — ICT cables shall comply with Category 5/6/6A/7/7A/8.1/8.2 as defined in BS EN 50173-1

content_gaps.missing: Normative connector testing requirements under Reg 716.2: BS EN 60512-99-001 (Test 99a — engage/separate under electrical load for remote-powered twisted-pair cabling) and BS EN 60512-9-3 (Test 9c — endurance under load). The page discusses 802.3bt-rated connectors but omits these BS 7671-normative test standards, which are the formal acceptance criteria for connector selection under Section 716.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 716.2(b)(c) — normative references for connectors in remote-powered cabling

content_gaps.missing: ELV minimum conductor cross-section of 0.75 mm² (Reg 524.2.3 / Table 52.3) is never mentioned. For PoE lighting fixed wiring this is directly relevant, particularly where designers specify flexible cables or non-standard accessories.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 524.2.3 — Table 52.3 minimum 0.75 mm² for ELV circuits

confirmed_issues.claim: Page notes a minimum reconnection delay of 60–180 seconds to allow capacitor discharge but does NOT cite or apply the mandatory discharge resistor requirement of Reg 559.5.6 (capacitors >0.5 µF must be used with discharge resistors)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 559.5.6 is confirmed in bs7671_facets: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 55 · Reg 559.5.6 · [requirement] · Capacitors > 0.5 µF require discharge resistors' — content: 'Compensation capacitors having a total capacitance exceeding 0.5 µF shall only be used in conjunction with discharge resistors. This requirement is mandatory for such capacitors connected in installations.' Also supported by Reg 462.4 facet: 'A DC capacitor bank in an inverter enclosure retains residual charge after isolation. Suitable means (a permanent bleed resistor ...) shall be provided and a warning label stating the calculated discharge time shall be fitted.' The page describes only a reconnection delay and omits this hard statutory obligation entirely — a genuine regulatory gap.

confirmed_issues.claim: Discharge resistors mandatory for capacitors >0.5 µF (Reg 559.5.6) — page omits this requirement
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 559.5.6 is confirmed in bs7671_facets with content: 'Compensation capacitors having a total capacitance exceeding 0.5 µF shall only be used in conjunction with discharge resistors. This requirement is mandatory for such capacitors connected in installations.' Reg 462.4 further requires a permanent bleed resistor and warning label for capacitor banks. The page discusses reconnection delay (implying discharge awareness) but never states the discharge resistor as a mandatory fitment obligation. This is a genuine omission of a hard BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 requirement.

content_gaps.missing: BS 7671 Reg 559.5.6 is completely absent: compensation capacitors >0.5 µF must be used with discharge resistors. For a PFC installation guide this is a mandatory compliance point — failure to mention it leaves installers exposed.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 462.4 / enclosure warning labels: DC capacitor banks that retain charge after isolation require a permanent bleed resistor AND a warning label with calculated discharge time. APFC panels shut down but capacitors retain charge — this safety obligation is not mentioned.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 462.4 — 'A permanent bleed resistor sized to discharge to safe voltage in X minutes shall be provided and a warning label stating the calculated discharge time shall be fitted.'

content_gaps.missing: Cable sizing for capacitor reactive current (Irc): PWI flags 'insufficient cable size for Irc' as a very common mistake. The page has no guidance on how to rate cables feeding capacitor steps — capacitor current exceeds nominal kVAr current at harmonic frequencies.
