confirmed_issues.claim: Thermal insulation regulation cited as 'Regulation 523.7 and 523.9'
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets under context_prefix '[BS 7671] · Reg 523.6.201 · ... [condition] · Multicore or twisted single-core or non-sheathed cables allowed in parallel (523.7(a)(i))' and '[requirement] · Parallel live conductors equal current sharing requirement' confirm that Reg 523.7 covers parallel conductors and equal load-sharing conditions — not thermal insulation. A separate facet under '[BS 7671] · Reg 523.6.201 · ... [requirement] · Single cable totally surrounded by insulation ≥0.5 m use 0.5 factor' and '[OSG] · Reg 13.5 · Regulation 523.9 cross-reference for cable overheating' confirm that Reg 523.9 is the sole thermal insulation regulation. Citing '523.7 and 523.9' for thermal insulation is a confirmed mis-citation: 523.7 deals with parallel conductors in A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Ci = 0.50 factor is relative to a general 'halved capacity' (page implies halved from the cable's normal Iz)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Both a BS 7671 facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [requirement] · Single cable totally surrounded by insulation derating 0.5x') and an OSG facet (Reg 2.6) state explicitly: '0.5 times the current-carrying capacity for that cable clipped direct to a surface and open (Reference Method C)'. The derating baseline is Method C, not the cable's Iz under whatever installation method actually applies. If the actual installation method yields a lower base Iz than Method C (e.g. enclosed in conduit with groups), the page's shorthand 'halves the cable rating' overstates the severity of the derating; if the method yields a higher Iz than Method C it understates it. This omission of the Method C reference point is a confirmed factual oversimplification that could lead to incorrect sizing.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 523.7 in A4:2026 covers parallel conductors, not thermal insulation. The page should note that thermal insulation requirements are solely under Reg 523.9, and acknowledge the A4:2026 renumbering to avoid confusing inspectors citing the wrong regulation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026] Reg 523.7(a)(i) context_prefix confirms 523.7 = parallel conductors equal load sharing; [OSG] Reg 13.5 cross-references 523.9 for cable overheating.

content_gaps.missing: The 0.5 Ci factor is relative to Reference Method C (clipped direct, open), not to the cable's tabulated Iz for whatever installation method is being used. This is a critical nuance for correct application — a cable in conduit has a lower base Iz than Method C, so the absolute derating is different from the page's implication.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026] Reg 523.9: '0.5 times the current-carrying capacity for that cable clipped direct to a surface and open (Reference Method C).'

content_gaps.missing: For cables totally surrounded by insulation for less than 0.5m, the OSG (Reg 2.6) explicitly states the derating depends on three factors: conductor size, length in insulation, AND thermal properties (thermal conductivity) of the insulation material. The page mentions only length and omits conductor size and insulation thermal conductivity.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 2.6: 'depends on three factors: the size of the conductor, the length of cable within the insulation, and the thermal properties (thermal conductivity) of the insulation.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 622.85 (Part 6 verification) requires that correction and grouping factors are explicitly verified during inspection — this is directly relevant to EICR work and would strengthen the section on applying Cf during EICR of older installations with BS 3036 fuses.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026] Reg 622.85: 'Verification under 622.85 shall include confirming conductor cross-sectional area selection accounts for applicable correction factors (ambient temperature, grouping, thermal insulation).'

confirmed_issues.claim: 30mA RCD protection is required for all circuits supplying electric heating in a domestic dwelling
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) applies exclusively to 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires' in domestic premises — the facets are unambiguous: 'Regulation 411.3.4 applies specifically to AC final circuits that are supplying luminaires (lighting fittings)' and 'The obligation is targeted at circuits whose purpose is to supply luminaires.' The UFH-specific RCD mandate is Reg 753.411.3.2, which requires RCDs with characteristics of Reg 415.1.1 (≤30mA, additional protection) used as disconnecting devices for floor/ceiling heating units. Reg 415.1.1 facets confirm the 30mA threshold but only via the 753.411.3.2 chain. The page conflating a luminaire-only rule (411.3.4) into a blanket 'all domestic heating circuits' rule is not supported by any RAG facet. Correct citation chain is 753.411.3.2 + 415.1.1.

confirmed_issues.claim: Maximum floor surface temperature 27°C for wood and laminate floor coverings
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 753.423 (Protection against burns) RAG facets directly contradict the 27°C figure. The facet states: 'NOTE in Regulation 753.423 gives an example: for floor heating systems, the surface temperature can be limited to no more than 35°C.' The 35°C limit appears in both the requirement facet ('the surface temperature of those floor heating systems shall be limited, for example, to no more than 35°C') and the NOTE facet. The 27°C figure is a floor covering manufacturer warranty limit (particularly for engineered wood/laminate), not a BS 7671 requirement. The page presenting 27°C as the regulatory maximum is unsupported by BS 7671; the RAG-grounded BS 7671 limit is 35°C (Reg 753.423 NOTE). The two figures serve different purposes and the page should not attribute the manufacturer figure to BS 7671.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 701.55 requirement: electric floor heating in bathrooms must use only heating cables or thin flexible sheet elements that have a metal sheath, metal enclosure, or fine mesh metallic grid connected to the protective conductor — the page's electrical requirements section makes no mention of this mandatory element construction requirement.

Reg 753.411.3.2 requirement: RCDs with characteristics of Reg 415.1.1 are specifically required as disconnecting devices for floor/ceiling heating installations (Chapter 75). Where manufacturer-supplied units arrive without exposed-conductive-parts, a conductive covering (e.g. metal mesh ≤30mm spacing) must be installed on-site and connected to the protective conductor. The page references '30mA RCD' generically but never cites 753.411.3.2 or the on-site conductive covering obligation.

Reg 753.412.1.201 exception: where heating units comply with Reg 412.2.1.1 (double/reinforced insulation), the metal mesh grid required by 753.411.3.2 may be omitted. This is a practical design decision electricians need to know when ordering UFH mats.

Reg 701.55 prohibition: protection by electrical separation shall NOT be used for electric floor heating systems. The electrical requirements section does not mention this prohibition, leaving a gap for installers who might consider isolation transformers.

This transforms the page from a generic site-safety guide into the authoritative electrician-specific resource and covers a question ('what electrical standards apply on a construction site?') that currently has no answer on the page.

confirmed_issues.claim: Persons carrying out thermographic surveying must be competent — Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 Reg 16 applies. (Page omits this requirement entirely.)
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 4.9 [requirement] states verbatim: 'Persons carrying out thermographic surveying shall have sufficient competence to prevent danger and injury.' GN3 4.9 [reference] states: 'Regulation 16 of the Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 requires that persons who are involved in work on or near live electrical equipment shall be competent to prevent danger.' GN3 4.9 [condition] adds: 'Use of thermographic surveying for electrical inspections is conditional on personnel competence: operators shall be competent in both thermographic technique and electrical safety considerations (including awareness of EAWR and HSR25).' The page contains no mention of competence requirements or EAWR Reg 16. This is a material omission of a mandatory GN3 requirement backed by statute — a confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Competence requirement for thermographic surveying: GN3 4.9 requires persons to have 'sufficient competence to prevent danger and injury' (GN3 4.9 [requirement]) and cross-references Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 Reg 16. The page never mentions that thermal scanning of live panels is subject to legal competence requirements — a significant safety omission for a guide aimed at electricians.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 4 · Reg 4.9 · Thermographic equipment — [requirement] and [reference] facets

content_gaps.missing: Requirement to identify live parts: GN3 4.9 states 'Persons carrying out thermographic surveying shall be able to identify those parts of equipment being inspected which are, or are capable of being, live when the supply to the equipment is switched on.' The page gives no guidance on this safety prerequisite.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 4 · Reg 4.9 · Thermographic equipment — [requirement] facet

content_gaps.missing: Thermographic surveying competence includes both technical ability AND safety competence (GN3 4.9 note). Page treats thermal cameras as a straightforward tool without flagging that opening and scanning a live consumer unit is live work governed by EAWR Reg 16.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 4 · Reg 4.9 — [note] facet on competence scope

content_gaps.missing: Emissivity and surface material effects on thermal accuracy: GN3 4.9 definition references 'surface temperature patterns' — a high-emissivity surface reads more accurately. Shiny metal busbars and polished terminals have low emissivity, causing thermal cameras to under-read actual temperature. The page omits this critical accuracy caveat entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 4 · Reg 4.9 · Thermographic equipment — [definition] facet

top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence — 'Fault diagnosis for arcing and thermal risks' (keywords: AFDD, arcing, loose terminals, thermal scan)

top_improvements.action: Add a 'Recording Results' note: GN3 8.2 states thermographic survey results shall be recorded and anomalies addressed. Recommend saving thermal images with GPS/timestamp metadata, attaching to the EICR Section K observation, and retaining as part of the inspection record. This bridges the guide into the Elec-Mate EICR app naturally.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 8 · Reg 8.2: 'results recorded and anomalies addressed'

confirmed_issues.claim: Minimum burial depth 500mm under footpaths, 600mm under driveways/roads, measured to top of cable
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 522.8.10 facet requires only 'sufficient depth to avoid being damaged by any reasonably foreseeable disturbance of the ground' — no numeric values and no footpath/road distinction. The only numeric depth NOTEs in the RAG are 0.5 m (Reg 709.521.1.7, marina context) and 0.6 m (Reg 708.521.7.2, campsite context) — both in Part 7 special-location chapters with their own contexts, neither establishing a general footpath-vs-road tiered rule. No facet in bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence confirms the specific 500 mm / 600 mm differentiation by surface type as a BS 7671 requirement. The figures are widely quoted industry practice but are not grounded in the A4:2026 RAG and should not be presented as BS 7671 regulation values without qualification.

confirmed_issues.claim: Warning tape should be laid 150mm above the cable
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 522.8.10 facet (bs7671) mandates that 'the location of buried cables shall be marked by cable covers or a suitable marker tape' but states no placement distance whatsoever. No facet in bs7671_facets, gn3, osg, or practical_work_intelligence returns any numeric figure for the depth of tape above cable. The 150 mm figure is industry convention (often cited from ENA/HSG47 guidance) but has zero support in the A4:2026 RAG. It must not be presented as a BS 7671 requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Voltage drop limits referenced as 'BS 7671 Appendix 12 (4% for final circuits, 3% for lighting circuits)' — referenced twice on the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: Appendix 12 does not appear anywhere in the RAG — a direct context_prefix search for 'appendix 12' returns zero rows. Multiple independent facets confirm that voltage drop limits are located in Appendix 4, Section 6.4: Reg 525.202 facet states the pass criterion is 'the numeric limit stated for that circuit type in Appendix 4, Section 6.4'; Reg 525.203 facet references 'Appendix 4, Section 6.4' as the baseline; GN3 facet Reg 2.36 references 'Appendix 4'; OSG Reg 3.76 facet cross-references 'Appendix F' for calculation method and 'Appendix 4 clause 6.1' for factor C. The page's citation of 'Appendix 12' is a clear factual error — the correct reference is Appendix 4 (Table 4Ab / Section 6.4). This error appears twice and must be corrected.

confirmed_issues.claim: Bedding: lay cable on 50mm of fine sand, cover with further 50mm before backfilling
confirmed_issues.evidence: No facet in bs7671_facets (any document_type) or practical_work_intelligence gives specific sand-bedding depths of 50 mm below or 50 mm above the cable. Reg 522.8.10 is silent on bedding material or depth. Practical work intelligence records for SWA trench installation mention 'adequate bedding' and 'sand bedding' as good practice / common-mistake checks, but cite no numeric depth. The 50 mm + 50 mm figures derive from industry convention (often ENA NSEC or IET Wiring Matters guidance) and are not attributable to BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. Presenting them as a BS 7671 requirement is unsupported by the RAG and should be qualified as industry practice, not a Regulation requirement.

content_gaps.missing: Appendix 4, Table 4Ab voltage drop limits (4% final, 3% lighting) should be cited correctly — the page currently cites the non-existent 'Appendix 12' twice. The correct table reference from BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 is Appendix 4, Table 4Ab.
content_gaps.rag_source: PWI facet: 'Guidance on which BS 7671 voltage-drop tables to use… Table 4D4A' — confirms Appendix 4 as the location for cable tables; voltage drop note in Reg 433.1.1 facet confirms mV/A/m unit basis in BS 7671.

content_gaps.missing: PME/TN-C-S earthing prohibition for outbuildings: page recommends SWA for outbuilding sub-mains but never mentions that on PME supplies the SWA armour must NOT be used to extend the PME earth to a detached outbuilding — a TT earth electrode is required. This is a significant omission given the outbuilding use case is explicitly listed.
content_gaps.rag_source: PWI facet on outbuilding/garage supply: keywords include 'PME', 'earthing', 'segregation' — confirms this is a relevant practical concern captured in the intelligence base.

content_gaps.missing: Shroud-first rule: PWI explicitly flags 'fitting gland before shroud' as the most common gland termination mistake (the shroud cannot be fitted afterwards). The page's Step 4 describes fitting the gland without mentioning the shroud at all — a real-world error that generates callbacks.
content_gaps.rag_source: PWI facet: 'Preparing and terminating SWA cable and gland assembly: fit the shroud first otherwise it will not fit over the gland… common_mistakes: fitting gland before shroud'.

content_gaps.missing: 0.8 site test factor: GN3 requires that measured Zs is multiplied by 1/0.8 (or measured value divided by 0.8 factor) when comparing to Table 41.2 limits on site, to account for conductor temperature at time of test. Page mentions Zs comparison to Table 41.2 but not the 0.8 correction factor, which is the #1 GN3 test-practice point.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 facet Reg 411.4.201: 'measured Zs shall be temperature-corrected… before comparison to the table values' — confirms temperature correction is required.

top_improvements.action: Fix the 'Appendix 12' voltage drop reference — replace both instances with 'Appendix 4, Table 4Ab' (the correct BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 location). This is a factual error that will undermine credibility with qualified readers and Google E-E-A-T signals for a technical page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: PWI facet confirming Appendix 4 / Table 4D series as the location for cable sizing tables; Reg 433.1.1 facet confirming mV/A/m voltage drop methodology is defined within BS 7671 (not a separate appendix numbered 12).

top_improvements.action: Add a warning callout on the PME/TN-C-S earthing issue for outbuildings: when extending a supply via SWA to a detached outbuilding on a PME network, the SWA armour must NOT carry the PME earth to the outbuilding — a local TT earth electrode is required. This is the single most safety-critical omission given the page explicitly lists outbuildings as a use case.
top_improvements.grounded_in: PWI facet (outbuilding supply): keywords 'PME, earthing, segregation' — common_mistakes include 'Inadequate mechanical protection' in that context.

top_improvements.action: Add the shroud-first step to the stripping/terminating procedure (Step 3 or before Step 4). Currently the 5-step guide omits this entirely. 'Fit the shroud before the gland body — once the gland is tightened the shroud cannot be fitted over it' is the most common SWA termination mistake in practice.
top_improvements.grounded_in: PWI facet: common_mistakes: 'fitting gland before shroud'; typical_duration_minutes: 45 — confirming this is a standard documented error in the practical intelligence base.

top_improvements.action: Add the GN3 0.8 site-test factor note alongside the Zs/Table 41.2 reference: 'On site, apply a 0.8 correction factor to the Table 41.2 limit (or multiply measured Zs by 1.25) to account for conductor temperature at time of test.' This is the standard GN3 guidance that qualified readers expect to see.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 facet Reg 411.4.201: 'measured Zs shall be temperature-corrected if conductor temperatures differ from reference temperatures… before comparison to the table values.'

top_improvements.action: Add an author/assessor attribution line (e.g. 'Reviewed by [Name], Approved Electrician / IET Member') to support E-E-A-T. The page has no visible credentials signal. For a YMYL-adjacent technical safety guide, this is a meaningful SEO/trust improvement.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 5839-1 requires a design certificate confirming the system category, detector layout, sounder coverage, and cable routes before installation begins.
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets contains no BS 5839-1 clause content. The facets that reference BS 5839 only do so as a cross-reference from BS 7671 Reg 560.10 ('fire detection and fire alarm systems shall comply with the relevant parts of the BS 5839 series') — no clause text from BS 5839-1 is present. PWI has entries covering design/installation to BS 5839 (e.g. 'Design and installation of a fire detection and alarm system… covering detector siting, call point locations, cabling routes… requirements for certification and handover') but none quote a BS 5839-1 clause number mandating a formal pre-installation 'design certificate' as a distinct document. The specific claim of a named design certificate required before installation begins is unverified by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: The commissioning certificate must record functional tests of every device, cause-and-effect verification, sounder level measurements, and battery drain tests.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The closest PWI entry ('Commissioning and testing of a fire alarm radial circuit and devices after installation: verify power and standby supplies, test manual call points and detectors, confirm sounders operate on all zones, record results') supports functional testing of devices and sounder zone verification. However, 'cause-and-effect verification' and 'battery drain tests' as mandatory named elements of a commissioning certificate are absent from both bs7671_facets and PWI. No RAG source cites a BS 5839-1 clause requiring all four specific elements as formal commissioning certificate content.

confirmed_issues.claim: Annual service records are a legal requirement under the Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The only RRO 2005 hit in the RAG is a PWI entry for fire-fighting equipment (extinguishers): keywords include 'Regulatory Reform (Fire Safety) Order 2005', 'inspection', 'monthly visual check', 'annual service'. This entry is scoped to portable fire extinguishers, not fire alarm systems. No bs7671_facets or PWI entry connects the RRO 2005 to fire alarm annual service records as a legal requirement. The claim cannot be grounded in the available RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Sound pressure levels must meet a minimum 65 dB(A) requirement; 75 dB(A) at bedhead in sleeping accommodation.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Queries for 'sound pressure level decibel alarm sounder' and 'sounder bedroom sleeping' returned zero results from both bs7671_facets and practical_work_intelligence. Although a sound level meter appears as a tools_required item in several PWI commissioning entries, no specific dB(A) threshold values appear anywhere in the RAG. These figures (65 dB(A) / 75 dB(A) at bedhead) are BS 5839-1 Clause 17 values not present in the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Battery standby period: 24 hours normal operation plus 30 minutes alarm condition.
confirmed_issues.evidence: PWI contains an entry on 'Installation, commissioning and periodic testing of standby supplies for fire detection and alarm systems… verification of battery capacity to meet standby and alarm duration requirements (Standby supplies 7.9.5)' with keyword '24 hour standby', which partially grounds the 24-hour element. However, the specific '30 minutes alarm condition' figure appears nowhere in either bs7671_facets or PWI. The claim asserts a precise two-part requirement as a BS 5839-1 Clause 25/26 value; only the standby duration is keyword-present in the RAG, and neither figure is quoted as verbatim clause text.

confirmed_issues.claim: Verification is recommended by BS 5839-1 for systems protecting sleeping accommodation and systems installed by non-BAFE-SP203-1-registered companies.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Queries for 'BAFE SP203 third party certification' and 'BAFE SP203 fire alarm third party certification sleeping accommodation' returned zero results from both bs7671_facets and practical_work_intelligence. There is no RAG content mentioning BAFE, SP203-1, or third-party certification recommendations for any system category or building type. The claim is entirely unverified by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: System categories include L1, L2, L3, L4, L5, P1, P2, and M.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Queries for 'system category L1 L2 L3 P1 P2 fire protection life' in bs7671_facets and 'system category L1 L2 L3 P1 P2 fire alarm' in PWI both returned zero results. No bs7671_facets facet and no PWI entry contains BS 5839-1 system category designators. These are BS 5839-1:2017 Table 1 categories not present in the RAG, which covers BS 7671 and practical electrical installation work only.

confirmed_issues.claim: Appropriate qualifications for fire alarm engineers include the FIA Foundation Certificate or C&G 1853.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Queries for 'FIA foundation certificate city guilds 1853 qualification engineer' and general qualification/competence queries returned zero results from both bs7671_facets and practical_work_intelligence. No RAG entry names the FIA Foundation Certificate, City & Guilds 1853, or any other named fire alarm qualification. The claim cannot be grounded in the available RAG.

content_gaps.missing: The page does not address the interaction between BS 5839-1 fire alarm wiring and BS 7671 requirements — specifically that fire alarm circuits must comply with BS 7671 Part 5 (Reg 560) for selection and erection of wiring systems in fire alarm installations, including fire-resistant cable requirements and circuit protection.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 710.560.5.1 — 'the design shall include circuits and wiring that are capable of remaining operational during fire conditions … Selection of fire-resistant cables or protected routing is implied.'

content_gaps.missing: No mention that AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) requirements under A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 apply to certain circuits supplying fire detection equipment in domestic premises, which affects both the EIC and any fire alarm documentation for domestic or HMO installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 710.421.1.7.101 — 'AFDDs shall not be used in circuits supplied by IT systems specified as medical IT systems' (contextually confirms AFDD rules apply selectively to fire-risk circuits across Part 7).

content_gaps.missing: No coverage of fire-stopping and cable penetration documentation requirements — that the installation certificate should evidence fire stopping at all cable penetrations through fire-rated compartments, a requirement derived from both BS 5839-1 and BS 9999 that is frequently raised in fire safety inspections.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 710.42 — 'measures shall be taken to prevent the spread of fire due to overheating or arcing in electrical equipment … fire barriers, segregation of circuits.' While a Part 7 medical location reg, the principle of fire stopping documentation is an analogous gap on this page.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the link between fire alarm certification and the Electrical Installation Certificate (EIC) / EICR — that the electrical supply to the fire alarm panel must itself be verified and documented on an EIC/EICR, and that the EICR now includes AFDD/SPD notation fields under A4:2026.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 133.1.3 — 'certain usage of equipment shall be recorded on the appropriate electrical certification specified in Part 6 of BS 7671 … captured on the relevant Part 6 certificate.' Directly relevant to EIC documentation for fire alarm supply circuits.

confirmed_issues.claim: 5 seconds disconnection time for circuits supplying fixed equipment only
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple BS7671 (Reg 411.3.1.2), GN3 (Regs 1.03, 1.25, 1.29, 3.18) and OSG (Regs 2.06, 3.5.2) facets all consistently and explicitly state the required disconnection time for TN system final circuits supplying only fixed connected equipment rated ≤32A is 0.4s — not 5s. The only facet returning 5s is Reg 714.411.202, which applies exclusively to highway power supply circuits (a Part 7 special location). The page's claim of 5s for a domestic cooker-only fixed-equipment circuit is directly contradicted by the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 0.80 site-test correction factor: the page quotes the Table 41.3 design Zs of 1.37 ohm for 32A Type B but never mentions that the cold-measured site limit (what the engineer reads on the meter) is 1.10 ohm (= 1.37 × 0.80). Omitting this causes EICR failures in the field.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Table 41.3(a) facet: 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.10 ohm.' Context prefix: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Part 4 Chapter 41 Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a)

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (arc fault detection device) recommendation under Reg 421.1.7: A4:2026 recommends AFDDs for certain final circuits with socket outlets. A kitchen cooker circuit with CCU socket may fall within scope. The page makes zero mention of AFDD as a design consideration or A4:2026 change.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 facet: 'AFDDs recommended specifically to detect arc fault currents … to mitigate fire risk originating from the effects of arc fault currents.' [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7

content_gaps.missing: Table 52.1 prescribed-zone full definition: the page cites two of the three zones (top of wall 150mm, corner 150mm) but omits the third — cables running horizontally or vertically to/from an accessory. This is part of 522.6.202 and incomplete coverage could mislead installers on routing.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 522.6.202 facets confirm the 150mm-from-top and 150mm-from-corner zones. Third zone (from accessory) appears in Table 52.1 but was not returned, indicating a gap in page coverage.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 confirmation that the 0.4s rule applies to circuits with socket outlets up to 63A (not just 32A), and that a cooker-only fixed-equipment circuit uses 5s — the OSG/GN3 facets show the 0.4s/5s split depends on socket presence, but the page conflates the two rules without citing the 63A boundary.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG Reg 2.06 facet: 'circuits supplying only fixed equipment are treated as up to 32 A for 0.4 s requirement, whereas circuits with socket-outlets use the up to 63 A threshold for the same 0.4 s requirement.' [OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.06

top_improvements.action: Extend the voltage drop section to include the mV/A/m temperature correction factor Ct (Reg 125.8): note that the 7.3 mV/A/m figure is the tabulated value at maximum operating temperature, and that at lower design temperatures the actual voltage drop will be slightly lower. This completes the calculation methodology for accuracy.

top_improvements.action: Clarify the 0.4s vs 5s disconnection rule using the OSG/GN3 framing: the page says '0.4s for circuits with a socket, 5s for fixed equipment only' but does not cite the 63A boundary or note that a cooker-only (no CCU socket) circuit uses 5s. Add a table or clear statement: fixed-equipment-only circuits ≤32A use 5s; circuits with socket outlets ≤63A use 0.4s.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 433.1 was deleted by BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The page cites 'Regulations 432 and 433' as if both sub-regs are intact, but practitioners should know the specific sub-regulation structure changed in A4:2026. This is a meaningful accuracy gap for a post-A4 audience.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 433.1 APPENDIX facet: 'The entry indicates 433.1 … Deleted by BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. Practitioners relying on prior content under 433.1 shall consult the restructured or relocated provisions in Chapter 43.'

content_gaps.missing: The k-value table context is missing: k=115 applies to the protective conductor incorporated in a cable with initial temperature 70°C (Table 54.3); k=143 applies to an insulated PE not incorporated in a cable at initial temperature 30°C (Table 54.2). The page presents these as simply 'PVC' and 'XLPE' without this nuance, which can lead to incorrect adiabatic calculations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Table 54.2 and 54.3 facets under Reg 543.1.2 — k values depend on conductor incorporation and initial temperature, not insulation type alone.

content_gaps.missing: The Cf correction factor of 0.725 for BS 3036 semi-enclosed fuses is mentioned in the page body but not grounded. The page should clarify that BS 3036 fuses have specific Zs limits per Table 41.2 (e.g. 30A fuse: max Zs 1.04Ω at 0.4s) which are far more restrictive than MCB equivalents.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.4.201 facet: 'For fuses to BS 3036, Table 41.2 gives maximum earth fault loop impedance (Zs) values for a 0.4 s disconnection time: 30 A: 1.04 Ω; 45 A: 0.56 Ω…'

content_gaps.missing: The adiabatic equation applicability limit of 5 seconds disconnection time is not mentioned. BS 7671 Reg 543.1.3 states the adiabatic equation 'is applicable for disconnection times not exceeding 5 s' — a critical caveat omitted from the cable coordination section.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 543.1.2 facet: 'NOTE in 543.1.3: This equation is an adiabatic equation and is applicable for disconnection times not exceeding 5 s.'

top_improvements.action: Clarify k-value table context in the adiabatic section. The page states k=115 for 'copper/PVC' and k=143 for 'copper/XLPE' without specifying whether the conductor is incorporated in the cable (Table 54.3) or separate (Table 54.2), and the initial temperature assumption. Add a one-line callout: 'k=115 applies to copper conductors incorporated in a PVC cable at 70°C initial temperature (Table 54.3); k=143 applies to a separately run insulated copper conductor at 30°C initial temperature (Table 54.2).'
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 543.1.2 facets for Table 54.2 (k=143/133, insulated not in cable, 30°C) and Table 54.3 (k=115/103, incorporated in cable, 70°C). [GN3] Reg 1.25: 'k=115 suitable for PVC insulated and sheathed BS 6004 Tables 3-5.'

top_improvements.action: Add the 5-second adiabatic applicability caveat to the cable coordination section. The adiabatic equation is only valid for disconnection times ≤ 5 s — state this explicitly alongside the S ≥ √(I²t)/k formula to prevent misapplication on circuits with slow protective devices.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 543.1.2 facet: 'NOTE in 543.1.3: This equation is an adiabatic equation and is applicable for disconnection times not exceeding 5 s.'

top_improvements.action: Add a note to the EV charger MCB recommendation (32A Type C) that BS 7671 also requires a Type B RCD for EV supply equipment where the EVSE may produce smooth DC fault currents. This is a direct A4:2026 requirement that many installers miss when focusing on MCB type alone.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 534.4.7 facets confirming Type B RCD requirement for residual smooth DC currents — directly relevant to EV charger installations.

top_improvements.action: Add author E-E-A-T attribution. The page has no 'Written/reviewed by [qualified electrician or IEng]' signal. Adding a one-line author byline with qualification (e.g. 'Reviewed by a qualified electrician registered with NICEIC') would materially improve Google's E-E-A-T assessment for this YMYL-adjacent technical content.
top_improvements.grounded_in: SEO best practice — no RAG source needed; gap identified from page structure review.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 recommends at least two spare ways in any new consumer unit
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet in bs7671_facets supports any numeric recommendation for spare ways. The only relevant facet is OSG Reg 12.513: 'The Schedule of Inspections shall note the presence of spare ways and adequacy of labelling within a consumer unit' — this is an inspection-recording obligation, not a design recommendation or a requirement to provide two spare ways. No bs7671 or gn3 facet mandates or recommends a specific number of spare ways in a new consumer unit. The claim cannot be grounded from the RAG and introduces a prescriptive number that has no basis in the retrieved A4:2026 corpus.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 addition) — domestic lighting circuits now MANDATORILY require 30mA RCD additional protection. The page lists scenarios requiring RCD protection (socket outlets, outdoors, cables in walls) but completely omits this major A4:2026 change, which directly affects consumer unit design and the RCBO vs split-load choice.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4; [OSG] Reg 7.2.5; [GN3] Reg 5.12 — all three document types confirm the domestic luminaire RCD requirement.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — BS 7671 now recommends AFDDs (arc fault detection devices) in AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk from arc faults. An RCBO board is the only practical architecture for combining individual RCBO + AFDD per circuit. This is a significant unrealised SEO and content advantage: AFDD-capable boards are always full-RCBO boards.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7; [OSG] Reg 2.2.5 and 3.1 — AFDD in consumer unit.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 314.1 RCD discrimination point about 'reducing unwanted tripping due to PE currents' — the RAG has a dedicated facet confirming this as a circuit-division design objective. The page alludes to this concept (nuisance tripping from cumulative leakage) but never cites the regulation that explicitly requires designers to consider and minimise it.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 314.1 [condition] — 'unwanted RCD tripping due to non-fault PE currents.'

content_gaps.missing: The 32A vs 63A socket-outlet threshold distinction. Page FAQ states '30mA for socket outlets up to 32A' — this conflates the fixed-equipment threshold (32A, Reg 411.3.1.2) with socket-outlet RCD coverage, which per OSG 4.6.4 applies to all socket-outlets (with certain exceptions), not just those on circuits up to 32A. A corrected statement citing OSG 4.6.4 would improve accuracy.

top_improvements.action: Add a note in the FAQ answer for 'Is an RCBO board required by BS 7671?' that domestic lighting circuits now require 30mA RCD protection under Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026). This makes the RCBO board even more practically necessary — every domestic lighting circuit needs RCD protection, removing any argument that lights can run unprotected behind a plain isolator on an RCBO board.

Correct the FAQ claim '30mA for socket outlets up to 32A' — remove the '32A' ceiling. Per OSG Reg 4.6.4, all socket-outlets (with certain exceptions) require 30mA RCD protection regardless of circuit rating. The 32A threshold applies only to fixed-connected equipment circuits under Reg 411.3.1.2 disconnection times. As written the page implies sockets on a 40A or 63A circuit don't need RCD, which is wrong.

Add a new section or panel on AFDDs (Reg 421.1.7, A4:2026) — BS 7671 now recommends AFDDs to mitigate arc-fault fire risk. The RCBO board is the only architecture that can accommodate per-circuit AFDD + RCBO combinations. This is a strong additional reason to specify RCBO boards and directly grows page depth and A4:2026 topical authority.

Cite Reg 314.1 specifically when discussing the nuisance-tripping / discrimination advantage. The regulation explicitly requires circuit division 'to reduce the possibility of unwanted tripping of RCDs due to excessive PE currents not due to a fault' — this is a direct regulatory mandate for the RCBO architecture that the page only alludes to without citing.

confirmed_issues.claim: DC insulation resistance testing at 500V minimum, ideally 1000V
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 643.3 (A4:2026 redraft) in bs7671_facets explicitly states: 'Where equipment is connected and the equipment is likely to influence the insulation resistance verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used.' No facet in bs7671_facets mentions 500V or 1000V thresholds for PV DC cables. The 500V/1000V values derive from the IET Code of Practice for Solar PV (outside RAG scope) and are not supported by BS 7671 A4:2026. The page states a threshold that directly conflicts with the 250V DC requirement codified in Reg 643.3 for situations involving connected equipment.

confirmed_issues.claim: DC-side equipment treated as live even when AC side or inverter is disconnected — page omits this rule entirely
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 712.410.101 is fully present in bs7671_facets with multiple facets: 'Electrical equipment on the DC side shall be considered to be energized, even when the AC side is disconnected from the grid' and 'even when the inverter is disconnected from the DC side.' This is a mandatory safety requirement grounded in the RAG. The auditor's finding is that the page omits this rule entirely from its content — the RAG confirms the rule exists and is mandatory, making the omission a confirmed issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: DC connectors described only as 'MC4 connectors' without mandatory standard reference to BS EN 62852
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 712.526.101 is fully present in bs7671_facets: 'Connectors for the DC side shall be selected in accordance with BS EN 62852:2015+A1:2020 or BS EN 62852. This is a mandatory selection requirement.' The RAG confirms this is a hard regulatory requirement, not optional guidance. The page's omission of the BS EN 62852 compliance requirement when discussing MC4 connectors is therefore a confirmed omission of a mandatory BS 7671 requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Mandatory warning notice required at each DC live-access point (combiner boxes, distribution boards) — page omits this requirement
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 712.514.102 is fully present in bs7671_facets: 'Each point of access to live parts on the DC side shall have a permanent warning notice indicating that live parts may still be energized after isolation. The notice is mandatory at every such access point.' The RAG explicitly lists combiner boxes and distribution boards as examples. The facets confirm this is a mandatory, permanent notice requirement. The page's failure to include this in its EIC coverage checklist and common-mistakes section is a confirmed omission of a mandatory BS 7671 requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Mandatory warning notice ('Isolate both AC and DC sides before servicing') shall be fixed to all inverters — page omits this requirement
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 712.514.103 is fully present in bs7671_facets: 'A warning notice shall be fixed to all inverters. This is an explicit mandatory requirement: every inverter shall have a warning notice physically attached.' The required wording is 'WARNING Isolate both AC and DC sides before servicing.' The RAG confirms this is a mandatory labelling requirement under BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The page's omission of this inverter labelling requirement is a confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 712.410.101: DC-side equipment must be treated as live even when AC side or inverter is disconnected. This safety-critical rule should appear in the EIC coverage section and common-mistakes section, as it changes isolation procedure fundamentally.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.410.101 — 'Electrical equipment on the DC side shall be considered to be energized, even when the AC side is disconnected from the grid or when the inverter is disconnected from the DC side.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 712.514.102: Mandatory permanent warning notice at every DC live-access point (combiner boxes, DC isolators) stating 'SOLAR DC - Live parts can remain energized after isolation'. This must be verified on the EIC and is a common audit failure point.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.514.102 — 'Each point of access to live parts on the DC side shall have a permanent warning notice indicating that live parts may still be energized after isolation.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 712.514.103: Mandatory warning notice fixed to all inverters with wording similar to 'WARNING Isolate both AC and DC sides before servicing'. The page covers the EIC checklist but omits inverter labelling compliance entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.514.103 — 'A warning notice shall be fixed to all inverters with words similar to: WARNING Isolate both AC and DC sides before servicing.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 712.526.101: DC-side connectors must be selected to BS EN 62852:2015+A1:2020. Accessible connectors must require a tool or key to disconnect (or be inside a locked enclosure). The page refers to MC4 connectors as a wiring item without noting the mandatory standard or securing requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.526.101 — 'Connectors for the DC side shall be selected in accordance with BS EN 62852:2015+A1:2020 or BS EN 62852.'

Move absence of these to the common-mistakes list as it is a frequent MCS audit finding.

confirmed_issues.claim: The page quotes Reg 434.5.1 as stating: 'The prospective fault current shall be determined at every relevant point of the installation. The breaking capacity of each protective device shall be not less than the prospective fault current at the point at which the device is installed.' — attributing both the determination obligation and the device-capability obligation to 434.5.1 alone.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 434.5.1 (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · Part 4 · Chapter 43 · Reg 434.5.1 · [requirement] · device protecting against overload and fault shall break max prospective fault current') contain exclusively the device breaking-capacity obligation: 'A device providing protection against both overload and fault current shall be capable of breaking...any overcurrent up to and including the maximum prospective fault current at the point where the device is installed.' The determination/measurement obligation appears under Reg 643.7.3.201 ('[BS 7671] · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.7.3.201 · [requirement] · Measurement of prospective short-circuit and earth fault current at origin'): 'The prospective short-circuit current and prospective earth fault current shall be measured, calculated or determined at the origin of the installation.' A separate generic procedure facet ('Determination of prospective fault current at relevant points') also ties the 'every relevant point' language to Reg 434.1, not 434.5.1. No RAG facet places the determination sentence inside 434.5.1. The over-attribution is confirmed.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Reg 434.5.1 Note 1 allows a device with lower breaking capacity if a backup protective device with adequate breaking capacity is installed upstream.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The substance of the backup-protection permission is RAG-grounded in an OSG facet ('[OSG] · Ch7 · Reg 7.2.7 · [condition] · Downstream lower breaking capacity conditioned on upstream device capability'): 'A downstream protective device with a lower rated breaking capacity is permitted only where an upstream device permits this.' However, no RAG facet attributes this permission as 'Note 1' within Reg 434.5.1 specifically. The RAG facets for Reg 434.5.1 cover (a) the device-capability obligation and (b) the overload-only exception referencing Reg 432.2 — neither surfaces a Note 1 backup-protection permission inside 434.5.1. The permission exists in the RAG but under OSG Ch.7, not attributed to 434.5.1 Note 1. Attributing it specifically to '434.5.1 Note 1' is unverified by the RAG and is a confirmed mis-citation of reg location.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page attributes fault current determination requirement to 'Regulation 434.5.1 and Regulation 643.7' — the body text blends them, implying 434.5.1 covers determination when in fact only 643.7.3.201 does.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG is unambiguous on the split: Reg 643.7.3.201 ('[BS 7671] · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.7.3.201 · [requirement]') carries the determination/measurement obligation ('shall be measured, calculated or determined at the origin...and at other relevant points'). A generic procedure facet ('Determination of prospective fault current at relevant points') ties the 'every relevant point' language to Reg 434.1 (not 434.5.1). Reg 434.5.1 RAG facets deal exclusively with device breaking capacity. Citing both regs is not itself wrong, but presenting 434.5.1 as the source of the determination obligation (as the page does in the blended quoted passage) is a confirmed mis-attribution — the same root issue as Claim 1, separately confirmed by cross-checking Reg 433.4.2 and the standalone procedure facets which all anchor determination in 434.1/643.7.3.201.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 0.80 site-test correction factor: measured loop impedance at site should be multiplied by 0.8 (or the measured Ips divided by 0.8) when comparing against tabulated maximum Zs values to allow for conductor temperature at 20°C vs 70°C at full load. The page's worked examples and calculator description make no mention of this factor, which is critical for correct compliance checking.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.29 — EFLI tester range and measurement guidance; GN3 Reg 1.8 reference to the 0.8 test-factor principle

content_gaps.missing: Distinction between Ips (peak prospective fault current) and Isc (steady-state short-circuit current) is absent. GN3 uses the symbol Ips and notes the instrument displays the peak value. The page uses Ipf throughout without explaining this symbol relationship or that modern instruments display Ips directly.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.29 — 'Use the prospective fault current range of a suitable EFLI tester; the basic measuring principle is the same as for EFLI testers'

content_gaps.missing: The requirement that Ips must be determined at every point where a protective device must operate — not just the origin — is mentioned in passing but not explained with the GN3 definition of 'every relevant point'. Adding this with the GN3 citation would strengthen the page for inspection professionals.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.29 — 'every relevant point shall mean every point where a protective device is required to operate under fault conditions'

content_gaps.missing: OSG guidance that the installer must establish Ze from the distributor before starting work, and that a loop impedance test at the supply terminals is required when the distributor does not provide Ze. This is a practical gap — the page only mentions measuring at the origin, not the distributor-enquiry step.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 1.3 — 'Before starting work…establish with the local electricity distributor the typical maximum external earth fault loop impedance (Ze)'

The page title tag uses a pipe-separated format which is fine. The page does not use HowToSchema's estimated time or supply fields which could add richer snippet data.

top_improvements.action: Correct the dual-attribution error: the page attributes the fault current determination mandate ('shall be determined at every relevant point') to Reg 434.5.1 in the body text, but the RAG shows this obligation sits in Reg 643.7.3.201. Separate the two regulations with correct citations — 434.5.1 for device breaking capacity, 643.7.3.201 for the measurement/determination requirement at the origin and every relevant point.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 643.7.3.201 and Reg 434.5.1 — RAG facets confirm the split

top_improvements.action: Add a paragraph (and calculator note) about the GN3 0.80 test-temperature correction factor. When a measured impedance at 20°C is compared against a tabulated maximum Zs, the measured value must be divided by 0.8 (or the table value multiplied by 0.8) to account for conductor temperature at full load. The worked examples currently show raw Uo/Z without this step, which could lead users to a false sense of compliance.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 2.29 — EFLI tester guidance and 0.8-factor measurement principle

top_improvements.action: Add a direct-answer paragraph immediately below the H1 (before the CTA buttons): 'Prospective fault current (Ipf) is calculated as Uo / Zloop — for UK single-phase this is 230 V divided by the measured loop impedance in ohms. BS 7671 Reg 434.5.1 requires every protective device to have a breaking capacity at least equal to the prospective fault current at its point of installation.' This satisfies Google's featured-snippet answer-first expectation which the current hero section misses.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 434.5.1 — 'maximum prospective fault current at the point where the device is installed'

top_improvements.action: Replace the unverified 'Regulation 434.5.1 Note 1' backup-protection claim in the FAQ with the correct cross-reference to Reg 536.1 and Reg 536.5. These are the actual regulations governing energy let-through coordination when a downstream device has insufficient breaking capacity, as confirmed by Reg 533.2.2 in the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Government funding age threshold inconsistency: FAQ says 'aged 24 or over' (line 82) while costs section says 'aged 19 or over' (line 496)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Direct inspection of NVQLevel3ElectricalPage.tsx confirms the internal contradiction. Line 82 (faqs array, index 6, 'How much does the NVQ Level 3 cost?' answer): 'Government funding may be available for eligible candidates through Adult Skills funding (for those aged 24 or over in England).' Line 496 (costs section bullet): 'Adult Skills funding (for those aged 19 or over in England) may cover some or all of the NVQ cost'. These two statements are on the same page and directly contradict each other. The RAG has no content on Adult Skills funding eligibility (zero rows returned), so neither figure can be grounded in the RAG — but the contradiction is self-evident from the source. The '19 or over' figure in the costs section (line 496) is the more accurate DfE threshold for Advanced Learner Loan and co-funding eligibility; the '24 or over' in the FAQ (line 82) is incorrect and must be corrected to '19 or over' to match DfE rules and the page's own costs section.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 521.10 of BS 7671 requires fire alarm wiring to use fire-resistant cable
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh RAG queries confirm Reg 521.10.202 is located in Chapter 52 (Selection and Erection) and 'gives requirements for the methods of support of wiring systems' — a mechanical-support regulation, not a fire-resistance requirement. The correct locus for fire-resistant cabling on safety-service circuits is Chapter 56: Reg 560.5.1 facets return 'To comply with Regulation 560.5.1 when safety services must operate through fire conditions, the installer shall use appropriately fire-resistant or fire-tested cable systems', and Reg 560.8.1 facets list the permitted wiring systems (mineral-insulated cable BS EN 60702, fire-resistant cables per IEC 60331 series, BS EN 50200, or a wiring system maintaining necessary fire and mechanical protection). No facet links Reg 521.10 to fire-resistance of cables. The page's citation of Reg 521.10 for fire-resistant cable is a clearly wrong reg cite.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.1.2 of BS 7671 governs main protective bonding conductors connecting the MET to all metallic services
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh RAG query on Reg 411.3.1.2 returns only disconnection-time facets: 'Regulation 411.3.1.2 establishes which categories of final circuits the maximum disconnection times in Table 41.1 shall apply to — socket-outlet circuits up to 63 A and fixed-load circuits up to 32 A.' It contains nothing about protective bonding. A separate query on Reg 544.1.2 returns: 'Regulation 544.1.2 requires that the main protective bonding connection to any extraneous-conductive-part (for example gas, water or other metallic pipework or service) shall be made as near as practicable to the point of entry of that part into the premises.' Reg 544.11 facets additionally govern cross-sectional area selection of main protective bonding conductors. The page's citation of Reg 411.3.1.2 for main protective bonding is a clearly wrong reg cite; the correct regulation is 544.1.2 (Chapter 54).

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Appendix 12 provides the voltage drop limits — 3% on sub-main circuits and 5% total from origin
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet returns any content containing 'Appendix 12'. Queries on voltage drop limits return Regs 525.202 and 525.203, which explicitly cite 'Appendix 4, Section 6.4' as the home of voltage drop limits: 'The requirements are deemed to be satisfied if the voltage drop does not exceed the limits given in Appendix 4, Section 6.4.' A further facet under Reg 722.411.4.1 confirms Appendix 14 now holds prospective fault current determination (moved from Zs/ring), and no appendix numbered 12 appears anywhere in the A4:2026 structure. Appendix 12 does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The 3%/5% values are a recognised industry convention but the Appendix 12 cite is a stale 17th-edition reference that is factually wrong.

content_gaps.missing: Correct regulation for fire-resistant cable in safety services (Chapter 56, Reg 560.8.1 and 560.5.1) — the page wrongly cites Reg 521.10 instead. The page should cite Reg 560.8.1 listing BS EN 60702-1 MICC, IEC 60331-series, BS EN 50200, and BS 8491 as permitted cable types.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Chapter 56 · Reg 560.5.1 and 560.8.1 — wiring systems for safety services in fire conditions

content_gaps.missing: Correct citation for main protective bonding — the page cites Reg 411.3.1.2 (which is Table 41.1 disconnection times) when it should cite Reg 544.1.2 (connection near point of entry) and Reg 542.3.2 (MET connections).
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Chapter 54 · Reg 544.1.2 and Reg 542.3.2

content_gaps.missing: Voltage drop reference should be Appendix 4 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, not 'Appendix 12'. Appendix 12 does not exist in the 18th edition. The internal link also points to a stale 17th-edition guide.

confirmed_issues.claim: All consumer units in domestic premises must be constructed from non-combustible material (typically metal) under Amendment 4 of BS 7671:2018 — key-takeaways section says 'Amendment 4', but the same page's FAQ block says 'Amendment 1'.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms Reg 421.1.201 in bs7671_facets (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.201 · [requirement] · Enclosure manufactured from non-combustible material') mandates non-combustible enclosures for domestic consumer units. However, no RAG facet records which amendment introduced 421.1.201 — the facets only index the current A4:2026 edition text. The internal contradiction is independently evidenced: the same page's FAQ block correctly attributes this to Amendment 1 (the industry-accepted fact), while the key-takeaways block says 'Amendment 4'. The RAG does not resolve introduction history but the contradiction is confirmed by the auditor's own page evidence. The page contains internally inconsistent attribution — one block will be wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 requires a risk assessment for surge protection (Regulation 443) on all new installations and alterations.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns detailed Reg 443.4 facets (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 44 · Reg 443.4'). The regulation is conditional, not universal: SPD protection is mandatory only where specific harm criteria apply (443.4(a)–(d): risk to life, public services, cultural heritage, commercial interruption, large co-located groups). For all other cases a risk assessment must determine whether protection is needed. There is an explicit exception: 'no transient protection for single dwelling units in certain situations'. Framing this as a requirement 'on all new installations and alterations' materially overstates Reg 443.4 — a domestic single-dwelling may be entirely exempt, and the regulation does not mention alterations as a trigger.

confirmed_issues.claim: Warning notices Regulation 514.12 — SAFETY ELECTRICAL CONNECTION and RCD test quarterly notice must be permanently fixed at the consumer unit.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets confirm the 'Safety Electrical Connection – Do Not Remove' notice requirement exists, but it is attributed to Reg 514.13.1 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 51 · Reg 514.13.1 · A warning notice clearly and durably marked with the words Safety Electrical Connection'), not Reg 514.12. Separate facets for Reg 514.12 confirm it covers 'notices: periodic inspection and testing' — a different subject entirely. The page incorrectly cites Reg 514.12 as the home of the 'Safety Electrical Connection' notice; the correct regulation is 514.13.1. The RCD quarterly-test notice attribution to 514.12 also cannot be grounded from any returned facet.

confirmed_issues.claim: All outdoor lighting circuits must be protected by a 30mA RCD under BS 7671 Regulation 411.3.3
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns three bs7671 facets for Reg 411.3.3 all stating the same scope: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception is met.' The regulation is explicitly limited to socket-outlets; hard-wired outdoor luminaire circuits are not covered by 411.3.3. A4:2026 added Reg 411.3.4 which mandates 30mA RCD for domestic AC final circuits supplying luminaires (confirmed by six separate 411.3.4 facets). The page conflates two distinct regulations: 411.3.3 (sockets ≤32A) and 411.3.4 (domestic lighting final circuits).

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD test button should cause the RCD to trip within 40 milliseconds
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple GN3 facets (Reg 2.36, 2.31) confirm the test button verifies a mechanical trip — 'RCD test buttons should be operated to see that the RCD trips' — but no facet anywhere in the RAG specifies a 40ms criterion for a test-button press. GN3 explicitly distinguishes the test button (basic functional check) from formal instrument testing: 'the built-in test button proves a mechanical trip and some internal circuitry but is not a substitute for periodic full trip testing with an RCD tester that measures trip time.' No 40ms figure appears in any bs7671, gn3 or osg facet for the test-button procedure.

confirmed_issues.claim: Armoured cables (SWA) in gardens require minimum burial depth of 0.5 metres (500mm)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns no universal 0.5m minimum for SWA/armoured cables. Reg 522.8.10 and 730.521.101.3.2 both state the principle as 'sufficient depth to avoid foreseeable ground disturbance' with no fixed number. The 0.5m figure appears only as a NOTE to Reg 709.521.1.7 (marinas/berths context): 'A depth of 0.5 m is generally considered as a minimum depth to fulfil this requirement' — labelled as 'illustrative guidance and not an absolute regulatory limit.' For the more general underground distribution reg (730.521.101.3.2), NOTE 1 states 0.6 m as the generally considered minimum. Presenting 0.5m as a firm universal SWA rule is unsupported by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Cables in conduit require minimum burial depth of 0.45 metres (450mm)
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet returns a 0.45m figure for cables in conduit. A search across all content for '0.45' returns only cable separation distances (Table A444.2) and TN disconnection times — nothing about conduit burial depth. The RAG confirms conduit counts as 'additional mechanical protection' under Reg 730.521.101.3.2 and Reg 709.521.1.7 (which may allow reduced depth), but gives no specific conduit depth figure. The 0.45m figure is entirely absent from the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Warning tape must be laid 150-200mm above buried cables
confirmed_issues.evidence: A full-text search for 'warning tape', 'marker tape' and 'cable route' returns zero facets from bs7671_facets. A content search for '150' combined with 'tape' or 'marker' also returns nothing. The specific 150-200mm placement depth for cable route warning tape is completely absent from the RAG (bs7671, gn3 and osg all searched). Claim is unverified — the figure cannot be grounded in the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 411.3.3 covers all outdoor lighting circuits (not just socket circuits)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Three separate RAG facets (scope, requirement and definition facets for Reg 411.3.3 A4:2026) all confirm the regulation is limited to 'socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A.' One facet explicitly states: 'socket-outlets with a rated current greater than 32 A are not covered... and must be considered against other applicable Regulations.' Hard-wired garden lighting final circuits (not socket-fed) fall under Reg 411.3.4 (domestic luminaires) confirmed by six 411.3.4 facets. Attributing outdoor lighting circuit RCD requirements to 411.3.3 is directly contradicted by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Transformer replacement (plug-in) costs £20-£80; hard-wired £80-£200 all-in

No pricing rows for plug-in or hard-wired garden lighting transformer replacement exist in the RAG. The cost figures are unverifiable against the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: Buried cable depth — the page quotes specific figures (0.5 m SWA, 0.45 m conduit) as definitive rules. The page understates the required depth for standard conditions.

content_gaps.missing: RCD test-button trip time — the page states 40ms without RAG support. The correct verifiable criterion (from GN3/OSG) is that a 30mA RCD shall not trip in less than 0ms and shall trip within the operating time stated by the manufacturer (typically published as <300ms at rated current). The 40ms figure appears to be an error.

confirmed_issues.claim: Flat-rate VAT is 14.5% for electrical installation services in first year, 16.5% thereafter
confirmed_issues.evidence: The page states at line 367-368: 'typically 14.5% for electrical installation services in the first year, 16.5% thereafter'. Neither bs7671_facets nor practical_work_intelligence contains any VAT/HMRC data — this is correctly outside RAG scope. However, the auditor's HMRC analysis is accurate on its own terms: the HMRC flat-rate scheme for 'electrical installation services' is 12%, reduced to 11% in the first year via the standard 1% first-year discount. The page's 14.5%/16.5% figures are both wrong. 16.5% is the HMRC limited-cost-trader rate, which is a separate override for businesses whose VAT-inclusive goods spend is under 2% of turnover — it is not a sector-specific progression from 14.5%. The 14.5% figure does not correspond to any current HMRC flat-rate category for electrical work. The RAG cannot contradict or support this (HMRC domain), but the auditor's external evidence is well-grounded and the error is material: a VAT-registered electrician reading this page would set up their flat-rate scheme at the wrong percentage, potentially underpaying or overpaying HMRC. Confirmed issue on the strength of the auditor's HMRC evidence; RAG abstains.

confirmed_issues.claim: Join 1,000+ UK electricians using Elec-Mate (CTA section)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Direct SQL on the profiles table returned 963 total rows across all tiers (null: 552, electrician: 198, employer: 93, apprentice: 76, Electrician: 12, electrician_yearly: 13, founder: 9, trial: 4, business_ai: 3, others: 3). Total profiles (963) is below 1,000, so even the most generous reading — counting every account regardless of role — does not reach the claimed threshold. The CTA copy says '1,000+ UK electricians' (line 622), which is doubly wrong: (a) total accounts are 963, not 1,000+, and (b) electrician-tier accounts specifically (electrician + Electrician + electrician_yearly = 223) are far below 1,000. The CLAUDE.md database rules themselves state '683+ registered accounts' as a known figure, and the live count of 963 still does not clear 1,000. This is a confirmed issue: the marketing claim overstates both the total user count and the electrician-specific count.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDD / arc fault detection devices not mentioned anywhere on the page (content gap)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms Reg 421.1.7 (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, Part 4 Ch.42) using the exact wording: 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' Six facets on Reg 421.1.7 are present, confirming scope (AC final circuits), purpose (fire mitigation), and that the wording is advisory not mandatory. Additionally, Reg 710.421.1.201 specifies AFDD requirements in special locations, and Chapter 53 Reg 538.4 covers AFDD selection. A commercial kitchen is a high-fire-risk environment — exactly the type of location where A4:2026 guidance directs AFDD consideration. The page's complete omission of AFDDs is a genuine content gap against current A4:2026 guidance. The auditor correctly identified this as a confirmed issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: SPD (surge protective device) provisions not mentioned on the page (content gap)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG strongly confirms the issue. Reg 443.4(c) (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, Part 4 Ch.44) states that where transient overvoltage could result in 'interruption of commercial or industrial activity', protection against transient overvoltages shall be provided — using mandatory 'shall' language. A commercial restaurant kitchen with significant connected equipment (refrigeration, comms, EPOS, extraction controls) directly satisfies this criterion. Reg 443.4(a) additionally covers risk to human life, and Reg 443.4(d) covers large numbers of co-located individuals. Section 534 (completely revised in A4:2026 per RAG facet) and Reg 534.4.10 give detailed SPD installation requirements (Type 1 min 16mm² PE conductor, Type 2 min 6mm²). The RAG facet for Reg 534 scope explicitly states it 'focuses mainly on the requirements for the selection and erection of Surge Protective Devices (SPDs) for protection against transient overvoltages where such protection is required by Section 443.' A best-practice commercial kitchen guide that omits any SPD risk assessment discussion is deficient against current A4:2026 mandatory requirements. Confirmed issue — stronger than a mere content gap.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD recommendation (Reg 421.1.7, A4:2026): Commercial kitchens with high fire-load circuits are prime candidates for the A4:2026 AFDD recommendation. The page should note that installers should consider AFDDs on final circuits in high fire-risk zones.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'recommends the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.3 commercial non-dwelling RCD exception: The page states sockets 'must' have RCD protection without acknowledging the documented risk-assessment exception that applies to non-domestic premises. A sophisticated reader (electrical contractor) needs to know this.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3 exception facet: 'omission of RCD protection for socket-outlets (≤ 32 A) where, other than for a dwelling, a documented risk assessment determines that RCD protection is not necessary.'

content_gaps.missing: SPD consideration (Reg 534.4.1.6): No mention of surge protection for sensitive BMS / kitchen management controls. The regulations require consideration of SPDs for transient overvoltage sources, which is relevant in a commercial kitchen with large inductive motor loads.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 534.4.1.6: 'Consideration shall be given to the provision of SPDs to protect from other sources, such as [switching transients]'

content_gaps.missing: Prospective fault current measurement for 3-phase supplies: The page discusses 3-phase TPN distribution boards extensively but makes no mention of the requirement to measure Ipf at the board (live-to-live and live-to-earth). This is directly relevant to commissioning/certification.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 10.3.7: 'For three-phase supplies, prospective fault current measurements should be made at the distribution board between the relevant conductors (live-to-live and live-to-earth).'

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 3 adds one new regulation relating to bidirectional/unidirectional devices — the page omits any mention of the two new definitions introduced by A3:2024.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG facet at context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201 · [reference] · Amendment 3:2024 introduction and immediate implementation' states: 'This Amendment provides for the inclusion of two new definitions and one new Regulation relating to the use of bidirectional and unidirectional devices.' The two definitions are 'bidirectional protective device' and 'unidirectional protective device', both present as separate RAG facets under Reg 530.3.201 in Chapter 53. The page not mentioning these two new definitions is a real omission and leaves readers with an incomplete picture of what A3:2024 added.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4 is expected in 2026 (not yet published, no precise date confirmed) — the page's FAQ treats A4 as a future event.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG facet at context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201 · [reference] · Amendment 4:2026 issuance and withdrawal timeline' states explicitly: 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 was issued on 15th April 2026 and may be implemented immediately. BS 7671:2018+A2:2022+Corrigendum (May 2023)+A3:2024 remains current but will be withdrawn on 15th October 2026.' A4 has been published and is already in effect. Any FAQ language treating it as forthcoming or unconfirmed is factually wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: Reg 551.7.1(d) prohibits connecting a source to the load side of an RCD under certain conditions — the page makes no mention of this indent at all.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms Reg 551.7.1(d) exists and prohibits 'the connection of a source to the load side of an RCD under certain conditions' (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 55 · Reg 551.7.1 · [requirement] · 551.7.1(d) prohibits connecting a source to RCD load side under conditions'). The RAG also confirms this indent was added as part of the A4:2026 redraft of 551.7.1. A page covering the amendment landscape that omits 551.7.1(d) leaves out a substantive A4:2026 requirement directly relevant to prosumer/generation installations. The page should reference this even if its primary focus is A3.

confirmed_issues.claim: Voltage drop limit: 3% for lighting, 5% for other circuits (Regulation 525)
confirmed_issues.evidence: The 3%/5% figures ARE grounded in the RAG — bs7671 facet for Reg 125.8 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 1 · Chapter 12 · Reg 125.8 · [requirement] · Voltage drop limits from origin: Lighting 3% / Other 5% (public LV)') states verbatim: 'the recommended maxima are 3% for lighting and 5% for other uses.' The values themselves are correct. However the regulation cited on the page is wrong: the 3%/5% figures live in Reg 125.8 and Table 4Ab / Appendix 4 Section 6.4, not in Regulation 525. Reg 525.202 merely says voltage drop 'shall not exceed the values stated in Appendix 4, Section 6.4' without quoting the percentages. Citing 'Regulation 525' as the source of the 3%/5% limits is a mis-citation that would mislead readers checking the standard.

confirmed_issues.claim: 6 mm² 6242Y twin-and-earth: Iz approximately 47 A (clipped direct, 30°C ambient, no grouping)
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671 facet 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Appendix 4 Table 4D1A PVC T+E 70°C Method C clipped direct' states: 'Maximum current-carrying capacity (Iz) for 6 mm² PVC-insulated 70°C twin and earth (T+E) cable installed Method C (clipped direct) is 41 A.' The page claims 47 A. The RAG also contains a separate facet for 'Table 4D4A SWA 3-core XLPE 90°C Method C' showing 47 A for 6 mm² three-phase SWA — a different cable type entirely. The page's 47 A figure belongs to XLPE armoured SWA, not 6242Y flat T+E. Overstating Iz by 6 A (15%) for this cable type is a meaningful design error.

confirmed_issues.claim: Appendix 12 — voltage drop limits for lighting (3%) and other circuits (5%)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero facets exist for 'Appendix 12' anywhere in bs7671_facets (query: context_prefix ilike '%Appendix 12%' returned 0 rows). The 3%/5% voltage drop limits are grounded in Reg 125.8 / Table 4Ab: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 12 · Reg 125.8 — The voltage drop between the origin of an installation and any load point should not be greater than the values in Table 4Ab… the recommended maxima are 3% for lighting and 5% for other uses.' Appendix 12 does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026; the page is citing a non-existent appendix number.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 530.3.2 for bidirectional protective devices in installations with solar PV, battery storage, or V2G capability
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms Reg 530.3.2 exists but covers an entirely different topic: neutral contact sequencing in multipole switching devices — 'The moving contacts of multipole switching devices that are marked for the connection of the neutral or midpoint shall be permitted to close before the other contacts / open after the other contacts.' (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 530.3.2'). Bidirectional protective devices for PV/battery/V2G are covered by Reg 530.3.201: 'Selection and erection of equipment for protection shall take account of appropriate use of either a unidirectional protective device or a bidirectional protective device.' Citing 530.3.2 for bidirectional devices is materially wrong — it is a different regulation with a different subject.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4:2024 — referenced as the edition of BS 7671 containing Reg 530.3.201
confirmed_issues.evidence: Query 'select count(*) from bs7671_facets where context_prefix ilike \'%A4:2024%\'' returned 0. Every facet in the RAG (all 100% of them) uses the designation 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026'. Example: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 — Selection and Erection of Equipment · Chapter 53 · Reg 530.3.201'. There is no 'Amendment 4:2024' in this standard; the correct label is A4:2026.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — mandatory 30 mA RCD protection on ALL domestic lighting circuits. The page discusses RCD requirements generically under Chapter 41 but never surfaces this A4:2026 addition, which is one of the most significant compliance changes for domestic consumer unit design.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — AFDD recommendation for final circuits supplying socket-outlets. Page covers SPDs (Section 443) and mentions A4 changes but never mentions AFDDs, which is a key A4:2026 addition affecting domestic design.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs).' Also Reg 411.9.3 requires installer to confirm AFDD indication operational.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 530.3.201 correctly described — bidirectional vs unidirectional protective device distinction including product standard marking requirement (relevant for PV/battery/V2G installations). Page cites wrong reg number and omits the 'in'/'out' orientation marking detail.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 530.3.201: 'selection and erection of equipment for protection shall take account of appropriate use of either a unidirectional protective device or a bidirectional protective device' + note on product standard orientation markings.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 site-test correction factor (Cin/0.80 factor) — the inspector's use of temperature-adjusted Zs when comparing measured values to Table 41.1 limits. Relevant to the claim that AI verifies earth fault loop impedance 'from Table 41.1' — the actual acceptance criterion requires the adjustment.
