confirmed_issues.claim: Grade A = full panel-based system with fire-resistant FP200 or MICC cabling; Grade D1 = mains-powered interlinked by hardwiring; Grade D2 = mains-powered interlinked by radio; Grade F1 = battery-only interlinked by radio; Grade F2 = battery-only standalone
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG partially contradicts the Grade D2 and F1/F2 definitions. PWI explicitly states: 'D1 incorporates a tamper-proof battery and D2 a user-replaceable battery' — D2 is defined by battery type (user-replaceable), NOT by radio interlinking. Separately: 'F1/F2 describe detectors powered by tamper-proof or user-replaceable batteries respectively — used when mains power is not provided.' F1 = tamper-proof battery, F2 = user-replaceable battery; neither is defined as 'standalone'. The page's characterisation of D2 as 'mains-powered interlinked by radio' and F1 as 'battery-only interlinked by radio' / F2 as 'standalone' does not match the RAG's battery-type distinction. Grade A as 'full panel-based' is mentioned in passing in the RAG's A-F range overview but no contradiction exists there.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD testing — testing each RCD at 1x, 5x, and (where applicable) 1/2x rated residual operating current.
confirmed_issues.evidence: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 643.3 has been redrafted in A4. Multiple returned facets confirm: (1) 'An alternating current test at rated residual operating current (IΔn) shall be used to verify the effectiveness of the RCD, as revised in Regulation 643.3.' (2) 'Table 3A (Time/current performance criteria for RCDs) in Appendix 3 has been deleted. Therefore, the previous requirement to test according to that table is no longer applicable; instead the single alternating current test at IΔn is used for verification regardless of type.' The 5x IΔn step (previously in Appendix 3 Table 3A) has been removed by A4:2026. The GN3 RCD-testers definition (GN3 Reg 4.2) does reference '0.5 x IΔn, IΔn, and 5 x IΔn as applicable' as tester capabilities, and the OSG confirms a 50% no-trip check, but the operative BS 7671 Reg 643.3 A4:2026 now mandates only a single AC test at IΔn. The page's claim that inspectors test at '1x, 5x, and 1/2x' overstates current A4:2026 BS 7671 requirements and references a test regime (5x step from old Table 3A) that has been deleted.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the GN3 0.80 site-correction factor to be applied to measured Zs values when comparing against tabulated maximum values. This is a critical practical detail for inspectors that best-in-class EICR guides include.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) site test factor / Zs correction

No mention of the A4:2026 requirement for RCD protection on domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4) — a significant change that surveyors must assess and report on, especially relevant to the 'what will the survey check' section.

content_gaps.missing: The RCD testing description mentions '1x, 5x, and 1/2x' multiples. GN3/OSG facets confirm the Schedule of Test Results records trips at 100% and 180% of rated residual current. content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — [GN3] Chapter 2 Reg 2.32 RCD trip time schedule columns

confirmed_issues.claim: Gaming machine circuits in public areas must be protected by 30mA RCD in accordance with Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns eight facets for Reg 411.3.3 A4:2026. The [exception] facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3 · [exception] · Exception allows omission of RCD where documented risk assessment finds it unnecessary') states: 'There is an exception in the revised Regulation 411.3.3 permitting omission of RCD protection for socket-outlets (≤32A) where, other than for a dwelling, a documented risk assessment determines that RCD protection is not necessary.' A pub is a non-dwelling. The page's blanket 'must be protected by 30mA RCD' framing ignores the A4:2026 risk-assessment exception route and is a misrepresentation of the current regulation. The OSG also confirms the risk assessment must be appended to the EIC. A compliant A4:2026 page must state that 30mA RCD is required unless a documented risk assessment (appended to the EIC) determines it unnecessary.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.3 requires 30mA RCD protection on gaming machine circuits (the reg number is cited as authority for a blanket commercial RCD mandate)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets for Reg 411.3.3 A4:2026 confirm the regulation has been revised. The [requirement] facet states RCD protection applies to ≤32A socket-outlets 'unless an express exception in the Regulation is met'. The [exception] facet states omission is permitted for non-dwellings where a documented risk assessment determines RCD protection is not necessary. The [condition] facet confirms the exception is 'limited to situations other than for a dwelling'. Citing Reg 411.3.3 as authority for a blanket commercial RCD mandate misrepresents the A4:2026 text — the regulation is a default-on requirement with an explicit non-dwelling risk-assessment exception, not a mandatory rule without qualification. The OSG further requires the risk assessment to be appended to the EIC (context_prefix: '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 7 · Reg 7.2.5 · [requirement] · Risk assessment must be appended to the appropriate Electrical Installation Certificate (EIC)').

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 revision to Reg 411.3.3: the page presents RCD protection on commercial socket-outlets as a blanket mandatory requirement, but A4:2026 introduced a risk-assessment exception route for non-dwellings. Page should note that a documented risk assessment (recorded on the EIC/EICR per Reg 120.3) can justify omission, and that the revised regulation changes the EICR inspector's assessment criteria for commercial premises.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 411.3.3 · [exception] + [BS 7671] · Reg 120.3 · [requirement] · Details of permitted exceptions and risk assessment attachment

This is a practical detail electricians would value.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 706 applies to wet kitchen environments ('restrictive conductive locations — relevant to wet kitchen environments')
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Reg 704.537 [condition] facet is explicit: 'The requirements in Section 706 apply where a location is both conducting (containing conductive parts that could become live) and has restricted movement for persons. If either conducting parts are absent or movement is not restricted, Section 706 provisions may not apply.' A commercial catering kitchen is not a conducting location with restricted movement — persons move freely and the location is not enclosed by conductive parts. The page misnames the section ('restrictive conductive locations') and misapplies it; a commercial kitchen is not in scope of Section 706. Additionally Reg 704.537 [scope] confirms the full title is 'Conducting locations with restricted movement', not 'restrictive conductive locations'. Confirmed mis-labelling and mis-application.

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR is required at least every five years for commercial premises
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple Reg 652.1 facets (document_type='bs7671', context_prefix 'Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 65 · Reg 652.1') confirm the frequency 'shall be determined having regard to the type of installation', equipment present, use and operation, frequency of maintenance, external influences, and previous condition reports. No facet in bs7671_facets or GN3 sets a hard 5-year maximum for commercial premises. GN3 Reg 1.3 (from earlier audit) links 5 years to the private rented sector residential regulations only. Presenting 5 years as a statutory requirement for commercial premises is unsupported by the RAG and is therefore a confirmed issue with the page claim.

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR is assessed under BS 7671 Section 631
confirmed_issues.evidence: A distinct-prefixes query on Part 6 returns only Chapter 62, Chapter 64, and Chapter 65 — there is no Chapter 63 or Section 631 in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The EICR is governed by Chapter 65 (Regs 651.1, 651.4, 652.1, 652.2). Reg 651.1 [note] facet states: 'Periodic inspection and testing is commonly performed as an Electrical Installation Condition Report (EICR).' Reg 652.2 [note] states the EICR shall be produced to the model in Appendix 6. 'Section 631' does not exist in A4 edition numbering — this is stale 17th-edition or entirely incorrect numbering. Confirmed issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDD (Reg 421.1.7) not mentioned anywhere on the page — content gap for a high-fire-risk commercial kitchen
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns 8 facets for Reg 421.1.7, all from document_type='bs7671', confirming: (1) Reg 421.1.7 was introduced by Amendment A4:2026; (2) it recommends AFDD installation 'in AC final circuits of a fixed installation'; (3) its stated purpose is 'to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents'; (4) wording is recommendatory ('recommending') not mandatory. A commercial catering kitchen is a high-fire-risk environment with dense fixed wiring for high-load cooking equipment — precisely the scenario where A4:2026's AFDD recommendation is most relevant. The page's complete omission of this A4:2026 addition is a confirmed content gap/issue.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Reg 421.1.7) — A4:2026 recommends arc fault detection devices on AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk. Directly relevant to a commercial catering kitchen given fire-risk environment; page makes no mention.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 scope: 'recommends AFDD installation in AC final circuits of a fixed installation'

content_gaps.missing: Section 706 is titled 'Conducting locations with restricted movement', NOT 'restrictive conductive locations for wet kitchens'. The correct special section for commercial kitchen wet areas is standard Part 4 protection measures. The page should either correct this or remove the section reference.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 704.537 scope: 'Section 706 titled Conducting locations with restricted movement — locations where movement is restricted AND conducting parts present'

content_gaps.missing: EICR interval for commercial premises is guidance-based (determined by installation type per Reg 652.1), not a hard statutory 5-year maximum. The 5-year statutory maximum applies specifically to private rented residential under the 2020 Regulations. The page should clarify this distinction.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 652.1: 'frequency shall be determined having regard to the type of installation'; [GN3] Reg 1.3: 5-year statutory maximum is for private rented sector England only

content_gaps.missing: EAWR (Electricity at Work Regulations) formal maintenance arrangements — GN3 explicitly states commercial/industrial installations require formal maintenance and interim routine check arrangements in addition to periodic EICR. The page mentions EAWR in one FAQ but omits this ongoing maintenance obligation in the EICR section.

confirmed_issues.claim: At the basic tax rate, a GBP 2,000 tool kit reduces your tax bill by GBP 580
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG source covers tax, so this cannot be grounded or contradicted by bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence. However the arithmetic is independently and unambiguously wrong: UK basic rate income tax is 20%. 20% of GBP 2,000 = GBP 400, not GBP 580. GBP 580 implies a 29% effective rate (580/2000), which does not correspond to any standard UK income tax band (basic 20%, higher 40%, additional 45%). The figure of GBP 580 would only be correct if the rate were 29% — a rate that does not exist. This is a factual arithmetic error in the page copy and constitutes a confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 0.8 site correction factor for measured Zs: the page discusses earth loop impedance testing as a multifunction tester function but never mentions that measured Zs values must be multiplied by 0.8 (or compared against 0.8 × tabulated maximum Zs) to account for temperature variation. This is a critical GN3 guidance point that determines whether a circuit passes or fails.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) — Chapter 3 on Zs measurement and Table acceptance criteria (site factor 0.8 applied to tabulated values)

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) as a new A4:2026 item of test equipment: Reg 421.1.7 introduced mandatory AFDD in certain new/rewired domestic circuits. Electricians now need a multifunction tester that can test AFDD operation, or a dedicated AFDD test function. The page makes no mention of AFDD testing tools.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 (AFDD requirement) and GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) test procedures

content_gaps.missing: RCD on domestic lighting circuits (A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 change): from A4:2026, RCD protection is required for all lighting circuits in domestic premises. This affects what the electrician's test equipment must verify and is relevant to the RCD tester discussion.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 — RCD protection for lighting circuits in dwellings

content_gaps.missing: Calibration certificate requirement: GN3 Reg 4.8 references that accuracy of instruments should be established and calibration certificates held. The page mentions calibration costs and sending instruments away but does not explicitly state the electrician must retain the calibration certificate with the instrument for scheme/LABC inspection.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) Reg 4.8 — instrument calibration and certificate of conformity

confirmed_issues.claim: If [RCD currents] differ by more than 30 mA, the RCD trips
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets directly contradict this framing. Reg 411.3.4 ([BS 7671] Part 4 Ch.41) states the RCD 'rated residual operating current shall not exceed 30 mA' — i.e. the device must operate AT OR BEFORE 30 mA of residual current, not 'more than 30 mA'. GN3 Reg 3.3 confirms: 'An RCD selected to provide additional protection shall have a rated residual operating current (IΔn) not exceeding 30 mA.' Reg 559.5.6 likewise requires 'a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA'. The page's wording 'differ by more than 30 mA' inverts the threshold: it implies the device only trips above 30 mA, whereas BS 7671 / GN3 require it to trip at ≤ 30 mA (i.e. it must have operated by the time imbalance reaches its rated IΔn). This is a technically incorrect statement that could mislead readers into thinking a 30 mA imbalance is required before the RCD acts.

content_gaps.missing: The 30 mA RCD rated residual operating current (IΔn) threshold for general-purpose RCDs under Reg 415.1.1 / 531.3 is stated imprecisely as 'if they differ by more than 30 mA'. The page should state the IΔn = 30 mA (operates at ≤30 mA, not greater than) and cite Reg 415.1.1.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — Reg 415.1.1 / Reg 531.3 (RCD operating current); query returned no facet but the regulatory requirement is well-defined in BS 7671

content_gaps.missing: No mention of Reg 524.2.1 / 524.2.3 requirements that the neutral must not be undersized and conditions under which neutral overcurrent protection per Reg 431.2 applies. A borrowed neutral that carries double the design current may breach Reg 524.2.1.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — [BS 7671] Reg 524.2.1 Note and Reg 524.2.3 Condition (b)

content_gaps.missing: No mention of Reg 431.2.1 nuance: disconnection under overcurrent operates on LINE conductors, not necessarily the neutral — which explains why the MCB on the live side does NOT protect the shared neutral section. This is the regulatory grounding for the practical claim about unprotected neutral.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — [BS 7671] Reg 431.2.1 Note: 'overcurrent detection shall cause disconnection of the line conductors, but not necessarily of the neutral conductor'

content_gaps.missing: No explicit citation of GN3 Chapter 3 EICR coding definitions (C1/C2/C3/FI) with the C2 definition ('potentially dangerous, requires remedial action'). The page uses C2 correctly but without a GN3 reference to give it authority.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — [GN3] Reg 3.11: C1/C2/C3/FI coding purpose for EICR Section K observations

confirmed_issues.claim: Since January 2016, Amendment 4 to BS 7671 mandated that all new domestic consumer units must be housed in a non-combustible (metal) enclosure.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The non-combustible enclosure requirement is confirmed by Reg 421.1.201 facets (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.201 · Enclosure manufactured from non-combustible material'). However, the page attributes this January 2016 mandate to 'Amendment 4'. No RAG facet supports this — A4:2026 is the current amendment to BS 7671:2018 and is entirely distinct from the 2015 amendment to the 17th Edition. The requirement was introduced by Amendment 3 to BS 7671:2008 (A3:2015), which came into effect January 2016. Calling it 'Amendment 4' is historically incorrect and no RAG source supports that framing. The RAG contains only A4:2026 content and makes no claim that A4 introduced the metal enclosure rule.

confirmed_issues.claim: An Electrical Installation Certificate (EIC) must be issued after every consumer unit replacement, as confirmed by Regulation 421.1.201.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The EIC requirement is confirmed as correct in substance: Reg 644.4.201 facets (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 644.4.201') explicitly state 'Regulation 644.4.201 applies, upon completion of the verification of the replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit' and 'An Electrical Installation Certificate based on the model given in Appendix 6 shall be issued to the person ordering the work.' However, the page attributes this obligation to Reg 421.1.201, which covers only enclosure material and BS EN 61439-3 compliance. No 421.1.201 facet mentions EIC issuance. The correct citation is Reg 644.4.201, not 421.1.201. The attribution is wrong and constitutes a confirmed issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: The risk assessment under Regulation 443.4 almost always results in SPD installation being necessary for domestic properties.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 443.4 facets (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 44 · Reg 443.4') show SPD protection is only mandatorily required for specific consequence criteria (a) human life risk, (b) public services/cultural heritage, (c) commercial/industrial interruption, (d) large numbers of co-located individuals. For all other cases — which includes typical domestic dwellings — 'a risk assessment has to be performed in order to determine if protection against transient overvoltage is required.' Critically, a further RAG facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [exception] · Exception: single dwelling units may be excepted from SPD requirement') states that single dwelling units may be excepted from the requirement in certain situations. The RAG directly contradicts the page's claim that the assessment 'almost always' results in SPDs being necessary for domestic properties — the standard is a structured consequence-based assessment with an explicit single-dwelling exception, not a near-universal presumption of necessity.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 528.3 requires secure connections for meter tails.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 528.3.4 facets (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 52 · Reg 528.3.4') confirm that 528.3.4 covers requirements where an electrical service is in close proximity to non-electrical services (gas pipes, water mains, etc.), requiring suitable protection against hazards and fault protection per Section 411. No facet in 528.3 or 528.3.4 mentions meter tails or secure connections for meter tails. A broad tsv search for 'meter tails connection sizing secure' also returned zero rows. The citation of Reg 528.3 for meter tail secure connections is unsupported by the RAG and appears to be an incorrect or invented citation.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Devices) requirement — Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026 addition) permits/recommends inclusion of AFDDs in consumer units to mitigate arc fault ignition risk. The page makes no mention of AFDDs at all, despite this being a significant A4:2026 addition directly relevant to consumer unit specification.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) Reg 2.2.5: 'A consumer unit assembly may include arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the effect of an arc fault, which might include the arc being a source of ignition.' OSG Reg 3.1: 'AFDDs detect the signature of hazardous arc faults and operate to disconnect the circuit to reduce the risk of fire from arcing.'

content_gaps.missing: The precise consequence-based risk assessment procedure under Section 443 for SPD decisions — the page oversimplifies this as 'almost always required.' The RAG shows Section 443 uses a structured consequence-based method (risk to life, safety circuits, loss of service, economic loss). A best-in-class guide should explain the four AQ consequence categories.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 422.2.1: 'the decision procedure in Section 443 requires a consequence-based assessment to determine whether protection against transient overvoltages is necessary.'

content_gaps.missing: The alternative enclosure option — that a plastic consumer unit may be placed inside a non-combustible cabinet (per Reg 132.12) as an alternative to having the enclosure itself be non-combustible. The page implies only metal enclosures are acceptable, missing this compliant alternative.
content_gaps.rag_source: Reg 421.1.201 facet: 'As an alternative to having the consumer unit enclosure manufactured from non-combustible material, the consumer unit or similar switchgear assembly shall be enclosed in a cabinet or enclosure constructed of non-combustible material and complying with Regulation 132.12.'

content_gaps.missing: The Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate (MIEWC) distinction — the page states an EIC is required for all CU replacements, but Reg 120.3 clarifies that if no new circuits are introduced, a MIEWC may be used. For a pure like-for-like swap with no circuit additions, this distinction is technically important.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 120.3: 'For an addition or alteration which does not extend to the introduction of new circuits, a Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate may be used instead of an Electrical Installation Certificate.'

Fix the historical attribution error: the January 2016 non-combustible enclosure requirement came from Amendment 3 to the 17th Edition (BS 7671:2008+A3:2015), not 'Amendment 4'. Amendment 4 = A4:2026. This is a factual error that undermines E-E-A-T credibility.

AFDDs detect the signature of hazardous arc faults and operate to disconnect the circuit to reduce the risk of fire from arcing.'

confirmed_issues.claim: The page describes a single combined schedule layout (no mention of the A4:2026 split into separate circuit-details and test-results pages).
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 72 · Reg 722.826.3.201 · [requirement] · Separate pages for schedule of circuit details and schedule of test results states verbatim: 'The single page generic schedule of test results used for EIC and EICR has been redrafted. There is now a separate page for the schedule of circuit details and a separate page for the schedule of test results. Practitioners shall use the updated separate pages when completing EIC/EICR documentation.' The page at /guides/schedule-of-test-results (ScheduleOfTestResultsPage.tsx) treats the schedule as a single unified document throughout — no section, FAQ, or key takeaway mentions the A4:2026 two-page split or the existence of a distinct 'schedule of circuit details'. This is a real gap: the page is structurally out of date with A4:2026.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 structural change: the single-page generic schedule has been split into two separate pages — one for circuit details and one for test results. This is a mandatory change for EIC/EICR documentation and is entirely absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 722.826.3.201 (A4:2026): 'The single page generic schedule of test results used for EIC and EICR has been redrafted. There is now a separate page for the schedule of circuit details and a separate page for the schedule of test results.'

content_gaps.missing: The requirement to record the test voltage on the schedule when an IR test is carried out at a reduced voltage (e.g. 250 V DC after connection of sensitive equipment). GN3 Reg 2.24 makes this mandatory for traceability.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 2.24: 'When an insulation resistance test is carried out at a reduced voltage (e.g. 250 V DC after connection of sensitive equipment), the test voltage shall be recorded on the schedule of test results. The recorded value provides traceability of the test condition used.'

content_gaps.missing: The 1×IΔn RCD test result must be recorded in the Schedule of Test Results as required by BS 7671 and GN3. The page mentions 'RCD trip times' generically but omits the specific 1×IΔn test requirement and its recording obligation.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 4.7: 'When the 1×IΔn RCD test is carried out (with instrument set to Type AC where applicable), the result shall be recorded in the Schedule of Test Results as required by BS 7671 and Guidance Note 3.'

content_gaps.missing: No reference to Section 643 of BS 7671 as the normative authority defining which tests are required and in what format. Linking the schedule to Part 6 Chapter 64 (Section 643) would add authority and search value.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 653.4: 'The tests and the format for Schedule(s) of Test Results required in the Report are detailed in Section 643 of BS 7671.'

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 522.6 governs cable safe zones in walls
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh RAG queries confirm that the only facets under Reg 522.6 are: (a) Reg 522.6.4 — IP/IK degree of protection of equipment after cable installation (completely unrelated to routing safe zones); (b) Reg 522.6.2 — mechanical impact protection for AG2/AG3 severity. No facet attributes the wall-cable-routing safe-zone requirement to a bare 'Reg 522.6'. The concept of safe zones is grounded in OSG 9th Ed:2022 A4 Reg 9.2.2 ('Inspection shall include checking the routing of cables in safe zones…') and GN3 Reg 2.10.1, but neither of those facets cites 522.6 as the underlying BS 7671 regulation for safe zones. The actual UK national sub-regulation (Reg 522.6.201, introduced in BS 7671:2008 for wall cable routing) is absent from the facets entirely — the only 522.6.201 facet in the RAG is Reg 753.522.6.201, which is about heating-free zones for floor/ceiling heating units, not cable routing. The page's shorthand citation of 'Regulation 522.6' for safe zones is therefore unsupported and misleading.

confirmed_issues.claim: Cable routes in walls should follow safe zones (referenced as BS 7671 Regulation 522.6)
confirmed_issues.evidence: The safe-zone concept is grounded: OSG Reg 9.2.2 confirms 'Inspection shall include checking the routing of cables in safe zones or that appropriate protection against mechanical damage is provided', and its definition facet describes safe zones as 'enclosed trunking, within walls above dado, or suitably protected ceiling voids'. However the specific Reg 522.6 citation is not supported — the only 522.6 sub-regs in the RAG are 522.6.2 (mechanical impact protection AG2/AG3) and 522.6.4 (IP/IK preservation after cable install). No facet maps the safe-zone requirement for wall routing to Reg 522.6. The concept is valid but the regulation citation attached to it is wrong in the same way as Claim 1.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 522.6.201 (UK national requirement) specifies the precise safe-zone routing rules for cables concealed in walls — permitted routes are horizontal from an outlet or fitting, vertical to an outlet or fitting, within 150mm of a wall angle, or within a zone formed above dado height. The page cites only 'Regulation 522.6' without the .201 sub-regulation and without describing the actual permitted routes. This is the most important practical BS 7671 detail an electrician needs when deciding where to drill.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) Chapter 9 Reg 9.2.2 and OSG Chapter 12 Reg 12.513 — confirm safe-zone inspection and mechanical protection requirements but Reg 522.6.201 detail not surfaced in facets returned

content_gaps.missing: The requirement for mechanical protection where cables are NOT routed in safe zones (e.g. RCD additional protection or earthed metallic conduit) is absent from the page. GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) explicitly states that concealed cables not in safe zones require mechanical protection — failure to cover this leaves electricians without the full picture of their compliance options.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) Chapter 5 Reg 5.9 — example EICR observation 'Need additional protection by RCD, or suitable mechanical protection, evidence for cables concealed in walls'

content_gaps.missing: RCD protection as a compensating measure for cables at risk in walls is not mentioned. This directly addresses the inspection/EICR implication — missing safe-zone routing is a C2/C3 code defect on an EICR.

top_improvements.action: Add a paragraph in the budget wall scanner section explicitly warning that metallic conduit installations (common in commercial/industrial properties pre-1980s) cannot be reliably scanned by either budget wall scanners or passive CAT modes, because the conduit masks the cable's EM field. confirmed_issues.claim: RCDs are required for additional protection on every final circuit of a group 2 medical location
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms the page is partially correct but materially incomplete: Reg 710.411.4.205 sub-clause (b) does require RCDs on ALL group 2 final circuits (no rated-current threshold), so that part of the page is supported. However, sub-clause (a) imposes a critical 32 A threshold for group I equipment: 'only final circuits supplying group I equipment with rated current not exceeding 32 A fall under sub-clause (a). Circuits with rated current above 32 A are not captured by this sub-clause' (context_prefix: Reg 710.411.4.205 [condition] Applicability condition: rated current threshold). If the page implies RCDs are universally required on ALL final circuits regardless of equipment group or current rating, it omits the 32 A ceiling for group I circuits and will mislead installers designing circuits for group I equipment rated above 32 A. The conflation of the two sub-clauses into a single blanket statement is a confirmed issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: GN3 recommends maximum periodic inspection interval of 1 year for hospitals and 3 years for surgeries/clinics
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple targeted queries against GN3 and OSG facets for medical location periodic inspection intervals returned no results. Queries used: 'medical location periodic inspection interval year', 'maximum period between inspections medical location', '1 year 3 years recommended interval' — all returned empty from GN3/OSG. The only GN3 medical/hospital facets found relate to continuity of supply during testing (GN3 Chapter 4 Reg 4.9), not inspection intervals. The 1 year / 3 year figure and the GN3 attribution cannot be verified from the RAG. Per the grounding rules, this remains unverified and cannot be presented as RAG-grounded content. The specific numeric intervals and their GN3 attribution are unverified — confirmed issue on the page.

content_gaps.missing: Supplementary protective equipotential bonding requirement (Regulations 710.415.2 and 710.419.3) — the page makes no mention of the patient environment bonding requirement, which is a fundamental Section 710 requirement for group 1 and 2 locations. The bonding must limit Uc to 25 V AC / 60 V DC where ADS cannot achieve required disconnection times.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] acceptance criterion facet: 'supplementary protective equipotential bonding installed in accordance with Regulations 710.415.2 and 710.419.3 shall be deemed to meet the requirement that Uc does not exceed 25 V AC or 60 V DC in medical locations of group 1 and 2 where ADS is not achievable.'

content_gaps.missing: PELV in group 2 operating theatres — luminaire bonding rule under Reg 710.414.4.1 (exposed-conductive-parts of luminaires supplied by PELV in group 2 shall be connected to the circuit protective conductor). The page discusses PELV voltage limits but omits this specific bonding requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 710.414.4.1 facet: 'operating theatre luminaires in medical locations of group 2 where PELV is used shall have their exposed-conductive-parts connected to the circuit protective conductor.'

content_gaps.missing: The 32 A threshold nuance in Reg 710.411.4.205(a): RCDs are required for group I medical equipment circuits rated not exceeding 32 A; circuits above 32 A supplying group I equipment are not captured by sub-clause (a). The page oversimplifies this as 'all final circuits of group 2 location'.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 710.411.4.205 facet: 'only final circuits supplying group I equipment with rated current not exceeding 32 A fall under sub-clause (a). Circuits with rated current above 32 A are not captured by this sub-clause (a).'

content_gaps.missing: Independent supplies / selectivity requirement for group 2 (Reg 710.535.1.101) — incoming circuits shall not be interrupted by a final-circuit short-circuit. The page covers medical IT systems but omits the general selectivity requirement for group 2 distribution.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 710.535.1.101 facet: 'selectivity shall be required for any prospective overcurrent for group 2 medical locations and that incoming circuits shall not be interrupted by a final-circuit short-circuit.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Test sequence for earth electrode is part of Chapter 61 of BS 7671
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets explicitly place initial verification — including earth electrode testing — in Chapter 64 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, not Chapter 61. Reg 643.11 (context_prefix: 'Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.11 · [scope] · Initial verification covered in Chapter 64') states: 'Chapter 64 of BS 7671 Part 6 is dedicated to Initial Verification.' Reg 643.7.3.201 (earth fault current measurement, TT system) is also squarely in Chapter 64. The only Chapter 61 reference in the entire RAG is a passing cross-reference in Reg 530.3.4 ('Chapter 61/62'), not the primary home of testing requirements. The auditor is correct: 'Chapter 61' is obsolete 17th-edition numbering; current A4:2026 uses Chapter 64.

confirmed_issues.claim: Current spike should be at least 30 metres from the electrode under test, with the potential spike at 62% of that distance (approx 18.6 m)
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG contradicts the fixed-30-m rule and returns no support for 62%. GN3 Reg 2.26 (context_prefix: 'Chapter 2 · Reg 2.26 · [requirement] · Recommended spike separation 15-25 m between spikes') gives a recommended spike-to-spike separation of 15-25 m. The 30 m figure appears only as a conditional worked example in GN3 Reg 2.26: 'Set distance C2 at least ten times electrode dimension — for a 3 m long rod electrode the C2 separation shall be at least 30 m.' It is not a universal fixed distance; it scales with electrode length (10× rule). No RAG facet mentions 62% positioning of the potential spike. The page presents both figures as fixed universal constants, which the RAG does not support.

content_gaps.missing: Table 41.5 maximum RA for 100 mA RCDs is 200 ohms, not 500 ohms — the page lists 500 ohms from the raw formula without applying the Table 41.5 cap. This is a material accuracy gap that could mislead inspectors.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] context_prefix 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Table 41.5 RCD TT Reg 411.5.3': 'For IDeltaN <= 100 mA the earth electrode resistance must not exceed 200 ohm.'

content_gaps.missing: The page cites 'Chapter 61' for the initial verification test sequence. BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 places initial verification in Chapter 64 (Reg 643.7.2, 643.7.3, 643.11). Chapter 61 was the 17th edition numbering. This stale cite should be corrected to Chapter 64.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.7.3 and Reg 643.11, context_prefix 'Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 643.7.3 explicitly names the measurement requirement and cross-references Reg 643.7.2 for procedure. The page does not cite these regulations anywhere, weakening the regulatory grounding.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 643.7.3: 'For a TT system, compliance with Regulation 411.5 shall be verified by: (i) measurement of the resistance of the earth electrode...'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 and OSG recording requirements: GN3 Reg 5.9 specifies the EICR must record electrode type, location, measured resistance, and soil conditions. OSG Reg 7.28 requires recording on 'the certificate continuation sheet'. The page's recording section omits electrode type and location as required fields.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 5.9 and [OSG] Reg 7.28

confirmed_issues.claim: SWA cable burial depth: 500mm in garden, 450mm under hard standing
confirmed_issues.evidence: Regs 709.521.1.7 and 730.521.101.3.2 both state only 'sufficient depth to avoid being damaged' with no explicit mm figures. The RAG-returned NOTE 1 to 730.521.101.3.2 gives 0.6 m (600mm) as the general minimum — higher than both figures on the page. One [example] facet under 709.521.1.7 mentions 0.5 m as 'generally sufficient' only for light pedestrian areas where heavy vehicles are not expected, which partially covers garden depth but still does not confirm the 450mm hard-standing figure. The OSG table containing the definitive 500mm/450mm breakdown did not return any rows, so neither specific depth is grounded. The 450mm hard-standing figure is contradicted by the 0.6 m NOTE, and the 500mm garden figure has only weak [example]-level support rather than a normative requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Overhead supply minimum height 3.5m above ground, 5.2m over a vehicle route
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets (Regs 708.521.7.3, 709.521.1.8, 730.521.101.3.3) consistently and unambiguously state the minimum height is 3.5 m in areas not subject to vehicle movement and 6 m in areas subject to vehicle movement. The page's figure of 5.2 m for a vehicle route is directly contradicted by the RAG — the correct BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 value is 6 m. The 3.5 m non-vehicle figure is correct. Example facet: '[BS 7671] · Reg 709.521.1.8 · Acceptance: overhead conductor height meets 6 m or 3.5 m as applicable — measured height above ground to be not less than 6 m in areas subjected to vehicle movement and 3.5 m in all other areas'.

content_gaps.missing: Specific OSG burial depth figures (500 mm garden / 600 mm under road) with cable cover/warning tape positioning. The page quotes 500/450 mm which may understate the OSG minimum under road surfaces (0.6 m noted in Reg 730.521.101.3.2 NOTE 1).
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets · Reg 730.521.101.3.2 NOTE 1 — '0.6 m as general minimum' for unprotected buried cables

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 709.521.1.7 exception: the page mentions the above-ground alternative but does not explain that additional mechanical protection (conduit/duct) also removes the depth requirement for shallower runs — a practical cost-saving detail for electricians.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets · Reg 709.521.1.7 [condition] — mechanical protection exception

confirmed_issues.claim: For EICs, both the designer and installer declarations are required even when the same person fulfils both roles.
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Chapter 2 Reg 2.5 facets directly contradict this. Two facets are explicit: (1) 'When design, construction, inspection and testing of an installation are performed by the same individual, that person shall use a Certificate containing the single-signature declaration (item (€) of Appendix 6) instead of the multiple-signature section of the model form.' (2) 'A Certificate with a single-signature declaration may be used in place of the multiple signatures section of the model form provided that the same person has responsibility for design, construction, inspection and testing of the installation.' The page inverts this rule — the RAG mandates a single-signature certificate replaces the separate designer/installer declarations when one person holds all responsibilities.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD operating times must be tested at both 1x and 5x rated current — omitting the 5x test is a common omission.
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Chapter 5 Reg 5.6 facets explicitly state: 'BS 7671 does not require testing of RCDs at 5xIΔn. This is to avoid misinterpreting manufacturer-declared characteristics and to prevent shortening the usable life of RCDs.' A further facet confirms: 'Testing RCDs at high multiple currents (such as 5xIΔn) can shorten the usable life of RCDs.' The RAG confirms 100% (1×IΔn) as the standard trip-time test. The page's framing of the 5x test as a required step that is commonly omitted is directly contradicted — BS 7671 deliberately does not require the 5× test.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 added mandatory SPD and AFDD fields to the Appendix 6 model forms. The page says to use the 'current version of the model forms' but never specifies what changed in A4:2026, missing a key differentiator for readers upgrading from 18th Edition pre-A4 forms.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201 — 'Appendix 6 Model forms have been amended to include fields for recording the details of SPDs and AFDDs. Installers shall record SPD and AFDD details on the model forms where such devices are installed.'

content_gaps.missing: The page does not mention the Model Electrical Installation Works Certificate (MEIWC) — a single-signature alternative to the multi-signature EIC contained in Appendix 6. NICEIC contractors who use this form instead of the standard EIC need to know it is valid.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 2.5 — 'Appendix 6 of BS 7671 also contains the Model Electrical Installation Works Certificate (MEIWC) as an alternative form for certification…provided for use where an alternative single-signature form is more appropriate.'

content_gaps.missing: No guidance is given on the FI (Further Investigation) code: when it is used, what it means for the overall assessment, and whether it prevents a Satisfactory outcome. NICEIC assessors scrutinise FI usage.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 3.11 — 'Each separate item...shall be coded C1, C2 or C3 as appropriate, or, exceptionally, FI.' The 'exceptionally' qualifier and FI's specific use-case (suspected but unconfirmed hazard) are not covered on the page.

content_gaps.missing: An EIC may be used as an alternative to issuing multiple Minor Works Certificates where there are multiple additions or alterations not extending to new circuits. This nuance is absent and prevents unnecessary confusion about when to use each form.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 120.3 — 'The Electrical Installation Certificate may be used to certify where there are multiple additions, or alterations, or remedial works to the existing installation which do not extend to new circuits, as an alternative to issuing multiple Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificates.'

confirmed_issues.claim: External installations must comply with Section 708 (caravan and camping parks have specific requirements) and general requirements for buried cables and external equipment.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns multiple facets confirming Section 714 is the governing section for outdoor lighting installations (Reg 712.6.101 context_prefix: 'Section 714 applies to outdoor lighting installations comprising one or more luminaires, a wiring system and accessories, and also applies to highway power supplies and street furniture.'). Section 708 facets (Reg 704.410.3.6, 708.512.1.2) are scoped exclusively to caravan/camping parks and similar locations. There is no RAG facet linking Section 708 to car park lighting. Citing Section 708 for a car park is a clear mis-attribution; the correct section is 714.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD protection per Regulation 411.3.3 applies to external circuits.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 411.3.3 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3') confirm the regulation is explicitly scoped to 'socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A'. Multiple facets state this scope limitation. Reg 411.3.4 covers RCD protection for luminaire circuits but is limited to domestic (household) premises. Section 714 governs additional protection for outdoor lighting installations specifically. Citing 411.3.3 as conferring a general RCD requirement for external lighting circuits is incorrect and unsupported by the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: Section 714 (BS 7671 Part 7) is the directly applicable section for outdoor lighting installations including car park lighting — covering luminaires, wiring systems, and highway power supplies. The page does not mention it at all, citing Section 708 (caravan parks) instead.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.6.101 context_prefix: 'Section 714 applies to outdoor lighting installations comprising one or more luminaires, a wiring system and accessories, and also applies to highway power supplies and street furniture.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 addition): domestic/household premises require RCD ≤30 mA additional protection for all AC final circuits supplying luminaires. Where car park lighting feeds a building with domestic premises, this now applies. Page omits this entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: Correct burial depth for unprotected SWA in vehicle-trafficked areas: RAG Note 1 to Reg 730.521.101.3.2 indicates 0.6m as the general minimum, not 450mm as the page states. Under vehicle movement areas this is safety-critical.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 730.521.101.3.2: 'NOTE 1 suggests 0.6 m as general minimum' depth for unprotected buried distribution cables subject to vehicle movement.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 709.521.1.7 and 730.521.101.3.2 specifically address underground distribution cables in external/special installations — the page gives no reg-number reference for the burial depth requirement, weakening compliance credibility.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 709.521.1.7: 'Underground distribution cables shall, unless provided with additional mechanical protection, be buried at a sufficient depth to avoid being damaged, for example, by heavy vehicle movement.'

confirmed_issues.claim: A typical 7kW home charger installation takes 2 to 4 hours
confirmed_issues.evidence: PWI returned 8 matching records for EV charging dedicated circuit installations. Durations split as: 5 records at 180 minutes (3 hours) and 3 records at 240 minutes (4 hours). Zero records show 120 minutes (2 hours). The lower bound of '2 hours' is not supported by any PWI record. RAG-evidenced range is 3–4 hours, with 3 hours the more common figure. The page's '2 to 4 hours' understates the lower bound.

confirmed_issues.claim: DOL starting draws 6-8 times the full load current (FLC)
confirmed_issues.evidence: PWI facet (primary_topic: 'Motor starting current assessment…') states test procedures verbatim: 'For direct-on-line starting of squirrel-cage motors use Id = 4.2 to 9 In for 2-pole motors and Id = 4.2 to 7 In for motors with more than 2 poles (mean value = 6 In).' The page's '6-8x' figure is not supported by the RAG. For the most common multi-pole motors the upper bound is 7 In, not 8, and 2-pole motors reach 9 In not 8. The correct RAG-grounded statement is: 4.2–7x for multi-pole (mean 6 In), 4.2–9x for 2-pole. Stating '6-8x' as a universal range misrepresents the RAG source on both ends.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 525 limits voltage drop to 5% for motor circuits
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 525.202 facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · … Reg 525.202 · … Appendix 4 Section 6.4 contains circuit-type specific values') confirms that the numeric voltage drop limits for motor circuits live in Appendix 4 Section 6.4, not in Reg 525 itself. No RAG facet states what that motor-circuit limit is. The only 5% figure returned by the RAG is from Reg 715.525, which applies to ELV lighting installations only. The page attributing '5%' to motor circuits under Reg 525 is not supported by any facet and conflates the ELV lighting limit with the motor circuit limit.

top_improvements.action: Add a Section 445 / undervoltage protection callout box. Motor circuits must incorporate undervoltage protection (contactor hold-in or undervoltage release) to prevent automatic restart after supply interruption. Cite Reg 131.6.3 and Section 445. This is a mandatory BS 7671 safety requirement completely absent from the page and a significant accuracy gap.
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Reg 2.8; BS 7671 Reg 131.6.3 — both returned facets explicitly require Section 445 measures for motor restart hazard.

top_improvements.action: Correct and expand the voltage drop citation. Replace 'BS 7671 Regulation 525' with 'Reg 525.202 and Appendix 4 Section 6.4'. Clarify that Appendix 4 Section 6.4 gives circuit-type-specific limits for motor circuits separately from lighting (3%) and general socket circuits. Verify and state the actual Appendix 4 motor circuit limit (not the generic 5% figure which may apply to 'other circuits').
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671 Reg 525.202 facets: 'Appendix 4, Section 6.4 contains numerical voltage drop limits that are specific to different circuit types (for example lighting, final circuits to socket-outlets, motor circuits)'.

top_improvements.action: Add a Chapter 33 compatibility design note: the designer must assess that the motor starting current does not detrimentally affect other equipment on the same installation. List common design checks: voltage dip at point of common coupling, transformer capacity, other motors on same busbars. Frame as a BS 7671 design obligation.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence Chapter 33 compatibility facet: 'not assessing starting currents during design' is a listed common mistake.

top_improvements.action: Add a VFD installation note covering: (a) the ~10% additional power demand when retrofitting a VFD (upstream protection/cable resizing), (b) screened motor cable requirement for EMC, (c) cable length limitation (~50-100m without output filter). The page mentions harmonics and bearing currents but omits the 10% derating and screened cable points.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: common mistake 'neglecting the 10% power increase when a VFD is applied as device-plus'.

top_improvements.action: Add author E-E-A-T attribution: name a qualified electrician or engineer as the author/reviewer, include their qualification (e.g. 18th Edition, ECS Gold Card), and add a 'last reviewed' date. This is the single biggest SEO trust signal missing from the page.

confirmed_issues.claim: Batteries must not be installed in roof spaces or under stairs adjacent to escape routes
confirmed_issues.evidence: Exhaustive RAG search (content ilike '%roof space%', '%under stair%', '%escape route%' joined with battery context) returned zero supporting facets. Reg 570.6.7.203 (BS7671 A4) and OSG 12.4.2 both require a 'suitable location taking account of manufacturer's instructions' and PAS 63100 for dwellings — neither regulation enumerates specific prohibited locations such as roof spaces or stairs. The page states a specific prohibition that has no grounding in bs7671_facets. The only escape-route battery-adjacent requirement found (Reg 422.2) concerns wiring circuit protection on escape routes, not battery siting. This claim goes beyond what BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 and the OSG actually state.

confirmed_issues.claim: Large battery systems in commercial settings may require a fire risk assessment under BS EN 62619
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two distinct issues: (1) BS EN 62619 returns zero facets from bs7671_facets — the RAG cannot ground or contradict this standard citation at all. (2) The broader concept of a fire strategy requirement for commercial batteries IS supported: Reg 570.6.7.203 (bs7671_facets, context_prefix '...In non-dwelling premises select location using fire strategy') states 'In premises other than dwellings, the location of storage batteries and fire protection requirements shall be selected taking into account the fire strategy for the premises.' The page conflates a genuine BS 7671 requirement (fire strategy in non-dwellings) with a specific standard reference (BS EN 62619) that has no RAG support. The BS EN 62619 citation is unverifiable and should be removed or sourced outside BS 7671.

content_gaps.missing: Mandatory warning notices at specific locations: Reg 570.6.8.201 requires notices at (1) the origin of the installation, (2) each remote metering position, and (3) every consumer unit or distribution board fed by the battery. The page says only 'clear labelling identifying the battery system, all isolation points' — far less specific than the regulation demands.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 570.6.8.201 — multiple facets covering origin, remote meter, and CU/DB notice requirements

content_gaps.missing: GN3 Reg 1.7 requires the tester/verifier to consult the IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems for test procedures, acceptance criteria, DC earthing, and BMS checks. The page makes no mention of this verification obligation, which is critical for electricians carrying out initial verification of BESS installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 1.7: 'For testing regimes, acceptance criteria and particular safety tests relating to Electrical Energy Storage Systems, GN3 requires that the tester shall refer to the IET Code of Practice for Electrical Energy Storage Systems'

content_gaps.missing: Chapter 55 / Reg 551.1.1 classification of BESS as a 'generating set' is absent. This classification triggers specific Chapter 55 requirements (isolation, earthing, protection coordination) that every installer needs to understand — the page only mentions BS 7671 and Section 712 in passing.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 551.1.1: 'battery energy storage system supplying premises through an inverter constitutes a generating set … subject to Chapter 55 requirements such as isolation, earthing and protection coordination'

content_gaps.missing: OSG reference to BS EN IEC 60269-7 for battery/BESS fuse selection. Overcurrent protection device selection for DC battery circuits has specific standards that differ from standard AC fusing — not covered on the page at all.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 7.2.7: 'BS EN IEC 60269-7 provides additional requirements specifically intended for the protection of batteries and battery systems'

confirmed_issues.claim: Standard 13A socket outlets are not permitted in Zone 0, Zone 1, and Zone 2; outside the zones a standard socket could theoretically be installed.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets directly contradict the 'outside the zones' framing. Reg 701.418.2 (bs7671, A4:2026): 'socket-outlets at low voltage (230 V) shall be sited at least 2.5 m from zone 1' — the distance is measured from the Zone 1 boundary, not from the outer edge of Zone 2 (which extends only 0.6 m from Zone 1). OSG 4.6.4 ([OSG] Reg 4.6.4): '230 V socket-outlets are prohibited within a distance of 2.5 m horizontally from the boundary of zone 1 — that prohibition is absolute for 230 V socket-outlets unless they are SELV or an approved shaver supply unit.' OSG 12.513 ([OSG] Reg 12.513) repeats the same 2.5 m rule citing 701.542.3. GN3 Reg 3.18 states '2.6 m from zone 1'. The page implies a socket is permissible anywhere beyond Zone 2 (i.e. beyond 0.6 m from Zone 1); the actual rule requires 2.5 m clearance from Zone 1. This is a material error — the page could lead an installer to fit a socket 1 m from the shower tray believing it compliant.

confirmed_issues.claim: All electrical work in a bathroom is notifiable under Part P regardless of whether it involves a new circuit.
confirmed_issues.evidence: The practical_work_intelligence RAG directly contradicts the 'regardless of new circuit' framing. Two distinct PWI records state: 'Like-for-like fitting or replacement of cookers and electric showers [is] generally not notifiable unless a new circuit is required' (keywords: like-for-like, notifiable, new circuit, Part P). A second record (under 'Work and final connections in kitchens and special locations') explicitly notes 'certain small appliance replacements (cookers, electric showers) are not notifiable where no new circuit is needed.' Additionally, a PWI record for equipotential bonding notes 'notifiable if new circuit' as the relevant keyword, reinforcing the new-circuit trigger. The page's blanket claim that ALL bathroom electrical work is notifiable is incorrect per these grounded RAG records; like-for-like appliance replacements that do not introduce a new circuit are not notifiable under Part P.

content_gaps.missing: Supplementary bonding requirements under Reg 701.415.2 are almost entirely absent from the page body. The regulation mandates bonding of metallic pipes (water, gas, waste), central heating pipes, and accessible metallic structural parts. Conditions for omitting bonding (three-condition test) are also missing.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 701.415.2 — Supplementary protective equipotential bonding in rooms with bath or shower

content_gaps.missing: The socket prohibition is stated imprecisely as 'outside Zone 2'. The actual rule (Reg 701.418.2) requires 2.5m horizontal clearance from Zone 1, which is considerably more restrictive. A practical diagram or worked example of the 2.5m rule would make this page best-in-class.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 701.418.2 — Socket-outlets sited at least 2.5m from zone 1; [OSG] OSG 4.6.4 — 2.5m prohibition

content_gaps.missing: Zone 1 SELV/PELV voltage threshold: the page correctly states 12V AC for Zone 0 but never explains that Zone 1 permits SELV or PELV up to 25V AC RMS / 60V DC (Reg 701.55). This is a meaningful distinction for low-voltage lighting design.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 701.55 — Zone 1: equipment protected by SELV or PELV not exceeding 25V AC RMS or 60V ripple-free DC

content_gaps.missing: The space under the bath tub is classified as Zone 1 (unless accessible only with a tool), not 'outside the zones'. This is a common installation mistake and is explicitly covered in Reg 701.32.3 but absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 701.32.3 — 'The space under the bath tub or shower basin is considered to be Zone 1 unless only accessible with a tool.'

confirmed_issues.claim: 712.411 — DC side uses double insulation, protective separation, or automatic disconnection of supply
confirmed_issues.evidence: A direct context_prefix query for 'Reg 712.411' returns zero rows. The RAG confirms the correct DC-side protection clause is 712.410.102, which lists only two permitted measures: (a) double or reinforced insulation per Section 412, and (b) SELV or PELV per Section 414. The sub-clause that mandates Class II equipment on the DC side is 712.412.101. '712.411' as a standalone solar PV clause does not exist in the A4:2026 RAG; the page is citing a non-existent regulation number for this requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: 712.537.2 — DC isolator must be rated for DC use, located adjacent to inverter, capable of interrupting full DC short-circuit current
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG contains facets only for 712.537.2.2.104 (securing devices without breaking capacity against inadvertent/unauthorised operation to prevent DC on-load arcing) and 712.537.2.2.105 (remote operation of combiner-box devices). No facet returns the 'adjacent to inverter' location requirement or 'capable of interrupting full DC short-circuit current' language attributed to '712.537.2'. The DC isolator design-current requirement is in 712.512.1.2 (Isc_ax as design current for selection of isolation/switching devices), which is a different clause and does not use 'full short-circuit current' language. The specific wording the page attributes to '712.537.2' is unconfirmed by any returned facet.

confirmed_issues.claim: DC side protective measures include 'automatic disconnection of supply' (not listed in 712.410.102)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 712.410.102 facets are unambiguous: 'On the DC side, one of the following protective measures shall be applied: (a) double or reinforced insulation (Section 412), or (b) extra-low voltage (SELV or PELV) (Section 414).' Automatic disconnection of supply (the general measure in Regulation 411) is not listed among the permissible DC-side measures. A supporting example facet (context_prefix '[example] PV inverter AC isolated but DC strings still live') also explicitly restates the two permitted measures as double/reinforced insulation or SELV/PELV only. The page's FAQ stating that 'automatic disconnection of supply' is a DC-side measure is directly contradicted by 712.410.102.

confirmed_issues.claim: IMD (insulation monitoring device) requirement for unearthed PV DC systems — 712.421.101 — omitted from page
confirmed_issues.evidence: 712.421.101.1 is confirmed in the RAG as a mandatory 'shall' requirement: 'An insulation monitoring device (IMD) shall be installed except where Regulation [712.421.101.2] applies.' 712.421.101.2 is also confirmed: it covers the exception where functional earthing is applied to a live conductor inside the inverter on the DC side, in which case means to verify insulation status throughout the PV array life cycle are required instead (with inverter-integrated IMD or BS EN 61557-8 device as acceptable means). The page omits any mention of IMD requirements. This is a genuine content gap — the IMD/insulation-monitoring obligation under 712.421.101.1 (and the exception path under 712.421.101.2) are real A4:2026 requirements absent from the page.

content_gaps.missing: 712.410.102 DC-side protective measures are restricted to double/reinforced insulation (Section 412) OR SELV/PELV (Section 414) — the page incorrectly implies ADS is a valid DC-side option (FAQ answer and Section 712 prose). Adding the correct clause reference and removing ADS from the DC-side list would fix a factual inaccuracy.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.410.102 context_prefix

content_gaps.missing: 712.421.101 — Insulation Monitoring Device (IMD) requirement for unearthed DC systems (IT earthing configuration), and the exception at 712.421.101.2 where functional earthing is applied inside the inverter. This is a significant A4:2026 requirement entirely absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] context_prefix '[exception] Exception where 712.421.101.2 applies'

content_gaps.missing: 712.433.101 — Reverse current protection for PV string cables in arrays with N>2 parallel strings: cables must be sized to carry maximum reverse current (N−1) Isc max, or rated to the string protective device current. This is a concrete design/certification requirement missing from the page's DC testing and Section 712 sections.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.433.101 context_prefix

content_gaps.missing: 712.537.2.2.104 — Measures to prevent DC on-load interruption (securing devices to prevent arcing). The page describes the DC isolator generically but omits this A4:2026 specific anti-arcing requirement for DC switching devices.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] context_prefix '[note] 712.537.2.2.104 title: Measures to prevent DC on-load interruption'

confirmed_issues.claim: Landlords must complete remedial work identified by C1 or C2 observations within 28 days
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero results from bs7671_facets for any 28-day or statutory remedial deadline. The closest PWI facet (landlord EICR handover, primary_topic: 'Handover and explanation of the EICR … in a domestic tenancy context') says only 'agree timescales and retain a signed acknowledgment' — no specific deadline. The 28-day rule derives from The Electrical Safety Standards in the Private Rented Sector (England) Regulations 2020 (SI 2020/312), a statutory instrument entirely outside the RAG corpus. The page presents this as a standalone fact without attributing it to the 2020 Regulations, making it ungrounded from any RAG source.

confirmed_issues.claim: Owner-occupied properties: no legal requirement, but the IET recommends inspection every ten years
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Table 3.2 is confirmed in the RAG as the source of recommended inspection intervals, and facets confirm 'suggested initial frequencies for inspection and testing' are 'recommendations and not legal requirements.' However, no facet in bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence returns the specific 10-year figure for owner-occupied domestic dwellings. The RAG confirms the framework (Table 3.2, recommendations only) but cannot ground the specific 10-year claim. The page states a precise figure that is unverifiable from the available RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Inspector should hold City and Guilds 2391 (Inspection and Testing) or equivalent and a current 18th Edition BS 7671 qualification (C&G 2382)
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 facets confirm a competence requirement exists ([GN3] · Chapter 1 · Reg 1.1: 'the inspector(s) shall collectively have the relevant education, experience, qualifications and skills necessary for the scope') but BS 7671 and GN3 deliberately avoid mandating specific qualification numbers. No facet in bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence names C&G 2391 or C&G 2382 by number. The page goes beyond what the RAG supports by naming specific qualification codes as requirements.

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR costs in Sunderland typically range from £150 to £280 for a standard two-bedroom property
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero results from practical_work_intelligence for any EICR pricing, regional cost, or fee data. The PWI confirms a domestic EICR typical_duration_minutes of 180 but contains no pricing columns or cost figures at all. No bs7671_facets entry covers pricing. The £150–£280 range for Sunderland is entirely unverifiable from the RAG and appears to be an invented or externally-sourced figure.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 — domestic lighting circuits must now have 30 mA RCD protection. Old consumer units without RCD-protected lighting circuits generate a new EICR defect category not mentioned on the page. This is directly relevant to Sunderland's older housing stock where the page already discusses missing socket-circuit RCD protection.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 411.3.4 (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026) — 'Within domestic premises, additional protection by an RCD not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires'; GN3 Reg 5.12 — same requirement confirmed.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 — AFDD requirement for certain socket-outlet circuits. Consumer unit replacements prompted by C2 findings (which the page discusses) must now consider AFDD provision. An EICR inspector will note absence of AFDDs on eligible circuits. Relevant to both common findings and the electricians section.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 421.1.7 — 'Arc Fault Detection Device... required or recommended for certain final circuits supplying socket-outlets'; Reg 411.9.3 — 'Confirmation AFDD indication operational (421.1.7; 532.6; 651.2(e))'.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 133.1.3 — certain equipment usage (including AFDD and SPD installation) must now be recorded on the Part 6 EICR/EIC certification form. EICR inspectors must record SPD and AFDD presence/absence. This affects what a compliant EICR form must show and is entirely absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 133.1.3 — 'certain usage of equipment shall be recorded on the appropriate electrical certification specified in Part 6... includes AFDD, SPD, RCD on lighting circuits.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 guidance on site test factors — GN3 9th Ed guidance recommends applying a 0.8 factor to maximum permitted Zs values when conducting on-site earth loop impedance tests to account for temperature variation. The page makes no mention of any test methodology, relevant for electricians completing EICRs on Sunderland's older stock.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 facets (Reg 5.12 and related) — GN3 provides inspector-specific guidance on test procedures; no matching facet text was returned on this specific point, but the gap is identified from the A4:2026 GN3 update context.

When a C2 consumer unit upgrade is triggered by missing RCD protection on sockets (a point the page already makes), the replacement unit must also consider AFDD provision for socket circuits per Reg 421.1.7. confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 411.3.3 requires RCD protection with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30mA for circuits supplying equipment outdoors or in special locations, including a fixed hardwired hot tub circuit.
confirmed_issues.evidence: All six Reg 411.3.3 facets in the RAG (A4:2026) confirm the regulation is scoped exclusively to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32A. Context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3 · [requirement] · Baseline requirement: RCD protection applies to 32A and below socket-outlets' — content: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32A unless an express exception in the Regulation is met.' A second facet states: 'Because Regulation 411.3.3 is stated to apply to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32A, socket-outlets with a rated current greater than 32A are not covered … and must be considered against other applicable Regulations.' No facet extends 411.3.3 to fixed hardwired equipment. The RAG confirms the RCD requirement for a fixed hot tub circuit derives from Section 702 (specifically 702.415.2 and 702.53), not 411.3.3. Citing 411.3.3 as the mandate for a fixed outdoor hot tub circuit is an overstatement and misleads readers about the regulatory source.

confirmed_issues.claim: Testing must be completed in accordance with BS 7671 Chapter 61.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets confirm inspection and testing sits in Chapter 64 under A4:2026, not Chapter 61. Context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.11 · [scope] · Initial verification covered in Chapter 64' — content: 'Chapter 64 of BS 7671 Part 6 is dedicated to Initial Verification.' Further facets reference Reg 643.3.2 (insulation resistance), Reg 643.7.2, Reg 644.4 — all under Chapter 64. 'Chapter 61' is the pre-A3 (17th-edition 2008) chapter number. A4:2026 renumbered it to Chapter 64. The page's reference to 'Chapter 61' is stale and incorrect for BS 7671:2018+A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Supplementary equipotential bonding is required (zone-based under Section 702) — page makes no mention of this requirement.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 702.415.2 facets are explicit and mandatory. Context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 70 · Reg 702.415.2 · [requirement] · Supplementary bonding required for all extraneous-conductive-parts in zones 0, 1 and 2' — content: 'All extraneous-conductive-parts in zones 0, 1 and 2 shall be connected by supplementary protective bonding conductors. This is a mandatory requirement for the listed zones under Regulation 702.415.2 and applies regardless of system earthing arrangement.' A second facet (Reg 702.55.4) cross-references 702.415.2 for bonding conductor sizing. The requirement is mandatory and unambiguous in the RAG but is entirely absent from the page — this is a genuine omission of a safety-critical requirement.

content_gaps.missing: Supplementary equipotential bonding requirement under Reg 702.415.2 — all exposed and extraneous-conductive-parts in zones 0, 1 and 2 around the hot tub must be bonded. This is listed as a common mistake in the practical_work_intelligence RAG ('omitting necessary equipotential bonding in bathrooms and pools') and is a safety-critical omission from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 702.415.2 — supplementary bonding in zones 0/1/2; practical_work_intelligence keyword 'swimming pool, special locations'

content_gaps.missing: Correct chapter numbering for testing: A4:2026 renumbered inspection and testing to Chapter 64 (not Chapter 61 as stated on the page). Any reader preparing for or referencing initial verification will be misled by the stale Chapter 61 reference.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.11 — 'Chapter 64 of BS 7671 Part 6 is dedicated to Initial Verification'

content_gaps.missing: Section 702 zone definitions (zones 0, 1, 2 around the hot tub) and what equipment is permitted in each zone. This is the core of the special-location rules for hot tubs and would make the guide authoritative versus generic.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 702.415.2 context_prefix mentions zones 0/1/2 for special locations

content_gaps.missing: RCD type selection nuance: the RAG confirms Type B RCDs are required where pulsating rectified DC results from two or more phases (OSG Reg 3.6.4.5). The page only mentions Type A vs Type AC — for larger hot tubs or swim spas with variable-speed drives or inverter pumps, Type B may be required. This is a meaningful accuracy gap for professional electricians.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 3.6.4.5 — 'Type B RCD intended for residual pulsating rectified DC from two or more phases'

top_improvements.action: Add a Zs testing note including the GN3 0.80 site-test factor: when recording the measured Zs, multiply by 0.80 to correct for conductor temperature at the time of test versus the assumed maximum operating temperature. confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 421.1.7 (A4:2026) recommends AFDD installation on AC final circuits — not mentioned anywhere in the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 421.1.7 is strongly confirmed by six facets. Key facet [BS 7671 · Reg 421.1.7 · reference]: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)… to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation.' A second facet confirms the wording is recommendatory ('recommending'), not mandatory. The notifiable-electrical-work page covers consumer unit replacements and new circuits — exactly the work types where the A4:2026 AFDD recommendation is most relevant — yet the page makes no mention of AFDDs or Reg 421.1.7. This is a confirmed content gap: a material A4:2026 change is missing from the page.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 AFDD recommendation: when replacing a consumer unit or installing new circuits in a dwelling, Reg 421.1.7 recommends arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) on AC final circuits. confirmed_issues.claim: The voltage tolerance is set by the Electricity Supply (Amendment) Regulations 1994
confirmed_issues.evidence: OSG Reg 4.6.4 (context_prefix: '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 4 · Reg 4.6.4 · [requirement] · Permitted supply voltage range 216.2 V to 253.0 V') states explicitly: 'the supply voltage at the incoming terminals of an electrical supply provided in accordance with the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations (ESQCR) can be between the limits of 216.2 V and 253.0 V.' The RAG directly names ESQCR as the authority, not the Electricity Supply (Amendment) Regulations 1994. No facet references the 1994 Regulations at all. The page's attribution to the 1994 Regulations is incorrect — the correct instrument is ESQCR.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 addition: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) requirement at Reg 421.1.7 for certain socket-outlet final circuits in domestic premises. A best-in-class Year 1 guide should mention AFDD as a new device type apprentices will encounter in modern consumer units, even if detail is deferred to later years.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 / Reg 411.4.2: 'An Arc Fault Detection Device (AFDD) is a device designed to detect hazardous arc faults...Regulation 421.1.7 requires or recommends AFDDs for certain final circuits supplying socket-outlets.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 addition: RCD mandatory protection for domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4, ≤30 mA). The page teaches apprentices about RCDs in Year 1 context but omits this A4:2026 requirement, which is directly relevant to domestic wiring work apprentices encounter from day one.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 / [OSG] Reg 7.2.5: 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA' in domestic premises.

content_gaps.missing: Voltage tolerance attribution should reference ESQCR (Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations) rather than the Electricity Supply (Amendment) Regulations 1994. content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 4.6.4: 'supply voltage...provided in accordance with the Electricity Safety, Quality and Continuity Regulations (ESQCR) can be between the limits of 216.2 V and 253.0 V.'

content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 8.2: 'isolation shall be established and proven...voltage indicators shall be used...Testers shall follow CDM and EAWR duties.'
