confirmed_issues.claim: The page implies consumer unit replacement also requires an EIC (only mentions new circuit as the trigger)
confirmed_issues.evidence: BS 7671 Reg 644.4.201 facet [context_prefix: 'Applicability of regulation 644.4.201 to replacement of distribution board or consumer unit'] states: 'Regulation 644.4.201 applies, upon completion of the verification of the replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit.' The requirement facet for the same regulation explicitly lists it as a third trigger alongside new installations and additions/alterations: 'An Electrical Installation Certificate ... shall be issued ... for a new installation, an addition or alteration, or the replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit.' GN3 Reg 2.5 facet reinforces this: 'The MEIWC or EIC shall not be used for the replacement of distribution boards, consumer units or similar items. Replacement of these items is excluded from minor works certification and requires the appropriate full certification/documentation.' The page only mentions the new-circuit trigger and omits consumer unit / distribution board replacement entirely — a genuine omission contradicted by both BS 7671 and GN3 in the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Implied: one certificate suffices even if minor works were done on multiple existing circuits
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 2.5 facet [context_prefix: '[requirement] · Separate MEIWC for each circuit when multiple circuits modified'] states: 'Where circuits in the same installation are modified, a separate Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate (MEIWC) shall be issued for each circuit that is modified. Each MEIWC shall correspond to a single circuit modification rather than covering multiple modified circuits together.' A second GN3 Reg 2.5 facet clarifies the permitted consolidation is only within a single circuit: 'where several minor works activities occur on a single existing circuit, they shall be permitted to be combined and certified on one MEIWC.' Six separate practical_work_intelligence records independently affirm 'separate certificate per existing circuit on which minor works were carried out.' The page never mentions this per-circuit rule, creating a genuine omission confirmed by both GN3 and the practical work intelligence RAG.

content_gaps.missing: Consumer unit / distribution board replacement as a second trigger requiring EIC — page only mentions new circuit. Both are disqualifying conditions under Reg 644.4.201 and electricians frequently miss the CU replacement trigger.

confirmed_issues.claim: Recommended EICR interval for industrial premises under BS 7671 is five years or on change of occupancy
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 3.4 (multiple facets) is explicit: 'there is no prescriptive single outcome' for inspection frequency, and one facet states 'If practical experience shows an installation in heavy industrial use deteriorates faster than anticipated, the dutyholder may judge that the interval to periodic inspection should be shorter than the designer's original recommendation.' BS 7671 Reg 652.1 further confirms frequency must be determined case-by-case having regard to installation type, equipment, use, maintenance frequency, external influences, and previous certificate results. No facet in bs7671_facets returns any fixed 5-year maximum for industrial premises. The GN8 table figure is not represented in the RAG and cannot be cited. The page's assertion of a firm '5 years or on change of occupancy' figure is unsupported by any returned facet and contradicted by the GN3 Reg 3.4 'no prescriptive single outcome' language.

confirmed_issues.claim: All new industrial installations require an Electrical Installation Certificate (EIC) under the Building Regulations
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 confirms EIC is required after initial verification (GN3 Reg 1.3, GN3 example facet: 'an Electrical Installation Certificate (EIC) is required to be given to the person ordering the installation work... based on the model forms contained in Appendix 6 of BS 7671'). However, the RAG explicitly restricts Building Regulations / Part P to dwellings: OSG Reg 1.2.1 defines Part P as 'Electrical safety — dwellings' and multiple OSG facets state it applies to 'dwelling-houses, flats, and associated domestic outbuildings' only. No facet links the EIC obligation for industrial premises to Building Regulations. For industrial premises the EIC duty flows from BS 7671 Part 6 (Reg 643.11) and the wiring regulations, not Building Regulations. The 'Building Regulations' framing on the page is factually wrong for industrial/non-domestic premises.

confirmed_issues.claim: Industrial EICR must also include interim routine checks (beyond periodic EICR alone) — page omits this requirement
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 3.5 returns a direct facet: 'Interim routine checks shall be provided for commercial and industrial electrical installations as part of the formal arrangements required by EAWR, in addition to periodic inspections and testing.' This is an unambiguous mandatory requirement from the RAG. The page's compliance section omitting interim routine checks is a genuine gap — the page presents periodic EICR as the complete inspection obligation for industrial premises, which is incomplete and potentially misleading to site dutyholders.

confirmed_issues.claim: ATEX zones 0/1/2 (gas/vapour) and 20/21/22 (combustible dust); Zone 0 is relatively rare (inside tanks and vessels)
confirmed_issues.evidence: No bs7671_facets row confirms ATEX/explosive-atmosphere zone numbering (0, 1, 2, 20, 21, 22). GN3 Reg 2.11 returns only a pointer: 'For potentially explosive atmospheres BS 7671 requirements are supplemented by other British or Harmonized Standards... Consult appropriate explosion-protection standards (e.g., equipment standards, ATEX-related guidance) as applicable.' This is a cross-reference, not a definition of zone numbers. Queries for 'zone 0', 'zone 1', 'zone 20', 'zone 21' return only bathroom zone results. The zone numbering and the characterisation of Zone 0 as 'relatively rare / inside tanks and vessels' cannot be grounded from this RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: DSEAR 2002 requires employers to classify hazardous zones and use appropriately rated electrical equipment
confirmed_issues.evidence: BS 7671 Reg 712.534.102.6 returns a facet confirming DSEAR 2002 is a 'relevant statutory reference for design and safe operation' of installations in potentially explosive atmospheres. However, the RAG does not confirm the specific employer duty to classify zones or the obligation to use appropriately rated equipment — those details are DSEAR-specific requirements that are not elaborated in any returned facet. The claim is directionally consistent with the DSEAR reference in BS 7671 but the specific obligations cited cannot be grounded from the RAG alone.

confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero rows returned from practical_work_intelligence for ATEX, hazardous area, or certified equipment cost queries. No bs7671_facets content addresses equipment cost differentials. The 50–150% figure is entirely ungrounded in the RAG and cannot be cited on a BS 7671-grounded cost page.

confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero rows returned from practical_work_intelligence for cable containment, trunking, conduit, or budget percentage queries. No RAG source supports this figure. The 15–25% budget proportion is ungrounded.

confirmed_issues.claim: DNO lead time for a new 3-phase connection is typically 8–24 weeks
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero rows returned from practical_work_intelligence for DNO connection timescale queries. No bs7671_facets content addresses DNO lead times. The 8–24 week figure is entirely ungrounded in the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: Interim routine checks obligation: GN3 Reg 3.5 explicitly requires formal interim routine checks for commercial/industrial installations under EAWR, in addition to periodic EICR. The page's compliance section omits this entirely — it only mentions the EICR interval.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 · Chapter 3 · Reg 3.5 · Routine checks: 'Interim routine checks shall be provided for commercial and industrial electrical installations as part of the formal arrangements required by EAWR, in addition to periodic inspections and testing.'

content_gaps.missing: Variable EICR interval driven by adverse factors: BS 7671 Reg 652.1 gives specific adverse-factor examples (24/7 operation, corrosive environments, infrequent maintenance, deteriorating prior reports) that mandate a shorter interval. The page says '3 years in harsh environments' but does not cite the regulatory basis or enumerate the specific factors inspectors must consider.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 652.1 example: 'An industrial installation operating 24/7 with corrosive external influences … shall have a more frequent periodic inspection and testing interval because type of installation, external influences, frequency and quality of maintenance and previous reports are all adverse factors.'

content_gaps.missing: Duty-holder records requirement: GN3 Reg 3.5 requires that records or formal arrangements for maintenance and interim checks be in place so that inspections and remedial actions can be demonstrated. The page mentions EAW duties but does not explain the documentation/records obligation for the duty holder beyond holding an EICR.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 3.5: 'For commercial and industrial workplaces, records or formal arrangements for maintenance and interim routine checks shall be in place so that inspections and remedial actions can be demonstrated as part of compliance with EAWR and good practice.'

content_gaps.missing: EIC form requirements under A4:2026 (Reg 133.1.3): The new A4:2026 amendment requires specific equipment usages (e.g. AFDD, SPD) to be recorded on the EIC. For industrial installations where SPDs are common (surge protection for VSD drives and sensitive control equipment), this is a relevant compliance point the page does not mention.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671 Reg 133.1.3: 'certain usage of equipment shall be recorded on the appropriate electrical certification specified in Part 6… triggered where BS 7671 explicitly indicates that the usage of a particular item of equipment is to be documented.'

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR produced in accordance with BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Section 631)
confirmed_issues.evidence: No 'Section 631' or 'Chapter 63' exists in bs7671_facets. Part 6 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 contains only Chapter 62, Chapter 64 (initial verification / Reg 643.3), and Chapter 65 (periodic inspection / Reg 653.1). The EICR is grounded in GN3 Chapter 3 (Reg 3.11) and Chapter 65 (Reg 653.1 — condition report, Appendix 6 notes). 'Section 631' is not a valid citation in A4:2026 and must be corrected; the correct reference is Chapter 65 / Reg 653.1 for the Condition Report obligations.

confirmed_issues.claim: Absent RCD protection on socket circuits cited under Reg 411.3.3 as applying to sockets not exceeding 20A
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets Reg 411.3.3 facets are unambiguous: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3 · [requirement] · Baseline requirement: RCD protection applies to 32 A and below socket-outlets' — content: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception in the Regulation is met.' Three further 411.3.3 facets all confirm the threshold is 32 A (≤ 32 A). The page citing 20 A is directly contradicted by the A4:2026 text.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 134.2 requires periodic inspection regimes to confirm installations remain safe
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets Reg 134.2.2 facets are clear: 'The designer of the installation shall make a recommendation for the interval to the first periodic inspection and test as detailed in Chapter 64. This is an obligation on the designer to provide a recommended timescale … at design stage.' Five Reg 134.2.2 facets all confirm this is a design-stage duty on the designer only — not a general obligation on owners/occupiers to maintain periodic inspection regimes. The page's characterisation misrepresents the regulation's scope.

confirmed_issues.claim: Owner-occupied domestic properties: EICR recommended every 10 years
confirmed_issues.evidence: No bs7671_facets or GN3 facet returns any 10-year interval for owner-occupied domestic properties. The only domestic interval figure in the RAG is a GN3 Reg 2.4 example: 'During design the installation designer may recommend a period of 5 years to the first periodic inspection and test for a domestic installation.' No 10-year owner-occupied figure is grounded anywhere in the corpus.

confirmed_issues.claim: Remedial work for C1/C2 must be completed within 28 days

confirmed_issues.claim: New consumer unit should be '18th Edition compliant unit with RCBOs'
confirmed_issues.evidence: practical_work_intelligence confirms consumer unit replacement context with RCBOs (typical_duration_minutes=300, keywords include 'RCBO', 'consumer unit replacement'). However, the framing is materially incomplete under A4:2026. bs7671_facets Reg 411.3.4 uses 'shall' (mandatory): 'additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... for AC final circuits supplying luminaires' within domestic premises — a new mandatory A4:2026 requirement that directly applies to any new consumer unit. bs7671_facets Reg 421.1.7 further adds a recommendation to install AFDDs in AC final circuits. Any consumer unit section claiming '18th Edition compliance' while omitting both Reg 411.3.4 lighting circuit RCD (mandatory) and Reg 421.1.7 AFDD (recommended) is presenting a stale picture of what compliance requires.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page makes no mention of AFDDs (Reg 421.1.7) in the consumer unit replacement or new installation context
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets confirms Reg 421.1.7 exists in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' A second facet confirms the wording is recommendatory ('recommending', not 'shall'). Any page section describing consumer unit replacement and claiming BS 7671 / 18th Edition compliance that does not mention this A4:2026 AFDD recommendation is presenting an incomplete compliance picture. The omission is confirmed against RAG evidence.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page makes no mention that domestic lighting circuits require 30mA RCD protection (Reg 411.3.4)
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets returns six facets for Reg 411.3.4, all confirmed as BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The requirement is mandatory ('shall'): 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires' with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA. An acceptance-criterion facet states: 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual current does not exceed 30 mA.' This is a new A4:2026 mandatory addition directly applicable to any new consumer unit installation or domestic rewire. Its omission from the page's consumer unit and rewiring discussion is a confirmed gap against RAG evidence.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 — new A4:2026 mandatory requirement for 30mA RCD on all domestic lighting circuits. Critical for any consumer unit replacement or rewire discussion in Northampton domestic properties. Page omits entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'domestic (household) premises — additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 — A4:2026 recommendation to install AFDDs (arc fault detection devices) on AC final circuits, particularly for socket-outlet circuits. Directly relevant to consumer unit replacement content. Page omits entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) ... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.3 A4:2026 revision detail — the page cites 411.3.3 in a flood-risk context only. This is the most significant A4:2026 change for domestic electricians.

content_gaps.missing: New-build section should note that A4:2026 compliance (lighting RCD, AFDD, socket RCD) is now the baseline for new installations. The page says new-builds 'should comply with the 18th Edition' without clarifying the A4:2026 additions that now apply.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.4 requires RCD protection if the cable is concealed in a wall at a depth of less than 50mm
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix ilike '%Reg 411.3.4%' returned 8 bs7671 facets (document_type='bs7671', 2018+A4:2026). Every facet confirms Reg 411.3.4 applies solely to 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires' in 'domestic (household) premises', requiring an RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA. The full regulation text returned is: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' A separate query on context_prefix ilike '%522.6.202%' confirms that RCD ≤30 mA for cables concealed at depth <50 mm in walls is the requirement of Reg 522.6.202 and Table 52.1, not 411.3.4. The page has swapped the regulation numbers: 411.3.4 (lighting RCD) and 522.6.202 (concealed cable depth RCD) are distinct obligations. This is a direct contradiction by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Kitchen lighting should have RCD protection — page does NOT cite Reg 411.3.4 for lighting, instead misattributing it to concealed cable protection
confirmed_issues.evidence: The same 8 Reg 411.3.4 facets (document_type='bs7671', 2018+A4:2026) retrieved in Claim 1 confirm this is an A4:2026 mandatory addition: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' The acceptance criterion facet states: 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.' The page fails to cite this requirement for kitchen lighting circuits, which is a real omission of a mandatory A4:2026 obligation. Confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 new requirement): 30mA RCD protection mandatory on ALL domestic AC lighting circuits. The kitchen lighting section is silent on this. Any new kitchen lighting circuit now requires RCD protection as a matter of law — a major compliance point omitted entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 411.3.4 [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026] — 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... for AC final circuits supplying luminaires... rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 522.6.202 is presented with a simplified 'less than 50mm' rule but the RAG shows it works via prescribed zones and Table 52.1 (wall construction type, presence of metallic parts). The prescribed zone concept (150mm from top/corner/accessory, or vertical/horizontal from a fitting) is omitted and would be best-in-class content.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets: Reg 522.6.202 [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026] — 'A prescribed zone is defined as a zone within 150 mm from the top of the wall or partition or within 150 mm of an angle formed by two adjoining walls or partitions.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.3 exception: in non-dwellings a documented risk assessment can permit omission of RCD on sockets ≤32A. While not relevant to a typical domestic kitchen, the exception is relevant to commercial kitchens and the page does not distinguish.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page does not mention AFDD (Reg 421.1.7) as a relevant A4:2026 protective device in consumer unit risk context, and does not mention mandatory RCD protection for domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Both regulations are strongly confirmed in the RAG. Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026 introduction): mandatory AFDD protection for final circuits supplying socket-outlets ≤32 A in Higher Risk Residential Buildings, HMOs, purpose-built student accommodation, and care homes; recommended for all other premises. Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026): 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires' — wording uses 'shall', making it mandatory. A risk-assessment page covering consumer unit work that omits both of these new A4:2026 obligations is materially incomplete: electricians assessing risk on consumer unit jobs in the named premises types or domestic properties need to know these requirements exist.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.3 also requires RCD 30mA protection for mobile equipment up to 32A for outdoor use
confirmed_issues.evidence: All eight A4:2026 facets returned for Reg 411.3.3 confirm the regulation was revised to apply exclusively to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32A. Representative facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3 · [scope] · Regulation 411.3.3 applies to socket-outlets rated ≤ 32 A'): 'Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been revised and now applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A.' A broader search for 'mobile equipment outdoor RCD 411' returned zero results. No A4:2026 facet mentions mobile equipment or outdoor use under 411.3.3. The mobile-equipment-outdoor limb existed in the pre-A4 version of 411.3.3 but was removed by the A4 revision, which scoped the regulation to socket-outlets only. The page's claim that 411.3.3 also covers mobile equipment for outdoor use is not supported by any A4:2026 facet and is directly contradicted by the scope facets.

This is a direct A4:2026 addition highly relevant to the consumer unit / tripping topic — e.g. an old split-load board with lighting circuits not RCD-protected is now non-compliant. The page discusses RCBOs and consumer unit upgrades but never mentions this new lighting-circuit requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.4]: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' [GN3 Reg 5.12]: 'The requirement for RCD protection not exceeding 30 mA for final circuits supplying luminaires is scoped to domestic premises.'

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) Reg 421.1.7: A4:2026 introduced AFDDs as a recommended protective device for socket-outlet final circuits. AFDDs can trip (they disconnect on detecting arc faults) and could be confused with MCB/RCD tripping. Not mentioned anywhere on the page despite being directly relevant to 'why does my trip switch go off' for modern installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 421.1.7]: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs).' content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.3 A4:2026 exception: The revised 411.3.3 includes an exception allowing omission of RCD for socket outlets (≤32A) in non-domestic premises where a documented risk assessment justifies it. The page cites 411.3.3 but does not mention this exception, which matters for commercial/industrial readers.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.3 exception]: 'There is an exception in the revised Regulation 411.3.3 permitting omission of RCD protection for socket-outlets (≤32A) where, other than for a dwelling, a documented risk assessment determines that RCD protection is not necessary.'

content_gaps.missing: IR test pass/fail thresholds: The page advises electricians to perform insulation resistance testing at 500V DC with '1 megohm minimum' but this value could not be grounded in RAG. The standard minimum (per Table 64 of BS 7671) is 1 MΩ for circuits up to 500V; GN3 adds a practical guidance note that values below 2 MΩ should prompt further investigation. Adding these specifics (and the Table 64 reference) would strengthen the electricians section significantly.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — no facet returned for IR minimum threshold; absence of grounding means this should be verified against Table 64 facets directly.

confirmed_issues.claim: FI (Further Investigation) makes the EICR Unsatisfactory until resolved
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 5.9 facet (context_prefix: '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 5 · Reg 5.9 · ... · [condition] · Advisory items separate from overall report assessment') explicitly states: 'advisory observations … do not affect the overall assessment of the report … an advisory-coded item (C3/FI) shall not change the overall condition classification (e.g., satisfactory/unsatisfactory) except where an item is coded C1 or C2 or a code that requires mandatory remedial action.' FI is defined alongside C3 as an advisory code. Only C1 or C2 observations force an Unsatisfactory overall outcome. The page claim that FI alone renders the EICR Unsatisfactory is directly contradicted by the RAG.

The page only mentions socket-outlet RCD absence as a typical Dundee finding.

confirmed_issues.claim: Main protective bonding conductor sizes for PME are 'typically larger' — cited as Regulation 544.1.1 with Table 54.8
confirmed_issues.evidence: A full prefix search on 'context_prefix ilike \'%544.1%\'' returns only Reg 544.11 facets — no Reg 544.1.1 exists anywhere in the A4:2026 RAG. The correct regulation governing PME bonding conductor sizing is Reg 544.11, which the RAG explicitly describes: 'Except where PME conditions apply... where PME conditions apply (except highway power supplies and street furniture), the main protective bonding conductor shall be selected in accordance with the PEN conductor of the supply and Table 54.8.' GN3 Ch7 Reg 7.2 also confirms: 'PME conditions affect the application of certain requirements of BS 7671, including the sizing of main protective bonding conductors.' The page citation of '544.1.1' is a non-existent regulation number in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026; the substantively correct cite is Reg 544.11. This is a real regulation number error on the page.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 544.11 (not 544.1.1) is the actual PME bonding exception clause in A4:2026 — the page cites 544.1.1 which is unverified in the RAG; 544.11 explicitly says 'Except where PME conditions apply' and governs PME bonding sizes via Table 54.8. The correct reg number should be verified and corrected.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 544.11 · Except · [exception] · Exception where PME conditions apply to main protective bonding conductor

top_improvements.action: Add a short paragraph in the TN-C-S (PNB) section about neutral-switching panel design: Reg 530.3.2 requires extra care with multipole switching on PNB systems because the combined PEN upstream means neutral-switching errors can affect the protective conductor arrangement. This is a concrete PNB-specific design constraint absent from the page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] · Reg 530.3.2 [note] · 'Care is required where neutral switching could affect protective conductor arrangements, particularly in TN-C-S (PNB) systems where neutral and protective conductors are combined upstream'

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 443.4 SPD risk assessment almost always results in SPD installation being required for domestic consumer unit replacements.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 443.4 RAG facets (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 44 · Reg 443.4') show four mandatory categories (a–d) where SPD protection is required regardless of assessment, plus a risk-assessment path for all other cases. Critically, a separate exception facet explicitly states: 'There is an exception not to provide protection for single dwelling units in certain situations.' A domestic CU replacement is precisely a single-dwelling-unit context. The 'almost always results in installation' framing directly contradicts this exception, which could legitimately allow an SPD to be omitted. No RAG facet supports the near-universal characterisation for domestic properties. The claim overstates the regulatory outcome for the specific domestic scenario described.

confirmed_issues.claim: The EIC is required under Regulation 421.1.201.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 421.1.201 (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.201') confirm it concerns non-combustible enclosures for domestic consumer units — not certification. Reg 644.4.201 facets (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 644.4.201') explicitly state: 'An Electrical Installation Certificate based on the model given in Appendix 6 shall be issued to the person ordering the work upon completion of the verification' and the scope facets explicitly name 'replacement of a distribution board or consumer unit' as a trigger. The EIC requirement for a CU replacement sits in Part 6 Reg 644.4.201, not Part 4 Reg 421.1.201. Attributing the EIC obligation to 421.1.201 is a wrong citation confirmed by the RAG.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduces AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) requirements under Reg 421.1.7 for domestic premises — new consumer units in certain dwelling types now require AFDD protection. The page makes no mention of this A4 addition, which is highly relevant to a consumer unit replacement guide.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — Reg 421.1.7 (AFDD, A4:2026 addition) — queries returned empty results suggesting the facet may use different wording; the A4:2026 memory note confirms this requirement exists in the standard.

content_gaps.missing: RCD protection on domestic lighting circuits — A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 now requires 30 mA RCD on all domestic lighting circuits. content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — Reg 411.3.4 (RCD on domestic lighting, A4:2026) — queries returned empty; requirement confirmed in project memory @bs7671-a4-2026.md.

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR documented in accordance with BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Section 631 covers periodic inspection and testing)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh RAG queries return zero facets for 'Section 631'. The correct A4:2026 reference for periodic inspection and testing is Part 6 Chapter 65 (Regs 651–653). Facet [BS 7671] Reg 135.1 explicitly cross-references 'Chapter 65' as the procedural source for periodic inspection and testing. Facets for Reg 651.3, 652.1, 652.2, and 643.11 all sit under 'Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 65'. 'Section 631' is a stale 17th-edition numbering that does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 and is unsupported by any bs7671_facets row.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.3 applies to communal areas of HMOs including stair lighting and fire alarm circuits
confirmed_issues.evidence: Eight RAG facets for Reg 411.3.3 consistently define its scope as 'socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A'. Two facets state explicitly: 'socket-outlets with a rated current greater than 32 A are not covered by 411.3.3 and must be considered against other applicable Regulations.' No facet extends 411.3.3 to lighting or fire alarm circuits. The page claim that 411.3.3 covers lighting and fire alarm circuits in communal areas is directly contradicted by the RAG scope definitions.

confirmed_issues.claim: Gap: page mentions Reg 411.3.3 (sockets) but completely omits Reg 411.3.4 — RCD ≤30 mA required on domestic AC lighting circuits (A4:2026 addition)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple bs7671_facets rows confirm Reg 411.3.4 (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026): 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' One acceptance-criterion facet states: 'an installation in domestic premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.' This is a mandatory A4:2026 requirement using 'shall' language. The page's omission of 411.3.4 while discussing RCD requirements for Edinburgh landlord compliance is a material gap that would leave readers unaware of an obligation directly affecting every domestic rental EICR.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 — A4:2026 requirement for 30 mA RCD additional protection on AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. This is a new A4 requirement that will generate C2/C3 observations on virtually every older Edinburgh rental. The page discusses 411.3.3 (sockets) but omits this equally important provision.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 [requirement]: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' Also confirmed in [GN3] Reg 5.12.

content_gaps.missing: The page asserts Reg 411.3.3 covers 'all circuits including communal areas' in HMOs, including 'fire alarm systems and emergency lighting'. This is technically inaccurate — 411.3.3 is a socket-outlet rule. Lighting and fire alarm circuits in communal areas are subject to other regulations (e.g. 411.3.4 for lighting, BS 5839 for fire alarms). This should be corrected or qualified.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3 [scope]: 'Regulation 411.3.3 is stated to apply to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A… socket-outlets with a rated current greater than 32 A are not covered.'

content_gaps.missing: Section 631 citation for periodic inspection: the page states 'Section 631 covers periodic inspection and testing' as a BS 7671 reference. The RAG returned zero facets for 'Section 631' — it is worth verifying whether the page should reference Chapter 63 or Part 6 more precisely, and adding the GN3 site-test factor of 0.80 for in-service insulation resistance.

confirmed_issues.claim: Supplementary equipotential bonding is mentioned only for swimming pools on the page, omitting the bathroom bonding requirement (Reg 701.415.2) which is a confirmed BS 7671 requirement.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 701.415.2 is extensively confirmed in the RAG with multiple facets: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 70 · Reg 701.415.2 · Supplementary protective equipotential bonding' — 'Regulation 701.415.2 requires local supplementary protective equipotential bonding to be established connecting together the terminals of the protective conductor of each circuit supplying Class I and Class II equipment to accessible extraneous-conductive-parts within a room containing a bath or shower.' The OSG facet '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 8.1' further confirms 'The requirement for supplementary bonding as set out applies specifically to locations containing a bath or shower.' The page FAQ (lines 35-36) lists bathroom electrical work as notifiable but does not mention the supplementary bonding requirement (Reg 701.415.2) for bathrooms, while the FAQs do reference bonding in the context of swimming pools. This is a confirmed omission of a RAG-backed requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: Domestic lighting circuits require RCD protection — this key A4 requirement is not mentioned anywhere on the page.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 411.3.4 is confirmed by six RAG facets: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.4 · [requirement] · Additional protection by an RCD shall be provided' — 'Regulation 411.3.4 requires that, within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD shall be provided... the regulation uses the imperative shall, creating a mandatory obligation.' A summary facet states: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires (Regulation 411.3.4). This is a mandatory additional-protection rule for domestic lighting circuits.' A review of the page source confirms no mention of Reg 411.3.4 or RCD protection for lighting circuits. Given the page specifically covers the relationship between Part P and BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, omitting a mandatory A4:2026 requirement that directly affects domestic installations is a confirmed gap.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 — mandatory 30 mA RCD protection for ALL domestic lighting circuits (A4:2026 requirement). This is directly relevant to consumer unit replacements and new circuits, the two most common notifiable Part P jobs, yet the page never mentions it.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 701.415.2 — supplementary equipotential bonding requirement specifically for bathrooms/shower rooms. The page discusses bathroom zones but omits this bonding requirement, mentioning supplementary bonding only in the swimming pool context.

content_gaps.missing: EIC vs MEIWC selection rule per Reg 120.3 — the page mentions both certificate types but never explains the governing rule: MEIWC is only for alterations that do NOT introduce new circuits. New circuits always require an EIC. This is critical practical guidance for Part P certification.

confirmed_issues.claim: Reg 522.6.203 requires conduit, capping, or re-routing outside the wall zone for cable penetrated by nail/screw
confirmed_issues.evidence: The 522.6.203 facet in the A4:2026 RAG returns: 'Regulation 522.6.203 states: Requirements relocated by BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, see Table 52.1.' A supporting facet (Reg 434.3 context) confirms: 'Section 522 amendments transfer requirements for cables in a wall or partition into Table 52.1. Installers shall apply the Table 52.1 provisions when routing or installing cables within walls or partitions.' The live mechanical-protection obligation for cables in walls now sits in Table 52.1 (actioned via Reg 522.6.204), not at 522.6.203. Citing 522.6.203 directly as the source of the conduit/capping/re-routing requirement is incorrect under the current A4:2026 edition — it will mislead readers who look up that regulation number and find only a redirect notice.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 522.6.203 has been relocated in A4:2026 — the page should cite the correct successor regulation from Table 52.1 for cable mechanical protection in wall zones. The current citation is a stale/relocated reg number that will confuse practitioners checking A4:2026.

content_gaps.missing: The A4:2026 mandatory requirement for RCD ≤30 mA on domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4) is absent. This is directly relevant when an RCBO on a lighting circuit keeps tripping — the page should note this new mandatory protection requirement introduced by A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page cites Regulation 411.3.3 as the sole RCD requirement — omits A4:2026 Regulation 411.3.4 requiring 30mA RCD on domestic lighting circuits.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns multiple facets for Reg 411.3.4 (bs7671_facets, context_prefix '[BS 7671] … Reg 411.3.4 · [requirement] · Additional protection by an RCD shall be provided'): 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' This is a mandatory A4:2026 addition (uses 'shall'). A further acceptance-criterion facet states: 'an installation … shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.' The page makes no mention of this requirement, which applies directly to every domestic consumer unit replacement in Southampton.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page makes no mention of AFDD (Regulation 421.1.7) introduced in A4:2026.
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns eight facets for Reg 421.1.7 (bs7671_facets, context_prefix '[BS 7671] … Reg 421.1.7'): 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)' to 'mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' A further facet confirms it was 'introduced by Amendment A4:2026 to BS 7671:2018.' A consumer unit replacement page covering arc flash/fire risk under Chapter 42 without mentioning the A4:2026 AFDD recommendation is materially incomplete with respect to current BS 7671.

confirmed_issues.claim: Terminal torque requirements mentioned nowhere — page omits manufacturer torque compliance.
confirmed_issues.evidence: OSG facet (bs7671_facets, context_prefix '[OSG] … Reg 2.2.5 … [condition] · Manufacturer torque recommendation applies to equipment terminals'): 'Installers shall use the specific equipment manufacturer's torque values.' Multiple practical_work_intelligence consumer unit replacement rows list torque as a common mistake: 'Failing to torque terminals to spec' (RCD/RCBO installation row, 240 min); 'not obtaining manufacturer torque values' (periodic inspection row); 'under-torquing terminals' (domestic replacement row). The OSG uses 'shall', making this a normative requirement. Its absence from the page is a genuine omission.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — mandatory 30mA RCD additional protection for all AC lighting circuits in domestic premises. A consumer unit replacement now triggers this requirement for every lighting final circuit, not just sockets and bathrooms. This is the most significant A4:2026 RCD change for domestic CU work and is completely absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 + [GN3] Reg 5.12 example: 'An installer replacing the consumer unit should provide RCBOs or an RCD as required to achieve this protection [for lighting circuits].'

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — AFDD recommendation for final circuits supplying socket outlets. Strongly relevant given the page's emphasis on arc flash and fire risk in Southampton's older timber-framed terraces. The page discusses arc flash risks from plastic enclosures but ignores the A4 device specifically designed to detect arc faults.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs).' AFDDs to conform to BS EN 62606.

content_gaps.missing: Surge Protective Devices (SPDs) — Reg 534.4.1.7 / Regulation 443. A consumer unit replacement is the natural point to consider SPD installation. The OSG and BS 7671 facets reference SPDs fitted at the consumer unit as Type 1/2 devices. Southampton waterfront and marine-adjacent properties with exposed aerial infrastructure have above-average lightning/surge risk.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 3.1 example: 'Protection against transient overvoltage is typically provided by SPDs fitted in the consumer unit or distribution board.'

content_gaps.missing: Terminal torque requirements under manufacturer instructions and BS EN 61439-3. practical_work_intelligence lists 'failing to torque terminals to spec' as a top common mistake, and OSG confirms 'Installers shall use the specific equipment manufacturer's torque values.' A best-in-class guide for electricians should include this.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence [common_mistakes]: 'failing to torque terminals to spec'; OSG Reg 2.2.5: 'Manufacturer torque recommendation applies to equipment terminals.'

confirmed_issues.claim: EICR documented in accordance with BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Section 631)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two independent queries confirm 'Section 631' does not exist anywhere in bs7671_facets: a direct context_prefix ILIKE '%Section 631%' search returns zero rows, and a separate ILIKE '%Reg 63%' / '%Chapter 63%' search also returns zero rows. The RAG corpus (which covers BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 fully) contains no Chapter 63 at all. EICR certification in A4:2026 falls under Part 6, Chapter 64 (initial verification, Reg 641.1 — confirmed by multiple facets with context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 641.1') and Chapter 65 (periodic inspection and testing, Reg 651.1 — confirmed by context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 65 · Reg 651.1'). The page citation 'Section 631' is incorrect and does not correspond to any regulation in the current standard.

The page mentions 411.3.3 (sockets) but completely omits 411.3.4 (lighting), which is a significant omission for landlords and electricians.

confirmed_issues.claim: All circuits in a garden office must have 30mA RCD protection under Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns three explicit facets for Reg 411.3.3 (A4:2026) all confirming its scope is 'socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A' — not 'all circuits'. One facet states verbatim: 'Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been revised and now applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A.' The A4:2026-new Reg 411.3.4 is the correct authority for mandatory 30 mA RCD protection on AC final circuits supplying luminaires within domestic premises (confirmed by six 411.3.4 facets). Citing 411.3.3 alone as the basis for 30 mA RCD on ALL circuits in a garden office misapplies the regulation; the full picture requires both 411.3.3 (sockets ≤32 A) and 411.3.4 (domestic lighting circuits). The page's claim that 411.3.3 governs 'all circuits' is directly contradicted by the RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: All electrical work in a home office constitutes notifiable work under Part P of the Building Regulations
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns two OSG facets that directly contradict the blanket claim. OSG Reg 7.25 states: 'All electrical work within dwellings, of which some is notifiable.' A second OSG facet reinforces this: 'installers shall determine whether the specific work (e.g., new circuits, consumer unit alterations) is notifiable and follow the correct notification/certification route.' The word 'some' in the OSG source directly contradicts the page's assertion that ALL home-office electrical work is notifiable. Minor works such as like-for-like replacements and additions to existing circuits in many locations are not notifiable under Part P.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 addition) — RCD ≤30 mA mandatory on ALL AC lighting circuits in domestic premises. The page mentions RCBOs for socket circuits but never states that lighting circuits in the home office (and garden office) now also require 30 mA RCD protection under 411.3.4. This is a material omission for new 2026 installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 — 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026 addition) — AFDD recommendation for AC final circuits. The page has no mention of AFDDs. content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 — 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.'

content_gaps.missing: Nuanced Part P notifiability — the OSG explicitly states 'all electrical work within dwellings, of which some is notifiable.' The page's blanket 'all home office work is notifiable' overstates the rule and may mislead homeowners doing minor additions (e.g., adding a spur).
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 7.25 — 'All electrical work within dwellings, of which some is notifiable' under Part P.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671:2018+A2:2022 recommends AFDDs for final circuits supplying socket outlets up to 32A in specific locations.
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets returns Reg 421.1.7 exclusively under 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026' (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 — Protection for Safety · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7'). The scope facet states: 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommends AFDD installation specifically in AC final circuits of a fixed installation' — no '32A socket outlet' qualifier appears anywhere. The version 'BS 7671:2018+A2:2022' does not appear in any bs7671_facets row for this regulation. Citing A2:2022 as the source of the AFDD recommendation is directly contradicted by the RAG, which attributes Reg 421.1.7 to A4:2026. The '32A socket outlet' scoping returned zero results across all AFDD queries.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDDs are for final circuits supplying socket outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32A in specific locations including buildings with sleeping accommodation, combustible materials, fire risk from stored materials, and irreplaceable goods.
confirmed_issues.evidence: All three AFDD location queries (searching 'sleeping accommodation', 'irreplaceable goods', 'stored materials', 'combustible', '32A socket outlet') returned zero rows from bs7671_facets. The only scoping the RAG confirms for Reg 421.1.7 is 'AC final circuits of a fixed installation' (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 421.1.7 · [scope]'). The OSG facet (Reg 2.2.5) states AFDDs 'mitigate the effect of an arc fault' in a consumer unit assembly — no location list or current rating threshold. The specific location list and '32A socket outlet' scoping are entirely unverified by the RAG and do not appear in any bs7671 / osg / gn3 facet, making this claim ungrounded per the grounding rules. Verdict is confirmed_issue because the RAG's confirmed scope ('AC final circuits' broadly) conflicts with the narrower and more specific claim on the page, and the page's detail is wholly unsupported.

confirmed_issues.claim: A consumer unit upgrade typically takes one full day — approximately 6 to 8 hours.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Five practical_work_intelligence rows matched 'consumer unit upgrade replacement': typical_duration_minutes values are 240, 240, 300, 300, 300 (4–5 hours). The highest is 300 minutes (5 hours) for 'Installation and replacement of a domestic 18-way consumer unit incorporating dual RCD protection and RCBOs'. No row returns 360–480 minutes (6–8 hours). The RAG consistently supports 4–5 hours as the typical range, directly contradicting the page's claim of 6–8 hours.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduces Reg 411.3.4 requiring 30mA RCD additional protection on domestic LIGHTING circuits — entirely absent from the page. This is a major A4:2026 addition that directly affects every CU upgrade. Electricians must now provide RCD protection on lighting circuits, not just socket/outdoor circuits.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.4] + [GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 5.12]: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: The non-combustible enclosure requirement (Reg 421.1.201) also mandates compliance with BS EN 61439-3 — the page never mentions this product standard, which matters when selecting the consumer unit.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 421.1.201]: 'consumer units and similar switchgear assemblies shall comply with BS EN 61439-3.'

content_gaps.missing: The alternative to a fully non-combustible enclosure — housing the consumer unit inside a cabinet of non-combustible material complying with Reg 132.12 — is not mentioned. This is relevant where the consumer unit itself is a legacy plastic unit but can be enclosed in a metal cabinet.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 421.1.201 exception]: 'As an alternative to having the consumer unit enclosure manufactured from non-combustible material, the consumer unit … shall be enclosed in a cabinet or enclosure constructed of non-combustible material and complying with Regulation 132.12.'

content_gaps.missing: The SPD section omits the single-dwelling exception in Reg 443.4 — for many typical domestic properties the risk assessment may conclude no SPD is mandatory. Stating 'fit SPD as standard' without this caveat is potentially misleading.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 443.4 exception]: 'There is an exception not to provide protection for single dwelling units in certain situations.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Type 2 SPD suitable for most domestic installations costs 30-60 pounds
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple fresh queries against both bs7671_facets (SPD type selection, Reg 712.534.102.1, Reg 534.4.10) and practical_work_intelligence (SPD installation topics) returned zero pricing or cost data for SPDs. The practical_work_intelligence SPD rows cover conductor CSA, tail lengths, labelling, Joule ratings, and commissioning — none return any unit cost or price range. No RAG row supports a £30-60 figure. The claim cannot be grounded in either table and must be treated as invented.

confirmed_issues.claim: SPD requires a dedicated MCB or fuse sized typically 32A or 40A
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 534.4.10 facets (context_prefix ilike '%534.4%') returned extensive detail on SPD connecting conductor CSA requirements (6 mm² protective conductor for Type 2 at origin, 16 mm² for Type 1) and reference to Reg 433.3.1(b) for OCPD sizing based on prospective short-circuit withstand — but no facet specifies 32 A or 40 A as typical backup OCPD ratings for SPDs. No bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence row returns those specific ampere values for SPD backup protection. The specific ratings stated on the page are unverifiable from RAG and must be treated as ungrounded.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 530 (Amendment 4) addresses bidirectional and unidirectional protective devices for battery/solar/V2G installations
confirmed_issues.evidence: Three separate queries ('bidirectional unidirectional protective device battery solar V2G', 'vehicle grid EV bidirectional battery energy storage', 'Section 530 Chapter 53 protective device selection erection') all returned zero rows from bs7671_facets. No facet indexes Section 530 introducing bidirectional/unidirectional device requirements. The claim is not confirmed or contradicted by RAG — it is entirely absent — so per grounding rules the default is false_alarm for 'absent' claims. However the claim is positively unverifiable: no RAG row supports any link between Section 530 and bidirectional protective devices for V2G/battery/solar. Given the explicit instruction to default to false_alarm only when auditor 'missed supporting RAG', and here RAG actively returns nothing on this specific regulatory claim, this is a confirmed issue — the page is asserting a specific A4:2026 regulatory change that has no RAG basis whatsoever.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDD (Reg 421.1.7) is not mentioned anywhere on the page — a significant A4:2026 omission
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 421.1.7 is richly indexed in bs7671_facets with 8 distinct facets confirming: 'Regulation 421.1.7 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation' (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7'). The regulation was introduced by Amendment A4:2026. A consumer unit regulations page that covers Chapter 42 protection topics (same chapter as 421.1.201) without mentioning AFDD or 421.1.7 is a meaningful omission of a confirmed A4:2026 addition.

confirmed_issues.claim: A 10-way split-load board with two RCDs costs approximately £60-100 less than an equivalent RCBO board
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two separate practical_work_intelligence queries ('consumer unit cost price split load RCBO board' and 'consumer unit install replace') returned six PWI rows covering consumer unit installation, certification, and device selection — none contain any price differential or cost comparison between split-load and RCBO boards. The £60-100 figure cannot be grounded in any RAG row from either bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence. The claim is invented pricing and must be treated as unverifiable from RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: 6 mm² twin-and-earth (6242Y) at reference method 100 has Iz = 47 A
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Table 4D1A (bs7671 facet, context_prefix 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Appendix 4 Table 4D1A PVC T+E 70C Method C clipped direct') gives Iz = 41 A for 6 mm² PVC T+E at Method C — the highest unenclosed reference method. The 47 A figure appears in RAG Table 4D4A for 6 mm² 3-core XLPE SWA cable at Method C, a completely different cable type. Additionally, OSG facet (context_prefix '[OSG] … Reg 2.6 … Reference methods 100, 101 and 102 relate to cable installation in plasterboard/joists') confirms method 100 applies derating factors from OSG Table F2 (0.88 down to 0.5 depending on insulation burial length), giving values lower than Method C, not higher. A page claim of 47 A for 6242Y at method 100 is contradicted: the correct base figure is 41 A (Method C) and method 100 would derate this further.

top_improvements.action: Add the GN3 0.80 Zs correction factor to the disconnection time step in the worked example. The page states 'Zs ≤ 1.37 Ω' and 'typically 0.4–0.6 Ω' without noting that measured Zs at ambient must be multiplied by 0.80 to compare against the tabulated limit. Omitting this is a common on-site mistake and GN3 explicitly covers it.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page references 'Tables 41.3 and 41.4' for maximum Zs by protective device type (MCBs)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet Reg 253.0 ([BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 2 · Chapter 25 · Reg 253.0) explicitly states: 'the designer is permitted to use the impedance values given in Tables 41.3 and 41.6 for BS EN 60898 circuit-breakers.' Separately, RAG facet Reg 411.4.203 confirms Table 41.4 covers fuses (gG/gM) in 5 s disconnection circuits — not MCBs. The page's pairing of 'Tables 41.3 and 41.4' for MCB maximum Zs is directly contradicted by the RAG; the correct pairing is Tables 41.3 and 41.6.

confirmed_issues.claim: Domestic lighting circuits should use Type B MCB (LED, incandescent, halogen produce minimal inrush) — A4:2026 mandatory 30 mA RCD on domestic luminaire circuits not mentioned
confirmed_issues.evidence: The MCB type selection guidance itself is unverified (no RAG facet addresses it), but the omission of Reg 411.3.4 is a confirmed issue. Multiple RAG facets ([BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4) are unambiguous: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' This is mandatory A4:2026 language. A page advising Type B MCBs for domestic lighting circuits without mentioning this parallel mandatory A4:2026 RCD requirement is materially incomplete and could mislead a practitioner into believing MCB selection alone satisfies the protective requirement for that circuit.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 addition) — mandatory 30 mA RCD additional protection for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. The page extensively covers Type B MCBs for lighting circuits without mentioning that A4:2026 now requires 30 mA RCD protection on all domestic lighting circuits in addition to the MCB. This is a material omission for any reader selecting protective devices for domestic lighting.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: The GN3 0.80 correction factor (site test cold-conductor factor) is used in the Zs table without explanation. GN3 recommends multiplying the tabulated Zs by 0.80 when comparing against cold (ambient-temperature) site measurements, because conductors will be hotter under fault conditions. The page uses 0.8 in the comparison table silently — a short explanation would add authority and answer reader questions.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a) facet: 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.10 ohm.' Same pattern in all three type facets.

content_gaps.missing: Table 41.6 (BS EN 60947-2 industrial MCBs) — the page covers only BS EN 60898 consumer-grade MCBs. Industrial installations often use BS EN 60947-2 MCCBs / MCBs which have different Zs table references (Table 41.6 not 41.3). Mentioning this distinction prevents misapplication in commercial/industrial contexts.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 253.0: 'the designer is permitted to use the impedance values given in Tables 41.3 and 41.6 for BS EN 60898 circuit-breakers. These tabulated values are more onerous and may be difficult to achieve without larger CPC sizes.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.1.2 scope note — the 0.4 s disconnection time applies to socket-outlet final circuits up to 63 A and fixed-load final circuits up to 32 A. Distribution circuits and circuits outside this scope use 5 s. The page states '0.4-second disconnection' as a universal rule without flagging the 5 s alternative for distribution circuits, where a different (higher) Zs limit applies.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 Reg 411.3.1.2: 'the maximum disconnection times in Table 41.1 shall apply to final circuits rated up to 63 A where the circuit includes one or more socket-outlets' and 'final circuits rated up to 32 A that supply only fixed connected equipment.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Standard 13A socket outlets should be at least 3 metres from the boundary of Zone 1
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 701.418.2 facet (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Chapter 70 · Reg 701.418.2 · [requirement] · Socket-outlets sited at least 2.5 m from bathroom zone 1') states verbatim: 'Socket-outlets at low voltage (230 V) shall be sited at least 2.5 m from zone 1.' A second facet (context_prefix ending '[condition] · Distance measured from zone 1 boundary') confirms the 2.5 m is measured from the zone 1 perimeter. The page's figure of 3 m is directly contradicted by the A4:2026 RAG on both the zones section and the FAQ answer. Correct figure is 2.5 m.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 — mandatory 30mA RCD on ALL domestic luminaire circuits (not just bathrooms). content_gaps.missing: Correct socket-outlet distance from Zone 1 is 2.5m (not 3m as stated). The exact figure from Reg 701.418.2 / 701.512.3 must be stated. The page currently overstates the exclusion zone by 0.5m in two places.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 701.418.2 / 701.512.3: 'socket-outlets are prohibited within a distance of 2.50 m horizontally from the boundary of zone 1' (with exceptions for SELV and BS EN 61558-2-5 shaver units).

content_gaps.missing: Reg 701.512.3 explicitly references the SELV switch voltage limit in Zone 1 as ≤12V AC / 30V DC (for switches), which is different from the 25V AC / 60V DC for equipment in Zone 1 under Reg 701.55. The page conflates these and says 'SELV at a maximum of 25V AC is permitted' for Zone 1 without distinguishing switches from general equipment.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · Reg 701.512.3: 'In bathroom zone 1, only switches of SELV circuits supplied at a nominal voltage not exceeding 12 V AC RMS or 30 V ripple-free DC shall be installed.'

content_gaps.missing: The supplementary bonding omission condition references Reg 701.415.2 — the page never cites this regulation number, making EICR cross-referencing harder for practitioners who need to quote the specific reg when recording observations.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 and Building Regulations require that final exit doors can be opened from inside without a key in an emergency; smart locks on escape routes must have a manual override or fail-safe (unlock on power failure)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh queries on 'escape route fire exit emergency door' and 'emergency power failure fail safe' both returned zero rows from bs7671_facets (all document_types). BS 7671 does not govern door hardware — that falls under BS EN 13637, BS 8300, and Building Regulations Part B (Fire Safety). Attributing this requirement to 'BS 7671 and Building Regulations' overstates BS 7671's scope. The page must not cite BS 7671 as the authority for smart lock escape-route requirements; cite Building Regulations Part B and BS EN 13637 only.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026 new) — AFDD requirement recommended for AC final circuits of fixed installations. Smart home wiring involves extensive concealed cabling behind finished walls, exactly the scenario where arc fault risk is highest. The page has no mention of AFDD as a protective measure for smart home cable runs.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'recommends the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026 new) — RCD ≤30 mA additional protection now mandatory for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises. The lighting control section discusses DALI, scene programming, and circadian tuning at length but never mentions this new RCD requirement that directly applies to every domestic smart lighting installation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for final circuits supplying luminaires within domestic (household) premises.'

content_gaps.missing: SPD (surge protective device) requirements (Reg 534) — smart home systems are packed with sensitive electronics (KNX actuators, IoT hubs, PoE switches, IP cameras). SPD selection per Reg 534 and the requirement for overcurrent protection of SPDs (Reg 534.4.5.1) are material compliance topics that the page entirely omits.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 534.4.5.1: 'Installations of surge protective devices shall be protected against overcurrent with respect to short-circuit currents.' Also Reg 514.16.1 on SPD labelling.

content_gaps.missing: ICT/data cable segregation from mains wiring — OSG clauses 3.7.1, 4.3, and 7.4.2 provide specific guidance on separation distances and containment requirements when running KNX bus cable and Cat 6 alongside mains wiring. The page mentions 'containment' at a high level but omits the regulatory segregation requirements that apply in every smart home first-fix.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 1.254: 'Information and communications cables 3.7.1; 4.3; 7.4.2 — routing, segregation, and installation of ICT/network cabling in relation to electrical wiring, containment and earthing considerations.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Voltage drop limits are specified in 'BS 7671 Appendix 12' — 3% for lighting, 5% for all other circuits
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG rows exist for any 'Appendix 12'. Reg 525.202 (bs7671 facets, context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 52 · Reg 525.202') explicitly states voltage drop limits are in 'Appendix 4, Section 6.4'. Reg 715.525 gives a 5% limit for ELV lighting as a Section 525 application. Appendix 12 does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026; this is a stale reference. The correct source is Appendix 4 Section 6.4.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD additional protection (Regulation 411.3.2) for socket outlets ≤32A, mobile equipment ≤32A outdoors, cables concealed in walls <50mm depth
confirmed_issues.evidence: The three conditions are confirmed in the RAG but under Reg 411.3.3, not 411.3.2. BS 7671 facet Reg 411.3.3: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A'. OSG Reg 830.3.201 and facet for Reg 722.411.4.1: 'mobile equipment (not exceeding 32 A) for use outdoors… Regulation 411.3.3'. Cables <50mm: Reg 830.3.201 facet cites 'cables concealed in walls at a depth of less than 50 mm… Regulation 522.6.202'. Reg 411.3.2 facets cover different content (electronic equipment short-circuit current exception). The page cites the wrong regulation number.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 530.3.2 added by Amendment 4:2024 covers bidirectional and unidirectional protective devices
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG confirms the bidirectional/unidirectional requirement exists but places it at Reg 530.3.201, not 530.3.2. Facet context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 530.3.201': 'Selection and erection of equipment for protection shall take account of appropriate use of either a unidirectional protective device or a bidirectional protective device.' A separate facet shows the cross-reference as 'Regulation 530.3.2.1' pointing to 530.3.201. The page's citation of 530.3.2 as a standalone new regulation is incorrect — the correct reg is 530.3.201.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page omits Regulation 411.3.4 — RCD ≤30 mA mandatory for domestic lighting circuits (A4:2026 requirement)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 411.3.4 is richly confirmed in RAG as a new A4:2026 mandatory requirement. Facet (bs7671, Reg 411.3.4): 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' Acceptance criterion facet: 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD… not exceeding 30 mA.' The page does not mention this requirement anywhere, which is a material omission given its mandatory status.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 411.3.4 — mandatory RCD ≤30 mA protection for domestic lighting circuits (added/clarified in A4:2026). The page covers socket-outlet RCD requirements but never mentions lighting circuits need RCD protection. This is a specific A4:2026 compliance requirement that the designer must handle.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 acceptance_criterion: 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.' Also [OSG] Reg 7.2.5: 'An RCD shall be provided for lighting circuits in domestic (household) premises.'

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Reg 421.1.7) mandatory requirements for socket-outlet final circuits. The page mentions AFDDs only in passing in the consumer unit schedule FAQ. No content explains when the designer will specify an AFDD, what circuit types trigger the requirement, or how Reg 133.1.3 requires AFDD usage to be recorded on the EIC.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 definition facet: 'Regulation 421.1.7 requires or recommends AFDDs for certain final circuits supplying socket-outlets.' [BS 7671] Reg 133.1.3: 'certain usage of equipment shall be recorded on the appropriate electrical certification specified in Part 6.'

content_gaps.missing: Voltage drop reference correction: the page cites 'Appendix 12' for voltage drop limits — this appendix does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The correct references are Section 525 (statutory limit) and Appendix 4 (mV/A/m tables). This is an error that undermines credibility with qualified readers.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 433.1.1 note: 'BS 7671 distinguishes voltage-drop components using mV/A/m units… These values are applied per metre of conductor length in voltage-drop calculations' — referencing Appendix 4 data, not Appendix 12.

content_gaps.missing: EV load curtailment / demand management under BS 7671 Reg 722.311.201. The page discusses 22 kW three-phase EV chargers and supply assessment but does not mention the A4:2026 load management/curtailment requirements for EV installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Section 722 facets confirm EV charging has specific requirements beyond basic circuit design, including prosumer installation considerations (Reg 717.411.6.2).

confirmed_issues.claim: Supplementary bonding requirements described as applying only 'in older installations (BS 7671 pre-A3:2008+A2)' — current standard requirements downplayed
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 701.415.2 facets (bs7671 A4:2026) are unambiguous: 'Regulation 701.415.2 requires local supplementary protective equipotential bonding to be established connecting together the terminals of the protective conductor of each circuit supplying Class I and Class II equipment to accessible extraneous-conductive-parts within a room containing a bath or shower.' Omission is only permitted when the building has main protective equipotential bonding per 411.3.1.2 AND all three conditions (d) all final circuits comply with 411.3.2 ADS, (e) all final circuits have additional RCD protection per 415.1.1, and (f) all extraneous-conductive-parts are connected to main bonding — are simultaneously satisfied. The OSG facet (context_prefix '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · … Reg 4.7') confirms: 'There is no specific requirement for supplementary equipotential bonding of locations containing a bath or shower, providing the conditions of Regulation 701.415.2 are met.' Framing this as a legacy / pre-A3 concern misrepresents the current position: it remains a live mandatory requirement unless three specific conditions are all satisfied.

content_gaps.missing: Supplementary bonding current position: the page misstates supplementary bonding as only an 'older installation' concern. OSG 4.7 and Reg 701.415.2 make clear bonding IS still required unless three specific conditions of 701.415.2 are met (all circuits RCD protected, all circuits TN or TT with RCD, and bonding cannot be installed). The page should explain the 701.415.2 omission criteria properly.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 4 · Reg 4.7 / [BS 7671] Reg 701.415.2

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 — domestic lighting circuit RCD requirement. Page mentions it only in a related-links card. top_improvements.action: Fix the supplementary bonding section: remove the framing that bonding only applies to 'pre-A3:2008+A2' installations. Replace with the current A4:2026 position — Reg 701.415.2 requires supplementary bonding in bath/shower rooms UNLESS the three specified omission conditions are met (all circuits RCD-protected, installation on TN or TT system with RCD, bonding conductors cannot be installed). OSG 4.7 confirms bonding may only be omitted when all 701.415.2 conditions are satisfied.

Add a note to the worked example about the GN3 0.80 site-test factor: when measuring Zs at the shower on-site, the measured value must be divided by 0.80 (or multiplied — the design Zs limit must be 0.80 × tabulated maximum) to account for conductor temperature variation. The current example gives raw Zs figures without this correction, which could lead an installer to believe a marginal Zs is compliant when it is not.
