confirmed_issues.claim: Table 61 minimum insulation resistance is 1M ohm for circuits up to 500V tested at 500V DC
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple bs7671_facets for Reg 643.3.2 (A4:2026) explicitly place the 500V DC / 1.0 MΩ rule in Table 64, not Table 61. Context prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3.2 · … Low voltage up to 500 V insulation minimum 1.0 MΩ at 500 V DC'. The facet content states: 'Numeric minima from Table 64: … Circuits up to and including 500 V (excluding SELV/PELV) = 1.0 MΩ (at 500 V DC)'. No facet in bs7671_facets mentions Table 61. The 1 MΩ threshold is correct; the table number 'Table 61' is stale A3/17th-edition numbering and is contradicted by the A4:2026 RAG. The regulation now lives at Reg 643.3.2 / Table 64.

confirmed_issues.claim: Readings 0.5M to 1M ohm are C2 (Potentially Dangerous); below 0.5M ohm are C1 (Danger Present)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG returns GN3 facets confirming C1/C2/C3/FI coding is used on EICRs for insulation observations (GN3 Ch.3 Reg 3.11, GN3 Ch.5 Reg 5.10), but no facet anywhere provides a 0.5 MΩ numeric sub-threshold separating C1 from C2. The only insulation resistance numeric thresholds in the RAG are from Table 64 (Reg 643.3.2): 1.0 MΩ pass/fail minimum. The specific 0.5 MΩ C1/C2 boundary is absent from all bs7671_facets, gn3, and osg facets queried. The GN3 facets explicitly describe C1/C2 assignment as requiring professional judgement without numeric sub-thresholds for insulation resistance codes.

confirmed_issues.claim: New installation should read 200M ohm or higher (1M ohm is the absolute minimum floor)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Exhaustive queries across bs7671_facets, gn3, and osg using terms '200 megohm', '200 MΩ', 'new installation insulation benchmark', and 'typical new wiring' returned zero results. The only numeric insulation resistance figures in the RAG are the Table 64 minima (Reg 643.3.2): 0.5 MΩ for SELV/PELV, 1.0 MΩ for circuits up to 500V, 1.0 MΩ for circuits above 500V. No facet mentions 200 MΩ as a benchmark, guideline, or typical value for new installations. This figure is entirely ungrounded in the RAG and should not be presented as a standard expectation.

content_gaps.missing: Table 64 (A4:2026) replaces Table 61 (A3) as the insulation resistance reference — the page cites the stale table number. Any reader checking their copy of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 will not find a 'Table 61' for this purpose; the correct chapter is Chapter 64 / Reg 643.3.2.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 643.3.2 / Table 64 — 'Table 64 test voltages and minimum insulation resistance values'

content_gaps.missing: The page gives no guidance on earthing system type (TN-S, TN-C-S, TT) and how it affects diagnosis and Zs interpretation. For TT systems the fault current is limited by electrode resistance, making MCB operation unreliable — RCD protection is essential. This is a major practical gap for any engineer diagnosing earthing faults.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Part 3 Ch.31 · Earthing arrangements; also [OSG] OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) references earthing system selection

content_gaps.missing: Earth fault loop impedance (Zs) testing is the follow-up verification after locating and repairing an earth fault. The page discusses Zs conceptually in the 'low-impedance fault' bullet but does not explain that Zs must be measured and recorded post-repair to confirm adequate disconnection time per Reg 643.7.1.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 643.7.1 — 'The measured Zs shall demonstrate that the protective device will operate within the disconnection time required by 411.4'

content_gaps.missing: SELV/PELV circuits (e.g. extra-low voltage lighting, fire alarm wiring) use 250V DC test voltage with a 0.5M ohm minimum per Table 64. The page only covers the 500V/1M ohm case. Missing this creates a gap for engineers testing ELV circuits.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Table 64: 'For SELV and PELV circuits use 250 V DC with minimum 0.5 MΩ'

confirmed_issues.claim: Soil resistivity: wet clay 5–40 ohm-metres; garden loam 10–150; chalk 60–400; dry sand/gravel 200–3000; rock 1000–100,000+
confirmed_issues.evidence: No bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence facet returns any soil resistivity table or numeric ohm-metre ranges by soil type. All searches on terms including 'soil resistivity', 'clay', 'loam', 'chalk', 'sand', 'gravel', 'ohm metre' returned zero rows. These values are engineering convention (sourced from BS 7430 or similar) but are entirely absent from the RAG (bs7671_facets covers BS 7671, GN3, OSG only). Presenting specific numeric resistivity ranges as part of BS 7671 course content without RAG grounding is a confirmed issue per the grounding rules.

confirmed_issues.claim: Typical earth resistance ranges: 10–50 Ω wet clay; 50–200 Ω damp loam; 200–500 Ω dry sandy soil; 500–5000+ Ω rock/gravel
confirmed_issues.evidence: No bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence facet returns any table of typical earth resistance ranges correlated to soil types. They are also distinct from the soil resistivity table in claim 6 (resistivity in ohm-metres vs. electrode resistance in ohms) — neither table is present in the RAG. Both sets of numeric values are unattributable to BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, GN3, or OSG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Metallic water pipes should not be relied on as sole means of earthing
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 542.2.6 is present in the RAG and states unambiguously that 'The metallic pipe of a water utility supply shall not be used as an earth electrode' — an outright prohibition, not merely a caution against sole reliance. The page claim ('should not be relied on as sole means') materially understates the regulation: the prohibition under 542.2.6 is absolute for water utility supply pipes regardless of whether other means are also present. Additionally the same regulation prohibits metallic pipes for gases or flammable liquids as earth electrodes entirely. The page framing misrepresents what the code actually requires and is therefore a confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 531.3.5.3.2 and Table 53.1: the normative table correlating maximum Ra (Ω) to maximum RCD IΔn is never mentioned. Readers need to know they must select IΔn from Table 53.1 — not just compute RA × IΔn ≤ 50V — to cover seasonal worst-case. This is a separate and additional requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS7671 2018+A4:2026, Part 5, Chapter 53, Reg 531.3.5.3.2 — 'RCD rated residual current shall not exceed value from Ra table (Table 53.1)'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 643.7.3 / Reg 643.7.2: the specific BS 7671 inspection-and-testing regulations requiring RA measurement for TT systems are never cited. The page discusses recording values but does not reference the legal mandate in Chapter 64.

content_gaps.missing: Ra definition precision: the page uses RA to mean only the electrode resistance, but BS 7671 Reg 411.5.3 defines Ra as 'the sum of the resistances of the earth electrode AND the protective conductor connecting it to the exposed-conductive-parts'. The protective conductor resistance component is omitted throughout.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS7671 2018+A4:2026, Part 4, Chapter 41, Reg 411.5.3 definition facet — 'Ra is the sum of the resistances of the earth electrode and the protective conductor connecting it to the exposed-conductive-parts.'

content_gaps.missing: GN3/OSG guidance on recording soil conditions and environmental state at time of test is present in the RAG but absent from the 'Recording Values' section. confirmed_issues.claim: Page omits that domestic lighting circuits now require 30 mA RCD protection under Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) facets are extensive and unambiguous. The verbatim regulatory text returned: 'within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' The word 'shall' makes this mandatory. The acceptance criterion facet states: 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA.' If the page discusses RCD requirements for sockets and concealed cables but not lighting circuits, it is materially incomplete under A4:2026.

confirmed_issues.claim: Amendment 4 to BS 7671 (expected 2026) may strengthen the AFDD recommendation — framing it as future/expected
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 421.1.7 facets explicitly state: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)' and 'This regulation is a new insertion introduced by Amendment A4:2026 to BS 7671:2018.' A4:2026 is already the current published edition referenced throughout the RAG. The page framing A4 as 'expected 2026' or future is factually wrong — A4:2026 is already in force and Reg 421.1.7 is already the operative regulation. This is a confirmed staleness error on the page.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDDs recommended specifically for sleeping accommodation, combustible constructional materials, fire-propagating structures, irreplaceable goods (museums)
confirmed_issues.evidence: All Reg 421.1.7 facets (bs7671, gn3, osg) confirm only the general scope: 'AC final circuits of a fixed installation to mitigate the risk of fire due to arc fault currents.' The regulation's wording is general — no facet in the A4:2026 RAG names sleeping accommodation, combustible construction, fire-propagating structures, or museums as specific target locations for 421.1.7. That location-based list reflects older 17th Edition / pre-A4 guidance (Section 421 sub-clauses from the 17th Ed). It has no grounding in the current A4:2026 text as held in bs7671_facets or the GN3/OSG companion documents. Presenting it as the basis for the A4:2026 AFDD recommendation is misleading.

confirmed_issues.claim: Job typically takes one full day — approximately 6 to 8 hours
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple practical_work_intelligence entries for straightforward domestic consumer unit replacement (removal, installation, re-termination, testing, EIC issue) consistently show typical_duration_minutes = 240 (4 hours). Entries with 300 minutes (5 hours) cover certification-inclusive or notifiable-work scenarios. Entries at 360 minutes cover more complex work such as managing lead/rubber cable discoveries or full RCBO upgrades. No standalone CU replacement entry reaches 360–480 min (6–8 hours) without a complicating factor. The page's claim of '6 to 8 hours' overstates the RAG-supported typical duration by 50–100%.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026): domestic lighting circuits now require 30 mA RCD additional protection — a mandatory A4:2026 change that directly affects consumer unit design. Page mentions only socket outlets and concealed cables, omitting this requirement entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' GN3 Reg 5.12 confirms scope.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 is already in force as part of A4:2026 — the page frames it as future/expected. The AFDD section should be updated to cite 421.1.7 explicitly and note it applies to AC final circuits generally (not just the old location list), with the recommendatory wording confirmed.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation.'

content_gaps.missing: AFDD operational confirmation requirement on completion: Reg 421.1.7, 532.6 and 651.2(e) require the installer to confirm AFDD(s) show correct operational indication at handover. No mention of this on-completion check in the step-by-step or certification sections.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.9.3: 'Regulations 421.1.7, 532.6 and 651.2(e) require confirmation that Arc Fault Detection Device(s) (AFDDs) are operational... installer shall confirm AFDDs show the correct operational indication.'

content_gaps.missing: Type B RCDs: for EV charger circuits (especially 3-phase or V2G) and certain inverter equipment, Type B RCDs may be required rather than Type A. The page discusses EV chargers as a trigger for board replacement but never mentions the RCD type upgrade requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 534.4.7 and OSG Reg 3.6.4.5: Type B RCDs required for residual smooth DC and pulsating DC superimposed on smooth DC — relevant where EV chargers or PV inverters generate DC residual fault currents.

confirmed_issues.claim: An EICR is documented in accordance with BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 (Section 631)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh SQL: `select ... where context_prefix ilike '%Section 631%'` returns zero rows across all document types. The A4:2026 RAG places periodic inspection reporting exclusively in Part 6 Chapter 65 — confirmed by facets at '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 65 · Reg 653.1' (multiple facets returned). 'Section 631' is a 17th-edition numbering artefact that does not exist in the A4:2026 RAG. The page's reference to 'Section 631' is structurally wrong and unsupported by any RAG facet.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 132.13 of BS 7671 specifically addresses the requirement to issue a periodic inspection report (EICR) for rented properties
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh SQL: `select ... where context_prefix ilike '%Reg 132.13%'` returns zero rows. Querying `context_prefix ilike '%Reg 132.%'` confirms Reg 132.x is in Part 1 Chapter 13 (e.g. Reg 132.4: 'Electrical supply systems for safety services or standby electrical supply') — design principles, not periodic inspection. The EICR report obligation is in Part 6 Chapter 65 (Reg 652.2, 653.1, 653.4). Reg 132.13 has no presence in the RAG and appears invented.

confirmed_issues.claim: The EICR report includes Schedules of Circuit Details and Test Results as required by Section 631
confirmed_issues.evidence: The schedules requirement itself is strongly grounded: GN3 facet '[GN3] · GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 1 · Reg 1.3 · [requirement] · EICR to include Schedule(s) of Circuit Details' states verbatim 'An Electrical Installation Condition Report (EICR) shall be accompanied by Schedule(s) of Circuit Details following periodic inspection and testing.' However, the attribution to 'Section 631' is wrong — fresh SQL returns zero facets for 'Section 631', and GN3 Reg 1.3 cross-references Appendix 6 and Chapter 64/65, not Section 631. The substantive claim is correct; the cited regulation is an A4:2026 non-existent reference.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — mandatory RCD additional protection (≤30 mA) on ALL AC lighting circuits in domestic premises. This is a new A4:2026 requirement that will trigger C2 observations on the majority of Manchester's Victorian terraced houses, which have unprotected lighting circuits. The page covers socket-circuit RCD protection but completely omits this major new requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4 · 'Regulation 411.3.4 requires that, within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — AFDD recommendation for AC final circuits. EICR inspectors assessing Manchester terraces should note AFDDs as a C3 improvement where absent. The page never mentions AFDDs despite covering Victorian wiring hazards at length.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7 · 'Regulation 421.1.7 recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits — wording is advisory (not mandatory).'

content_gaps.missing: Correct BS 7671 A4:2026 regulation numbering for EICR reporting — periodic inspection reports are governed by Chapter 65 (Reg 653.1, 653.4) and Chapter 64 (Reg 643.11), not 'Section 631'. The page cites Section 631 twice and Reg 132.13 once; neither exists in A4:2026.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 · Chapter 65 · Reg 652.2 / 653.1 — 'REPORTING FOR PERIODIC INSPECTION AND TESTING' is the correct chapter heading and regulation family.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 0.8 site-test derating factor: when verifying Zs values at periodic inspection, test results must be multiplied by a correction factor (typically conductor temperature at time of test) before comparing to tabulated maximum Zs. Relevant to reporting test results on Manchester terraces in winter when conductor resistance is lower.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] · Chapter 2 · Reg 2.29 — 'Measuring Ze at the origin is intended to determine the external component used to calculate circuit Zs by addition with internal conductor resistances (R1+R2). This supports verification of protective device disconnection times.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Type B MCBs trip magnetically at 3–5 times rated current; Type C at 5–10 times; Type D at 10–20 times
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh RAG queries against bs7671_facets (all three document_types) and practical_work_intelligence returned zero rows containing the 3–5×/5–10×/10–20× instantaneous-trip multiplier bands. The PWI does return rows confirming Type B/C/D as selection categories (keywords: 'Type B', 'Type C', 'Type D', 'switching surge', Table 7.2.7(ii)) but none of those rows state the specific numeric multiples. These multipliers originate from BS EN 60898, which is not represented in the corpus. The figures are widely accepted industry values but are entirely ungrounded in the available RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: Breaking capacity of domestic MCBs is typically 6kA
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh queries for '6kA', '6000 ampere', 'domestic MCB breaking capacity' returned zero rows in bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence. The GN3 facet at [GN3] Reg 7.0 confirms 'rated breaking capacity shall be considered … to ensure it can clear the prospective fault current' but gives no specific domestic kA value. The page's assertion of 6kA as a typical domestic figure is ungrounded in the corpus.

confirmed_issues.claim: Maximum Zs values are tabulated in BS 7671 Appendix 3, Tables 41.3 and 41.4
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG is definitive on location: Table 41.3 is in Chapter 41, cited under Reg 411.4.202 ('values specified in Table 41.3 … used instead of calculation'); Table 41.4 is also in Chapter 41 under Reg 411.4.203 ('maximum values of earth fault loop impedance … stated in Table 41.4'). Appendix 3 is described in Reg 253.0 as giving 'time/current characteristics … used as the basis for determining limiting values of earth fault loop impedance prescribed in Chapter 41' — i.e. the curves that underpin the tables, not the tables themselves. The page attribution 'Appendix 3, Tables 41.3 and 41.4' is factually wrong: Tables 41.3 and 41.4 are Chapter 41 tables, not Appendix 3 tables.

confirmed_issues.claim: Rewirable fuses in older properties during EICR attract a C3 (Improvement Recommended) — not a defect alone
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 facets confirm C3 = 'Improvement recommended' (non-urgent, advisory) in general. However, no facet in bs7671_facets (bs7671, gn3, or osg document_types) specifically addresses the EICR coding for rewirable/semi-enclosed fuses. The claim that rewirable fuses attract C3 rather than C2 is unverified against the corpus — the RAG provides no facet that either confirms or refutes the specific C3 assignment for this observation type. Following the grounding rules, absence of supporting evidence means this cannot be confirmed as grounded.

confirmed_issues.claim: 20A radial socket circuits serve areas up to 50m² floor area — per Appendix 15 of the On-Site Guide
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh queries for 'radial socket circuit floor area', '50 square metres', and 'Appendix 15 On-Site Guide' returned zero rows in bs7671_facets (all document_types including osg). OSG facets returned for socket circuits (Reg 7.5.3, 12.513) address CPC continuity arrangements and ring/radial topology but contain no floor-area limits or reference to an 'Appendix 15'. Neither the 50m² figure nor an OSG Appendix 15 covering socket-circuit areas is grounded in the corpus.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 0.80 cold-measurement correction factor: measured site Zs must not exceed 0.80 × tabulated Zs (e.g. 1.10 Ω for B32, not 1.37 Ω). The page discusses Zs compliance but never mentions this critical on-site adjustment that catches real-world failures.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Part 4 Chapter 41 Reg 411.4.204(a) Table 41.3(a) Type B MCB 230V': 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.10 ohm.'

content_gaps.missing: Actual Zs table values for common MCBs (B6: 7.28 Ω / 5.82 Ω; B16: 2.73 Ω / 2.18 Ω; B32: 1.37 Ω / 1.10 Ω). Including a condensed table would make this page the authoritative on-site reference electricians bookmark.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets Table 41.3(a) rows for B6, B16, B20, B32 MCBs — Reg 411.4.204(a).

content_gaps.missing: The disconnection time framework is stated as '0.4s socket / 5s fixed' but the page does not explain that these come from Table 41.1 (socket circuits, Reg 411.3.1.2) and Reg 411.8.3 (absolute 5s cap). Adding the regulation references would strengthen E-E-A-T credibility.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets Reg 411.3.1.2 and Reg 411.8.3.

top_improvements.action: Fix the FAQ's table reference: change 'Appendix 3, Tables 41.3 and 41.4' to 'Chapter 41, Tables 41.3 and 41.4 (within Appendix 3 of the Regulations book)' — or simply 'BS 7671 Tables 41.3 and 41.4'. Appendix 3 in the regs is the measurement correction procedure, not the Zs tables, and confusing these will mislead practitioners.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets Reg 253.0 (Appendix 3 = temperature-correction procedure) + Reg 411.4.202/411.4.204 (Table 41.3 = Zs limits in Chapter 41).

top_improvements.action: Add a Zs reference table for common domestic MCBs (B6/B16/B32 at minimum) showing both the tabulated BS 7671 Zs and the GN3 0.80 cold-site-measurement limit. This is the most-referenced data an electrician needs on a job — making it available on this page turns it into a bookmark. Example: B32 = 1.37 Ω (book) / 1.10 Ω (site measured).

Add a callout explaining the GN3 0.80 site-measurement correction: measured Zs on site must not exceed 80% of the tabulated maximum because the conductor is measured cold and the table assumes operating temperature. This is the single most common compliance error electricians make when reading Zs values.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets Table 41.3(a) facets: 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor)' explicitly stated for every MCB row.

top_improvements.action: Add regulation references to the '0.4s / 5s' disconnection time statements — cite Reg 411.3.1.2 (socket circuits → Table 41.1) and Reg 411.8.3 (5s absolute maximum). This strengthens E-E-A-T and gives readers a verifiable source.

confirmed_issues.claim: Alarm cables routed with general power circuits (implied acceptable by the page's silence on this requirement in the 'For Electricians' section)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Four distinct PWI rows explicitly list cable co-routing as a common mistake: (1) primary_topic 'Design and installation of alarm and emergency systems' — common_mistakes includes 'routing alarm cables with power circuits'; (2) 'Installation of low-current radial circuits for burglar and fire alarm systems' — common_mistakes includes 'Running alarm cable alongside power cables without segregation'; (3) 'Installation and maintenance considerations for alarm and emergency systems' — common_mistakes includes 'Routing alarm/emergency cabling in the same trunking as general power'; (4) 'Installation and segregation of alarm and emergency systems' — common_mistakes includes 'Running alarm cabling in same containment as power cables'. Additionally, OSG facet [OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4), Reg 7.4.1] states safety-critical circuits including fire alarm must be separated from other cables. The page's electrician-facing content omits this requirement entirely, leaving a misleading gap that the PWI consistently flags as the most common installation mistake on alarm work.

content_gaps.missing: Cable segregation requirement: alarm/emergency system cables must be separated from mains power circuits and must not share containment with general wiring. Failure to segregate is flagged as the single most common installation mistake.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence — primary_topic: 'Design and installation considerations for alarm and emergency systems to ensure separation from other circuits'; common_mistakes: 'routing alarm cables with power circuits', 'routing alarm cables with general power cables', 'Routing alarm/emergency cables in same containment as power circuits'; keywords include 'BS 7671, segregation'

confirmed_issues.claim: Where 230V is unavoidable, a 30mA RCD must be used; in wet conditions a 10mA RCD should be considered
confirmed_issues.evidence: The 30mA part is grounded: practical_work_intelligence has multiple facets on '30mA RCD supplementary protection for socket-outlet and portable equipment circuits' (referencing Reg 411.3.3) and 'Provision and testing of supplementary RCD protection not exceeding 30 mA for socket-outlets and mobile equipment used outdoors'. However, no facet in either bs7671_facets or practical_work_intelligence references a 10mA RCD threshold for wet conditions in confined spaces or anywhere else. The 10mA wet-conditions recommendation is not grounded in the RAG at all. While it is consistent with some manufacturer and HSE guidance, it is not supported by BS 7671 or PWI, and the page presents it alongside the 30mA claim without qualification. The 10mA figure is unverifiable from the RAG and the page does not caveat it as non-BS-7671 guidance.

confirmed_issues.claim: Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 require employers to record risk assessments where there are five or more employees
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets Reg 712.534.102.6 (broad risk assessment query) returned a facet with context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 · Reg 712.534.102.6 · [reference] · Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 require risk assessments' with content: 'The Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 require employers and self-employed persons to assess risks to workers… a risk assessment should involve identification of measures needed to comply with the law; guidance is available from HSE INDG163.' This grounds the MHSWR 1999 risk assessment requirement in the RAG. However, the facet says nothing about the five-employee threshold for written records (MHSWR Reg 3(5)). The page claim adds the specific five-employee trigger, which is not present in the RAG facet and is not something BS 7671 states. The RAG evidence actually shows BS 7671 cites MHSWR in a PV context (Reg 712), not in a confined-space context, making the regulatory citation on the page also potentially misapplied. The five-employee threshold is legally correct per MHSWR Reg 3(5) but is unverifiable from the RAG and is technically superseded — since 2003 MHSWR Reg 3(5) has been interpreted as all employers having a duty to record significant findings regardless of headcount.

content_gaps.missing: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 712.534.102.6 explicitly cross-references DSEAR 2002 and the Electricity at Work Regulations 1989 for potentially explosive atmosphere installations — the page mentions ATEX 2016 but omits DSEAR 2002 and EAWR 1989 from its ATEX section entirely, which are the primary UK legal instruments an electrician needs to know.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — Reg 712.534.102.6 [reference] DSEAR and related regs for potentially explosive atmospheres

content_gaps.missing: The BS 7671 Part 7 Chapter 71 skilled/instructed person definitions (Reg 710.1) are directly relevant — confined space electrical work must only be carried out by a 'skilled person (electrically)' as defined. The page mentions 'competent' generically but never maps this to the BS 7671 / EAWR Reg 16 definition hierarchy (skilled / instructed / ordinary person).
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — Reg 710.1 [definition] Person definitions: skilled, instructed, ordinary; GN3 Reg 4.9 [reference] Regulation 16 EAWR requirement for competence

content_gaps.missing: Equipotential bonding considerations for metallic structures in confined spaces (tanks, vessels, cable ducts) — the prohibition on earth-free local equipotential bonding in special locations (Reg 701.410.3.6, 721.411.3.1) is highly relevant where a worker is in contact with metalwork and the installation may be modified. The page mentions 'increased shock risk from earth contact' but does not link this to the BS 7671 bonding requirements.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — Reg 721.411.3.1 [procedure] Documentation requirement for bonding arrangements in special locations; Reg 701.410.3.6 [requirement] Earth-free local equipotential bonding prohibition

content_gaps.missing: HSR25 and GN3 guidance on live vs dead working in confined spaces — GN3 Reg 1.1 explicitly states inspectors/testers shall follow HSR25 for live and dead working. The page covers isolation well but never states that all work in a confined space should be dead working under EAWR and that live work requires an additional positive justification under EAWR Reg 14.
content_gaps.rag_source: GN3 Reg 1.1 [reference] HSE HSR25 guidance on live and dead working; GN3 Reg 1.1 [requirement] Inspector shall follow HSR25 for regulatory compliance

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation 134.2 requires periodic inspection regimes that confirm installations remain safe
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh RAG query on context_prefix ilike '%134%' AND '%Reg%' returns only Reg 134.1.4 facets (joints/connections) under Part 1 Chapter 13. No Reg 134.2 exists anywhere in bs7671_facets. Periodic inspection is grounded in Part 6 Chapter 65 (Reg 651.3, Reg 652.1) — a completely different part of the standard. 'Regulation 134.2' is a fabricated reg number; Chapter 13 numbering stops at 134.1.x.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Regulation Section 621 establishes that installations must be periodically inspected
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG facets returned for context_prefix ilike '%621%' that relate to inspection (only inverter regs 712.x appeared). Full-text search on 'periodic inspection frequency' returns Reg 652.1 under Part 6 Chapter 65 — not Section 621. 'Section 621' does not exist in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026; it is a stale 17th-edition reference. The correct A4:2026 location is Part 6 Chapter 65 (Section 651 onwards).

confirmed_issues.claim: Owner-occupied domestic: every 10 years recommended; properties over 25 years old every 5 years
confirmed_issues.evidence: Full-text queries on 'periodic inspection frequency domestic interval' and '10 year domestic inspection recommended' both return zero facets with specific numeric intervals. The only relevant facet is Reg 652.1 which states frequency 'shall be determined having regard to the type of installation' without specifying any numeric intervals. No RAG facet in bs7671_facets (bs7671, gn3, or osg document types) supports the 10-year or 25-year-old threshold claims. These figures originate from GN3 guidance tables that are not present in the RAG, making the claims ungrounded against the available source of truth.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDD Reg 421.1.7 — page omits this A4:2026 requirement entirely
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG fully confirms Reg 421.1.7 exists: context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7' with content 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs).' This is an A4:2026 addition. A page covering EICR requirements and citing A4:2026 that makes no mention of AFDDs has a material content gap — inspectors must check AFDD installation and operational indication (per Reg 651.2(e)) on an EICR.

confirmed_issues.claim: Domestic lighting circuits require RCD protection (411.3.4) — not mentioned on page
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms Reg 411.3.4 with mandatory wording: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' Multiple facets confirm the 'shall' obligation and that non-compliance is a valid EICR observation. This is an A4:2026 requirement entirely absent from the page — a real content gap for an EICR-focused guide targeting domestic properties.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 new requirement: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) installation recommended under Reg 421.1.7 for certain final circuits. An EICR on a Bristol property built or rewired after A4:2026 must check for AFDD compliance. The page claims A4:2026 compliance in its opening section but does not mention AFDDs at all.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs).' Also Reg 411.9.3 confirmation checklist and Reg 532.6.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 new requirement: Domestic lighting circuits now require RCD ≤30 mA additional protection under Reg 411.3.4. This is directly relevant to Bristol Georgian/Victorian rewires and consumer unit upgrades — an inspector finding unprotected lighting circuits in a domestic property must now record this as a C2/C3 depending on context.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires.' GN3 Reg 5.12 confirms scope to domestic premises.

content_gaps.missing: EICR Schedule of Circuit Details and Test Results must now include fields for AFDD presence and SPD functionality confirmation (per Reg 133.1.3 equipment recording obligation and Reg 651.4 cross-reference to 411.9.3). Page mentions Schedules of Circuit Details generically but does not tell landlords or electricians about the new EICR form fields introduced by A4:2026.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 133.1.3: 'certain usage of equipment shall be recorded on the appropriate electrical certification specified in Part 6.' Reg 411.9.3 checklist items 4.23 (AFDD) and SPD functional indication (651.4).

content_gaps.missing: RCD testing procedure detail: GN3 confirms RCD verification must be performed at 0.5×IΔn, 1×IΔn and 5×IΔn. The page describes 'RCD operation times' in the testing section but gives no thresholds or multi-test detail that would help a landlord understand what pass/fail means or help an electrician verify the methodology claimed.
content_gaps.rag_source: [practical_work_intelligence] 'Verification of RCD trip performance using 0.5 × IΔn, 1 × IΔn and 5 × IΔn instrument tests to confirm correct sensitivity and timing characteristics of residual current devices during initial inspection and periodic testing.'

confirmed_issues.claim: B20 MCB: maximum Zs = 2.18Ω (page states 2.18Ω)
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Table 41.3(a), Reg 411.4.204(a)) explicitly states: 'Maximum earth fault loop impedance Zs for a Type B MCB … rated 20 A at 230 V is 2.19 ohm.' The page states 2.18Ω. This is a direct 0.01Ω discrepancy with the tabulated standard value. Note also that the B16 cold-site limit (GN3 0.80 × 2.73 = 2.18Ω) matches 2.18 exactly, suggesting the page may have accidentally cited the B16 GN3 site-limit instead of the B20 table value. This is a real error.

confirmed_issues.claim: The 0.8 temperature correction factor is 'a design guideline recommended by the IET, not a regulation within BS 7671 itself'
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 411.4.204(a) (Table 41.3(a)) embed the 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor)' directly within each tabulated Zs entry — e.g. B20: '2.19 ohm … Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor): 1.75 ohm.' Reg 411.4.204 NOTE 2 states that if conductors are at a different temperature when tested the reading 'should be adjusted accordingly (see Appendix 3).' The 0.80 factor is therefore embedded in the normative table structure and required by Appendix 3 methodology, not merely an optional IET recommendation. Framing it as a non-regulatory 'design guideline' understates its standing in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 practice.

content_gaps.missing: Table 41.5 (Reg 411.5.3) — maximum Zs values for RCD-protected TT circuits (e.g. 30mA RCD: Zs limit 1667Ω; 100mA: 500Ω; 300mA: 167Ω; 500mA: 100Ω). Page only explains RCDs bypass Zs limits qualitatively but omits the actual Table 41.5 Zs limits for TT/RCD combinations, which are highly practical.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS7671 Table 41.5 Reg 411.5.3 facets: IDeltaN 30mA → Zs max 1667Ω; 100mA → 500Ω; 300mA → 167Ω; 500mA → 100Ω

content_gaps.missing: The GN3 0.80 site-test factor is built into GN3 normative guidance (confirmed as 'Cold-measured site limit (GN3 0.80 factor)' in Table 41.3 facets). The page soft-pedals it as merely an optional IET recommendation, which may mislead electricians who treat it as skippable.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS7671 Table 41.3(a) Reg 411.4.204(a): all entries include the GN3 0.80 corrected value as a standard reference alongside the tabulated maximum

content_gaps.missing: Reg 643.7.3.1 defines 'relevant impedances' for earth fault loop assessment and specifies that these shall be measured or determined by alternative method. The page describes measurement well but does not cite this testing regulation, which is the direct authority requiring the test.
content_gaps.rag_source: BS7671 Reg 643.7.3.1: 'relevant impedances refers to the earth fault loop impedance values... These shall be measured or determined by alternative method when required.'

content_gaps.missing: OSG Reg 1.3 / OSG requirement to obtain Ze from the distributor before starting work on new installations — a practical pre-work step the page omits. Designers and installers on new-build work should get the distributor's stated Ze rather than always measuring.
content_gaps.rag_source: OSG 9th Ed:2022 Reg 1.3: 'installer shall obtain the typical maximum external earth fault loop impedance (Ze) from the distributor; this value can be used with the consumer's protective device characteristics to calculate Zs'

confirmed_issues.claim: IMD triggers alarm when insulation resistance drops below 50 kohms
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG rows returned for any numeric insulation resistance threshold ('50 kilo ohm insulation resistance medical', 'kohm kiloohm resistance threshold alarm medical IT'). Reg 710.538.1.101 facets confirm MED-IMD is mandatory and shall conform to BS EN 61557-8:2015 Annexes A and B, but those annexes are not reproduced in the RAG and no threshold figure appears anywhere in bs7671_facets. The 50 kΩ value is technically associated with BS EN 61557-8 Annex B but is absent from the RAG's source of truth. Under the grounding rules, a specific numeric claim not returned by any facet is unverifiable and must be treated as a confirmed issue — the page states a precise figure (50 kΩ) that cannot be grounded in bs7671_facets.

confirmed_issues.claim: Group 1 locations require 30 mA RCD protection on all circuits supplying medical equipment
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG rows returned for '30mA RCD medical equipment circuit protection' or 'Group 1 medical location RCD 30 mA'. What the RAG does confirm at Reg 710.411.5.201 is that RCDs 'shall be used as protective devices' in Group 1 and Group 2, with an exception for medical IT system circuits (per 710.411.6). Reg 710.531.3.3.101 states Type AC RCDs shall not be used in Group 1/2 — meaning a specific Type is prohibited but no rated operating current (30 mA) is named. The 30 mA figure is not grounded in any facet; the RAG actually indicates that circuits of a medical IT system in Group 1/2 are exempt from the RCD requirement entirely (710.411.5.201 exception). The specific claim that 30 mA RCDs are required on all Group 1 circuits supplying medical equipment is unverifiable from the RAG and partially contradicted by the exception at 710.411.5.201.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page references BS 7671:2018+A2:2022 in the body copy and related page description
confirmed_issues.evidence: Every facet in bs7671_facets carries context_prefix stamped '2018+A4:2026'. A facet at Reg 433.1 explicitly lists the amendment chronology: A1:2020, A2:2022, A3:2024, A4:2026 — confirming A2:2022 is a superseded interim amendment. The current edition is BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. Any page copy citing 'BS 7671:2018+A2:2022' is referencing a stale superseded edition and directly contradicts the edition string carried by every RAG facet.

confirmed_issues.claim: AFDDs shall not be used in circuits in Group 0 and Group 2 medical locations (page omits this entirely)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 710.421.1.7 facet (context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 7 … Reg 710.421.1.7') states verbatim: 'AFDDs shall not be used in circuits in medical locations of group 0, and 2.' Six facets confirm this prohibition applies separately to Group 0 circuits and Group 2 circuits. Reg 710.421.1.7.101 adds a further prohibition: 'AFDDs shall not be used in circuits supplied by IT systems specified as medical IT systems in Regulation 710.411.6.' Both are A4:2026 additions that are fully grounded in the RAG. A page covering hospital electrical systems that omits these safety-critical prohibitions has a genuine and material content gap — this is a confirmed issue, not merely a gap in the auditor's evidence.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 addition: Reg 710.421.1.7 prohibits AFDDs in Group 0 and Group 2 medical location circuits; Reg 710.421.1.7.101 specifically prohibits AFDDs in circuits supplied by medical IT systems (Reg 710.411.6). This is a clinically important and practically significant A4:2026 change that the page entirely omits.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Section 712 governs prosumer installations — a V2H charger that exports into the dwelling is a prosumer source and must be designed to Section 712
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Reg 711.42 (context_prefix: 'Reg 711.42 · Applicability condition for Section 712 to three PV scenarios') states explicitly: 'Section 712 applies under three distinct conditions: (a) PV installations not connected to public distribution; (b) PV installations in parallel with public distribution; (c) PV installations as an alternative to public distribution.' V2H is not a PV installation; none of these three conditions applies to a bidirectional EV charger. RAG Reg 826.7 / 722.826.3.201 and OSG 12.1 confirm that Chapter 82 is the correct framework for 'all types of low voltage electrical installations that include local production and/or storage of energy', designated as Prosumer's Electrical Installations (PEIs). RAG Reg 570.4 note is the direct cross-reference: 'For the supply of the fixed installation by electric vehicles, refer to Section 722 and Chapter 82' — Section 712 is not mentioned. The page is wrong to cite Section 712 as the governing section for a V2H prosumer installation.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 712 labelling, isolation and protective coordination requirements apply to V2H bidirectional chargers
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Reg 712.514.102 (context_prefix: 'Reg 712.514.102 · Each point of access to live parts on the DC side, such as distribution boards and combiner boxes') shows the Section 712 labelling requirement is specifically the 'SOLAR DC — Live parts can remain energized after isolation' notice for PV DC-side access points — it is scoped to PV DC circuits and has no application to a V2H charger. The correct regulation for isolation, earthing and ADS across operating modes for a V2H / PEI is RAG Reg 826.1.1.1 (Chapter 82): 'Where the system earthing may differ between operating modes and ADS is relied on, the designer shall take into account the impact of the different earthing arrangements on ADS operation'; and 'Any switching device used to disconnect the PEI from the public distribution network shall be suitable for isolation per Chapter 53.' The auditor's finding is fully supported: Section 712 isolation/labelling clauses are PV DC-only; Chapter 82 Reg 826.1.1.1 is the correct provision for V2H.

content_gaps.missing: Chapter 82 (Prosumer's Electrical Installations, Part 8) is the correct BS 7671 framework for V2H prosumer rules — not Section 712. The page never mentions Chapter 82. Chapter 82 Reg 826.1.1.1 governs earthing across operating modes (connected vs island), ADS requirements in island mode (Reg 551.4), and isolation device compliance (Chapter 53).
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 8 — Functional Requirements · Chapter 82 · Reg 826.1.1.1 / Reg 722.826.3.201

content_gaps.missing: Reg 722.311.201 — A4:2026 explicit permission for EV load curtailment (automatic or manual load reduction/disconnection) to be taken into account when determining maximum demand. Relevant to V2H installs where smart-charging or V2H discharge reduces peak demand on the supply. The page has no mention of this regulation.

Reg 722.312.2.1 — explicit prohibition on including a PEN conductor in the circuit supplying EV charging equipment in TN-C-S (PME) supplies. The page mentions TN-C-S earthing generally but never cites this regulation, which is one of the most practical compliance points an installer needs to know.

Annex A722 (informative guidance on TN systems where PME/PEN conditions apply) is referenced in the RAG and provides practical earthing guidance specific to PME/TN-C-S EV installs. Useful complementary material for V2H earthing design sections.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [exception] · Annex A722 is informative guidance for TN systems where PME applies

These would strengthen the UPS and safety-services sections considerably.

No discussion of IP rating requirements for electrical enclosures within cleanrooms under BS 7671 Reg 134.1.1 and 416.2. Given cleanrooms use aggressive cleaning agents and may have wash-down regimes, specifying appropriate IP ratings (e.g. IP54, IP65) is a key gap.

Fire barrier and fire-stopping requirements under BS 7671 Reg 830.3.201 / GN3 Reg 5.19 are mentioned only briefly in the sealed-cable-entries section without citing the regulatory basis. Adding the explicit reg reference and noting that the fire-stop must maintain the wall's fire rating would add compliance depth.

No mention of AFDDs (Arc Fault Detection Devices, Reg 421.1.7) — in A4:2026 these are now required/recommended for certain socket-outlet final circuits. In pharmaceutical cleanrooms where arc faults could cause catastrophic contamination or fire, AFDDs are highly relevant and their omission is a gap versus a best-in-class guide.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.1.2 covers main protective bonding to gas and water services
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG query on context_prefix ilike '%411.3.1.2%' returns 6 facets, all unambiguously placing Reg 411.3.1.2 in Chapter 41 covering Table 41.1 maximum disconnection times for final circuits (socket-outlet circuits ≤63 A and fixed-load circuits ≤32 A). Separately, a topic query on 'main protective bonding gas water extraneous conductive' returns Reg 544.1.2 (Chapter 54) as the correct regulation: 'Regulation 544.1.2 requires that the main protective bonding connection to any extraneous-conductive-part (for example gas, water or other metallic pipework or service) shall be made as near as practicable to the point of entry of that part into the premises.' The page attribution of bonding requirements to 411.3.1.2 is factually wrong per the A4:2026 RAG.

confirmed_issues.claim: The EICR classification system is defined in 'BS 7671 Section 631'
confirmed_issues.evidence: A query on context_prefix ilike '%631%' returns zero rows related to EICR classification — only unrelated PAS 63100 battery-storage references. A direct query on context_prefix ilike '%Chapter 65%' or '%Reg 651%/%652%/%653%' returns multiple facets confirming Chapter 65 (Reg 653.1, Reg 643.11) as the correct location for Condition Reports and C1/C2/C3/FI coding: 'Reporting for periodic inspection and testing shall be completed following an inspection, including completion of the Periodic Inspection Report and any recommendations, codes (C1/C2/C3/FI) and remedial actions.' No Section 631 periodic-inspection facet exists anywhere in the RAG. Section 631 does not cover EICR classification in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 544.1.2 (not 411.3.1.2) is the correct citation for main protective bonding to gas and water services. The page currently misstates the regulation number, which could mislead electricians reading the guide.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 544.1.2 context_prefix: 'Main protective bonding to any extraneous-conductive-part such as gas, water or other metallic pipework or service shall be made as near as practicable to the point of entry.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced Reg 421.1.7 (AFDD recommendation for AC final circuits). content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'recommends the installation of AFDDs... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation.'

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced Reg 411.3.4 requiring 30 mA RCD protection on all domestic lighting circuits. Properties without this (the vast majority pre-2026) will likely receive a C2 or C3 observation on EICR. The page discusses RCD absence on sockets (411.3.3) but entirely omits this new lighting-circuit requirement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 and GN3 Reg 5.12: 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires ... protected by RCD not exceeding 30 mA in domestic premises.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.3 now explicitly states that the RCD exception (allowing omission where risk assessment permits) is NOT available for dwellings. The page discusses absence of RCDs as a common C2 finding but does not mention this tightened A4:2026 position, which strengthens the negotiating case for buyers of older properties.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3: 'omission of RCD protection on socket-outlets (≤32 A) is not permitted for dwellings under this exception.'

top_improvements.action: Fix the regulation citation in the 'Earthing and bonding' bullet: replace 'Regulation 411.3.1.2' with 'Regulation 544.1.2'. Reg 411.3.1.2 is the disconnection-time rule for Table 41.1 circuits; main protective bonding to gas and water is Reg 544.1.2. A factually wrong regulation number on a buyer-facing guide undermines credibility with any electrician reader.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 544.1.2: 'main protective bonding connection to any extraneous-conductive-part such as gas, water ... shall be made as near as practicable to the point of entry.'

top_improvements.action: Add an A4:2026 callout box in the 'Properties Over 25 Years Old' section noting that under the 2024 amendment (A4:2026), absence of RCD protection on domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4, 30 mA) is now an EICR observation point alongside socket circuits. Most pre-2026 properties will lack this protection — buyers of any home wired before 2026 should expect this as a likely C2/C3 finding.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.4 and GN3 Reg 5.12: 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires protected by RCD not exceeding 30 mA in domestic premises.'

top_improvements.action: Add a brief mention of AFDDs (arc fault detection devices, Reg 421.1.7) in the consumer unit section. Inspectors carrying out EICRs on pre-2025 boards are increasingly noting absence of AFDDs as a C3 observation. Buyers should understand this as a post-purchase upgrade cost, not a safety emergency — but it is a real cost that should be factored into negotiations for older properties with a full rewire or board replacement quote.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7: 'recommends the installation of AFDDs to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Dedicated 32A final circuit from consumer unit required (Regulation 722.533.101)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 722.533.101 exists in the RAG but is a fuse-standard cross-reference table — its facets cite BS EN 60269-1, BS HD 60269-2, BS HD 60269-3:2010+A2:2022, BS EN 60269-6:2011+A1:2023, and BS EN IEC 60309. No facet under 722.533.101 mentions a dedicated circuit, a 32A rating, or a consumer unit. The dedicated-circuit principle is supported by separate RAG evidence: PWI rows (primary_topic: 'provision of a dedicated circuit conforming to BS 7671 section 722') and a 722.1 scope facet (example: 'dedicated final circuit installed to supply a domestic EV charging point'), but neither attributes this to 722.533.101. The regulation number on the page is wrong — 722.533.101 is a fuse-standards reference, not a dedicated-circuit requirement.

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD protection under Regulation 722.531.101 — Type B RCD or Type A RCD with 6mA DC RDC-DD
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 722.531.101 does not exist in the RAG. A full enumeration of all 722.5xx regulation numbers present in bs7671_facets returns only: 722.511.1, 722.511.101, 722.5111, 722.531.3.101 (transformer siting), and 722.533.101 (fuse standards). There is no 722.531.101. Full-text searches for 'RCD Type B Type A RDC-DD electric vehicle charging' and 'RCD protection EV charging DC fault' returned zero rows across all document types. The regulation number cited on the page is fictitious — the real EV earthing/protection regs in the RAG are 722.411.4 (PME prohibition for outdoor charging on TN systems) and 722.421.1.7.201 (AFDD exemption). The Type B / RDC-DD claim may be technically accurate per IET Code of Practice but it cannot be grounded to this regulation number.

confirmed_issues.claim: Installation takes 3 to 5 hours for a standard 7kW EV charger
confirmed_issues.evidence: PWI does contain EV charger rows. Querying by keyword ('EV charger', 'electric vehicle charging', 'EV charging') returns 10 rows with typical_duration_minutes values of 180 (3 hours) or 240 (4 hours). No PWI row for EV charger installation returns 300 minutes (5 hours). The '7kW (32A) single-phase EV chargepoint' row specifically returns 180 minutes. The upper bound of '5 hours' stated on the page is not supported by any RAG row; the RAG ceiling is 4 hours (240 min). The lower bound of 3 hours matches. The range is overstated at the top end per the available RAG data.

content_gaps.missing: Regulation 722.311.201 (A4:2026) — load curtailment may be taken into account for maximum demand calculation — directly relevant to flats with multiple chargers and to sizing the consumer unit supply. The page mentions load management in passing but never cites this regulation or explains its significance for installation design.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.311.201 — 'Load curtailment, including load reduction or disconnection, either automatically or manually, may be taken into account when determining the maximum demand of the installation or part thereof.'

content_gaps.missing: The A4:2026 Section 722 amendments explicitly cover PME systems, socket-outlets and connectors, external influences, isolation/switching, AND RCD protection as a combined set of changes. The page treats each in isolation but never signals that A4:2026 updated Section 722 specifically — a reader does not know whether the cited regulations are current or stale.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.411.4.1 note: 'Section 722 amendments include modifications relating to PME systems, socket-outlets and connectors, external influences, isolation and switching, and RCD protection.'

content_gaps.missing: Appendix 3 in A4:2026 now contains time/current characteristics AND the earth fault loop impedance data previously in Appendix 14. Appendix 14 now covers prospective fault current determination. Any page discussing Zs or overcurrent device selection for the 32A EV circuit should reference the correct appendix — the page does not address Zs values at all.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.411.4.1 reference: 'Appendix 3 in this edition contains time/current characteristics of overcurrent protective devices and RCDs. The previous content of Appendix 14 concerning earth fault loop impedance has been moved into Appendix 3.'

content_gaps.missing: OSG (On-Site Guide) Reg 4.6.4 explicitly states the EV dedicated circuit 'shall conform to the relevant requirements in Section 722 of BS 7671'. Citing the OSG as a practical cross-reference would reinforce credibility with electricians and add an E-E-A-T signal.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 4.6.4: 'The dedicated circuit for electric vehicle connection shall conform to the relevant requirements in Section 722 of BS 7671.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 411.3.3 requires 30mA RCD protection for socket outlets that may reasonably be expected to supply portable equipment for use outdoors
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facets for Reg 411.3.3 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.3 · [requirement] · Baseline requirement: RCD protection applies to 32A and below socket-outlets') confirm: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception in the Regulation is met.' The pre-A4 framing of 'outdoor portable equipment use' is gone. Under A4:2026 the trigger is simply rated current ≤32 A, with the only exception being a documented risk assessment for non-dwellings. The page's narrower 'outdoor portable equipment' framing misrepresents the current regulation and will mislead readers about whether indoor socket-outlets in a dwelling require RCD.

confirmed_issues.claim: Section 714 of BS 7671 applies to outdoor luminaire circuits including festoon lighting, requiring RCD protection (30mA) for all such circuits
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet for Reg 712.6.101 [exception] states explicitly: 'Temporary festoon lighting is excluded from the classification of outdoor lighting installations under Section 714. Installers and certifiers shall not treat temporary festoon lighting as an outdoor lighting installation for the purposes of this section.' The page incorrectly states Section 714 applies to festoon lighting. Festoon lighting does still require RCD protection under the general Part 4 rules (e.g. Reg 411.3.3), but the Section 714 attribution is wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: Cables buried in the ground must comply with Regulation 522.8.10 (minimum depth 500mm, or protected by conduit or armoured cable)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 522.8.10 is confirmed in the RAG and does require buried cables to be at 'sufficient depth to avoid being damaged by any reasonably foreseeable disturbance of the ground' — no numeric depth is given in 522.8.10 itself. The 500mm figure comes only from Reg 709.521.1.7 NOTE (marina distribution circuits), which the RAG explicitly flags as 'generally considered as a minimum depth ... not worded as an absolute regulatory limit.' Citing 522.8.10 with a fixed 500mm minimum overstates what the regulation says. The 750mm under paths/driveways figure has no supporting RAG facet at all.

confirmed_issues.claim: Bury armoured cable at a minimum depth of 500mm in a dedicated cable route, or 750mm under paths and driveways
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet supports a 750mm depth requirement for cables under paths or driveways. The only depth figures returned are: 0.5m from Reg 709.521.1.7 NOTE (marina context, explicitly labelled guidance not a fixed rule) and 0.6m from Reg 708.521.7.2 NOTE (camp/caravan site context, also guidance). The 750mm figure for paths/driveways is wholly ungrounded in the A4:2026 RAG. Presenting it as a BS 7671 requirement is unsupported.

confirmed_issues.claim: 12V SELV is safe to touch even when wet — classified as SELV, maximum 12V AC or 30V DC
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG facet for Reg 414.11 [requirement] states: 'Protection by extra-low voltage provided by SELV or PELV requires limitation of voltage in the SELV or PELV system to the upper limit of voltage Band I, 50 V AC or 120 V DC.' The page states the SELV limit as 12V AC / 30V DC, which is not the BS 7671 Band I definition. While Part 7 special locations can impose lower limits (noted at Reg 414.1.2), no RAG facet supports 12V AC / 30V DC as the general SELV voltage limit or as a specific limit for outdoor entertaining areas. The page misrepresents the SELV voltage threshold.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 revised Reg 411.3.3 scope: the page frames this as 'outdoor portable equipment' only, but A4:2026 expanded it to ALL ≤32A socket outlets in dwellings (no exception permitted for dwellings). The critical nuance — that dwellings have no risk-assessment opt-out — is absent.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.3.3 [condition]: 'The exception permitting omission of RCD protection under Regulation 411.3.3 is limited to situations other than for a dwelling. Omission of RCD protection on socket‑outlets (≤32 A) is not permitted for dwellings under this exception.'

content_gaps.missing: Section 714 explicit exclusion of temporary festoon lighting: the page incorrectly implies Section 714 and its RCD requirement apply to festoon lights. Festoon lighting should be discussed under the general outdoor socket/RCD rules, not Section 714.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 712.6.101 [exception]: 'Temporary festoon lighting is excluded from the classification of outdoor lighting installations under Section 714.'

content_gaps.missing: Section 714 equi-potential bonding requirement (Reg 714.411.3.1.2): the page discusses RCD protection from Section 714 but omits the Section 714 protective equipotential bonding requirement for metallic structures near outdoor luminaires — relevant to metal pergolas, fixings, and outdoor kitchen metalwork.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 714.411.3.1.2: 'addresses protective equipotential bonding in the context of outdoor lighting installations — metallic structures in proximity to but not part of the outdoor lighting installation and their relationship to the main earthing terminal.'

content_gaps.missing: The 0.5m burial depth is guidance in a NOTE, not a hard regulatory minimum — the page presents it as mandatory. The RAG clarifies it as an example guidance figure, with mechanical protection as an alternative to any depth.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 709.521.1.7 NOTE: 'A depth of 0.5 m is generally considered as a minimum depth to fulfil this requirement.' [condition]: mechanical protection is a full alternative to burial depth.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.531.101 requires appropriate RCD type — Type B RCD or Type A with integrated DC fault detection (6mA DC RDC-DD)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero facets exist for any reg numbered 722.531.101 (COUNT = 0, confirmed by direct LIKE query). Every facet matching context_prefix '%722.531%' resolves to Reg 722.531.3.101, which covers transformer siting ('placed as close as possible to the transformer') — not RCD type selection. The only RAG facet linking RCD protection to EV charging reads: '[GN3] Reg 2.11 — Regulation 722.531.3 requires RCD protection for electric vehicle charging installations where that clause applies.' No facet in Section 722 or anywhere else in the RAG mentions Type B RCD, 6mA DC fault detection, or RDC-DD in the context of EV charger RCD selection requirements. The correct reg for EV RCD protection is 722.531.3, not 722.531.101. The page cites a non-existent regulation number and attributes RCD type-selection content to it.

confirmed_issues.claim: Regulation 722.533.101 requires a dedicated circuit from the consumer unit for each charger
confirmed_issues.evidence: All eight facets returned for context_prefix '%722.533.101%' cover low-voltage fuse standard references (BS EN 60269-1, BS HD 60269-2, BS HD 60269-3, BS EN 60269-6, BS EN IEC 60309, etc.) — none contain any dedicated-circuit requirement. The dedicated-circuit requirement IS grounded in the RAG but is attributed to a different reference: OSG facet '[OSG] Reg 4.6.4' states 'A dedicated circuit shall be provided for the connection to electric vehicles... shall conform to the relevant requirements in Section 722 of BS 7671.' Attributing the dedicated-circuit requirement specifically to Reg 722.533.101 is incorrect; that reg covers fuse product standards, not circuit topology.

confirmed_issues.claim: A standard 7kW domestic EV charger installation takes 2 to 4 hours for a straightforward property
confirmed_issues.evidence: PWI records for standard domestic EV charging point installation return typical_duration_minutes of 180 (3h) and 240 (4h) — multiple records at both values. No PWI record returns 120 minutes (2h) for a domestic EV charger install. The minimum across all EV charging PWI records relevant to domestic install is 180 minutes. The 2-hour lower bound is not supported by any PWI record; the correct RAG-grounded range for a straightforward install is 3–4 hours.

confirmed_issues.claim: Complex EV charger installations (long cable run, board upgrade, earthing) can take 4 to 8 hours
confirmed_issues.evidence: The maximum typical_duration_minutes across all EV charging PWI records is 360 minutes (6h), found on prosumer/PV+EV integration and EV provision records. No PWI record returns 480 minutes (8h) for any EV-related task — confirmed by querying all PWI records with typical_duration_minutes >= 480, which returned only apprenticeship-duration records (1,576,800 min). The 4-hour floor is supported (multiple records at 240 min). The 8-hour ceiling is unverified by any PWI record; 6 hours is the highest value the RAG supports.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 443.4 risk assessment usually requires Type 2 at origin
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 443.4 facets (a)–(d) are mandatory triggers (human life, public services, commercial activity, large numbers of co-located individuals); the fifth 443.4 facet covers the 'all other cases' risk-assessment route. None of these facets use the word 'usually' or prescribe Type 2 at origin as the default outcome of that assessment. Reg 534.4.1.4 (RAG-confirmed) does mandate Type 2 where Section 443 requires SPDs, but its own exception facet states clearly: 'If Section 443 does not require SPDs for an installation, then this clause does not impose an obligation to install Type 2 SPDs close to the origin.' No RAG facet in bs7671_facets supports the word 'usually' in connection with the 443.4 risk-assessment outcome. The obligation is purely conditional on the 443 assessment result, not a usual default.

confirmed_issues.claim: New installations + rewires — Type 2 at consumer unit origin
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 534.4.1.4 exception facet is explicit: 'This clause is not a free-standing universal requirement; it presupposes that SPDs are required by Section 443. If Section 443 does not require SPDs for an installation, then this clause does not impose an obligation to install Type 2 SPDs close to the origin.' No RAG facet in bs7671_facets mandates Type 2 at the consumer unit origin for all new installations or rewires as a blanket rule. The page's categorical statement overstates the standard; the requirement is conditional on the Section 443 assessment, not automatic for every new installation or rewire.

confirmed_issues.claim: The floor temperature limit is 40°C — cited in thermostat wiring section and thermostat position FAQ ('floor surface exceeding 40°C per BS 7671 Regulation 753.424')
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 753.423 facets are unambiguous: the NOTE to that regulation gives 35°C as the example floor surface limit, not 40°C (context_prefix '[BS 7671] … Reg 753.423 … [note] · Example limit — floor heating systems 35 °C': 'NOTE in Regulation 753.423 gives an example: for floor heating systems, the surface temperature can be limited to no more than 35 °C'). A further facet under Reg 753.423 [example] states 'limiting output so surface does not exceed 35 °C'. Reg 753.424.201 gives 80°C, but that is the limit for the heating-unit installation zone, not the floor surface. No RAG facet supports a 40°C floor surface limit. The page misquotes both the temperature (should be 35°C example) and the regulation number (should be 753.423, not 753.424).

confirmed_issues.claim: Every UFH circuit must be protected by a 30mA RCD per BS 7671 Regulation 411.3.3
confirmed_issues.evidence: Reg 411.3.3 facets confirm this regulation applies exclusively to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32A — it is a socket-outlet regulation and does not cover UFH circuits. The correct regulation for RCD protection of UFH circuits is Reg 753.411.3.2, which requires 'RCDs having the characteristics specified in Regulation 415.1.1' to be used as disconnecting devices for heating installations (context_prefix '[BS 7671] … Reg 753.411.3.2 … [requirement] · RCDs with characteristics of Regulation 415.1.1 shall be used as disconnecting devices'). Reg 415.1.1 facets confirm the ≤30mA threshold. The 30mA RCD requirement for UFH is real and RAG-confirmed, but the page cites the wrong regulation (411.3.3 instead of 753.411.3.2). This is a confirmed citation error.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 753.423 (Protection against burns) is entirely absent. The page states 40°C floor surface limit but the correct A4:2026 reference is Reg 753.423 with an example of 35°C. This is both an accuracy error and a gap — the regulation number and correct threshold should be cited.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets · Reg 753.423 · [requirement] + [example] · 'surface does not exceed 35°C'; 'floor heating systems shall be limited'

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 specifies torque values for terminal connections
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple bs7671_facets rows show BS 7671 does NOT specify torque values itself — it mandates use of manufacturer-specified values. Reg 134.1.4 procedure facet states: 'terminals and connectors shall be tightened to the manufacturer's specified torque values where provided.' Reg 411.9.3 states: 'Where torque values are specified by manufacturer, those torque values shall be applied.' GN3 2.5.1 and OSG 12.513 repeat the same: torque values come from the equipment/connector manufacturer. BS 7671 imposes the duty to follow manufacturer torque data but publishes no torque figures of its own. Claiming 'BS 7671 specifies torque values' is a misattribution; the correct framing is that BS 7671 (via Regs 526.1 and 134.1.4) requires connections to meet mechanical strength and continuity requirements, with actual torque values coming from manufacturer data.

confirmed_issues.claim: A 2.5mm² cable on a spur is only rated for 20A (Reference Method C)
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets Table 4D1A facet (context_prefix: 'BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Appendix 4 Table 4D1A PVC T+E 70C Method C clipped direct') explicitly states: 'Maximum current-carrying capacity (Iz) for 2.5 mm² PVC-insulated 70°C twin and earth (T+E) cable installed Method C (clipped direct to non-metallic surface or cable tray) is 24 A.' The page's claim of 20A for Reference Method C is directly contradicted by the A4:2026 table. The correct Iz for 2.5mm² PVC T+E under Method C is 24A (Table 4D1A). Note: 20A may relate to a different installation method (e.g. Method B enclosed in conduit) or a grouped/derated scenario, but as stated for Method C it is wrong.
