confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 A4:2026 introduced 'Prosumer's low voltage electrical installation(s)' as a new inspection item (14.0) on the Electrical Installation Certificate Schedule of Inspections
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet names a numbered 'item 14.0' on the EIC Schedule of Inspections. The closest facet (BS 7671 Reg 701.12.3 context_prefix: '[BS 7671] … Chapter 70 … Reg 701.12.3 … Prosumer's low voltage electrical installation inspections') states: 'inspectors shall list all inspection items relating to Chapter 82. The results of the particular inspections carried out shall be recorded separately and attached to the report.' GN3 Reg 3.18 repeats this identically. The model is a separate attached document, not a single numbered row called '14.0' on the main EIC schedule. The specific item number is unsupported and the procedural model described (single checkbox row) contradicts the attach-separately model in the standard.

confirmed_issues.claim: Prosumer installations include: solar PV + battery + grid; EV vehicle-to-grid (V2G); domestic wind; CHP with grid synchronisation; hybrid solar with islanding
confirmed_issues.evidence: OSG Reg 12.1 [example] facet explicitly lists: 'photovoltaic (PV) generators, battery energy storage systems (BESS), small wind turbines, micro-CHP units and other local generation or storage devices' as the A4:2026 BS 7671 examples of local sources. EV vehicle-to-grid (V2G) is not named in any RAG facet as a BS 7671 prosumer example. The page presents EV V2G as an explicit BS 7671 Chapter 82 prosumer installation type, which is unverified and potentially misleading — V2G is not enumerated in the standard's own examples. 'Hybrid solar with islanding' is also not named as a distinct type in any facet.

confirmed_issues.claim: Item 14.0 is conditional — for installations WITHOUT prosumer equipment, mark it as N/A
confirmed_issues.evidence: No RAG facet supports an 'item 14.0' row or an N/A marking convention for it. Both GN3 Reg 3.18 and BS 7671 Reg 701.12.3 specify the same procedural model: prosumer inspection results shall be 'recorded separately and attached to the report.' This is structurally different from ticking a single row N/A on the main schedule. The page's N/A instruction is not just unsupported — it describes a fundamentally different recording mechanism from what the standard requires.

confirmed_issues.claim: Prosumer-specific inspection points include: bidirectional power flow, anti-islanding protection (G98/G99), isolation arrangements, labelling (Regulation 514), earthing co-ordination
confirmed_issues.evidence: Isolation arrangements are strongly grounded: BS 7671 Reg 826.1.1.4 requires a main switch per source plus durable warning notice or interlock. Labelling under Regulation 514 is grounded: Reg 514.15.1 requires warning notices at the origin, meter position, consumer unit, and all isolation points for installations with alternative/additional sources. However, 'anti-islanding protection (G98/G99)' returns zero RAG hits — G98/G99 are ENA engineering recommendations, not BS 7671 content. 'Bidirectional power flow' and 'earthing co-ordination' also return zero RAG hits as named inspection points. The claim is partially grounded but conflates ENA G98/G99 content with BS 7671 inspection requirements.

confirmed_issues.claim: Anti-islanding: inverter disconnects from grid within less than 2 seconds on grid failure (G98/G99 requirement)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG hits for 'anti-islanding', 'G98', 'G99', or the 2-second disconnection threshold in bs7671_facets. This figure and framing originate from ENA G98/G99 engineering recommendations, not from BS 7671. The page presents this as an inspection point grounded in the standard when it is in fact external ENA content. Presenting ENA G98/G99 thresholds as BS 7671-derived inspection criteria is a grounding failure.

confirmed_issues.claim: ENA G98 applies to small-scale generation typically up to 16 A per phase (up to 3.68 kW single-phase or 11.04 kW three-phase); DNO notified within 28 days
confirmed_issues.evidence: Zero RAG hits for G98 current thresholds, power limits, or the 28-day DNO notification window in bs7671_facets. These are ENA G98 figures, not BS 7671 content. The RAG (bs7671_facets covering BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, GN3, and OSG) contains no facet referencing G98, G99, or DNO notification periods. The page is presenting ENA regulatory content as if it were part of BS 7671, which is a category error.

confirmed_issues.claim: Surge protection — SPDs on AC side per Section 443 risk assessment; SPDs on DC PV string per Section 712
confirmed_issues.evidence: Section 443 risk assessment is partially grounded: BS 7671 Reg 443.4 facets confirm Section 443 has been redrafted in A4:2026 and prescribes SPD protection based on risk criteria (life safety, public services, commercial activity, large numbers of people). The 'AC side per Section 443 risk assessment' claim is therefore supported. However, 'SPDs on DC PV string per Section 712' returns zero RAG hits — no facet in bs7671_facets links Section 712 to DC-side SPD requirements. The DC string SPD cite is unverified from the RAG and should not appear as a grounded BS 7671 claim without a supporting facet.

content_gaps.missing: Chapter 82 (Part 8, BS 7671:2018+A4:2026) is the normative home for all prosumer installation requirements — the page never mentions Chapter 82, only 'item 14.0'. Readers inspecting or designing prosumer installations need this chapter reference to look up the actual regulations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201 context_prefix: 'Chapter 82 scope: Prosumer's low-voltage electrical installations' — 'Chapter 82 is a new chapter providing requirements, measures and recommendations for design, erection and verification of all types of low-voltage electrical installations designated as Prosumer's Electrical Installations (PEIs).'

content_gaps.missing: GN3 Chapter 62 / Reg 3.18 procedural requirement: prosumer inspection results must be recorded SEPARATELY and attached to the main report — not just ticked on a single schedule row. This is a distinct documentation obligation the page misses entirely.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 3.18: 'The results of the particular inspections carried out for prosumer installations shall be recorded separately and attached to the report.'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 826.1.1.4 (Chapter 82) specifies that a suitable interlock system is a valid ALTERNATIVE to the durable warning notice for multi-source installations. The page only mentions warning notices, not the interlock option.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 826.1.1.4: 'Alternatively to providing a durable warning notice, a suitable interlock system shall be provided.'

content_gaps.missing: Chapter 82 explicitly covers compatibility with 'current and future ways to deliver electrical energy … from the public network or from other local sources' — framing the chapter as future-proofed for emerging prosumer technologies (V2G, microgrids). This forward-looking scope is absent from the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 722.826.3.201 context_prefix: 'Objective of Chapter 82 compatibility with delivery methods' — 'to provide requirements such that low-voltage electrical installations are compatible with current and future ways to deliver electrical energy.'

confirmed_issues.claim: Separation from power cables must comply with BS 7671 Chapter 52 electromagnetic compatibility requirements
confirmed_issues.evidence: The RAG conclusively shows that Chapter 52 (Reg 528.3 / 528.3.4) covers proximity of wiring systems to non-electrical services such as gas, water and heat sources — physical and fault-protection hazards — not EMC. The correct live regulation for IT/mains cable EMC separation is Reg 444.4.1 (Chapter 44, Part 4), which requires minimum separation including allowances for cable movement and 90-degree crossings where separation cannot be maintained (bs7671_facets, context_prefix '[BS 7671] … Chapter 44 · Reg 444.4.1'). The OSG facet explicitly states: 'The On-Site Guide requires that internal cables be separated according to the greater requirement of BS EN 50174-2 for electromagnetic compatibility (EMC)' (OSG facet, context_prefix '[OSG] … Use BS EN 50174-2 for EMC separation of internal cables'). Reg 528.3.4 is about protection against hazards from adjacent non-electrical services in normal use, not EMC. The auditor's sub-claim that Section 444 was deleted is contradicted by the weight of RAG evidence: nine or more properly-anchored facets confirm Reg 444.4.1 and related Chapter 44 regs are present in 2018+A4:2026; the single 'deleted' facet is misfiled under a Reg 434.3 context_prefix as a change-summary note and cannot override the authoritative Chapter 44 facets. The page's framing of Chapter 52 as the EMC/separation hook is wrong; the correct hook is Reg 444.4.1 (Chapter 44) with BS EN 50174-2 as the referenced standard for internal separation distances.

content_gaps.missing: The page cites 'BS 7671 Chapter 52 electromagnetic compatibility requirements' but Section 444 (Measures against electromagnetic disturbances) was DELETED in A4:2026. The correct citation for IT/mains cable separation is now Reg 444.4.1 (Chapter 44) — minimum separation, 90-degree crossings where minimum cannot be maintained. The page should be updated to cite Reg 444.4.1 explicitly and note the A4:2026 change.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet Reg 434.3 scope: 'Section 444 titled Measures against electromagnetic disturbances has been removed (deleted) from BS 7671 2018+A4:2026'; Reg 444.4.1: IT/mains separation and 90-degree crossing requirement

content_gaps.missing: The page omits the Band I / Band II segregation regime entirely. Data cabling circuits are Band I (SELV/ELV signal circuits). Where they share a route or cable management system with Band II (230V mains) circuits, Reg 444.6.1 mandates segregation measures. Where they share a multicore cable, an earthed metal screen sized to the largest Band II circuit is required (OSG). This is a direct BS 7671 compliance obligation that data cable installers must know.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facets Reg 444.6.1; OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) 'Separation method for Band J and Band II in same multicore cable'

content_gaps.missing: ICT functional earthing and equipotential bonding (Reg 444.5.7) is relevant to screened/shielded data cable installations. The page mentions bonding in passing but does not explain that BS 7671 Reg 444.5.7 explicitly permits and governs functional earthing for ICT installations, and that it must not compromise protective earthing. This is a practical compliance point for shielded Cat 6A / S/FTP installs.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet Reg 444.5.7: 'earthing arrangements and equipotential bonding of information and communications technology (ICT) installations for functional purposes'

content_gaps.missing: Chapter 52 Reg 528.3.4 imposes a mandatory duty on wiring systems in close proximity to non-electrical services (e.g. data cables near HVAC, pipework) to be suitably protected and to provide fault protection per Section 411. This practical rule applies on many commercial data cabling projects where cables share risers or ceiling voids with mechanical services.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671 facet Reg 528.3.4: 'wiring system shall be suitably protected against the hazards likely to arise from the presence of the other services in normal use'

This is one of the most commonly missed compliance points on commercial data cabling projects.

content_gaps.missing: The EIC must include a 'departures' section where any departure from BS 7671 is recorded (Regs 120.3, 133.1.2, 133.2.4). The page never mentions this mandatory section, which is critical for any installer who has deviated from a regulation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 133.1.2 — 'Such use shall be recorded as a departure on the appropriate electrical certification specified in Part 6'; [GN3] Reg 1.5 — 'When recording a Departure in certificates and reports, include the designer's declaration that the resultant degree of safety is not less than that achievable by full compliance'

content_gaps.missing: Section D of the EIC requires the designer(s) to recommend a 'next inspection interval' in years/months. This is a mandatory field on the model form (Appendix 6) that the page does not mention at all.

content_gaps.missing: The EIC is NOT to be used for periodic inspection and testing — an EICR is required instead (Reg 120.3 exception). The page's 'when an EIC is the right document' section does not draw this important boundary, which is a common source of confusion.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 120.3 — 'The Electrical Installation Certificate is not to be used for periodic inspection and testing. For periodic inspection and testing, an Electrical Installation Condition Report (EICR) is to be used instead.'

content_gaps.missing: The EIC has three distinct signatory roles: designer, constructor, and person responsible for inspection and testing — these can be different people or the same person. Reg 644.5 and GN3 2.5 both address how the responsible party signs Section C. The page treats the certificate as a single-person document.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 644.5 — 'Electrical Installation Certificates shall be compiled and signed or otherwise authenticated by one or more skilled persons, competent to verify that the requirements of BS 7671 have been met'; [GN3] 2.5 — 'If the person carrying out the inspection and testing is the responsible party, that person should sign either the Inspection and testing section in Section C of the EIC'

confirmed_issues.claim: South-facing UK solar arrays produce up to 950 kWh/kWp/year
confirmed_issues.evidence: Exhaustive full-text search of bs7671_facets on terms 'solar PV yield kWh kWp south facing', 'kWh kWp yield annual generation', and 'orientation south east west north array' returned zero rows. Four practical_work_intelligence facets cover PV azimuth and orientation (IDs a72e6f74, 76908a75, 74a7c620, ed8b7337) but contain only qualitative guidance — south-facing preferred, 30-40° tilt ideal, UK location irradiance differences — with no kWh/kWp numeric values in any column (primary_topic, acceptance_criteria, common_mistakes, common_defects, other_standards). The 950 kWh/kWp figure cannot be grounded against either RAG table.

confirmed_issues.claim: East or west orientation reduces yield by 15-20%
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets returns zero rows for yield-reduction or orientation percentage queries. The four PWI azimuth facets reference directional orientation (south-facing azimuth, incorrect tilt/facing listed as a common defect) but give no percentage reduction figure for east/west arrays. The 15-20% figure is absent from both RAG tables and cannot be verified or contradicted by the available sources.

confirmed_issues.claim: North-facing arrays drop below 700 kWh/kWp/year and are rarely viable
confirmed_issues.evidence: bs7671_facets returns zero rows for any north-facing yield query. PWI facets mention 'incorrect tilt or facing' as a common defect and flag poor orientation in site-suitability reports, but contain no numeric yield threshold (700 kWh/kWp or otherwise) and no viability conclusion for north-facing arrays. The claim is entirely absent from both RAG tables.

confirmed_issues.claim: BS 7671 Table 61 specifies minimum insulation resistance values — test voltage 500 V DC / minimum 1.0 MΩ for circuits up to 500 V
confirmed_issues.evidence: Every RAG facet that covers IR test voltages and minima cites Table 64, not Table 61. BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 context_prefix 'Reg 643.3.2 · Table 64 test voltages and minimum insulation resistance values' states: 'For circuits up to and including 500 V (except SELV/PELV) use 500 V DC with minimum 1.0 MΩ.' GN3 Chapter 4 Reg 4.4 reproduces the same values as 'Table 64 (reproduced as GN3 Table 2.10)'. A direct search for 'Table 61' in context_prefix returns zero rows. The numeric values the page quotes are correct, but the table citation 'Table 61' is stale 17th-edition numbering; the correct reference in BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 is Table 64.

confirmed_issues.claim: A4:2026 Reg 643.3 now permits 250 V DC test with equipment connected (where equipment would be damaged or influenced by higher voltage) — page makes no mention of this
confirmed_issues.evidence: Five bs7671_facets rows returned for context_prefix '%643.3%', all from BS 7671:2018+A4:2026. The [requirement] facet states verbatim: 'Where equipment is connected and the equipment is likely to influence the insulation resistance verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used to verify insulation resistance as clarified in the redraft to Regulation 643.3.' The [scope] facet confirms this is a A4:2026 redraft change. The page therefore omits a normative A4:2026 requirement and incorrectly implies disconnection is the only option.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 643.3 redraft: where equipment is connected and likely to be damaged or influenced by the standard test voltage, a 250 V DC post-connection test is now a permitted alternative verification method. This is a significant procedural change that affects how modern electronic-heavy circuits (with LED drivers, SPDs, EV controllers, smart devices) are tested.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.3 (redraft): 'Where equipment is connected and the equipment is likely to influence the insulation resistance verification test or be damaged by other test voltages, a 250 V DC insulation resistance test following connection of the equipment shall be used.'

content_gaps.missing: Table 64 (not Table 61) is the correct A4:2026 reference throughout. The page uses 'Table 61' in the page title meta description, FAQ answer, section heading, and step-by-step — every single citation is wrong. A best-in-class page must use the correct table number.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Chapter 64 · context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · [test] · Table 64 test voltages and minimum insulation resistance values'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 643.3 redraft also clarifies that main switchboard and distribution circuits may be tested with all final circuits connected but equipment disconnected — the RAG facet for 643.3.2 explicitly states this two-level approach (board-level vs circuit-level). The page only describes circuit-level testing.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 643.3.2: 'The insulation resistance measured with the test voltages indicated in Table 64 shall be considered satisfactory if the main switchboard and each distribution circuit tested separately, with all its final circuits connected but with current-using equipment disconnected.'

content_gaps.missing: The GN3 two-stage testing approach: stage (i) tests cables before equipment is installed (line-to-line and line-to-earth on cables alone); stage (ii) is the complete circuit test after installation. The page conflates both into a single procedure and does not distinguish new-installation stage (i) from complete-circuit testing.

confirmed_issues.claim: Diversity factor of 0.9 for lighting and 0.4 for socket-outlets from BS 7671 Appendix A (or IET On-Site Guide Table 1.1)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Two separate problems confirmed by RAG. First, the specific numeric values (0.9 lighting, 0.4 sockets) return zero hits across all bs7671_facets queries — they are not grounded in the RAG at all. Second, and more critically, the OSG facets confirm that diversity tables (Tables A1/A2) live in the On-Site Guide Appendix A, not in BS 7671 itself: '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · [scope] · Purpose of Appendix on maximum demand and diversity' and '[OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 12 · Reg 12.4.2 · [reference] · See Table A1 and Table A2 in IET On-Site Guide Appendix A.' The page attributing these values to 'BS 7671 Appendix A' is a confirmed reference error — BS 7671 has no Appendix containing diversity tables; this content is in the OSG. Whether the specific figures 0.9/0.4 are correct cannot be verified from the RAG, but the source attribution is definitively wrong.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page makes no mention of AFDDs for retail socket-outlet circuits — material A4:2026 omission
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG unambiguously confirms the omission. '[BS 7671] · Reg 421.1.7 · [requirement] · AFDD recommended for other premises single-phase socket-outlet circuits ≤32 A' states: 'For all other premises (i.e., premises not listed as Mandatory locations), Regulation 421.1.7 recommends AFDDs for single-phase AC final circuits supplying socket-outlets not exceeding 32 A.' A further facet '[BS 7671] · Reg 421.1.7 · [note] · Wording is recommendatory not mandatory' confirms the wording is advisory not mandatory for non-listed premises. Retail installations are definitively 'all other premises' — they are not HRRBs, HMOs, student accommodation, or care homes (those four types have mandatory AFDDs per '[BS 7671] · Reg 411.4.2 · [scope] · types of premises defined for mandatory AFDDs'). A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 was introduced specifically to recommend AFDDs on ≤32 A socket circuits in all premises not in the mandatory list. A retail electrical installation guide that does not mention this A4:2026 recommendation is materially incomplete.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD requirement/recommendation under Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026): for retail fit-outs the page should state that AFDDs are recommended (though not mandatory) for single-phase ≤32 A socket-outlet final circuits in all premises other than the named mandatory categories. This is a key A4:2026 change affecting every commercial distribution board spec.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 421.1.7 via context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.4.2 · AFDD recommended for other premises single-phase socket-outlet circuits ≤32 A'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 133.1.3 (A4:2026): certain equipment usage (including AFDD and SPD installation) must now be recorded on the appropriate Part 6 certification (EIC/EICR). A retail EIC must now carry these records where applicable — not mentioned anywhere on the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 133.1.3 via context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 1 · Chapter 13 · Reg 133.1.3 · requirement — recording equipment usage on certification'

content_gaps.missing: RCD on domestic luminaire circuits (Reg 411.3.4, A4:2026): The page correctly excludes commercial premises from Part P but does not explain the new A4:2026 rule requiring 30 mA RCD on ALL domestic lighting circuits (relevant if the retail unit is above or attached to a dwelling).

confirmed_issues.claim: GAP: The page makes no mention of the Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) requirement that domestic luminaire circuits require additional protection by an RCD not exceeding 30 mA.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh direct query on context_prefix '%Reg 411.3.4%' returned 6 facets from bs7671_facets (document_type='bs7671', BS 7671:2018+A4:2026). Key facets confirm: 'Within domestic (household) premises, additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires (Regulation 411.3.4). This is a mandatory additional-protection rule for domestic lighting circuits.' The wording uses 'shall' — a mandatory obligation. A page about smart home lighting installation in domestic premises that omits this A4:2026 mandatory requirement is materially incomplete and confirmed as a real issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: GAP: The page makes no mention of Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) recommending AFDDs for new AC final circuits, which is directly applicable where smart lighting involves new circuit runs.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh direct query on context_prefix '%Reg 421.1.7%' returned 6 facets from bs7671_facets (document_type='bs7671', BS 7671:2018+A4:2026). Key facets confirm: 'Regulation 421.1.7 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been introduced recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents.' One facet explicitly notes the wording is recommendatory not mandatory ('recommending', not 'shall'). Nevertheless a guide on smart lighting new circuits that entirely omits this A4:2026 AFDD recommendation leaves the reader unaware of a relevant safety recommendation. Confirmed issue.

confirmed_issues.claim: GAP: Section 715 of BS 7671 is only mentioned in a related-page link; the page body does not discuss its applicability to ELV-supplied DALI drivers.
confirmed_issues.evidence: Fresh query on context_prefix containing '715' returned facets from bs7671_facets (document_type='bs7671', Reg 710.421.1.201 context). Key facets confirm: 'Section 715 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 concerns Extra-low voltage (ELV) lighting installations' and 'designers and installers shall consult Section 715 when working with ELV lighting.' DALI systems commonly use ELV bus wiring and ELV-powered drivers. A page that discusses DALI installation without noting Section 715 applicability for ELV-supplied components is missing a directly relevant BS 7671 cross-reference. Confirmed issue.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) — mandatory 30 mA RCD additional protection for ALL domestic luminaire circuits. Any smart lighting job adding new lighting circuits in a dwelling must include this. The page mentions EIC/MWC paperwork but never the RCD requirement — the most safety-critical omission.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026, Reg 411.3.4] 'additional protection by an RCD with a rated residual operating current not exceeding 30 mA shall be provided for AC final circuits supplying luminaires in domestic premises'

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026) — AFDD recommendation for new final circuits. Smart lighting projects adding circuits in bedrooms, living rooms, and offices are exactly the scope. No mention anywhere on the page.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026, Reg 421.1.7] 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)... to mitigate fire risk originating from arc fault currents'; AFDDs shall conform to BS EN 62606

content_gaps.missing: Section 715 ELV applicability: DALI drivers and 12V LED strips commonly operate at ELV (SELV). Section 715 imposes specific installation, segregation, and protection rules that apply to low-voltage DALI bus wiring and 12 V SELV tracks. This is unmentioned despite DALI being the main focus.

confirmed_issues.claim: The testing regime is the same as for a permanent installation under BS 7671 Chapter 6
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple RAG facets under context_prefix '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 6 — Inspection and Testing · Chapter 64 · Reg 643.11' confirm that initial verification requirements live in Chapter 64, not 'Chapter 6'. Chapter 6 is the Part heading only; the substantive chapter is Chapter 64 (Initial Verification). Example facet: 'Chapter 64 of BS 7671 Part 6 is dedicated to Initial Verification. It contains the general requirements and procedures for the initial inspection and testing of new installations or altered circuits prior to being put into service.' The page citing 'Chapter 6' as the specific chapter is incorrect — it should cite Chapter 64.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page references RCD Types A and B but omits Type F
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG Reg 534.4.7 directly and explicitly defines all four types: 'The appropriate RCD shall be selected from the following types: Type AC, Type A, Type F, or Type B.' Facet context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 53 · Reg 534.4.7 · [definition] · Definition of RCD Type F' states: 'RCD Type F is defined as an RCD for which tripping is achieved as for Type A and in addition for composite residual currents whether suddenly applied or slowly rising, intended for circuits supplied between line and neutral or line and earthed middle conductor.' Additional facets at Reg 531.3.3 and 531.3.4.201 confirm Type F is a required selection option alongside A and B. Omitting Type F from a page about temporary installations (which commonly include variable speed drives and mixed-frequency loads) is a confirmed gap.

content_gaps.missing: Type F RCD: BS 7671 Reg 534.4.7 and GN3 list Types AC/A/F/B — Type F (for mixed-frequency loads with 50–1000 Hz components, e.g. variable speed drives) is omitted entirely from the RCD selection guidance. This is directly relevant to entertainment/events equipment.

Chapter 64 (not 'Chapter 6') citation for initial verification — the page writes 'BS 7671 Chapter 6' when the specific testing chapter is Chapter 64. GN3 confirms Chapter 64 as the Initial Verification chapter. A precision fix would improve credibility with professionals.

Regulation 704.1.1 exception: administrative locations (offices, canteens, toilets) on construction sites are explicitly excluded from Section 704's temporary-installation requirements. Useful nuance for readers dealing with site welfare facilities.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 704.1.1 — administrative-locations exception

content_gaps.missing: The TT disconnection time requirement: OSG Reg 3.76 explains that TT systems need RCDs specifically because high Zs prevents OCPDs achieving required disconnection times — this underpins the RCD-for-TT claim but the page does not link it to a disconnection time threshold or explain WHY 30 mA is the chosen rating.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] · OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 3.76

confirmed_issues.claim: RCD protection required for socket outlet circuits not exceeding 20A (Regulation 411.3.3)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Four separate bs7671_facets rows for Reg 411.3.3 (BS 7671:2018+A4:2026, Part 4, Chapter 41) explicitly state the threshold is 32 A, not 20 A. Verbatim: 'RCD protection applies to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A unless an express exception in the Regulation is met.' A further facet confirms: 'Regulation 411.3.3 of BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 has been revised and now applies specifically to socket-outlets with a rated current not exceeding 32 A.' The page's 20 A figure reflects superseded pre-A4 wording and is factually wrong under A4:2026.

content_gaps.missing: Reg 421.1.7 (A4:2026 new) — AFDD installation recommended on AC final circuits to mitigate fire risk from arc faults. Page contains zero mention of AFDDs.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 guidance that the recommended next inspection period must be based on the inspector's findings from the current inspection — not a fixed five-year default. An inspector's recommendation is acceptable when it demonstrably reflects assessed risk factors and test results from the inspection.'

content_gaps.missing: Corrected Reg 411.3.3 threshold: A4:2026 raised the RCD socket protection scope from 20A to 32A. The page uses the old 20A figure. top_improvements.action: Add a brief note on Reg 421.1.7 AFDD recommendation in the 'For Electricians' section: when a Southampton property has degraded 1960s wiring being replaced or a consumer unit is being upgraded following an EICR, BS 7671 A4:2026 now recommends AFDD installation on AC final circuits. Inspectors may record absence of AFDDs as a C3 observation on newly installed CUs.
top_improvements.grounded_in: BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 Reg 421.1.7 — 'recommending the installation of arc fault detection devices (AFDDs) to mitigate the risk of fire in AC final circuits of a fixed installation due to the effects of arc fault currents'

top_improvements.action: Strengthen the 'How Often' section by explaining the GN3 obligation: the inspector must recommend the next interval based on the actual condition found, not default to five years. A Southampton property with multiple C3 observations may correctly receive a 3-year recommendation. Cite GN3 guidance that the period 'should be based on the findings of the current periodic inspection and test.'
top_improvements.grounded_in: GN3 Ch2 Reg 2.4 — 'The recommended period to the next periodic inspection should be based on the findings of the current periodic inspection and test.'

top_improvements.action: Add author/E-E-A-T attribution: name a qualified author (e.g. 'Written by a NICEIC-registered electrician, reviewed for BS 7671:2018+A4:2026 compliance') in the GuideTemplate props. Google's helpful-content guidance and YMYL scoring for electrical safety pages strongly rewards author credentials. Currently zero attribution is shown.
top_improvements.grounded_in: SEO best practice — no RAG source; applies to all technical pages

confirmed_issues.claim: Install a Type 2 SPD (surge protection device) as recommended by BS 7671
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple bs7671_facets contradict 'recommended'. Reg 443.4 (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 44 · Reg 443.4') makes SPD protection MANDATORY in four named risk scenarios (risk to human life; public services/cultural heritage; commercial/industrial interruption; large numbers of co-located individuals) and requires a risk assessment for all other cases, with an explicit exception for single dwelling units. Section 534 scope facet (Reg 527.1.3) states SPDs are required 'where Section 443 or BS EN 62305 mandate it, or as otherwise stated in BS 7671' — conditional, not a blanket recommendation. The OSG facet (Reg 3.1) says a consumer unit 'MAY include protection against transient overvoltage (surge protection)' — permissive language that applies where the risk-assessment/443 threshold is not met. No facet in any document_type uses the word 'recommended' for SPDs. The page's blanket 'recommended by BS 7671' misrepresents the regulatory position: SPDs are mandatory under specific Reg 443.4 conditions and merely permissible otherwise.

content_gaps.missing: SPD mandatory vs optional distinction: the page says SPDs are 'recommended by BS 7671' but the RAG is clear that SPD obligation depends on a Section 443 risk assessment (lightning risk, Keraunic level, line type). This nuance — that SPDs are mandatory in some cases and optional in others, not a blanket recommendation — would make the quoting tips section more accurate and authoritative.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 Reg 527.1.3]; [GN3 Reg 2.8]: 'Surge protection shall be provided ... except where the installation qualifies as a simple installation under Sections 443 and 534 or the owner declares the risk tolerable.'

content_gaps.missing: AFDD (Arc Fault Detection Device) as a new A4:2026 consumer unit consideration: A4:2026 introduced AFDD requirements (Reg 421.1.7). A best-in-class pricing guide for 2026 should mention that CU quotes may need to include AFDD provision depending on installation type, which affects pricing.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets — AFDD/arc fault detection queries returned no results, suggesting the facet may exist under a different query. Memory note: @bs7671-a4-2026.md records AFDD 421.1.7 as a new A4:2026 addition.

content_gaps.missing: EV charger Section 722 requirements that affect pricing: load curtailment (722.311.201), RCD Type B or equivalent, dedicated circuit — these add cost vs a simple circuit addition and are relevant for accurate EV charger quote scoping.
content_gaps.rag_source: bs7671_facets [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 Reg 722.1]: 'dedicated final circuit ... is subject to the particular requirements of Section 722.'

content_gaps.missing: EIC requirement for consumer unit change: the page mentions EIC in the job price table but does not explain it is a legal requirement for any new installation or CU change, nor that the Schedule of Test Results is a mandatory part of the EIC. This is a key value differentiator electricians can explain to customers.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence: 'Initial verification and certification using the Electrical Installation Certificate (EIC): requirements ... change of consumer unit or distribution board ... not completing Generic Schedule(s) of Test Results [is a] common mistake.'

top_improvements.action: Correct the SPD claim from 'recommended by BS 7671' to an accurate statement: 'required by BS 7671 Section 443 where the installation risk assessment identifies lightning risk, and optional (but strongly advised) for simple domestic installations.' This removes a demonstrable inaccuracy that could undermine professional credibility.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 Reg 527.1.3] and [GN3 Reg 2.8]: SPDs are mandatory in risk-assessed scenarios, optional elsewhere — never a blanket 'recommendation'.

top_improvements.action: Add an 'A4:2026 pricing impact' callout box explaining that the 2026 amendments to BS 7671 may affect consumer unit quotes: AFDD devices (Reg 421.1.7) add cost in applicable installations, and EV charger circuits must comply with Section 722 requirements (RCD Type B or equivalent, load curtailment) which affects the scope and price. This makes the 2026 positioning in the title genuinely earned.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets [BS 7671 2018+A4:2026 Reg 722.1] re Section 722 EV requirements; memory note @bs7671-a4-2026.md re AFDD 421.1.7.

top_improvements.action: Add an author/reviewer E-E-A-T attribution — a named qualified electrician (JIB grade, NICEIC/NAPIT registered) with years of experience. top_improvements.action: Add a 'What jobs need Building Control notification?' sub-section or callout. Explain that CU changes, new circuits in kitchens/bathrooms, and rewires are Part P notifiable, and that competent person scheme registration (NICEIC/NAPIT) allows self-certification — saving the customer the BCO fee and the electrician from admin. Tie to the pricing benefit of being scheme-registered.
top_improvements.grounded_in: bs7671_facets [OSG 9th Ed:2022 (A4)]: 'All electrical work within dwellings, of which some is notifiable ... Part P ... require building control notification or competent person scheme certification.'

top_improvements.action: Add a note to the consumer unit pricing row explaining that RCBO boards cost more than dual-RCD boards but may be required where steel enclosures or high-impedance earths make achieving compliant Zs values difficult — and that the EIC's Schedule of Test Results is a legal requirement, not an optional add-on. This differentiates quality electricians in their quotes.

confirmed_issues.claim: Page references '18th Edition' consumer units but does not mention A4:2026 requirement for RCD protection on domestic lighting circuits (Reg 411.3.4)
confirmed_issues.evidence: Multiple bs7671_facets rows confirm Reg 411.3.4 (A4:2026) as a mandatory A4 addition: 'an installation in domestic (household) premises shall be accepted only if AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA' (context_prefix: '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 41 · Reg 411.3.4 · [acceptance_criterion]'). The regulation uses the imperative 'shall', making this a mandatory requirement. A page discussing consumer unit replacement in domestic premises that frames compliance only as '18th Edition' without referencing this A4:2026 addition materially understates current requirements.

confirmed_issues.claim: SPD / surge protection not mentioned anywhere on the page
confirmed_issues.evidence: RAG confirms SPD requirements under multiple facets: Reg 534.4.1.6 ('Consideration shall be given to the provision of SPDs to protect from other sources' — mandatory consideration obligation); Reg 514.16.1 (labelling requirement where SPDs are present, confirmed in multiple facets including '[BS 7671] · 2018+A4:2026 · Part 5 · Chapter 51 · Reg 514.16.1'); OSG facet confirms 'SPDs fitted in the consumer unit or distribution board, often at the incoming supply position as Type 1/2 devices'. A consumer unit replacement page that makes no mention of SPD consideration or labelling omits a mandatory A4:2026 design and documentation step.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 Reg 411.3.4 — domestic lighting circuits must now be protected by a 30 mA RCD. The consumer unit replacement section says 'RCBOs' but never mentions this specific new A4:2026 requirement for lighting circuits. Best-in-class content would call this out explicitly.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 411.3.4 · 'AC final circuits supplying luminaires are provided with additional protection by an RCD whose rated residual operating current does not exceed 30 mA'

content_gaps.missing: SPD (Surge Protective Device) requirement — A4:2026 introduced mandatory consideration of SPDs at consumer unit replacement. Reg 534 and 514.16.1 require labelling of SPD presence/absence. Neither is mentioned on the page despite a section on consumer unit replacement.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Reg 514.16.1 / 534.4.1.6 · SPD labelling and surge protective device consideration requirements

content_gaps.missing: GN3 guidance on periodic inspection: the EICR section does not mention that dangerous conditions discovered during inspection must be recorded and acted upon (Reg 651.4), nor that the inspection report assists in determining timing of the NEXT inspection.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 3.5 / [BS 7671] Reg 651.4 · 'If dangerous conditions are discovered during periodic inspection and testing, details shall be recorded in a report and appropriate action shall be taken'

content_gaps.missing: EICR documentation completeness: the page discusses EICR as a product/price but does not mention the legal requirement that a full EICR must be accompanied by the Condition Report Inspection Schedule — relevant to landlords checking they have received complete documentation.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Reg 1.3 · 'An EICR provided after periodic inspection and testing shall be accompanied by a Condition Report Inspection Schedule'

top_improvements.action: Clarify Part P scope: add a sentence distinguishing notifiable work (new circuits, CU replacement) from non-notifiable work (like-for-like replacements). content_gaps.missing: Visual inspection is listed as a mandatory component of initial verification and periodic inspection alongside the electrical tests, but the page's testing list omits it entirely. The page lists six electrical tests but misses visual inspection and earthing/bonding verification checks, which GN3 places at the same level.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 3.8.3: 'covers visual inspection, verification of earthing and bonding, continuity, insulation resistance, earth fault loop impedance, RCD operation and functional and polarity checks as part of initial and periodic inspection and testing.'

content_gaps.missing: The EICR observation code system (C1 = Danger present, C2 = Potentially dangerous, C3 = Improvement recommended, FI = Further investigation) is never mentioned. Homeowners asking about EICR results need this to interpret what the electrician hands them.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] Reg 3.10.3: 'An EICR records observations, defects, and any codes (C1, C2, C3, FI), and provides recommendations.' [GN3] Reg 3.12: 'C3 or records an observation, this shall be documented on the EICR with sufficient description for the responsible person to understand the issue.'

content_gaps.missing: The page says an EIC is issued for 'new installations' and a Minor Works Certificate for additions. It never explains the precise trigger: BS 7671 Reg 120.3 specifies that a MEIWC is valid only where the work does NOT extend to the introduction of new circuits. A homeowner told they need a 'new circuit' for a shower or EV charger needs to know an EIC is mandatory.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 120.3: 'For an addition or alteration which does not extend to the introduction of new circuits, a Minor Electrical Installation Works Certificate may be used instead of an Electrical Installation Certificate.'

content_gaps.missing: The defect-recording obligation on certificates is not mentioned: BS 7671 Reg 644.1.2 requires that any defects or omissions discovered in the existing installation must be recorded on the EIC or MEIWC even when the electrician is only doing an addition. This is important consumer information — a good electrician flags existing problems.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 644.1.2: 'the person responsible... shall record discovered defects in the existing installation on that certificate... irrespective of certificate type.'

top_improvements.action: Add the Schedule of Tests to the 'What to Expect' certification bullet. Currently the page says you receive a 'certificate with all test results recorded' — but electricians issue two documents: the certificate itself AND the Schedule of Tests. Homeowners should know to ask for both and what the Schedule contains (measured Zs values, IR readings, RCD trip times). This is a practical gap that also strengthens E-E-A-T.

top_improvements.action: Add a sentence on the defect-recording obligation: a good electrician will note any pre-existing faults in the existing installation on your certificate, even if they did not cause them. This is a legal requirement under BS 7671. Positioning it as a quality indicator ('a professional electrician will flag any pre-existing issues in writing') turns a technical requirement into a consumer-facing trust signal and differentiates quality operators.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 644.1.2 — discovered defects must be recorded on EIC or MEIWC

confirmed_issues.claim: Periodic EICRs every 3 to 5 years for industrial premises
confirmed_issues.evidence: The GN3 RAG actively undermines this claim. GN3 Reg 1.3 facets confirm 5 years as the statutory maximum for private rented domestic properties. GN3 Reg 3.4 facet (context_prefix: 'example: adjusting interval due to heavy industrial use') states that heavy industrial use causes faster deterioration and that the dutyholder 'may judge that the interval to periodic inspection should be shorter than the designer's original recommendation'. GN3 Reg 3.5 facet confirms installations must not be left without attention for 'the periods of years that are normally allowed between formal inspections' and that 'heavy industrial use shortens recommended interval'. Critically, no GN3 facet gives a '3 to 5 year' band for industrial premises — the RAG's GN3 content only yields a 5-year domestic/rented figure and a general principle that industrial premises warrant shorter (not longer) intervals. The page presents '3 to 5 years' as a defined industrial EICR range, but the RAG supports no lower bound of 3 years as a standard industrial interval, and GN3 implies intervals shorter than 5 years are expected for industrial use. The specific '3 to 5 years' figure is ungrounded and the framing is misleading.

content_gaps.missing: AFDD requirement under A4:2026 (Reg 421.1.7) — mandatory arc fault detection for new dwellings and certain premises since April 2026. Industrial electricians carrying out domestic EICR or new-build work need to understand this. Page mentions nothing about A4:2026 changes that affect industrial cross-over work.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 133.1.3 (A4:2026): 'certain usage of equipment shall be recorded on the appropriate electrical certification' — AFDD and SPD usage must now be recorded on Part 6 forms.

content_gaps.missing: Motor circuit protective device coordination — gM vs aM fuse-link selection (full-range vs partial-range), and back-up OCPD requirement (Reg 536.4.2.2). The page discusses overload tripping but omits the BS 7671-specific protective device coordination rules for motor circuits.
content_gaps.rag_source: [OSG] Reg 7.2.7 on gM/aM fuse-links; [BS 7671] Reg 536.4.2.2 on back-up protection for overload relays.

content_gaps.missing: Periodic inspection interval is not a fixed BS 7671 prescription — BS 7671 Reg 644.4 requires the designer to record a recommended interval on the EIC. The page states 'every 3 to 5 years' as if this is a fixed rule; the correct framing is that the interval is determined by the installation type, use, and risk — with GN3 guidance tables providing typical maxima. Industrial/commercial premises often warrant 3-year maximum under GN3 Table.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 644.4 / 134.2.2: 'The recommendation for the interval between initial verification and the first periodic inspection shall be recorded on the Electrical Installation Certificate.'

content_gaps.missing: PEN conductor prohibition in consumer installations (Reg 411.4.3) — relevant when discussing industrial TN-C-S supplies. The page mentions 11kV incoming supplies but does not explain TN-C-S supply arrangements or PEN conductor rules that industrial electricians encounter at the point of supply.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] Reg 411.4.3: 'PEN conductor means a combined Protective Earth (PE) and Neutral (N) conductor... PEN conductors occur in TN-C and TN-C-S arrangements.'

top_improvements.action: Correct and expand the periodic EICR interval claim. Replace 'every 3 to 5 years' with an accurate BS 7671-grounded statement: BS 7671 Reg 644.4 requires the designer to record a recommended interval on the EIC; GN3 guidance recommends a maximum of 3 years for industrial/commercial premises in normal use, shorter for harsh environments. This avoids misleading industrial clients who may be holding off inspections based on a 5-year assumption.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] Reg 644.4 / 134.2.2 — periodic inspection interval is designer-recommended, not a fixed 3-5yr rule

top_improvements.action: Add a section or callout on A4:2026 changes relevant to industrial electricians: (1) Reg 133.1.3 — SPD and AFDD usage must now be recorded on Part 6 certificates, affecting every industrial EICR and EIC issued post-April 2026; (2) AFDD requirement context for any domestic/light commercial crossover work. This positions the page as current and boosts E-E-A-T for the post-A4:2026 content refresh cycle.

Industrial electricians specifying or selecting motor circuit protection need this detail — it makes the motor control section more technically defensible and adds unique depth versus competitor guides.

top_improvements.action: Add Reg 643.9 phase rotation verification to the three-phase commissioning content: 'BS 7671 Reg 643.9 requires phase rotation to be confirmed at the point closest to the load before energising — not just at the incomer.' The practical_work_intelligence RAG explicitly cites this as a common mistake. This is a concrete, citable BS 7671 requirement that lifts the page above generic motor commissioning advice.

confirmed_issues.claim: The final outcome should make sense when the client reads it back later (satisfactory, unsatisfactory, or limited in scope).
confirmed_issues.evidence: GN3 Reg 3.11 [acceptance_criterion] (GN3 9th Ed:2022 A4, Chapter 3, Reg 3.11): 'An overall assessment of satisfactory on the EICR is not permitted where any observation has been coded C1 or C2. Presence of any C1 or C2 code shall result in an unsatisfactory overall assessment.' The RAG enumerates exactly two formal overall-assessment outcomes: Satisfactory and Unsatisfactory. No GN3, BS 7671, or OSG facet recognises 'limited in scope' as a third overall-assessment label. The phrase 'limited in scope' appears in BS 7671 Part 7 special-location facets only as a regulatory-scope qualifier, never as an EICR outcome. The page therefore presents a non-existent third outcome that could mislead inspectors completing the overall-assessment box.

confirmed_issues.claim: Sign off only when every box is filled in or explicitly marked LIM (limitation) with a reason.
confirmed_issues.evidence: No GN3, BS 7671, or OSG facet validates 'LIM' as a formal notation on the EICR schedule. GN3 schedule facets reference 'NP' (Not Permitted) as a defined abbreviation (GN3 Ch.1 Reg 1.03) and require recording the test voltage on the Schedule of Test Results (GN3 Ch.2 Reg 2.24), but no facet defines 'LIM' as a recognised cell-level code. PWI confirms 'limitation' and 'extent' are key EICR terms and that omitting them is a common mistake, but the specific abbreviation 'LIM' is not validated anywhere in the RAG. The page presents this as a formal notation without RAG support.

content_gaps.missing: A4:2026 introduced AFDD (Reg 421.1.7) requirements for certain final circuits supplying socket-outlets. An EICR template/example page should note that post-A4:2026 inspections may need to check for AFDD presence as a condition item, potentially generating C3 observations on older installations.
content_gaps.rag_source: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7: 'arc fault detection devices (AFDDs)' — defined and required for certain socket-outlet circuits.

content_gaps.missing: GN3 requires exactly ONE classification code per observation (C1, C2, C3 or FI — not combined). The page says 'use the codes literally' but omits this critical one-code-per-observation rule, which is a common field error.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 3 · Reg 3.11: 'Only one classification code is to be recorded against each observation.'

content_gaps.missing: FI (Further Investigation) is described by GN3 as used 'exceptionally' — distinct from C1/C2/C3 in nature. The page lists FI alongside the C-codes without this important qualification, which affects how inspectors should apply it.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 3 · Reg 3.11: 'coded C1, C2 or C3 as appropriate, or, exceptionally, FI.'

content_gaps.missing: The EICR must be accompanied by Schedule(s) of Circuit Details AND Schedule(s) of Test Results — the page only mentions 'schedule of test results' and omits the separate Schedule of Circuit Details, which is a required deliverable per GN3 Reg 5.7.
content_gaps.rag_source: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 5 · Reg 5.7: 'An EICR shall be issued with the appropriate number of generic Schedule(s) of Circuit Details and Schedule(s) of Test Results.'

seo.notes: The heroSubtitle is a generic framing sentence, not a direct answer to 'what does a good EICR example look like?' — there is no concise answer block immediately under the H1. The page has 4 FAQ entries which will generate FAQ schema. Internal links: /guides/eicr-what-to-include, /guides/schedule-of-test-results, /tools/eicr-certificate, /guides/eicr-common-mistakes, /guides/eicr-limitations = 5 links. No author byline, no qualification credentials, no E-E-A-T attribution anywhere on the rendered config. The meta description is accurate but passive — 'what a strong EICR example or template should include' could be rewritten with a stronger intent-matching opener. Title tag length is 62 chars — acceptable. The page targets a navigational/informational intent ('eicr example and template') but does not front-load a clear, concrete answer in the first paragraph.

top_improvements.action: Add a direct-answer paragraph immediately under the H1 (first block of the first section) stating what an EICR example/template must contain per GN3: client details, extent of inspection, agreed limitations, Schedule(s) of Circuit Details, Schedule(s) of Test Results, Section K observations each coded with a single C1/C2/C3 or (exceptionally) FI code, and the overall outcome. This addresses the missing answer-first block and grounds the page in GN3 Reg 3.11 / 5.7.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 3 · Reg 3.11 and Chapter 5 · Reg 5.7

top_improvements.action: Add a one-code-per-observation rule to the observations guidance: 'Assign exactly one code — C1, C2, C3 or (exceptionally) FI — to each observation. Do not combine codes or leave any observation unclassified.' This plugs a genuine accuracy gap and is one of the most common field errors per the RAG data.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 3 · Reg 3.11: 'Only one classification code is to be recorded against each observation.'

top_improvements.action: Add a Schedule of Circuit Details callout alongside the Schedule of Test Results — the page currently only references the test results schedule. The template should flag that GN3 requires both documents to be issued with every EICR.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [GN3] GN3 9th Ed:2022 (A4) · Chapter 5 · Reg 5.7: 'An EICR shall be issued with the appropriate number of generic Schedule(s) of Circuit Details and Schedule(s) of Test Results.'

top_improvements.action: Add a practical warning: do not issue an Electrical Installation Certificate for periodic inspection work — always use the EICR. This is flagged as the top common mistake in multiple PWI records and is a concrete, high-value tip for a template/example page.
top_improvements.grounded_in: practical_work_intelligence: common_mistakes: 'Using Electrical Installation Certificate instead of EICR for periodic inspection.'

top_improvements.action: Add a brief A4:2026 note: post-October 2026 EICRs should check for AFDD presence on final circuits supplying socket-outlets (Reg 421.1.7). Absence on a new installation may warrant a C3 observation. This makes the page forward-looking and best-in-class for the A4 update cycle.
top_improvements.grounded_in: [BS 7671] 2018+A4:2026 · Part 4 · Chapter 42 · Reg 421.1.7 — AFDD requirement for certain socket-outlet final circuits.

content_gaps.missing: The page references updating a competent person scheme registration (NICEIC, NAPIT) when changing business structure, but gives no guidance on what that actually involves, typical costs (NICEIC Ltd company registration is a separate fee), or how long it takes. This is a key practical electrician-specific concern that distinguishes this page from generic business-structure guides.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence — no facet covers CPS scheme transfer costs/timelines. This is a genuine content gap for electrician-specific depth.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of Public Liability Insurance (PLI) and Employers Liability Insurance (ELI) cost differences by structure. A sole trader PLI typically costs £150-£400/year; an Ltd company policy is often higher and must be in the company name. This is a direct cost affecting the incorporation break-even calculation.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence — no facet covers insurance cost differentials by structure.

content_gaps.missing: No coverage of the CIS (Construction Industry Scheme) interaction with business structure. Many electricians work on construction sites where CIS applies — sole traders suffer 20% deduction at source, Ltd companies can apply for gross payment status at £30k threshold. This is a major cash-flow differentiator for the target audience.
content_gaps.rag_source: practical_work_intelligence — no facet covers CIS. This is a significant electrician-specific gap.

content_gaps.missing: No mention of the Employment Allowance for Ltd companies (currently £10,500/year employer NI relief for 2026/27) — this materially affects the salary/dividend optimisation calculation and changes the break-even point.
content_gaps.rag_source: No RAG facet covers Employment Allowance. This is a numerical accuracy gap that affects the tax comparison table's reliability.

seo.notes: The page leads with 5 key takeaways immediately under the hero, which functions as an answer-first block — good for featured snippet eligibility. GuideTemplate almost certainly renders FAQ schema markup (structured data) given the faqs prop pattern. Internal links: 6 relatedPages links plus breadcrumb and 1 SEOAppBridge CTA = approximately 8-9 total. No author attribution or E-E-A-T signals (no named author, no credentials, no 'reviewed by qualified accountant' badge). This is a significant weakness for YMYL (financial advice) content — Google applies higher scrutiny to financial guidance pages. The title tag 'Partnership vs Sole Trader vs Ltd for Electricians UK 2026' is well-optimised with year signal. The description mentions '2026/27 figures' but is truncated in the meta — may lose the year signal in SERPs. No schema for the tax comparison table data. readingTime of 14 minutes is likely overestimated for this content volume.

top_improvements.action: Fix the confirmation statement fee from £13 to £34 throughout the page (appears in the admin burden table). This is a factual error on a page claiming 2026/27 accuracy — Companies House raised the fee to £34 in May 2024. Leaving it at £13 undermines credibility.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Companies House fee schedule — contradicted claim, no RAG facet but verifiable external fact.

top_improvements.action: Add a dedicated CIS (Construction Industry Scheme) section or callout. For electricians working on construction sites, CIS deductions at 20% (sole trader/standard Ltd) vs gross payment status eligibility (Ltd at £30k threshold) is a major financial differentiator that directly affects the structure decision. This electrician-specific angle makes the page meaningfully better than generic accountancy guides.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Content gap — no RAG facet; HMRC CIS guidance SI 2005/2045.

top_improvements.action: Add Making Tax Digital (MTD) for ITSA callout in the sole trader admin burden section. MTD for Income Tax applies from April 2026 (income >£50k) and April 2027 (income >£30k), requiring quarterly digital submissions — this materially changes the 'sole trader = simple admin' narrative for higher-earning electricians and is a significant omission given the page covers 2026/27.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Content gap — no RAG facet; Finance Act 2021 + HMRC MTD rollout schedule.

top_improvements.action: Add author E-E-A-T attribution: a named reviewer line such as 'Reviewed by [Name], chartered accountant specialising in trades businesses' with a brief bio. This is YMYL financial content — Google's Quality Rater Guidelines penalise financial pages without clear authorship and expertise signals. Even a generic 'verified by an FCA-registered accountant' badge improves trust.
top_improvements.grounded_in: SEO gap — no author_eeat on a YMYL financial page.

top_improvements.action: Add Employment Allowance (£10,500 employer NI relief for 2026/27) to the Ltd tax section and recalibrate the break-even narrative. A single-director Ltd where the director is the only employee cannot claim Employment Allowance — but a Ltd with one or more employees can. This distinction matters and its omission makes the tax comparison slightly overstate Ltd costs for growing businesses.
top_improvements.grounded_in: Content gap — affects numerical accuracy of tax comparison table.
